topics.title: Heat Transfer by Natural Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer by Natural Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Transfer by Natural Convection (Free Convection)** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the bulk fluid motion is generated **solely by buoyancy-induced density differences** caused by temperature gradients within the fluid — without any external agency like a pump, fan, or blower.

In the context of the KTU PCBBL507 laboratory experiment, a test cylinder (typically brass) is heated electrically and allowed to lose heat to the surrounding quiescent (still) air. The rate of heat loss depends on the **convective heat transfer coefficient (h)**, which is in turn a function of the geometry, the physical properties of air, and the temperature difference between the surface and the ambient.

The governing dimensionless groups that govern this process are:

| Symbol | Group | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nu** | Nusselt Number | Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer |
| **Gr** | Grashof Number | Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces |
| **Pr** | Prandtl Number | Ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity |
| **Ra** | Rayleigh Number | Product of Gr and Pr; governs transition to turbulence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Chai Cup" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a hot cup of chai placed on a table in a still room.** You do not blow on it — yet you see the steam rise and the surface cools down. Why? The air in immediate contact with the hot cup gets warmed up, becomes less dense, and *naturally* rises (like a hot-air balloon). Cooler, denser air from the surroundings rushes in to replace it, gets heated, and rises in turn. This self-sustaining circulation loop is **natural convection** — driven entirely by nature's tendency to mix hot (light) fluid upward and cold (heavy) fluid downward. No fan, no pump. Just **gravity acting on a density difference**.

The **laboratory analogue** of this everyday phenomenon is the heated test cylinder mounted vertically. Power input (Volts × Amps) supplies energy; thermocouples measure surface and ambient air temperatures; and from the heat balance, we extract `h`, then `Nu`, and finally plot it against `(Gr · Pr)` to verify the classical correlations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Properties of Air (for quick reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For air at an average film temperature of ~50 °C (323 K), the following properties are typically used in this experiment:
> - **Thermal conductivity, k** ≈ **0.02735 W/(m·K)**
> - **Kinematic viscosity, ν** ≈ **1.798 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**
> - **Prandtl number, Pr** ≈ **0.6987** (≈ 0.71)
> - **Volumetric expansion coefficient, β = 1/T_film** (K⁻¹)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity, g** = **9.81 m/s²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The phenomenon of natural convection in this experiment proceeds through the following sequence:

- **Step 1 — Heat Generation:** A nichrome heating element wound around (or embedded inside) the brass test cylinder supplies electrical power $Q = V \times I$. This power, under steady state, is entirely dissipated to the surrounding air by natural convection and a small amount of radiation.
- **Step 2 — Temperature Field Formation:** A thermal boundary layer develops adjacent to the cylinder surface. The thickness of this layer $\delta_t$ grows as one moves away from the leading edge (in vertical orientation, from the bottom).
- **Step 3 — Buoyancy-Driven Flow:** Within the boundary layer, density gradients ($\rho_{cold} > \rho_{hot}$) cause buoyancy forces to overcome viscous forces once the **Grashof number** is large enough. This generates a slow upward plume of warm air.
- **Step 4 — Energy Balance at Steady State:** All electrical energy supplied equals the convective loss (radiation is either neglected or corrected for using emissivity). This gives: $Q = h \, A_s (T_s - T_a)$.
- **Step 5 — Non-Dimensionalization:** The result is expressed in the universal form $Nu = f(Gr, Pr)$, eliminating dependence on fluid type, size, and absolute temperature.
- **Step 6 — Validation:** The experimentally determined Nu is plotted against $(Gr \cdot Pr)$ on a log-log plot, and its slope and intercept are compared to the standard theoretical correlations.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Dimensionless Number

- **Grashof Number (Gr)** — quantifies the ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces. It is the *natural-convection analogue* of the Reynolds number used in forced convection. A higher Gr means stronger buoyancy-driven flow.
- **Prandtl Number (Pr)** — characterizes the relative thickness of the velocity and thermal boundary layers. For gases like air, Pr ≈ 0.7, meaning both layers grow at nearly the same rate.
- **Rayleigh Number (Ra = Gr · Pr)** — combines the two to give an overall criterion. The flow regime transitions from **laminar to turbulent** at $Ra \approx 10^9$.
- **Nusselt Number (Nu)** — the *experimental* output. It tells us "how much better is convection than pure conduction across the same distance L?"

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on this experiment.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Heat input rate | $Q = V \cdot I$ | W |
| 2 | Heat flux | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, D \, L}$ | W/m² |
| 3 | Surface area (cylinder) | $A_s = \pi \, D \, L$ | m² |
| 4 | Temperature difference | $\Delta T = T_s - T_a$ | K or °C |
| 5 | Heat transfer coefficient | $h = \dfrac{Q}{A_s \, \Delta T}$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 6 | Nusselt number | $Nu = \dfrac{h \, D}{k_{air}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Grashof number | $Gr = \dfrac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, D^3}{\nu^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Volumetric expansion coeff. (ideal gas) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{T_{film}} = \dfrac{2}{T_s + T_a}$ | K⁻¹ |
| 9 | Prandtl number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \, C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Rayleigh number | $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Film temperature | $T_{film} = \dfrac{T_s + T_a}{2}$ | K |

### 2.4 Standard Correlations for Natural Convection (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **empirical correlations** the KTU examiner expects you to quote. The exact form depends on the geometry used in your college apparatus (vertical cylinder is most common).

**For a vertical cylinder (treated as vertical plate when $L \gg D$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^4 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^9 &: \quad Nu = 0.59 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4} \\[4pt]
10^9 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^{13} &: \quad Nu = 0.10 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a horizontal cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^3 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^9 &: \quad Nu = 0.53 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4} \\[4pt]
10^9 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^{12} &: \quad Nu = 0.13 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Natural convection governs the design of:
- **Transformer cooling tanks** and overhead transmission line conductors
- **Electronic chip heat sinks** (passive cooling)
- **Building ventilation and double-glazed window cavities**
- **Nuclear reactor containment cooling during accident scenarios**
- **Solar collector thermal storage tanks**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Power Input to Nusselt Number

The objective is to show rigorously how raw experimental readings (V, I, T_s, T_a) are processed into the dimensionless Nusselt number, ready for comparison with theory.

### Step 1 — Compute the heat input from the heater

The nichrome coil dissipates electrical power which, under steady state, is transferred entirely to the ambient air by natural convection (neglecting radiation loss for a first pass).

$$Q = V \times I$$

where $V$ is the voltmeter reading in Volts and $I$ is the ammeter reading in Amperes. The result $Q$ is in Watts.

### Step 2 — Compute the convective surface area

For a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ and heated length $L$:

$$A_s = \pi \, D \, L$$

The diameter $D$ is measured with vernier callipers and $L$ is the length of the heated test section, both converted to **metres** before substitution.

### Step 3 — Compute the heat flux

$$q = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, D \, L}$$

This is in units of W/m².

### Step 4 — Compute the temperature difference (driving force)

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a$$

where $T_s$ is the steady-state average surface temperature (in °C or K) read from the thermocouple in contact with the cylinder, and $T_a$ is the ambient air temperature. Note that the numerical *value* of a temperature difference is identical in K and in °C.

### Step 5 — Compute the convective heat transfer coefficient

By Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$h = \frac{q}{\Delta T} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, D \, L \, (T_s - T_a)}$$

This $h$ is the **key experimental result** of the entire lab session, expressed in W/(m²·K).

### Step 6 — Compute the Nusselt number

The Nusselt number is defined as the ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance across the characteristic length $D$:

$$Nu = \frac{h \, D}{k_{air}}$$

The value of $k_{air}$ must be evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_{film}$ (using standard property tables or correlations). The Nusselt number is dimensionless.

### Step 7 — Compute the Grashof number

The Grashof number represents the ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces:

$$Gr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, D^3}{\nu^2}$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity of air evaluated at $T_{film}$. The volumetric expansion coefficient for an ideal gas (air) is:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{T_{film}}$$

with $T_{film}$ in **Kelvin**.

### Step 8 — Compute the Rayleigh number

$$Ra = Gr \times Pr$$

For air in the temperature range of the experiment, $Pr \approx 0.71$ and is essentially constant, so $Ra$ is directly proportional to $Gr$.

### Step 9 — Verify the correlation

Plot $\log(Nu)$ against $\log(Gr \cdot Pr)$. The slope should be **0.25** (laminar) or **0.333** (turbulent), and the intercept should match the theoretical constant (0.59 or 0.10 for vertical; 0.53 or 0.13 for horizontal).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example:** A vertical brass test cylinder of diameter $D = 0.040$ m and heated length $L = 0.300$ m is supplied with $V = 60$ V and $I = 1.5$ A. The average surface temperature stabilizes at $T_s = 80 \, °C$ and ambient air is at $T_a = 30 \, °C$. Take air properties at the film temperature as $k = 0.028$ W/(m·K), $\nu = 1.9 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.71$. Determine $h$, $Nu$, $Gr$, and $Ra$, and identify the flow regime.

**Step 1 — Heat input:**

$$Q = V \times I = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Surface area:**

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.040 \times 0.300 = 0.03770 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Film temperature (for property evaluation):**

$$T_{film} = \frac{T_s + T_a}{2} = \frac{353 + 303}{2} = 328 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Convective heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h = \frac{Q}{A_s \, \Delta T} = \frac{90}{0.03770 \times 50} = 47.75 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 6 — Nusselt number:**

$$Nu = \frac{h \, D}{k} = \frac{47.75 \times 0.040}{0.028} = 68.21$$

**Step 7 — Grashof number** (with $\beta = 1/T_{film}$):

$$Gr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, D^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times (1/328) \times 50 \times (0.040)^3}{(1.9 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Gr = \frac{9.81 \times 0.003049 \times 50 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{3.61 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Gr = \frac{9.572 \times 10^{-5}}{3.61 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.651 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 8 — Rayleigh number:**

$$Ra = Gr \times Pr = 2.651 \times 10^5 \times 0.71 = 1.882 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 9 — Identify regime:** Since $10^4 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^9$, the flow is **laminar**. The applicable correlation is $Nu = 0.59 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4}$:

$$Nu_{theory} = 0.59 \times (1.882 \times 10^5)^{0.25} = 0.59 \times 20.88 = 12.32$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** The mismatch between $Nu_{experimental} = 68.21$ and $Nu_{theory} = 12.32$ in this example is **intentionally large** to highlight that in real apparatus there are radiation losses, end-conduction effects, and uncertainties in thermocouple placement. In your actual lab report, you must either correct for radiation (using an emissivity value) or discuss sources of error. A naive "they don't match ⇒ experiment failed" answer will lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below is a **block diagram** of the apparatus used in Module Experiment 2. It maps the flow of energy and information from the power source through the heated test cylinder to the data acquisition system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Variable AC/DC Power Supply] --> AMM[Ammeter A]
    PWR --> VLT[Voltmeter V]
    AMM --> HEATER[Nichrome Heating Coil]
    VLT --> HEATER
    HEATER --> CYL[Brass Test Cylinder Vertical or Horizontal]
    CYL --> TC1[Surface Thermocouple T sub s]
    CYL --> TC2[Ambient Air Thermocouple T sub a]
    TC1 --> DAQ[Digital Temperature Indicator]
    TC2 --> DAQ
    DAQ --> CALC[Calculation of h Nu Gr Ra]
    CALC --> PLOT[Log Log Plot of Nu vs Gr times Pr]
    PLOT --> VALIDATE[Compare with Standard Correlation]
```

## 4.2 Logical Calculation Flow — From Raw Data to Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure V and I from meters] --> B[Compute Q equals V times I]
    A2[Measure D and L of cylinder] --> C[Compute A sub s equals pi D L]
    A3[Measure T sub s and T sub a at steady state] --> D[Compute Delta T and T sub film]
    B --> E[Compute h equals Q over A sub s times Delta T]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    D --> F[Compute beta equals 1 over T sub film in K]
    F --> G[Compute Gr from g beta Delta T D cubed over nu squared]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Compute Nu equals h D over k]
    G --> I[Compute Ra equals Gr times Pr]
    H --> J[Plot Nu versus Gr times Pr on log log axes]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compare slope and intercept with standard correlation]
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Typical Vertical Test Cylinder (ASCII Representation)

```
                  ┌───── T_s (Surface TC) ─────┐
                  │                            │
                  │   ┌──────────────────┐     │
                  │   │  Nichrome Coil   │     │
                  │   │ ┌──────────────┐ │     │
                  │   │ │ Brass Cyl.   │ │     │
                  │   │ │     D = 4 cm │ │     │
                  │   │ │     L = 30 cm│ │     │
                  │   │ │              │ │     │
                  │   │ └──────────────┘ │     │
                  │   └──────────────────┘     │
                  │         ↑                  │
                  │       Heater               │
                  │     leads to V,I            │
                  │                            │
                  │   ↓ Buoyancy-driven ↑       │
                  │   ↑  air plume (hot)  ↓     │
                  │                            │
                  └───── T_a (Ambient TC) ─────┘
                       (placed ~10 cm away)
```

> [!TIP]
> In the **vertical orientation**, the heated plume rises against gravity, and the thermal boundary layer grows from the bottom upward. In the **horizontal orientation**, the plume rises perpendicularly and then drifts sideways, leading to slightly different correlations (Section 2.4). Always check with your lab manual which orientation your college apparatus uses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`**
> **Q1.** Define the **Grashof number** and state its physical significance in natural convection.

**Model Answer:**
The Grashof number is defined as:

$$Gr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

It represents the ratio of **buoyancy forces to viscous forces** acting on a fluid element near a heated surface. Physically, it is the natural-convection analogue of the Reynolds number in forced convection: it determines whether the flow regime is laminar or turbulent. A higher Gr implies stronger buoyancy-driven flow. For $Gr \cdot Pr < 10^9$, the boundary layer flow is **laminar**; above $10^9$, it transitions to **turbulent**.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]`**
> **Q2.** Why is the **Nusselt number** equal to 1 when a fluid is stagnant (no convection)?

**Model Answer:**
By definition, $Nu = hL / k$. When there is no fluid motion, the only mode of heat transfer is conduction, and the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ reduces to the conductive value $k/L$ (Fourier's law across a stagnant film of thickness $L$). Substituting:

$$Nu = \frac{(k/L) \cdot L}{k} = 1$$

A value of $Nu = 1$ therefore means convection has not enhanced heat transfer beyond pure conduction. Higher values of Nu (typically 10–100 in this experiment) confirm that natural convection is actively transporting heat.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q3]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat labelled schematic, describe the **apparatus and procedure** used to determine the convective heat transfer coefficient for natural convection from a vertical cylinder. List the major sources of error.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a vertical cylinder of diameter $D = 0.05$ m, the following data is obtained at steady state: $V = 75$ V, $I = 2.0$ A, $T_s = 95 \, °C$, $T_a = 32 \, °C$, $L = 0.40$ m. Air properties at film temperature: $k = 0.029$ W/(m·K), $\nu = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.71$. Calculate $h$, $Nu$, $Gr$, $Ra$, and verify the correlation.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### (a) Apparatus, Procedure & Sources of Error

**Apparatus consists of:**
1. **Brass test cylinder** (the heated surface) — vertical orientation.
2. **Nichrome heating coil** wound non-inductively around the cylinder, electrically insulated by a mica/ceramic layer.
3. **Variac (variable transformer)** to vary the input voltage.
4. **Ammeter and Voltmeter** to record power input.
5. **Two thermocouples (Chromel-Alumel / K-type):** one attached to the cylinder surface (T_s), one placed in surrounding air (T_a).
6. **Digital temperature indicator** with cold-junction compensation.
7. **Insulated end plates / wooden supports** to minimize end losses.

**Procedure:**
- Step 1: Measure the diameter $D$ of the cylinder (vernier callipers) and the heated length $L$.
- Step 2: Connect the heater to the variac, ammeter, and voltmeter as shown in the schematic.
- Step 3: Switch ON the variac and slowly increase the voltage to a preset value.
- Step 4: Wait **20–30 minutes** for the system to reach **steady state** (when $T_s$ and $T_a$ show negligible variation for ~5 minutes).
- Step 5: Record $V$, $I$, $T_s$, and $T_a$.
- Step 6: Repeat for **4–5 different power settings** to cover a range of $\Delta T$.

**Major Sources of Error:**
- Heat loss by **radiation** (especially at higher $T_s$), not accounted for in the simple energy balance.
- **End losses** from the top and bottom of the cylinder (conduction through supports and convection from end caps).
- **Uncertainty in thermocouple placement** and contact resistance at the surface.
- **Natural air currents** (droughts, AC vents) disturbing the still-air assumption.
- Variac voltage fluctuations causing non-constant $Q$.

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Heat input:**

$$Q = V \times I = 75 \times 2.0 = 150 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Surface area:**

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.40 = 0.06283 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 95 - 32 = 63 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Convective heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h = \frac{Q}{A_s \Delta T} = \frac{150}{0.06283 \times 63} = 37.90 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Nusselt number:**

$$Nu = \frac{h D}{k} = \frac{37.90 \times 0.05}{0.029} = 65.34$$

**Step 6 — Film temperature and β:**

$$T_{film} = \frac{(95 + 273) + (32 + 273)}{2} = 336.5 \text{ K}$$

$$\beta = \frac{1}{336.5} = 2.972 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Grashof number:**

$$Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T D^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 2.972 \times 10^{-3} \times 63 \times (0.05)^3}{(2.1 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Gr = \frac{9.81 \times 2.972 \times 10^{-3} \times 63 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Gr = \frac{2.297 \times 10^{-4}}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.21 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 8 — Rayleigh number:**

$$Ra = Gr \times Pr = 5.21 \times 10^5 \times 0.71 = 3.70 \times 10^5$$

**Step 9 — Identify regime and verify correlation:**

Since $10^4 < Gr \cdot Pr = 3.70 \times 10^5 < 10^9$, the flow is **laminar**, and we use:

$$Nu_{theory} = 0.59 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4} = 0.59 \times (3.70 \times 10^5)^{0.25} = 0.59 \times 24.62 = 14.53$$

**Comparison and Discussion:** Experimental $Nu = 65.34$ is about 4.5× the theoretical value of 14.53. The discrepancy is attributable to:
- **Radiation heat loss** being included in the calculated $h$, artificially inflating it.
- Possible **end effects** adding extra surface area.
- Property evaluation at an average film temperature (true local properties vary along the cylinder height).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T_s$ and $T_a$ to Kelvin** before computing $\beta$ and $T_{film}$ is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in this experiment. $\beta = 1/T$ demands absolute (Kelvin) temperature.
> 2. **Mixing units** (cm with m, °C with K in differences is fine, but NOT in absolute temperature for $\beta$) will cost you 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the regime** (laminar vs turbulent) explicitly before applying the correlation — examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Plotting raw Nu vs ΔT** instead of log(Nu) vs log(Gr·Pr) — the question explicitly asks for verification of the correlation, which requires a log-log plot.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention radiation correction** in the error analysis — this is a near-guaranteed follow-up question in viva.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between **forced and natural convection**. Why does the Nusselt number depend on $Gr \cdot Pr$ in natural convection but on $Re \cdot Pr$ in forced convection?

**(b)** [7 Marks] The experimentally obtained Nusselt numbers for a horizontal cylinder are: $Nu_1 = 18.5$ at $Gr_1 \cdot Pr = 2.1 \times 10^5$, and $Nu_2 = 41.2$ at $Gr_2 \cdot Pr = 1.5 \times 10^7$. Determine the **form of the correlation** $Nu = C(Gr \cdot Pr)^n$ that best fits these two data points.

#### (a) Model Answer (Conceptual)

**Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is caused by an **external agency** — a pump, fan, compressor, or the wind. The flow velocity is an independent variable.

**Natural Convection:** Fluid motion is caused **internally** by buoyancy forces arising from density differences due to temperature gradients. There is no external pump.

**Why the difference in correlation form:**
- In **forced convection**, the driving force is the externally imposed velocity, and the appropriate dimensionless group comparing inertia to viscous forces is the **Reynolds number**, $Re = \rho u L / \mu$. The Nusselt number correlates as $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$.
- In **natural convection**, the driving force is buoyancy, captured by the **Grashof number**, $Gr = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$. The Nusselt number correlates as $Nu = f(Gr, Pr)$ or equivalently $Nu = f(Gr \cdot Pr, Pr)$.
- For gases like air, $Pr \approx 0.71$ is essentially constant, so we simplify to $Nu = C(Gr \cdot Pr)^n$.

#### (b) Model Answer (Numerical)

We have two data points: $(x_1, y_1) = (2.1 \times 10^5, 18.5)$ and $(x_2, y_2) = (1.5 \times 10^7, 41.2)$, where $x = Gr \cdot Pr$ and $y = Nu$, and the model is $y = C x^n$.

**Step 1 — Find n by taking the ratio:**

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^n \implies \frac{41.2}{18.5} = \left(\frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^5}\right)^n$$

$$2.227 = (71.43)^n$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(2.227) = n \cdot \ln(71.43) \implies 0.801 = n \times 4.269$$

$$n = \frac{0.801}{4.269} = 0.1876 \approx 0.19$$

**Step 2 — Find C using the first data point:**

$$C = \frac{y_1}{x_1^n} = \frac{18.5}{(2.1 \times 10^5)^{0.1876}}$$

$$(2.1 \times 10^5)^{0.1876} = e^{0.1876 \times \ln(2.1 \times 10^5)} = e^{0.1876 \times 12.255} = e^{2.299} = 9.964$$

$$C = \frac{18.5}{9.964} = 1.857$$

**Step 3 — Final correlation:**

$$\boxed{Nu = 1.86 \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^{0.19}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The exponent $n \approx 0.19$ is close to (but slightly less than) the classical laminar value of $0.25$. The constant $C = 1.86$ is much larger than the textbook $0.53$ for a horizontal cylinder, indicating that the experimental data was likely affected by radiation losses and apparatus-specific factors. In a viva, you should be prepared to explain this discrepancy.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize This Block**

- [x] **Definition:** Natural convection = heat transfer driven by buoyancy forces, **no external agency**.
- [x] **Driving force:** Density difference caused by $\Delta T$; the warmer fluid rises.
- [x] **Heat input equation:** $Q = V \times I$ (in **Watts**).
- [x] **Heat flux:** $q = Q / (\pi D L)$.
- [x] **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $h = q / \Delta T = V I / [\pi D L (T_s - T_a)]$.
- [x] **Four key dimensionless numbers:** $Nu$, $Gr$, $Pr$, $Ra$.
- [x] **$Nu$ definition:** $hD/k$ — convective vs conductive heat transfer.
- [x] **$Gr$ definition:** $g \beta \Delta T D^3 / \nu^2$ — buoyancy vs viscous forces.
- [x] **$\beta$ for ideal gas:** $\beta = 1/T_{film}$ with **$T_{film}$ in Kelvin**.
- [x] **$Pr$ for air:** ≈ 0.71 (essentially constant in the lab range).
- [x] **$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$** — flow is **laminar** if $Ra < 10^9$, **turbulent** if $Ra > 10^9$.
- [x] **Vertical cylinder correlation (laminar):** $Nu = 0.59 (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4}$.
- [x] **Vertical cylinder correlation (turbulent):** $Nu = 0.10 (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/3}$.
- [x] **Horizontal cylinder correlation (laminar):** $Nu = 0.53 (Gr \cdot Pr)^{1/4}$.
- [x] **Plot required:** $\log(Nu)$ vs $\log(Gr \cdot Pr)$ — slope should be **0.25** or **0.33**.
- [x] **Common units trap:** $D$ and $L$ must be in **metres**; $T$ in **Kelvin** for $\beta$.
- [x] **Top sources of error:** radiation losses, end losses, thermocouple placement, ambient air currents.
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** Transformer tanks, overhead conductors, passive electronic cooling, solar water heaters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer by Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Transfer by Forced Convection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Transfer by Forced Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the fluid motion is **induced by an external agency** such as a fan, blower, pump, or compressor — *not* by natural buoyancy forces arising from density differences. The rate of heat transfer is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**, expressed as: $$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where $h$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K), $A$ is the heat transfer area (m²), and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference between the surface and the fluid bulk. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Wind Chilling Your Hand":** > Imagine holding your hand near a candle. The warmth you feel is by **natural convection** — hot air rises on its own. Now, if you blow air from your mouth onto the hot surface of a kettle, the cooling is dramatically faster. The moving air *forces* heat away from the surface much more efficiently than still air. That is **forced convection** — a mechanical push accelerating the heat removal process. ### Physical Mechanism 1. A cooler fluid (typically air) is mechanically driven past a heated surface (a heated cylinder, flat plate, or tube). 2. A **thermal boundary layer** develops near the surface — a thin film where the temperature gradient is steepest. 3. Faster fluid velocity → thinner boundary layer → higher temperature gradient → **larger heat transfer coefficient ($h$)**. ### Key Distinguishing Parameters - **Forced convection** is characterized by high **Reynolds Number (Re)** since inertia dominates over buoyancy. - The **Nusselt Number (Nu)** is the dimensionless heat transfer coefficient used for correlations. - Standard ambient values used as benchmarks: **$T_\infty = 25^\circ C$**, **$P_{atm} = 101.325\ kPa$**. ## 1.3 Significance in KTU Lab Curriculum > [!IMPORTANT] > This experiment typically uses a **test apparatus** consisting of a heater, a test section (cylindrical or flat plate), an air supply system (blower + orifice meter), and temperature-measuring instruments (thermocouples, RTDs, thermometers). Students measure temperatures at various airflow rates, compute $h$, and validate against established empirical correlations (e.g., Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate, Churchill-Bernstein). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework ### Step 1 — Identifying the Mechanism Heat transfer is governed by three mechanisms: **conduction**, **convection**, and **radiation**. In forced convection: - Conduction occurs *within* the boundary layer at the molecular level. - Convection occurs due to bulk fluid motion imposed externally. - Radiation may be present at high temperatures but is typically neglected in lab conditions or subtracted using a calibrated loss term. ### Step 2 — The Thermal Boundary Layer As the fluid flows over the heated surface, a **thermal boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_t$ develops: - Inside $\delta_t$ → conduction + convection. - Outside $\delta_t$ → bulk fluid temperature $T_\infty$. The local convective coefficient is defined as: $$h_x = \frac{-k_f \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}}{T_s - T_\infty}$$ where $k_f$ is the fluid thermal conductivity and the partial derivative is the temperature gradient at the wall. ### Step 3 — Dimensional Analysis → Dimensionless Numbers By Buckingham's $\Pi$-theorem, the problem reduces to four dimensionless groups: $$\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re},\ \text{Pr})$$ This forms the backbone of all forced convection correlations. ### Step 4 — Empirical Correlations Depending on geometry and flow regime: | Geometry | Correlation | Regime | |----------|-------------|--------| | Flat plate (laminar) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{0.5}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$ | | Flat plate (turbulent) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037\,\text{Re}_L^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | $\text{Re}_L > 5 \times 10^5$ | | Flow in pipe (heating) | $\text{Nu} = 0.023\,\text{Re}^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^n$ | $\text{Re} > 10{,}000,\ n=0.4$ | | Cross-flow over cylinder | $\text{Nu} = C\,\text{Re}^m\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Based on table | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Board Examination & Lab Viva** | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |----------|---------|---------------| | Heat transfer rate | $Q = h A \Delta T$ | W | | Newton's law applied | $Q = m c_p (T_o - T_i)$ | W (energy balance) | | Air mass flow rate | $m = \rho \cdot A_o \cdot V_o$ | kg/s | | Velocity from orifice | $V_o = C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta h \cdot g}{\rho_{air}}}$ | m/s | | Reynolds number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | | Nusselt number | $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h D}{k_f}$ | dimensionless | | Prandtl number | $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k_f} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless | | Grashof number | $\text{Gr} = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | (used to verify forced dominance) | | Peclet number | $\text{Pe} = \text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}$ | dimensionless | | Stanton number | $\text{St} = \dfrac{\text{Nu}}{\text{Re}\cdot\text{Pr}} = \dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p}$ | dimensionless | | Specific heat of air | $c_p = 1005$ | J/kg·K (approx) | | Thermal conductivity of air | $k_f = 0.026$ | W/m·K at 300 K | | Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s at 300 K | > [!NOTE] > **Air Property Convention:** Properties must always be evaluated at the **film temperature**, $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$, for accurate board-exam solutions. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Heat exchangers** in power plants, refineries, and HVAC systems. - **Cooling of electronic chips** (forced-air cooling in CPUs). - **Aerospace thermal management** (high-speed flight heat dissipation). - **Chemical reactor cooling jackets** with agitated impeller-driven flow. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Analogy (Momentum–Heat Transfer Analogy) The Reynolds analogy establishes a direct link between **skin friction ($C_f$)** and **Stanton number (St)** for a turbulent flow over a flat plate. ### Step 1 — Define Skin Friction Coefficient $$C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$$ where $\tau_w$ is the wall shear stress (N/m²). ### Step 2 — Define Stanton Number $$\text{St} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p V_\infty}$$ ### Step 3 — Assume Similar Velocity & Thermal Profiles (Pr ≈ 1) For fluids where momentum and thermal diffusivities are similar ($\text{Pr} \approx 1$, e.g., gases), the velocity and temperature profiles are analogous. ### Step 4 — Equate Momentum and Heat Diffusion at Wall After substituting the definitions of $\tau_w$ and $h$ from boundary-layer theory: $$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}, \qquad h = \frac{k_f \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}}{T_s - T_\infty}$$ Using the analogy assumption $\partial u / \partial y \propto (T_s - T_\infty)$: $$C_f / 2 \approx \text{St}$$ Therefore: $$h \approx \frac{C_f}{2} \rho c_p V_\infty$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the foundation for the **Chilton-Colburn analogy**, which extends the relation to all Pr via $j_H = \text{St}\,\text{Pr}^{2/3} \approx C_f/2$. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Cylinder in Cross-Flow > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical KTU Board Problem Structure** — solved in full. ### Problem Statement Air at $T_\infty = 30^\circ C$ flows across a heated cylinder of diameter $D = 50\ mm$ at velocity $V = 5\ m/s$. The cylinder surface is maintained at $T_s = 120^\circ C$. Determine: 1. The Reynolds number. 2. The Nusselt number using Churchill-Bernstein correlation. 3. The convective heat transfer coefficient. 4. Heat transfer rate per unit length. ### Property Evaluation (at $T_f = 75^\circ C = 348\ K$) - $k_f = 0.0298\ W/m\cdot K$ - $\nu = 2.07 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2/s$ - $\text{Pr} = 0.70$ ### Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 0.050}{2.07 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Re}_D = 12{,}077}$$ Since $4000 < \text{Re}_D < 40{,}000$, the Churchill-Bernstein correlation is **valid**. ### Step 2 — Apply Churchill-Bernstein Correlation $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.3 + \frac{0.62\,\text{Re}_D^{0.5}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{\left[1 + (0.4/\text{Pr})^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}} \left[1 + \left(\frac{\text{Re}_D}{282{,}000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$$ **Sub-step 2a:** Compute $\text{Re}_D^{0.5}$ $$\text{Re}_D^{0.5} = \sqrt{12{,}077} = 109.9$$ **Sub-step 2b:** Compute $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ $$\text{Pr}^{1/3} = (0.70)^{1/3} = 0.8879$$ **Sub-step 2c:** Compute bracket term $$\left[1 + (0.4/0.70)^{2/3}\right]^{1/4} = \left[1 + (0.5714)^{0.6667}\right]^{0.25}$$ $$(0.5714)^{2/3} = 0.6881 \quad\Rightarrow\quad [1 + 0.6881]^{0.25} = (1.6881)^{0.25}$$ $$(1.6881)^{0.25} = 1.1396$$ **Sub-step 2d:** Compute large-Re correction $$\frac{\text{Re}_D}{282{,}000} = \frac{12{,}077}{282{,}000} = 0.04283$$ $$(0.04283)^{5/8} = ? \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(0.04283) = -3.151 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (-3.151)(5/8) = -1.969$$ $$e^{-1.969} = 0.1395$$ $$\left[1 + 0.1395\right]^{4/5} = (1.1395)^{0.8}$$ $$\ln(1.1395) = 0.1307 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.1307 \times 0.8 = 0.1046 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{0.1046} = 1.1102$$ **Sub-step 2e:** Numerator assembly $$\text{Numerator} = 0.62 \times 109.9 \times 0.8879 \times 1.1102$$ $$= 0.62 \times 109.9 = 68.14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 68.14 \times 0.8879 = 60.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 60.50 \times 1.1102 = 67.16$$ **Sub-step 2f:** Final Nu $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.3 + \frac{67.16}{1.1396} = 0.3 + 58.93$$ $$\boxed{\text{Nu}_D = 59.23}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Heat Transfer Coefficient $$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{59.23 \times 0.0298}{0.050}$$ $$\boxed{h = 35.30\ W/m^2\cdot K}$$ ### Step 4 — Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Length $$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 120 - 30 = 90^\circ C$$ $$\frac{Q}{L} = h \cdot (\pi D) \cdot \Delta T = 35.30 \times (\pi \times 0.050) \times 90$$ $$= 35.30 \times 0.1571 \times 90$$ $$\boxed{\frac{Q}{L} = 499.0\ W/m}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix | Step | Quantity | Computed Value | Engineering Insight | |------|----------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | Re$_D$ | 12,077 | Confirms forced turbulent flow | | 2 | Nu$_D$ | 59.23 | Dimensionalizes $h$ | | 3 | $h$ | 35.30 W/m²·K | Higher than natural conv. (~5–10) | | 4 | $Q/L$ | 499 W/m | Linear scaling with $\Delta T$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Blower Motor] --> B1[Orifice Meter] B1 --> C1[Inlet Section] C1 --> D1[Flow Straightener] D1 --> E1[Test Section
Heated Cylinder] E1 --> F1[Outlet Section] F1 --> G1[Exhaust Duct] E1 --> H1[Heater Power Supply] E1 --> I1[Thermocouple T1
Inlet Air] E1 --> J1[Thermocouple T2
Outlet Air] E1 --> K1[Thermocouple T3
Surface] H1 --> L1[Variac / Dimmerstat] L1 --> M1[AC Mains] B1 --> N1[Manometer U-tube] I1 --> O1[Digital Indicator] J1 --> O1 K1 --> O1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Heat Transfer Calculation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start Experiment] --> S2[Set Heater Voltage
via Variac] S2 --> S3[Switch ON Blower] S3 --> S4[Wait for Steady State] S4 --> S5[Record Manometer
Reading delta_h] S4 --> S6[Record T1 Inlet] S4 --> S7[Record T2 Outlet] S4 --> S8[Record T3 Surface] S8 --> S9[Calculate Air
Velocity V] S9 --> S10[Calculate Re] S10 --> S11[Apply Correlation
Get Nu] S11 --> S12[Calculate h] S12 --> S13[Calculate Q] S13 --> S14[Plot Nu vs Re] S14 --> S15[Compare with
Standard Correlation] S15 --> S16[End / Repeat for
Next Velocity] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Boundary Layer Development ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Free Stream
T_infinity V_infinity] --> Q1[Leading Edge
x=0] Q1 --> R1[Laminar Zone
Re less than 5e5] R1 --> S1[Transition Point
Re equals 5e5] S1 --> T1[Turbulent Zone
Re greater than 5e5] T1 --> U1[delta Thermal
Boundary Layer] R1 --> U1 P1 --> V1[Velocity Profile
u_y versus y] Q1 --> V1 R1 --> V1 S1 --> V1 T1 --> V1 ``` ## 4.4 Component-Level Apparatus Description | Component | Function | Typical Specification | |-----------|----------|-----------------------| | Blower | Supplies forced air | Centrifugal, 0.5 HP | | Orifice meter | Measures air flow rate | $C_d \approx 0.62$ | | U-tube manometer | Reads pressure drop | Water-filled, mm scale | | Test section | Houses heater + test piece | Brass cylinder, 25–50 mm dia | | Heater | Supplies controlled heat input | Nichrome coil, 0–500 W | | Variac | Varies heater voltage | 0–270 V, 5 A | | Thermocouples | Measure temperatures | K-type, ±1°C accuracy | | Digital indicator | Displays T readings | Multi-channel, 0.1°C res | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Q1.** Define forced convection and state Newton's law of cooling. How does it differ from natural convection? > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > **Forced convection** is the heat transfer mode in which fluid motion is caused by an external mechanical means (pump, fan, blower). > **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$. > **Difference from Natural Convection:** > - *Natural:* fluid motion driven by buoyancy (density difference, $\Delta\rho$). > - *Forced:* fluid motion driven by external agency; Re >> Gr, $h$ values are 10–100× larger. > **Exam Tip:** Always mention Gr/Re ratio — for forced convection, Gr/Re² < 0.1. --- **Q2.** What is the significance of the Nusselt number in forced convection analysis? > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > $\text{Nu} = hL/k_f$ represents the **ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer** at a surface. It is the **dimensionless temperature gradient at the wall**. > **Significance:** > - Quantifies enhancement of heat transfer due to convection over pure conduction. > - $\text{Nu} = 1$ implies pure conduction (no convection). > - All empirical correlations express Nu as a function of Re and Pr. > - Higher Nu → more effective convective transport. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]`** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Heat Loss from Heated Cylinder **(a)** Derive the dimensionless form of forced convection over a heated flat plate and list the governing dimensionless groups. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Air at $40^\circ C$ flows across a $40\ mm$ diameter cylinder maintained at $150^\circ C$ with a velocity of $8\ m/s$. Using the Churchill-Bernstein correlation, calculate (i) Reynolds number, (ii) Nusselt number, (iii) average heat transfer coefficient, and (iv) heat transfer rate per unit length. **(7 Marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Derivation:** > Dimensional analysis via Buckingham $\Pi$-theorem: > - Variables: $h, k, V, L, \rho, \mu, c_p, \beta, g, \Delta T$ → 10 variables, 4 dimensions. > - Number of $\Pi$ groups: $10 - 4 = 6$. > - Result: $\text{Nu} = \phi(\text{Re}, \text{Pr}, \text{Gr})$. > - For forced convection $\text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2 \ll 1$, Gr is dropped: > $$\boxed{\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re},\ \text{Pr})}$$ > - Boundary conditions: > - At wall ($y=0$): $T = T_s$, $u = 0$ > - At free stream ($y \to \infty$): $T \to T_\infty$, $u \to V_\infty$ > > **(b) Numerical Solution:** > > Film temperature: $T_f = (40 + 150)/2 = 95^\circ C = 368\ K$ > Air properties at 368 K: > - $k_f = 0.0315\ W/m\cdot K$ > - $\nu = 2.18 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2/s$ > - $\text{Pr} = 0.69$ > > **(i) Reynolds number:** > $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{8 \times 0.040}{2.18 \times 10^{-5}} = \boxed{14{,}679}$$ > > **(ii) Nusselt number:** Using Churchill-Bernstein: > $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.3 + \frac{0.62\,(14679)^{0.5}\,(0.69)^{1/3}}{\left[1 + (0.4/0.69)^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}} \left[1 + \left(\frac{14679}{282000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$$ > Computation yields $\text{Nu}_D \approx \boxed{63.5}$ > > **(iii) Heat transfer coefficient:** > $$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{63.5 \times 0.0315}{0.040} = \boxed{50.0\ W/m^2 \cdot K}$$ > > **(iv) Heat transfer per unit length:** > $$\frac{Q}{L} = h \pi D \Delta T = 50.0 \times \pi \times 0.040 \times 110 = \boxed{691.2\ W/m}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Pipe Flow Heat Transfer **(a)** Explain the Dittus-Boelter and Sieder-Tate correlations for forced convection in pipes. State their applicability ranges. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Water flows through a $25\ mm$ diameter pipe at an average velocity of $2\ m/s$. The pipe wall is maintained at $80^\circ C$ and the water enters at $30^\circ C$. Calculate the exit temperature after $2\ m$ length, using an appropriate correlation. **(7 Marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Correlation Discussion:** > > | Correlation | Form | Range | > |-------------|------|-------| > | Dittus-Boelter | $\text{Nu} = 0.023\,\text{Re}^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^n$ | Re > 10,000, 0.7 < Pr < 160, n=0.4 (heating), n=0.3 (cooling) | > | Sieder-Tate | $\text{Nu} = 0.027\,\text{Re}^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\left(\mu/\mu_w\right)^{0.14}$ | Includes viscosity correction at wall | > > **Limitations:** Dittus-Boelter fails for large $\Delta T$; Sieder-Tate corrects via viscosity ratio. > > **(b) Numerical Solution:** > > Assume bulk mean $T_m = 55^\circ C = 328\ K$. Water properties: > - $\rho = 985\ kg/m^3$, $c_p = 4180\ J/kg\cdot K$ > - $\mu = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\ Pa\cdot s$, $k = 0.65\ W/m\cdot K$ > - $\text{Pr} = 3.2$ > > Reynolds number: > $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{985 \times 2 \times 0.025}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \boxed{98{,}500}$$ > > Nusselt number (Dittus-Boelter, heating n=0.4): > $$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \times (98500)^{0.8} \times (3.2)^{0.4}$$ > $$(98500)^{0.8} = 10^{0.8 \times \log_{10}(98500)} = 10^{0.8 \times 4.994} = 10^{3.995} \approx 9886$$ > $$(3.2)^{0.4} = 1.563$$ > $$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \times 9886 \times 1.563 = \boxed{355.4}$$ > > Heat transfer coefficient: > $$h = \frac{\text{Nu} \cdot k}{D} = \frac{355.4 \times 0.65}{0.025} = \boxed{9240\ W/m^2\cdot K}$$ > > Energy balance: > $$Q = h \pi D L (T_w - T_m) = m c_p (T_o - T_i)$$ > $$m = \rho V \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 985 \times 2 \times \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 0.967\ kg/s$$ > $$0.967 \times 4180 \times (T_o - 30) = 9240 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 2 \times (80 - T_o/2 - 30/2)$$ > > Iterating: $T_o \approx \boxed{37.8^\circ C}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Property evaluation temperature**: Using properties at $T_\infty$ instead of $T_f$ — *loses 1 mark*. > 2. **Units inconsistency**: Forgetting to convert mm → m in $D$, °C → K in $\Delta T$ — *loses 2 marks*. > 3. **Wrong correlation regime**: Applying Dittus-Boelter at Re < 10,000 — *loses 2 marks*. > 4. **Missing viscosity ratio**: Using Sieder-Tate without computing $\mu/\mu_w$ — *loses 1 mark*. > 5. **Sign convention**: Mixing heating vs cooling exponent in Dittus-Boelter — *loses 1 mark*. > 6. **No units in final answer**: Always state units of $h$ (W/m²·K), $Q$ (W), $L$ (m). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Heat Transfer by Forced Convection** - ✅ Forced convection = **external agency-driven** fluid flow. - ✅ Governing equation: **$Q = h A \Delta T$** (Newton's Law). - ✅ Four key dimensionless numbers: **Re, Pr, Nu, Gr** (Gr often neglected). - ✅ Always evaluate fluid properties at **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$. - ✅ **Reynolds Analogy**: $C_f/2 \approx \text{St}$ (for $\text{Pr} \approx 1$). - ✅ **Chilton-Colburn**: $j_H = \text{St}\,\text{Pr}^{2/3} \approx C_f/2$ (extended to all Pr). - ✅ Common correlations to memorize: - Flat plate laminar: $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.664\,\text{Re}^{0.5}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - Flat plate turbulent: $\overline{\text{Nu}} = 0.037\,\text{Re}^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - Pipe flow (Dittus-Boelter): $\text{Nu} = 0.023\,\text{Re}^{0.8}\,\text{Pr}^{n}$ - Cylinder (Churchill-Bernstein): full-form, valid for all Re$_D$. - ✅ Verify **forced dominance**: $\text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2 < 0.1$. - ✅ Typical lab apparatus: blower → orifice meter → test section → exhaust. - ✅ Standard air properties (300 K): $c_p = 1005$, $k = 0.026$, $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$. - ✅ Heat loss by radiation often neglected in KTU lab unless specified — check apparatus manual. - ✅ Plot expected: **Nu vs Re** on log-log paper → slope ≈ 0.5 (laminar) or 0.8 (turbulent).

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity of Metal Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity of Metal Rod # 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** is defined as the intrinsic property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat. Mathematically, it is the rate of heat transfer through a unit thickness of the material per unit area per unit temperature difference. According to **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**, the steady-state one-dimensional heat flux through a homogeneous isotropic solid is proportional to the negative temperature gradient: $$q = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$ Where: - $q$ = Heat flux (W/m²) - $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K) - $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = Temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (K/m) - The negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature (Second Law of Thermodynamics). For a uniform metal rod of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$ operating under **steady-state** conditions with no lateral heat losses: $$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$ Where $Q$ is the steady-state heat transfer rate (W). > [!NOTE] > **Steady State** is the operating condition where temperature at every point in the system remains **constant with respect to time**. This is the fundamental prerequisite for applying Fourier's Law in its simplest integrated form. Until steady state is reached, the readings are transient and **cannot** be used to compute $k$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a **metal spoon** in a cup of hot tea. After a few seconds, you feel the handle getting warm. This is thermal conduction in action — heat is travelling from the tea, through the metal, into your fingers. Now think of a **rod** as a "highway for heat": - **Hot end (boiling tea)** → molecules vibrate vigorously and pass this vibration to neighbouring molecules along the rod. - **Cold end (your fingers)** → receives this transferred energy. A material like **copper** ($k \approx 385$ W/m·K) acts like a 6-lane superhighway — heat zooms through effortlessly. A material like **stainless steel** ($k \approx 16$ W/m·K) is more like a single-lane country road — heat creeps through slowly. In our lab experiment, we essentially **measure the "traffic speed" of heat** through a specific metal rod by carefully applying a known amount of heat at one end, removing it at the other, and observing the resulting temperature profile. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical Thermal Conductivity Values of Common Metals (at 300 K / 27 °C):** > - **Silver:** $k \approx 429$ W/m·K > - **Copper:** $k \approx 385$ W/m·K > - **Aluminium:** $k \approx 205$ W/m·K > - **Brass:** $k \approx 109$ W/m·K > - **Iron / Steel:** $k \approx 50 - 80$ W/m·K > - **Stainless Steel (304):** $k \approx 16$ W/m·K > > **SI Unit of Thermal Conductivity:** $\text{W/(m·K)}$ or $\text{J/(s·m·K)}$ > > **CGS Unit:** $\text{cal/(s·cm·°C)}$ The thermal conductivity of pure metals generally **decreases with increasing temperature**, following approximately: $$k(T) = k_0 \cdot [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$ where $\alpha$ is a small negative temperature coefficient (typically $-10^{-4}$ to $-10^{-3}$ per K for pure metals). ## 1.4 Objective of the Experiment > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aim:** To determine the thermal conductivity of a given metal rod (typically **brass** or **mild steel**) by establishing **one-dimensional, steady-state linear heat conduction** and applying **Fourier's Law**. The two standard methods used to quantify the heat flow rate $Q$ are: 1. **Electrical Method:** $Q = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ (for DC, $Q = V \cdot I$) 2. **Cooling Water Method (Calorimetric):** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Basis of Heat Conduction in Metals Heat conduction in metals occurs via two primary mechanisms: 1. **Lattice Vibration (Phonons):** Atoms in the crystal lattice vibrate about their mean positions. Energy is transferred from hotter atoms (vibrating with higher amplitude) to cooler neighbours. 2. **Free Electron Diffusion:** Metals have a high concentration of "free" or conduction electrons. These mobile electrons act as efficient energy carriers, which is why metals conduct heat far better than non-metals. > [!NOTE] > **Wiedemann–Franz Law** (qualitative, advanced): The ratio of thermal to electrical conductivity in metals is approximately proportional to absolute temperature: > $$\frac{k}{\sigma} = L \cdot T$$ > where $L$ is the Lorenz number ($\approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W·Ω/K²). This explains why good electrical conductors (Cu, Ag, Al) are also good thermal conductors. ## 2.2 Governing Assumptions in the Linear Rod Experiment For the experiment to yield accurate values of $k$, the following assumptions must hold: | # | Assumption | Engineering Justification | |---|------------|---------------------------| | 1 | **Steady state** ($\partial T/\partial t = 0$) | Energy storage in the rod is zero | | 2 | **One-dimensional heat flow** (axial only) | Side insulation prevents radial losses | | 3 | **Constant thermal conductivity** | Small temperature range → $k$ approximately constant | | 4 | **Constant cross-sectional area $A$** | Uniform cylindrical rod geometry | | 5 | **No internal heat generation** | Heat only enters at the heater and exits at the cooler | | 6 | **Homogeneous and isotropic material** | Material properties identical in all directions | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $q = -k \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law (differential form) | W/m² | | 2 | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | Steady-state 1-D conduction (integrated) | W | | 3 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$ | Thermal conductivity (rearranged) | W/m·K | | 4 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$ | Cross-sectional area of rod | m² | | 5 | $Q_{elec} = V \cdot I$ | Heat input from electrical heater | W | | 6 | $Q_{water} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removed by cooling water | W | | 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot Q_v$ | Mass flow rate of cooling water | kg/s | | 8 | $\dfrac{T(x) - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Linear temperature distribution | dimensionless | | 9 | $\Delta T_{log} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD for composite/fin corrections | K | | 10 | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{k \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance of conduction | K/W | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for Table Use:** Whenever you see an absolute value or magnitude symbol in calculations (such as $\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert$), write it as `\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert` in your LaTeX to **avoid breaking markdown table syntax**. ## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Fourier's Law Application 1. **Apply heat** at one end of the rod through an electrical heater cartridge. 2. **Wait for steady state** — typically 45–90 minutes — until all thermocouple readings stabilize (variation < 0.5 °C over 10 min). 3. **Measure temperatures** $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, \ldots$ at fixed axial stations along the rod. 4. **Determine heat input $Q$** either from the heater wattage ($V \times I$) or from the cooling water enthalpy rise. 5. **Compute $k$** by rearranging Fourier's Law once $Q$, $A$, $L$, and the temperature difference are known. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where is this experiment relevant in industry?** > - **Heat Exchanger Design:** Choosing tube materials (Cu vs. SS) for evaporators and condensers. > - **Nuclear Reactor Fuel Rods:** Predicting temperature profiles in uranium dioxide pellets. > - **Electronics Cooling:** Heat sink material selection (Al vs. Cu vs. graphite composites). > - **Building Insulation:** Comparing metal studs vs. wood framing for thermal bridging. > - **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle leading edges; turbine blade cooling. > > The simple "linear rod" geometry taught here is the **building block** for every complex multi-dimensional conduction problem solved using finite-difference or finite-element methods later in the curriculum. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Heat Conduction Through a Uniform Rod ### Starting Point: Fourier's Law (Differential Form) $$q_x = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$ ### Step 1: Identify the heat flux The heat flux $q_x$ is the heat transfer rate per unit cross-sectional area: $$q_x = \frac{Q}{A}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute and rearrange $$\frac{Q}{A} = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$ ### Step 3: Separate variables $$Q \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$$ ### Step 4: Apply boundary conditions Integrate from $x = 0$ (hot end, $T = T_1$) to $x = L$ (cold end, $T = T_2$): $$Q \cdot \int_{0}^{L} dx = -k \cdot A \cdot \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$ ### Step 5: Evaluate the integrals $$Q \cdot L = -k \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$Q \cdot L = k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$ ### Step 6: Rearrange for $k$ (the design equation) $$\boxed{\,k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}\,}$$ This is the **principal formula** you will use in the lab calculation sheet. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Temperature Distribution Inside the Rod Starting again from $Q \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$, integrate from $0$ to any arbitrary position $x$ (where temperature is $T$): $$Q \cdot x = -k \cdot A \cdot (T - T_1)$$ $$T - T_1 = -\frac{Q \cdot x}{k \cdot A}$$ $$T(x) = T_1 - \frac{Q}{k \cdot A} \cdot x$$ This is a **straight-line equation** of the form $T = mx + c$. Hence the temperature profile inside a rod with uniform cross-section, no losses, and constant $k$ is **linear** along the length. ### Final form of the temperature profile: $$\boxed{\,\frac{T(x) - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{x}{L}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** A plot of $T$ vs $x$ should yield a straight line with slope $= -\dfrac{Q}{kA}$. The linearity of this plot is an **experimental verification** that steady-state, 1-D conduction has been achieved. If the plot is curved, side losses are significant. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Balance Verification (Energy Conservation Check) For an ideal, perfectly insulated rod, the heat supplied by the heater must equal the heat removed by the cooling water (Law of Conservation of Energy): $$Q_{input} = Q_{removed}$$ **Heat Input (Electrical Method):** $$Q_{in} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{DC heater}]$$ or $Q_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ for AC with phase angle $\phi$. **Heat Removed (Calorimetric Method):** $$Q_{out} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ where: - $\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \cdot \dot{V}_w$ is the mass flow rate of cooling water (kg/s) - $C_{p,w} \approx 4180$ J/kg·K is the specific heat of water - $(T_{out} - T_{in})$ is the temperature rise of water across the cooling jacket ### Energy Reconciliation: $$V \cdot I = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + Q_{loss}$$ In a well-insulated setup, $Q_{loss} \rightarrow 0$, and hence: $$V \cdot I \approx \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ The percentage deviation between the two sides indicates the magnitude of heat loss through insulation and is reported as **% Heat Loss**: $$\% \, \text{Heat Loss} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A heat loss greater than 10–15% indicates **poor insulation** and the experimental run must be repeated after improving the lagging around the rod. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Practice for KTU Exams) **Given:** - Metal rod: Brass, diameter $D = 0.025$ m, length between thermocouples $L = 0.30$ m - Heater: $V = 60$ V, $I = 1.5$ A - Steady-state temperatures: $T_1 = 90$ °C (hot end), $T_2 = 50$ °C (cold end) - Cooling water: $\dot{V}_w = 0.0001$ m³/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/kg·K - $T_{w,in} = 25$ °C, $T_{w,out} = 33$ °C **Find:** Thermal conductivity $k$ and verify using water calorimetry. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Heat supplied: $$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3:** Thermal conductivity: $$k = \frac{Q_{in} \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{90 \times 0.30}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times (90 - 50)}$$ $$k = \frac{27}{0.019636} = 1375 \text{ W/m·K}$$ > [!WARNING] > This number is **too high** for brass. The likely cause is that the actual heater voltage/current was smaller, or $L$ refers only to the section between thermocouples. Always **cross-check** with the cooling water side. **Step 4:** Cooling water heat balance: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1000 \times 0.0001 = 0.1 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q_{out} = 0.1 \times 4180 \times (33 - 25) = 0.1 \times 4180 \times 8 = 3344 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5:** Discrepancy check: $$Q_{out} \gg Q_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Unrealistic inputs — recheck meter readings.}$$ **Reconciliation with realistic values:** Suppose correct $V = 12$ V, $I = 0.5$ A, so $Q_{in} = 6$ W: $$k = \frac{6 \times 0.30}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 40} = \frac{1.8}{0.019636} = 91.7 \text{ W/m·K}$$ This is in excellent agreement with the standard thermal conductivity of **brass ($\approx 109$ W/m·K)**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Typical Observation Table | Sr. | Heater Power (W) | $T_1$ (°C) | $T_2$ (°C) | $T_3$ (°C) | $T_4$ (°C) | $T_{w,in}$ (°C) | $T_{w,out}$ (°C) | $\dot{V}_w$ (mL/s) | |-----|------------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 | 30 | 65 | 45 | 58 | 52 | 24 | 28 | 5 | | 2 | 45 | 78 | 52 | 68 | 60 | 24 | 30 | 5 | | 3 | 60 | 90 | 60 | 80 | 68 | 25 | 33 | 5 | > [!TIP] > Always tabulate the **distance of each thermocouple from the heater face** in your observation table. This allows you to plot the temperature profile and verify linearity. # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC or DC Power Supply
with Variac and Voltmeter Ammeter] --> B[Heater Cartridge
embedded in brass heater block] B --> C[Metal Test Rod
Brass or Mild Steel] C --> D[Throttling Section
insulated lagging] D --> E[Cooling Water Jacket
with inlet and outlet] E --> F[Constant Head Water Tank
overflow type] F --> G[Measuring Cylinder
for flow rate] C --> H[Thermocouples T1 T2 T3 T4
axially spaced] H --> I[Digital Temperature Indicator
multi-channel] I --> J[Data Recording and Plot
T versus x] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Component Interconnection Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HeaterEnd[HEATER END ASSEMBLY] H1[Variac 0 to 230 V] H2[Voltmeter] H3[Ammeter] H4[Heater Coil 200 W Cartridge] end subgraph TestSection[TEST SECTION - METAL ROD] R1[Rod Inlet
Heater Face x = 0] R2[Thermocouple T1 at x1] R3[Thermocouple T2 at x2] R4[Thermocouple T3 at x3] R5[Thermocouple T4 at x4] R6[Rod Outlet at x = L] end subgraph CoolingEnd[COOLING END ASSEMBLY] C1[Cooling Jacket Inlet Tw in] C2[Water Flow Channel] C3[Cooling Jacket Outlet Tw out] C4[Rotameter or Measuring Cylinder] C5[Constant Head Tank] end subgraph Instrumentation[INSTRUMENTATION] I1[Selector Switch] I2[Digital Indicator] I3[Cold Junction Compensation Box] end H1 --> H4 H2 --> H4 H3 --> H4 H4 --> R1 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 R6 --> C2 C5 --> C1 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 R2 --> I1 R3 --> I1 R4 --> I1 R5 --> I1 I1 --> I3 I3 --> I2 style HeaterEnd fill:#FFE4B5 style TestSection fill:#E0FFFF style CoolingEnd fill:#E6E6FA style Instrumentation fill:#FFFACD ``` ## 4.3 Energy Flow Pathway — Process Logic Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Electrical Energy V I] --> P2[Heat Generation in Heater] P2 --> P3[Conduction Through Metal Rod] P3 --> P4[Convection into Cooling Water] P4 --> P5[Enthalpy Rise of Water m Cp dT] P6[Heat Loss to Surroundings
Target less than 10 percent] -.-> P3 P2 --> P7[Q measured by V I method] P5 --> P8[Q measured by calorimetry] P7 --> P9[Reconciliation and k Calculation] P8 --> P9 style P1 fill:#FFB6C1 style P2 fill:#FFA07A style P3 fill:#87CEEB style P4 fill:#90EE90 style P5 fill:#FFD700 style P6 fill:#D3D3D3 style P9 fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 4.4 Standard Operating Procedure — Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Inspect apparatus and rod material] --> S2[Measure rod diameter and length L] S2 --> S3[Apply insulation lagging around rod] S3 --> S4[Switch ON cooling water flow at fixed rate] S4 --> S5[Set variac to low initial voltage] S5 --> S6[Switch ON heater and adjust voltage] S6 --> S7[Monitor all thermocouple readings] S7 --> S8{Steady state reached
Delta T less than 0.5 C in 10 min} S8 -->|No| S7 S8 -->|Yes| S9[Record T1 T2 T3 T4 V I and water temperatures] S9 --> S10[Measure water flow rate using cylinder and stopwatch] S10 --> S11[Calculate Q in and Q out] S11 --> S12[Compute k from Fourier Law] S12 --> S13[Repeat for two more heater settings] S13 --> S14[Plot T versus x and verify linearity] S14 --> S15[End: Tabulate results and compute average k] style S1 fill:#90EE90 style S8 fill:#FFD700 style S15 fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram of Apparatus (Text-Based Schematic) ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ VARIAC V A HEATER CARTRIDGE │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────┼───┘ ▼ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ HEATER FACE (T1) │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ TEST ROD SECTION │ │ │ │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐│ │ │ │ │T2 │ │T3 │ │T4 ││ │ │ │ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘│ │ │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ COOLING JACKET (T5) │◄── Cooling Water │ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Water Out ───► Rotameter ───► Drain │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Define thermal conductivity and state its SI unit.** **Model Answer:** Fourier's law of heat conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous isotropic solid is proportional to the negative temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow, and proportional to the area perpendicular to that direction. $$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$ **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** It is defined as the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient. Physically, it represents the ability of a material to conduct heat. **SI Unit:** $\text{W/(m·K)}$ or equivalently $\text{J/(s·m·K)}$. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: What is meant by steady-state heat conduction? Why is it essential to wait for steady state before recording observations in the thermal conductivity of metal rod experiment?** **Model Answer:** **Steady-state heat conduction** is the condition under which the temperature at every point within the system remains **constant with respect to time**, i.e., $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$. It is essential because: 1. The integrated form of Fourier's Law used to compute $k$ ($k = \dfrac{QL}{A(T_1-T_2)}$) is derived assuming **no energy storage** in the rod, which holds only at steady state. 2. During the transient (warm-up) phase, part of the heater energy is consumed in raising the rod's temperature, so $V \cdot I \neq Q_{conduction}$ and the calculated $k$ would be erroneous. 3. Only when temperatures stabilize is the heat flux constant and equal to $V \cdot I$ (electrical) or $\dot{m} C_p \Delta T_w$ (calorimetric). --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the thermal conductivity of a metal rod using Fourier's Law, stating all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the experimental setup used to determine the thermal conductivity of a metal rod and explain the procedure with a neat labelled diagram. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): Starting from Fourier's law in differential form: $$q = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$ For a uniform cylindrical rod of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$, with steady-state temperatures $T_1$ (hot end) and $T_2$ (cold end), we separate variables: $$Q \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$$ Integrating between limits: $$Q \int_0^L dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT \implies Q \cdot L = kA(T_1 - T_2)$$ Rearranging: $$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state heat flow. 2. One-dimensional conduction (axial). 3. Constant thermal conductivity. 4. Uniform cross-sectional area. 5. No heat loss through lateral surface (perfect insulation). 6. No internal heat generation. #### Model Solution (b): **Apparatus Required:** Metal rod (brass), electrical heater with Variac, V/A meters, water cooling jacket, rotameter or measuring cylinder, constant head tank, thermocouples, digital temperature indicator, insulating material. **Procedure:** 1. Measure the diameter and length of the rod. 2. Wrap the rod with insulating material. 3. Switch on cooling water at a steady flow rate. 4. Set the Variac to a low voltage and switch on the heater. 5. Gradually increase the voltage; allow 60–90 minutes for steady state. 6. Note $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$, voltmeter and ammeter readings, and water inlet/outlet temperatures. 7. Measure the water flow rate using a measuring cylinder and stopwatch. 8. Repeat for at least two more heater settings. 9. Plot $T$ vs $x$ — a straight line confirms 1-D conduction. **Labelled Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 (Mermaid component diagram) of these notes. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** In an experiment to determine the thermal conductivity of a brass rod, the following data were obtained: rod diameter $D = 25$ mm, distance between thermocouples $L = 300$ mm, heater voltage $V = 24$ V, current $I = 1.0$ A, steady-state temperatures $T_1 = 90$ °C and $T_2 = 50$ °C. Calculate the thermal conductivity of brass and compare with the standard value. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the sources of error in this experiment? How can the accuracy of the experiment be improved? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Given Data:** - $D = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $L = 300$ mm $= 0.30$ m - $V = 24$ V, $I = 1.0$ A - $T_1 = 90$ °C, $T_2 = 50$ °C **Step 1: Cross-sectional area** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Heat input** $$Q = V \cdot I = 24 \times 1.0 = 24 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3: Thermal conductivity** $$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{24 \times 0.30}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times (90 - 50)}$$ $$k = \frac{7.2}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 40} = \frac{7.2}{0.019636} = 366.7 \text{ W/m·K}$$ **Step 4: Comparison with standard value** The standard thermal conductivity of brass is approximately $109$ W/m·K. The obtained value is significantly higher, indicating **substantial heat loss** or **incorrect assumption that all heater energy flows through the rod section between thermocouples**. The actual fraction $Q_{through \, rod} < Q_{in}$, so the calculated $k$ is an overestimate. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often directly plug $V \times I$ into the formula without checking that the **entire heater energy enters the rod**. In reality, part is lost to surroundings, lowering the true $Q$. Using the **cooling water heat balance** gives a more accurate estimate of actual heat conduction. #### Model Solution (b): **Sources of Error:** 1. **Heat loss through insulation** — leads to overestimation of $k$. 2. **Contact resistance** between thermocouples and rod surface — gives wrong temperature readings. 3. **Non-uniform cross-section** due to machining imperfections. 4. **Inaccurate flow measurement** of cooling water (rotameter calibration errors). 5. **Steady state not truly reached** — transient readings skew the result. 6. **Thermocouple calibration error.** 7. **Radiation losses** from the high-temperature end. **Improvements:** 1. Use thicker and better-quality insulation (asbestos rope, glass wool). 2. Use calibrated, sheathed thermocouples with thermal paste for proper contact. 3. Use a precision flow meter (electronic flowmeter) instead of measuring cylinder. 4. Wait for a longer duration at steady state (≥ 90 min). 5. Apply a thin layer of high-conductivity thermal grease at thermocouple junctions. 6. Use the average $k$ from multiple heater settings and report standard deviation. 7. Maintain constant inlet water temperature using a thermostatic bath. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Many students write $k = 90 \times 0.3 / (4.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 40)$ and forget to convert mm to m. Always convert to **SI units** (m, s, K) before substitution. > > 2. **Mixing Up $T_1$ and $T_2$:** Use $T_1 > T_2$ always. If a negative $k$ appears, swap them. > > 3. **Ignoring Cooling Water Heat Balance:** Examiners love asking "verify using calorimetric method." Students who skip this lose 3–4 marks instantly. > > 4. **Not Stating Assumptions:** The "state assumptions" sub-question is worth 2 marks by itself. Always list all six assumptions explicitly. > > 5. **Skipping the Temperature Profile Plot:** Plotting $T$ vs $x$ and commenting on its linearity is a **free 2-mark** opportunity that most students miss. > > 6. **Sign Convention Errors:** In the differential form $q = -k(dT/dx)$, the negative sign is **mandatory**. Omitting it loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Fourier's Law:** $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$ — heat flows down the temperature gradient. - ✅ **Design Equation:** $k = \dfrac{QL}{A(T_1 - T_2)}$ — the working formula for the experiment. - ✅ **Cross-section:** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ — for cylindrical rods. - ✅ **Two Methods to find $Q$:** Electrical ($V \cdot I$) and Calorimetric ($\dot{m} C_p \Delta T_w$). - ✅ **Steady state** is non-negotiable — wait until $\Delta T < 0.5$ °C over 10 minutes. - ✅ **One-dimensional** flow requires proper side insulation (lagging). - ✅ **Temperature profile** is linear: $T(x) = T_1 - \dfrac{Q}{kA}x$. - ✅ **Units check:** $k$ is always in $\text{W/(m·K)}$. Convert all inputs to SI before plugging in. - ✅ **Energy balance:** $V \cdot I \approx \dot{m} C_p (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$ — the % deviation is your **heat loss** indicator. - ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** Cu ≈ 385, Al ≈ 205, Brass ≈ 109, SS ≈ 16 W/m·K. - ✅ **Common error sources:** Heat loss, contact resistance, uncalibrated instruments, premature readings. - ✅ **Plot of $T$ vs $x$** must be a straight line — its slope $= -\dfrac{Q}{kA}$, which can be used as an **alternate method** to compute $k$. - ✅ **Always report** average $k$ from multiple runs with **standard deviation**. - ✅ **KTU examiner hot-spots:** Derivation (7 marks), setup and procedure (7 marks), numerical calculation (7 marks), sources of error (7 marks).

topics.title: Heat Transfer Through Composite Wall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer Through Composite Wall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
**Heat Transfer Through a Composite Wall** refers to the steady-state, one-dimensional conduction of thermal energy through a structural assembly composed of **two or more distinct solid layers** (each possessing its own thermal conductivity, thickness, and surface area) arranged either in **series**, **parallel**, or a **combined series-parallel** configuration. The governing principle is **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**, applied across an equivalent thermal resistance network, with the objective of determining the rate of heat flow ($Q$) and the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** of the assembly.

In the context of the KTU Lab curriculum, this experiment is designed to experimentally validate the **electrical-resistance analogy** for heat conduction, by measuring the steady-state temperature profile across a multi-layered wall (typically comprising materials like **mild steel**, **asbestos**, **plywood**, or **insulating wool**) and comparing the observed heat flux with the theoretical prediction derived from the resistance-in-series model.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing in a queue at a canteen. Each person in the queue slows you down a little — the *bottleneck* is the slowest person. Now, picture heat as "you," trying to escape from a hot room to a cold corridor. It must pass through three barriers: a brick wall, a layer of foam, and a wooden panel. The brick, foam, and wood each offer a different "resistance" to heat flow. Just like a queue, the heat that finally emerges is limited by the **cumulative resistance** of all layers. A **composite wall** is simply this multi-layer barrier, and our job as engineers is to compute *how fast* heat sneaks through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In the **PCBBL507 lab**, the composite wall apparatus typically uses a circular disc of **mild steel** sandwiched between layers of **insulating material** (e.g., asbestos or glass wool), heated by a **steam chest / electric heater** on one side and cooled by a **water-jacket** on the other. Students must plot the **temperature gradient** across each layer and verify **Fourier's Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumptions in KTU Lab Experiments:**
> 1. **Steady-state** conditions (no temperature change with time).
> 2. **One-dimensional** heat flow (radial edge losses are neglected).
> 3. **Constant thermal conductivity** for each material within the operating temperature range.
> 4. **Perfect thermal contact** between layers (negligible contact resistance).

### Standard Metrics Used
- Heat flux ($q$): measured in **W/m²** or **kW/m²**
- Thermal conductivity ($k$): measured in **W/(m·K)**
- Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$): measured in **W/(m²·K)**
- Thermal Resistance ($R$): measured in **K/W** (per unit area: **m²·K/W**)
