# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic Breakdown
The thermal analysis of a composite wall rests on three foundational pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (Single Layer)
For a homogeneous slab of thickness $L$, area $A$, and thermal conductivity $k$, with faces maintained at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (where $T_1 > T_2$):

The heat transfer rate is governed by:
$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

The **why**: Heat spontaneously flows from a region of higher molecular vibration energy (high $T$) to lower molecular vibration energy (low $T$). The **how**: The rate of this energy transfer is directly proportional to the area $A$, the temperature difference $\Delta T$, and inversely proportional to the path length $L$.

### Pillar 2 — Thermal Resistance Analogy (Electrical Analogy)
By defining **thermal resistance** $R_{th}$ as the obstruction offered by a layer to heat flow, we can write:
$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad \left[\frac{K}{W}\right]$$

Then Fourier's Law becomes Ohm's Law analogously:
$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

The **why**: This is the most powerful tool in heat transfer because complex multi-layer problems collapse into simple series/parallel resistor networks. The **how**: Each material layer behaves like a resistor, and heat flow is the "current" driven by the temperature "voltage" difference.

### Pillar 3 — Resistance in Series (Composite Wall)
For a composite wall of $n$ layers in series (same heat flow $Q$ through all), the equivalent resistance is the **algebraic sum**:
$$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{i} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \cdots + \frac{L_n}{k_n A_n}$$

The total heat transfer:
$$Q = \frac{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}{R_{total}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Interface Temperature Rule**: In a series composite wall, knowing the heat flow $Q$ allows us to find the temperature at every interface using:
> $$T_{interface} = T_{hot} - Q \cdot R_{before\ interface}$$

### Pillar 4 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)
For a composite wall of equal cross-sectional area $A$:
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \cdots + \frac{L_n}{k_n}$$

The simplified heat transfer equation:
$$Q = UA(T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier's Law (single layer) | **W** |
| 2 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal Resistance of a layer | **K/W** |
| 3 | $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A_i}$ | Series resistance (composite wall) | **K/W** |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}}$ | Heat flow through composite wall | **W** |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i}$ | Reciprocal of overall $U$ (equal area) | **m²·K/W** |
| 6 | $Q = U A (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | Overall heat transfer equation | **W** |
| 7 | $T_{interface} = T_{hot} - Q \cdot R_{before}$ | Interface temperature | **°C** or **K** |
| 8 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}/A}$ | Heat flux | **W/m²** |
| 9 | $k = \dfrac{Q L}{A \Delta T}$ | Thermal conductivity (experimental) | **W/(m·K)** |
| 10 | $\%\ \text{Error} = \left\vert \dfrac{k_{exp} - k_{th}}{k_{th}} \right\vert \times 100$ | Experimental accuracy | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: Composite wall analysis is the backbone of designing **building insulation** (brick + plaster + air gap + drywall), **furnace linings** (refractory brick + insulation + steel shell), **cryogenic storage tanks** (inner vessel + vacuum gap + radiation shield + outer shell), and **electronic device packaging** (silicon chip + thermal paste + heat sink).

## Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Lab
- **At hot face (x = 0)**: $T = T_1$ (fixed by steam or heater)
- **At cold face (x = L)**: $T = T_n$ (fixed by cooling water)
- **At each interface**: Heat flux is **continuous** (no heat accumulation at steady state)
$$q_{in} = q_{out} \Rightarrow -k_1 \left(\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right)_{x=L_1^-} = -k_2 \left(\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right)_{x=L_1^+}$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Heat Flow Through a Two-Layer Composite Wall (Series)

### Given
A composite wall consists of two layers, each of cross-sectional area $A$:
- **Layer 1**: Thickness $L_1$, thermal conductivity $k_1$, faces at $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (interface).
- **Layer 2**: Thickness $L_2$, thermal conductivity $k_2$, faces at $T_2$ (interface) and $T_3$ (cold).

### To Derive
A unified expression for $Q$ in terms of $T_1$, $T_3$, and the geometry/material properties.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1** — Apply Fourier's Law to Layer 1:
$$Q_1 = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}$$

This is the heat conducted through the first material from the hot face to the interface.

**Step 2** — Rearrange to express the temperature drop across Layer 1:
$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q_1 L_1}{k_1 A}$$

The temperature drops linearly within a homogeneous slab, with the magnitude proportional to the heat rate and the resistance of the layer.

**Step 3** — Apply Fourier's Law to Layer 2:
$$Q_2 = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2}$$

This is the heat conducted through the second material from the interface to the cold face.

**Step 4** — Rearrange to express the temperature drop across Layer 2:
$$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q_2 L_2}{k_2 A}$$

**Step 5** — Apply the steady-state energy conservation principle:
At steady state, there is no heat accumulation within the wall. Therefore, the heat entering Layer 1 must equal the heat leaving Layer 2:
$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q$$

This is the **continuity of heat flux** boundary condition at the interface. The "why": If more heat entered than left, the interface temperature would continuously rise, violating steady state. The "how": Mathematically, this condition couples the two layers.

**Step 6** — Add the two temperature drops to get the total temperature difference:
$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A}$$

The interface temperature $T_2$ cancels because it is an internal state variable, leaving only the boundary temperatures.

**Step 7** — Simplify the left-hand side:
$$T_1 - T_3 = Q \left( \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} \right)$$

**Step 8** — Isolate $Q$ to obtain the unified heat transfer rate:
$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A}}$$

This is the canonical **composite wall heat transfer equation** for two layers in series.

**Step 9** — Recognize the denominator as total thermal resistance:
$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A}$ and $R_2 = \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A}$. This exposes the powerful **electrical-resistance analogy**: a series resistor network.

**Step 10** — Generalize to $n$ layers:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}}}$$

---

## Derivation 2: Interface Temperature in a Two-Layer Wall

### Given
The total heat flow $Q$ through a two-layer composite wall and the hot-face temperature $T_1$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1** — Express the heat flow through Layer 1:
$$Q = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the interface temperature $T_2$:
$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange to isolate $T_2$:
$$\boxed{T_2 = T_1 - \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}}$$

This is the standard **interface temperature formula** used in KTU lab calculations to plot the temperature profile across each material.

---

## Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem**: A furnace wall consists of three layers in series, each of area $A = 1\ \text{m}^2$:
- **Layer 1 (Refractory brick)**: $L_1 = 0.115\ \text{m}$, $k_1 = 1.05\ \text{W/(m·K)}$
- **Layer 2 (Insulating brick)**: $L_2 = 0.115\ \text{m}$, $k_2 = 0.18\ \text{W/(m·K)}$
- **Layer 3 (Building brick)**: $L_3 = 0.230\ \text{m}$, $k_3 = 0.69\ \text{W/(m·K)}$

The hot face is at $T_1 = 1200\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the cold face is at $T_4 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

**Find**: (a) Heat loss per unit area, (b) Interface temperatures $T_2$ and $T_3$.

### Solution

**Step 1** — Calculate the thermal resistance of each layer (per unit area):
$$\frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.115}{1.05} = 0.1095\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$
$$\frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.115}{0.18} = 0.6389\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$
$$\frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.230}{0.69} = 0.3333\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2** — Sum the resistances:
$$R_{total} = 0.1095 + 0.6389 + 0.3333 = 1.0817\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the heat flux (heat loss per unit area):
$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1200 - 50}{1.0817} = \frac{1150}{1.0817} = 1063.16\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4** — Compute the interface temperature $T_2$ (drop across Layer 1):
$$T_2 = T_1 - q \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 1200 - (1063.16)(0.1095) = 1200 - 116.42 = 1083.58\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 5** — Compute the interface temperature $T_3$ (drop across Layers 1 and 2):
$$T_3 = T_1 - q \cdot \left(\frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2}\right) = 1200 - (1063.16)(0.7484) = 1200 - 795.66 = 404.34\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 6** — Verify (drop across all three layers should equal $T_1 - T_4$):
$$T_4 = T_1 - q \cdot R_{total} = 1200 - (1063.16)(1.0817) = 1200 - 1150 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C} \checkmark$$

### Final Results
- **Heat flux**: $q = 1063.16\ \text{W/m}^2$
- **Interface $T_2$**: $\approx 1083.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Interface $T_3$**: $\approx 404.3\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Overall $U$**: $U = 1/R_{total} = 1/1.0817 = 0.9244\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation**: Although the **insulating brick** is geometrically equal in thickness to the refractory brick, it produces a **much larger temperature drop** (≈ 679 °C vs. ≈ 116 °C) because its $k$ value is ~6× smaller. This is why **low-$k$ materials** are used as insulation layers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State Fourier's Law of heat conduction and explain the concept of thermal resistance for a composite wall.**

**Model Answer**:
**Fourier's Law**: The rate of heat transfer by conduction through a homogeneous body is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to the direction of heat flow and the temperature gradient across the body.

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Thermal Resistance**: It is the opposition offered by a material layer to the flow of heat, defined as:
$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA}$$

For a **composite wall** of $n$ layers in **series**, the total thermal resistance is the sum of individual resistances:
$$R_{total} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A_2} + \cdots + \frac{L_n}{k_n A_n}$$

The heat flow through the composite wall is then:
$$Q = \frac{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}{R_{total}}$$

This concept is analogous to **Ohm's Law** in electrical circuits, where temperature difference acts as the potential and heat flow as the current.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) for a composite wall? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer**:
The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** is defined as the heat transfer rate per unit area per unit temperature difference across a composite wall or system. It is essentially the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area.

**Formula**:
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \cdots + \frac{L_n}{k_n}$$

The heat transfer rate is:
$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{overall}$$

**Unit**: **W/(m²·K)**

A **high $U$ value** indicates poor insulation (heat flows easily), while a **low $U$ value** indicates good insulation (heat flow is restricted).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Marks are deducted when students mix up **mm with m** for thickness without converting. Always convert all dimensions to **SI units (meters)** before substitution.
> 2. **Missing Area Calculation**: For circular discs, $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Students often use $A = \pi D L$ (cylindrical area) by mistake, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Heat Balance**: The experimental heat flow $Q$ should be cross-verified using the cooling water method. Marks are awarded for **showing both calculations**.
> 4. **No Sign Convention Justification**: Always state explicitly that $T_1 > T_2 > T_3 > T_4$ for the direction of heat flow to be physically consistent.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** A composite wall of a furnace is constructed of three materials in series, each of cross-sectional area **1.2 m²**:
- **Material A** (Refractory): $L_1 = 0.10\ \text{m}$, $k_1 = 1.0\ \text{W/(m·K)}$
- **Material B** (Insulation): $L_2 = 0.08\ \text{m}$, $k_2 = 0.05\ \text{W/(m·K)}$
- **Material C** (Structural): $L_3 = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $k_3 = 0.70\ \text{W/(m·K)}$

The hot face temperature is **1100 °C** and the cold face is at **40 °C**.

**Calculate:**
(i) The rate of heat loss through the wall in **Watts**.
(ii) The temperature at each interface.
(iii) The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$).

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1** — List known values:
$A = 1.2\ \text{m}^2$, $T_1 = 1100\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_4 = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T_{overall} = 1060\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 2** — Calculate thermal resistance of each layer:
$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{1.0 \times 1.2} = 0.0833\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.08}{0.05 \times 1.2} = 1.3333\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.20}{0.70 \times 1.2} = 0.2381\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3** — Compute total resistance:
$$R_{total} = 0.0833 + 1.3333 + 0.2381 = 1.6547\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 4** — Calculate the heat loss rate:
$$Q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}} = \frac{1060}{1.6547} = 640.59\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5** — Find interface temperature $T_2$ (after Layer A):
$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_1 = 1100 - (640.59)(0.0833) = 1100 - 53.36 = 1046.64\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 6** — Find interface temperature $T_3$ (after Layers A and B):
$$T_3 = T_1 - Q \cdot (R_1 + R_2) = 1100 - (640.59)(1.4166) = 1100 - 907.46 = 192.54\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 7** — Verification: $T_4 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_{total} = 1100 - (640.59)(1.6547) = 1100 - 1060 = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ ✓

**Step 8** — Calculate Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient:
$$\frac{1}{U} = R_{total} \times A = 1.6547 \times 1.2 = 1.9856\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$
$$U = \frac{1}{1.9856} = 0.5036\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

#### Final Results
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Heat loss $Q$ | **640.59 W** |
| Interface $T_2$ | **1046.64 °C** |
| Interface $T_3$ | **192.54 °C** |
| Overall $U$ | **0.5036 W/(m²·K)** |

---

**(b)** Describe the **experimental procedure** to determine the thermal conductivity of a material using the **composite wall apparatus** in the KTU Heat Transfer Lab. List the observations, the formula used, and the precautions to be taken.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Apparatus Used**: Composite wall test rig, thermocouples (4 nos.), digital temperature indicator, cooling water supply, flow meter, stopwatch, vernier caliper, and the test specimen (mild steel disc + asbestos + bakelite).

**Experimental Procedure**:

1. **Measure the dimensions** of each layer (thickness $L_i$ using vernier caliper, diameter $D$ using scale) and compute area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

2. **Assemble the composite wall** by stacking the layers in the test rig holder — usually **mild steel** (metal), then **asbestos** (insulation), then **plywood/bakelite** — and tighten the assembly with bolts for firm contact.

3. **Insert thermocouples** at the hot face, the two interfaces, and the cold face. Connect them to the digital temperature indicator.

4. **Start the heater** (or open the steam valve) to supply heat at one end and start the **cooling water pump** at the other end.

5. **Allow the system to reach steady state** (≈ 90–120 minutes). Steady state is confirmed when all four temperatures remain constant (± 0.5 °C) for 15 consecutive minutes.

6. **Record** the four steady-state temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$.

7. **Measure the cooling water** inlet temperature $T_{c,in}$, outlet temperature $T_{c,out}$, and the volumetric flow rate $V$ (in L/min or m³/s).

8. **Compute the heat flow rate** from the cooling water energy balance:
$$Q = \dot{m} C_p (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = \rho V C_p (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$
where $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $C_p = 4186\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for water.

9. **Calculate the thermal conductivity** of each material using:
$$k_i = \frac{Q L_i}{A (T_{i} - T_{i+1})}$$

10. **Compare** $k_{experimental}$ with the standard $k_{theoretical}$ and calculate:
$$\%\ \text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{k_{exp} - k_{th}}{k_{th}} \right\vert \times 100$$

11. **Plot the temperature profile** (Temperature on Y-axis, distance from hot face on X-axis) and observe the linear segments and the slope change at each interface.

**Precautions**:
- Ensure **perfect contact** between layers to minimize contact resistance.
- Wait for **true steady state** before recording readings.
- Insulate the **lateral surfaces** of the wall to prevent radial heat loss (1-D assumption).
- Calibrate the thermocouples before use.
- Maintain a **constant flow rate** of cooling water throughout the experiment.
- Take **multiple readings** and average them to reduce random error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** A composite wall consists of two layers in series. Layer 1 has $L_1 = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $k_1 = 50\ \text{W/(m·K)}$ (stainless steel). Layer 2 has $L_2 = 0.10\ \text{m}$, $k_2 = 0.04\ \text{W/(m·K)}$ (glass wool). The wall area is **0.5 m²**, hot face at **200 °C**, cold face at **30 °C**.

**Calculate**:
(i) The heat transfer rate.
(ii) The temperature at the interface.
(iii) The percentage of total resistance offered by the insulation layer.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1** — Calculate resistance of Layer 1 (stainless steel):
$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.05}{50 \times 0.5} = 0.002\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2** — Calculate resistance of Layer 2 (glass wool):
$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.10}{0.04 \times 0.5} = 5.000\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3** — Total resistance:
$$R_{total} = 0.002 + 5.000 = 5.002\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 4** — Heat transfer rate:
$$Q = \frac{200 - 30}{5.002} = \frac{170}{5.002} = 33.99\ \text{W} \approx 34\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5** — Interface temperature:
$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_1 = 200 - (34)(0.002) = 200 - 0.068 = 199.93\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 6** — Percentage resistance of insulation:
$$\% R_2 = \frac{R_2}{R_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{5.000}{5.002} \times 100 = 99.96\ \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: The stainless steel layer contributes only **0.04%** of the total resistance, while the glass wool contributes **99.96%**. This conclusively demonstrates that **insulation effectiveness is governed by the material with the lowest $k$**, regardless of its thickness. Doubling the steel thickness would barely change the heat loss, but doubling the insulation would halve it.

#### Final Results
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Heat transfer $Q$ | **≈ 34 W** |
| Interface temperature $T_2$ | **≈ 199.93 °C** |
| % Resistance of insulation | **≈ 99.96%** |

---

**(b)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the principle of heat transfer through a composite wall. State the assumptions made and derive the expression for heat flow through a **three-layer** composite wall.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Principle**:
Heat transfer through a composite wall occurs by **steady-state one-dimensional conduction** across multiple solid layers. Each layer offers a **thermal resistance** to the flow of heat, and the total resistance is the sum of individual resistances (series network). The driving potential is the **temperature difference** between the hot and cold faces.

**Sketch** (Refer to Diagram A and Diagram B in Section 4 of these notes for the complete schematic and resistance network).

**Assumptions**:
1. **Steady-state** heat flow (no temperature variation with time).
2. **One-dimensional** heat flow (heat flows only perpendicular to the wall surface; lateral losses are negligible).
3. **Constant thermal conductivity** for each material (independent of temperature).
4. **Perfect thermal contact** at the interfaces (no contact resistance or air gap).
5. **Isothermal surfaces** at each interface (no hot spots).

**Derivation of Three-Layer Composite Wall**:

Consider three layers in series, each of cross-sectional area $A$, with thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$. The hot face is at $T_1$ and the cold face is at $T_4$. Interfaces are at $T_2$ and $T_3$.

**Step 1** — Apply Fourier's Law to each layer:
$$Q_1 = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}, \quad Q_2 = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2}, \quad Q_3 = \frac{k_3 A (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$$

**Step 2** — Apply steady-state energy conservation:
$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = Q$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange each equation to express temperature drops:
$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}, \quad T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A}, \quad T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A}$$

**Step 4** — Add all three temperature drops (interface temperatures cancel):
$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A}$$

**Step 5** — Simplify:
$$T_1 - T_4 = Q \left( \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} \right)$$

**Step 6** — Solve for $Q$:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A}} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}}$$

**Step 7** — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient:
$$\frac{1}{UA} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{U} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}$$

The heat transfer can therefore be compactly written as:
$$Q = U A (T_1 - T_4)$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Fourier's Law**: $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ — the foundation of all conduction problems.
- ✅ **Thermal Resistance**: $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ — bridge between heat transfer and electrical circuits.
- ✅ **Composite Wall in Series**: $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A_i}$ — just add them up.
- ✅ **Overall U**: $\dfrac{1}{U} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i}$ — a single number representing the entire wall.
- ✅ **Heat Flow**: $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}}$ — Ohm's Law equivalent.
- ✅ **Interface Temperature**: $T_{int} = T_{hot} - Q \cdot R_{before\ interface}$ — the most-tested sub-question.

### Experimental KTU Lab Points
- ✅ The composite wall apparatus has **4 thermocouples**: 1 at hot face, 2 at interfaces, 1 at cold face.
- ✅ **Two methods** to compute $Q$: (1) Fourier's Law from $T_i$ readings, (2) Cooling water energy balance $\dot{m} C_p \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Steady state** = all four temperatures constant for ~15 minutes.
- ✅ **Cooling water properties**: $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 4186\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$.
- ✅ Convert **mm → m** and **L/min → m³/s** before substituting.
- ✅ Calculate **% Error** to validate the experiment.

### Common Student Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting to convert **all units to SI** (especially thickness and flow rate).
- ❌ Using **wrong area formula** for circular discs ($A = \pi D^2 / 4$, NOT $\pi D L$).
- ❌ Mixing up $R$ and $1/R$ when writing the final heat flow equation.
- ❌ Not verifying that interface temperatures lie between $T_{hot}$ and $T_{cold}$ (sanity check).
- ❌ Ignoring **edge/contact resistance** in real-world applications.

### One-Line Memory Aid
> **"The wall that resists the most governs the heat"** — the layer with the **largest $L/k$ ratio** controls the heat flow rate, not the layer with the largest $L$.

### Quick Unit Reference
| Quantity | SI Unit |
|----------|---------|
| Heat flow $Q$ | **W** |
| Heat flux $q$ | **W/m²** |
| Thermal conductivity $k$ | **W/(m·K)** |
| Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ | **K/W** |
| Overall $U$ | **W/(m²·K)** |
| Length $L$ | **m** |
| Area $A$ | **m²** |
| Temperature $T$ | **K** or **°C** (differences only) |

### Key Engineering Takeaway
The **composite wall experiment** is the gateway to understanding **insulation design**. Whether you are designing a **boiler shell**, a **cold storage chamber**, a **refrigerator wall**, or a **spacecraft thermal shield**, the principle remains the same: **stack materials of low $k$ to maximize thermal resistance, and verify with experimental rigs like the KTU composite wall apparatus**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer in Double Pipe Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer in Double Pipe Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Double Pipe Heat Exchanger** (also called a **Concentric Tube Heat Exchanger** or **Jacket Pipe Exchanger**) is the simplest and most fundamental type of heat transfer equipment used in chemical process industries. It consists of two concentric pipes where one fluid flows through the inner pipe and the other fluid flows through the annular space between the inner and outer pipes. Heat transfer occurs through the wall of the inner pipe by conduction, while the hot and cold fluids exchange thermal energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A double pipe heat exchanger is a 1-1 (one shell pass, one tube pass) heat exchanger in which one fluid flows through the inner pipe and the other through the outer annulus, with heat transfer taking place across the cylindrical wall of the inner pipe.*

The two possible flow arrangements are:
- **Co-current (Parallel) Flow:** Both hot and cold fluids enter from the same end and flow in the same direction.
- **Counter-current Flow:** Fluids enter from opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. This is preferred industrially because it provides a more uniform temperature driving force and higher log mean temperature difference (LMTD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this experiment in your KTU Lab Manual?**
> This experiment directly verifies two of the most important engineering relationships in heat transfer:
> 1. The **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** method.
> 2. The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** obtained experimentally vs. calculated from empirical correlations like Dittus-Boelter or Sieder-Tate.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **hot cup of tea** on a cold winter morning, and you slip a **straw** into it. The straw is at room temperature initially. As you hold the cup:
- **Tea (hot fluid)** moves continuously through the bulk of the cup.
- **Air (cold fluid)** flows over the outer surface of the straw.
- The **straw wall** acts exactly like the inner pipe wall, conducting heat from the hot tea to the cold air.

Now scale this up: replace the straw with a long metal pipe and the surrounding air with a counter-flowing stream of cold water in an outer pipe. That is essentially a **Double Pipe Heat Exchanger**.

> [!TIP]
> **Another Analogy — The Car Radiator:** The engine coolant (hot) flows through tubes while atmospheric air (cold) blows across the tube bank. Each tube behaves like an inner pipe transferring heat to the outside.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- Thermal conductivity of mild steel inner pipe: **k ≈ 45 W/m·K**
- Specific heat of water: **C_p ≈ 4180 J/kg·K**
- Density of water at 30°C: **ρ ≈ 995 kg/m³**
- Standard empirical correlation for turbulent flow: **Dittus-Boelter Equation — Nu = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^n**
- Standard reference velocity: flow rates typically range from **0.1 to 1.0 LPM** in lab-scale exchangers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Hot fluid entry:** Hot water enters the inner pipe at inlet temperature $T_{h,i}$ with mass flow rate $\dot{m}_h$.
2. **Convective heat transfer (inner):** Heat from the bulk hot water is transferred to the inner pipe wall by forced convection. Governed by the inner heat transfer coefficient $h_i$.
3. **Conductive heat transfer (wall):** Heat passes through the cylindrical metal wall by conduction. Governed by thermal conductivity $k$ and pipe dimensions.
4. **Convective heat transfer (outer):** Heat from the outer pipe wall is transferred to the cold water flowing in the annulus. Governed by $h_o$.
5. **Cold fluid exit:** Cold water gains heat and exits at a higher temperature $T_{c,o}$.
6. **Steady state:** At steady state, the rate of heat lost by hot fluid = rate of heat gained by cold fluid = total heat transfer rate $Q$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why LMTD and not arithmetic mean?** Because the temperature difference $\Delta T$ varies exponentially along the length of the exchanger. The arithmetic mean is inaccurate. The logarithm correctly averages the exponential decay of $\Delta T$.
- **Why counter-current is preferred?** Because it maintains a higher mean $\Delta T$ along the entire length and allows the cold fluid outlet temperature to exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature — impossible in co-current flow.
- **Why use overall coefficient $U$?** Because it lumps all three resistances (inner convection, wall conduction, outer convection) into one parameter, simplifying design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU 2024 exam question on this experiment will be solved using these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Heat transfer rate (hot side) | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})$ | W |
| 2 | Heat transfer rate (cold side) | $Q = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | W |
| 3 | Average heat duty | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_h + Q_c}{2}$ | W |
| 4 | LMTD (Counter-current) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K or °C |
| 5 | $\Delta T_1$ (counter-current) | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ | °C |
| 6 | $\Delta T_2$ (counter-current) | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ | °C |
| 7 | $\Delta T_1$ (co-current) | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ | °C |
| 8 | $\Delta T_2$ (co-current) | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$ | °C |
| 9 | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | W |
| 10 | Heat transfer area | $A = \pi D_i L$ | m² |
| 11 | Overall resistance (outer basis) | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{D_o}{h_i D_i} + \dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o / D_i)}{2k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | m²K/W |
| 12 | Reynolds number (pipe) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_i}{\mu}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Reynolds number (annulus) | $Re_a = \dfrac{\rho v_{eq} D_e}{\mu}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Equivalent diameter (annulus) | $D_e = D_o - D_i$ | m |
| 15 | Dittus-Boelter (heating) | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Dittus-Boelter (cooling) | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.3}$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | NTU | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | dimensionless |
| 18 | Effectiveness (1-1 exchanger) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1+C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$ | dimensionless |
| 19 | Heat capacity ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | dimensionless |
| 20 | Mean velocity in pipe | $v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot (\pi/4) D_i^2}$ | m/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Double pipe exchangers are used in (1) oil coolers in small engines, (2) condensers in distillation columns, (3) pre-heaters for viscous fluids, (4) pharmaceutical reactors where batch-type heat transfer is needed, and (5) chemical plants for low-to-medium flow rates of highly viscous or corrosive fluids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a small differential area $dA$ of the inner pipe wall. At this point:
- Hot fluid temperature = $T_h$
- Cold fluid temperature = $T_c$
- Local temperature difference = $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$

The rate of heat transfer through this elemental area is:

$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T
$$

For the hot fluid losing heat:
$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \cdot dT_h
$$

For the cold fluid gaining heat:
$$
dQ = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} \cdot dT_c
$$

The differential change in $\Delta T$ along the length (counter-current flow):
$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c
$$

Step-by-step expansion:
- $dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}}$
- $dT_c = \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}}$

Substituting:
$$
d(\Delta T) = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} - \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} = -U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)
$$

Rearranging:
$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right) dA
$$

Integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$, area = 0) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$, area = $A$):
$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right) \int_0^A dA
$$

$$
\ln\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)
$$

Since the total heat transfer $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$:

$$
UA = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)
$$

Therefore:
$$
\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Resistance Network)

The three thermal resistances in series for a double pipe exchanger (referred to outer pipe area) are:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_o A_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o / D_i)}{2 \pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}
$$

Since $A_o = \pi D_o L$ and $A_i = \pi D_i L$, dividing numerator and denominator by $A_o$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{D_o}{h_i D_i} + \dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o / D_i)}{2k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The wall conduction resistance $\dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o / D_i)}{2k}$ becomes significant for thick-walled pipes or low-conductivity materials (e.g., glass, PTFE-lined).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In a double pipe counter-current heat exchanger, hot water enters at $T_{h,i} = 80°C$ and leaves at $T_{h,o} = 50°C$. Cold water enters at $T_{c,i} = 20°C$ and leaves at $T_{c,o} = 40°C$. Mass flow rates: $\dot{m}_h = 0.5$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_c = 0.7$ kg/s. Take $C_{ph} = C_{pc} = 4180$ J/kg·K and overall coefficient $U = 1500$ W/m²·K. Find (a) Heat duty, (b) LMTD, (c) Heat transfer area.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Heat Duty:**
$$
Q_h = \dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 0.5 \times 4180 \times (80 - 50)
$$
$$
Q_h = 0.5 \times 4180 \times 30 = 62{,}700 \text{ W} = 62.7 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_c = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 0.7 \times 4180 \times (40 - 20)
$$
$$
Q_c = 0.7 \times 4180 \times 20 = 58{,}520 \text{ W} = 58.52 \text{ kW}
$$

Average heat duty:
$$
Q_{avg} = \dfrac{62{,}700 + 58{,}520}{2} = 60{,}610 \text{ W} \approx 60.61 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (b) — LMTD (Counter-Current):**
$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 80 - 40 = 40°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 50 - 20 = 30°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{40 - 30}{\ln(40/30)} = \dfrac{10}{0.2877}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = 34.76°C}
$$

**Step (c) — Heat Transfer Area:**
$$
A = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{60{,}610}{1500 \times 34.76}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 1.162 \text{ m}^2}
$$

If the inner pipe has $D_i = 0.025$ m, then length required:
$$
L = \dfrac{A}{\pi D_i} = \dfrac{1.162}{\pi \times 0.025} = 14.80 \text{ m}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Co-current vs. Counter-Current

| Property | Co-current Flow | Counter-current Flow |
|----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Flow direction | Same | Opposite |
| LMTD value | Lower | Higher (preferred) |
| Cold outlet temperature | Always $<$ Hot outlet | Can exceed Hot outlet |
| Temperature profile | Pinches at one end | More uniform |
| Industrial preference | Rarely used | Strongly preferred |
| Heat transfer area required | Larger | Smaller |
| Fouling tendency | Less uniform | Slightly more at one end |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HWT["HOT WATER TANK WITH HEATER"] --> P1["HOT WATER PUMP"]
    P1 --> V1["FLOAT TYPE ROTAMETER FMH"]
    V1 --> T1["INLET TEMPERATURE SENSOR THI"]
    T1 --> IP["INNER PIPE HOT WATER FLOW"]
    IP --> T2["OUTLET TEMPERATURE SENSOR THO"]
    T2 --> HWT
    CWT["COLD WATER OVERHEAD TANK"] --> V2["CONTROL VALVE"]
    V2 --> FMC["FLOAT TYPE ROTAMETER FMC"]
    FMC --> T3["INLET TEMPERATURE SENSOR TCI"]
    T3 --> AP["ANNULAR SPACE COLD WATER FLOW"]
    AP --> T4["OUTLET TEMPERATURE SENSOR TCO"]
    T4 --> CWOUT["COLD WATER OUTLET TO DRAIN"]
    IP -.HEAT TRANSFER THROUGH WALL.-> AP
    HWT -.STEAM OR ELECTRIC HEATER.-> HWT
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Transfer Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HOT FLUID INLET THI"] --> B["CONVECTIVE LAYER INSIDE INNER PIPE"]
    B --> C["PIPE WALL CONDUCTION LAYER"]
    C --> D["CONVECTIVE LAYER IN ANNULUS"]
    D --> E["COLD FLUID OUTLET TCO"]
    F["COLD FLUID INLET TCI"] --> D
    G["HOT FLUID OUTLET THO"] --> B
    H["RESISTANCE RH = 1 PER HI AI"] --> B
    I["RESISTANCE RW = LN D0 DI PER 2 PI K L"] --> C
    J["RESISTANCE R0 = 1 PER HO A0"] --> D
    K["OVERALL UO"] -. COMPUTED FROM .-> H
    K -. COMPUTED FROM .-> I
    K -. COMPUTED FROM .-> J
```

## 4.3 Typical Observation Table Format (For Lab Record)

| S.No. | $\dot{m}_h$ (kg/s) | $\dot{m}_c$ (kg/s) | $T_{h,i}$ (°C) | $T_{h,o}$ (°C) | $T_{c,i}$ (°C) | $T_{c,o}$ (°C) | $Q$ (W) | LMTD (°C) | $A$ (m²) | $U$ (W/m²K) |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|---------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 78 | 55 | 22 | 38 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 2 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 80 | 52 | 21 | 40 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 3 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 81 | 50 | 20 | 42 | ... | ... | ... | ... |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for Lab Records:** Always tabulate both **experimental U** and **theoretical U** (from Dittus-Boelter) in adjacent columns and plot a **log-log graph of $U$ vs $Re$**. The slope of this graph (typically 0.8) validates the Dittus-Boelter correlation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is it preferred over the Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD) in heat exchanger analysis?

**Model Answer:**
The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends of the exchanger. It represents the **exponentially averaged** temperature driving force for heat transfer along the length of the exchanger. Since the temperature difference $\Delta T$ between hot and cold fluids varies **exponentially** (not linearly) along the length, the arithmetic mean introduces significant error, especially when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 > 2$. The LMTD, derived from integration of the differential heat transfer equation, gives the correct effective driving force. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for "why" explanation)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between co-current and counter-current double pipe heat exchangers. Which is preferred industrially and why?

**Model Answer:**
In a **co-current (parallel flow)** exchanger, both hot and cold fluids enter from the same end and flow in the same direction. In a **counter-current** exchanger, the fluids enter from opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. Counter-current is **industrially preferred** because:
- It provides a higher and more uniform LMTD.
- It enables the cold fluid outlet temperature to **exceed** the hot fluid outlet temperature (impossible in co-current).
- It requires a smaller heat transfer area for the same duty.
- The temperature profile is more uniform, reducing thermal stress and fouling. **(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 2 for comparison and reasoning)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, ESE Module Internal Choice]**

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose Either A or B)**

**Question A (a):** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a double pipe heat exchanger. Derive the expression for the LMTD for counter-current flow. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction and Working:** A double pipe heat exchanger consists of two concentric pipes — an **inner pipe** through which the hot fluid flows, and an **outer pipe** that encloses the inner pipe, forming an **annular space** for the cold fluid. Standard construction uses mild steel inner pipe (for good heat conduction) and GI/MS outer pipe. The exchanger is fitted with inlet/outlet connections, thermometers or RTD sensors at all four ports, and rotameters for flow measurement. The entire assembly may include bends (e.g., hairpin or U-bend) to increase the effective length within a small footprint.

Hot water enters the inner pipe at $T_{h,i}$, flows through it, and exits at $T_{h,o}$. Cold water enters the annular space at $T_{c,i}$ (opposite end, for counter-current), flows in the opposite direction, and exits at $T_{c,o}$. Heat passes from hot water → inner pipe wall (conduction) → cold water in annulus (convection).

**Derivation of LMTD (Counter-Current):**
For a differential area $dA$:
$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)
$$
Also, $dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} \, dT_c$

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. For counter-current:
$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} - \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right) dA
$$

Integrating from $A=0$ to $A=A$ and $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$:
$$
\ln\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)
$$

Since $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ and using $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$:
$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \quad \text{and} \quad Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for working explanation, 3 for derivation]**

---

**Question A (b):** In a double pipe counter-current heat exchanger, hot oil ($\dot{m}_h = 0.3$ kg/s, $C_{ph} = 2100$ J/kg·K) cools from 120°C to 60°C, heating cold water from 25°C to 55°C ($\dot{m}_c = 0.4$ kg/s, $C_{pc} = 4180$ J/kg·K). If $U = 800$ W/m²·K, calculate: (i) Heat transfer rate, (ii) LMTD, (iii) Required heat transfer area. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat transfer rate:**
Hot side:
$$
Q_h = 0.3 \times 2100 \times (120 - 60) = 0.3 \times 2100 \times 60 = 37{,}800 \text{ W}
$$
Cold side:
$$
Q_c = 0.4 \times 4180 \times (55 - 25) = 0.4 \times 4180 \times 30 = 50{,}160 \text{ W}
$$
Average heat duty:
$$
Q_{avg} = \dfrac{37{,}800 + 50{,}160}{2} = 43{,}980 \text{ W} \approx 43.98 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) LMTD (Counter-current):**
$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 120 - 55 = 65°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 60 - 25 = 35°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{65 - 35}{\ln(65/35)} = \dfrac{30}{\ln(1.857)} = \dfrac{30}{0.6190} = 48.47°C
$$

**(iii) Heat transfer area:**
$$
A = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{43{,}980}{800 \times 48.47} = \dfrac{43{,}980}{38{,}776} = 1.134 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Final Answers:** $Q = 43.98$ kW, $\Delta T_{lm} = 48.47°C$, $A = 1.134$ m² **[7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3]**

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Question B (a):** Explain the NTU-Effectiveness method for heat exchanger analysis. Derive the effectiveness expression for a counter-current double pipe exchanger. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**NTU-Effectiveness Method:**
When the outlet temperatures of either fluid are **unknown**, the LMTD method cannot be directly applied. The **NTU (Number of Transfer Units) — Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)** method is used. It is defined as:

- **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{\text{Actual heat transfer}}{\text{Maximum possible heat transfer}}$
- $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$
- **NTU:** $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$

**Derivation (Counter-current):**
Define $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}$. Let $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$ be the smaller and larger of the two, and $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$.

From energy balance:
$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} \quad \text{(if } C_c = C_{min}\text{)}
$$
$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} \quad \text{(if } C_h = C_{min}\text{)}
$$

For counter-current flow, the closed-form solution is:
$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}}
$$

For $C_r = 0$ (one fluid with very large capacity), this reduces to $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU}$, which is the same as co-current. For $C_r = 1$ (balanced flow):
$$
\varepsilon_{counter} = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 for definitions, 3 for derivation, 2 for limiting cases]**

---

**Question B (b):** Hot water at 90°C flows through the inner pipe ($D_i = 25$ mm, $D_o = 32$ mm, $k = 50$ W/m·K) of a counter-current double pipe exchanger at $\dot{m}_h = 0.2$ kg/s. Cold water flows in the annulus at $\dot{m}_c = 0.15$ kg/s, entering at 25°C. Inner heat transfer coefficient $h_i = 3000$ W/m²·K and outer $h_o = 2500$ W/m²·K. Calculate: (i) Overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o$, (ii) The required length if cold water leaves at 50°C. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Overall heat transfer coefficient (referred to outer area):**
$$
\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{D_o}{h_i D_i} + \dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o / D_i)}{2k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

Compute each term:
- Term 1: $\dfrac{0.032}{3000 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.032}{75} = 4.267 \times 10^{-4}$ m²K/W
- Term 2: $\dfrac{0.032 \times \ln(32/25)}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{0.032 \times 0.2469}{100} = 7.90 \times 10^{-5}$ m²K/W
- Term 3: $\dfrac{1}{2500} = 4.00 \times 10^{-4}$ m²K/W

Sum:
$$
\dfrac{1}{U_o} = 4.267 \times 10^{-4} + 7.90 \times 10^{-5} + 4.00 \times 10^{-4} = 9.057 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_o = \dfrac{1}{9.057 \times 10^{-4}} = 1104.1 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Required length:**
Heat duty:
$$
Q = 0.15 \times 4180 \times (50 - 25) = 15{,}675 \text{ W}
$$

Hot water outlet:
$$
T_{h,o} = 90 - \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} = 90 - \dfrac{15{,}675}{0.2 \times 4180} = 90 - 18.75 = 71.25°C
$$

LMTD:
$$
\Delta T_1 = 90 - 50 = 40°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_2 = 71.25 - 25 = 46.25°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{40 - 46.25}{\ln(40/46.25)} = \dfrac{-6.25}{-0.1449} = 43.13°C
$$

Area:
$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{15{,}675}{1104.1 \times 43.13} = 0.3292 \text{ m}^2
$$

Length (using outer diameter since $U_o$ is on outer area basis):
$$
L = \dfrac{A}{\pi D_o} = \dfrac{0.3292}{\pi \times 0.032} = 3.27 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:** $U_o = 1104.1$ W/m²·K, $L = 3.27$ m **[7 marks: 3 for $U$, 4 for length]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> ⚠️ **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in LMTD for counter-current:** Many students incorrectly use $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ for counter-current. **Correct:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ (the two ends, not the two inlets).
>
> 2. **Mixing up the basis of $U$:** $U_i$ is based on inner area, $U_o$ is based on outer area. The area used in $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ **must match** the basis of $U$. If you use $U_o$, you must use $A_o = \pi D_o L$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units conversion:** Flow rate in **LPM** must be converted to **m³/s** (divide by 60,000) before using in SI formulas.
>
> 4. **Ignoring heat losses:** In practice, $Q_h \neq Q_c$ due to heat loss to surroundings. Always compute the **average** $Q$ for accuracy.
>
> 5. **Confusing NTU formula:** NTU uses $C_{min}$ (not $C_{max}$). Marks deducted if swapped.
>
> 6. **Not specifying flow arrangement:** When asked to find LMTD, **always** state whether the system is co-current or counter-current and define the two $\Delta T$ terms explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before KTU Exam**

- 🔹 A **double pipe heat exchanger** is a 1-1 concentric tube device; the inner pipe is the heat transfer surface.
- 🔹 The **LMTD** must always be calculated with $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ representing the temperature difference at the **two ends** of the exchanger.
- 🔹 For **co-current**: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$
- 🔹 For **counter-current**: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$
- 🔹 The fundamental heat duty equation: $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$
- 🔹 For a **1-1** exchanger (double pipe), the LMTD correction factor $F = 1$; no $F$-factor chart needed.
- 🔹 **Counter-current** is always preferred industrially due to higher LMTD and greater temperature cross capability.
- 🔹 The **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$ depends on three resistances: inner convection, wall conduction, outer convection.
- 🔹 **Dittus-Boelter correlation** ($Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^n$) is valid for fully developed turbulent flow in smooth pipes ($Re > 10{,}000$, $0.7 < Pr < 160$).
- 🔹 In the **NTU method**: $NTU = UA / C_{min}$, $\varepsilon = Q / Q_{max}$, where $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$.
- 🔹 Always **average** $Q_h$ and $Q_c$ to account for ambient heat loss in lab experiments.
- 🔹 The **equivalent diameter** for an annulus is $D_e = D_o - D_i$ (outer pipe ID minus inner pipe OD).
- 🔹 Standard lab values: $C_p$ of water = **4180 J/kg·K**; $k$ of mild steel = **45 W/m·K**.
- 🔹 The slope of the **log U vs. log Re** plot (in lab) ≈ **0.8** confirms the Dittus-Boelter correlation.
- 🔹 **Co-current** cannot produce a temperature cross; **counter-current** can, making it ideal for sensitive applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer in Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer in Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is a class of indirect-contact heat transfer equipment in which one fluid (the *hot fluid*) flows inside a bundle of tubes while the second fluid (the *cold fluid*) flows over the outside of these tubes within an enclosing pressure vessel called the *shell*. Heat transfer between the two streams occurs by **conduction through the tube wall** and **convection** on both the tube-side and shell-side fluid films. The geometric configuration (number of shell passes, number of tube passes, baffle arrangement) is engineered to maximize the *Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)* while satisfying the mechanical constraints of pressure drop, fouling allowance, and thermal stress.

For a KTU 2024 Scheme examination, the working definition expected is:

> *"A Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger is a device used to transfer heat between two fluids separated by a solid metallic wall, where one fluid moves through the tubes (tube side) and the other moves through the shell (shell side), with the rate of heat transfer governed by the overall heat transfer coefficient U and the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital IV Line and the Towel**
> 
> Imagine you have a hot cup of tea (hot fluid) and you want to cool it. If you simply blow air over it, cooling is slow. But if you pass the hot tea through a thin metal straw placed inside a glass of ice water, the tea cools very fast. The thin metal straw acts like the **tube wall**, the tea inside is the **tube-side fluid**, and the ice water outside is the **shell-side fluid**. Many such thin straws (tubes) packed together give a huge surface area, just like the many folds in a towel increase the surface area for drying your hands. The **more surface area and thinner walls**, the faster heat moves from tea to ice water. That, in essence, is a shell and tube heat exchanger — a *thermal "towel"* for industrial heat exchange.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical SI Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water | **Cp** | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** | Standard at ~30 °C |
| Density of water | **ρ** | **995.7 kg/m³** | At 30 °C |
| Thermal conductivity (tube wall) | **k** | **16.3 W/(m·K)** (for stainless steel 304) | Wall material property |
| Fouling factor (clean water) | **Rf** | **0.0001–0.0002 m²·K/W** | Build-up resistance |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | **σ** | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** | Not relevant here |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment aims to demonstrate the operation of a 1-2 (one shell pass, two tube pass) STHE in **counter-current flow** and to determine the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** and **Heat Transfer Rate (Q)** experimentally, then compare them with theoretical values obtained from standard correlations such as **Dittus-Boelter** and **Sieder-Tate**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Logic Flow of the Experiment

The experiment follows a strict sequence of analytical steps. Each step addresses the *Why* and *How*:

1. **Step 1 — Steady-State Establishment:** Hot water is circulated through the tube side and cold water through the shell side until the inlet/outlet temperatures become time-invariant. *Why?* The governing heat-transfer equations (Q = U·A·LMTD) assume steady-state operation; transient behavior violates this assumption.

2. **Step 2 — Energy Balance:** Heat lost by hot fluid = Heat gained by cold fluid + Heat loss to surroundings. *How?* Quantified via the mass-flow rates and specific heats measured for both streams.

3. **Step 3 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):** Calculated using the terminal temperatures. *Why LMTD and not arithmetic mean?* Because the temperature profile along the exchanger is exponential in counter-current flow; the LMTD correctly weights this geometric profile.

4. **Step 4 — Heat Transfer Area:** Computed from the geometric tube dimensions (length, outer diameter, number of tubes).

5. **Step 5 — Overall U Determination:** Rearranged from Q = U·A·LMTD as U = Q / (A·LMTD).

6. **Step 6 — Theoretical U Calculation:** Individual film coefficients (hi, ho) calculated using dimensional correlations (Dittus-Boelter for turbulent flow inside tubes; Kern's method for shell side).

7. **Step 7 — Comparison & Fouling Analysis:** Experimental vs. theoretical U values; the discrepancy is attributed to the fouling factor and the simplifying assumptions of the correlations.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility

Shell and tube heat exchangers are the **workhorses of the chemical process industry (CPI)**. Applications include:
- **Petroleum refineries** — preheating crude oil before distillation.
- **Power plants** — condensers and feedwater heaters.
- **Dairy/food industry** — pasteurization of milk.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — temperature control of fermentation broths.
- **HVAC** — chilled water systems in large buildings.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** a student must memorize for the KTU 2024 lab viva and end-semester exam on this experiment. Pay special attention to the **units** — they are the single biggest source of lost marks.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Heat lost by hot fluid (tube side) | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat gained by cold fluid (shell side) | W |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Fundamental heat exchanger design equation | W |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 5 | $A = n \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$ | Total heat transfer area | m² |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K or °C |
| 7 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | Hot-end temperature difference (counter-current) | K |
| 8 | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | Cold-end temperature difference (counter-current) | K |
| 9 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation (tube side, heating) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.3}$ | Dittus-Boelter (cooling fluid) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot d}{k}$ | Definition of Nusselt number | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \cdot d \cdot \mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow inside tube | dimensionless |
| 13 | $Pr = \dfrac{C_p \cdot \mu}{k}$ | Prandtl number | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{d_o}{d_i \cdot h_i} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w}$ | Overall resistance (fouled, outside-area basis) | m²·K/W |
| 15 | $F$ (correction factor) | Depends on P and R (read from charts) | dimensionless |
| 16 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Temperature effectiveness parameter | dimensionless |
| 17 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$ | Heat capacity ratio parameter | dimensionless |
| 18 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Thermal effectiveness | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Note:** In equations where $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$, always take $\vert \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 \vert$ in the numerator and invert the ratio inside the logarithm. KTU examiners specifically test this sign-convention trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a differential element of length $dx$ along a counter-current shell-and-tube exchanger. Let $T_h(x)$ and $T_c(x)$ be the local bulk temperatures of the hot and cold fluids respectively. Let $U$ be constant along the length.

**Step 1 — Differential Heat Transfer**

For an infinitesimal area $dA = \pi \cdot d_o \cdot dx$, the rate of heat transfer is:

$$\begin{aligned}
dQ &= U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Energy Balances on the Element**

For the hot fluid flowing in the negative x-direction (counter-current):

$$\begin{aligned}
dQ &= -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h
\end{aligned}$$

For the cold fluid:

$$\begin{aligned}
dQ &= +\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Local Temperature Difference**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T(x) &= T_h(x) - T_c(x) \\
d(\Delta T) &= dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}
\end{aligned}$$

Factoring out $dQ$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(\Delta T) &= -dQ \left[ \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Define the constant $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$. Then $d(\Delta T) = -\beta \, dQ$.

**Step 4 — Substitute from Step 1**

$$\begin{aligned}
d(\Delta T) &= -\beta \cdot U \cdot (\Delta T) \cdot dA
\end{aligned}$$

Separating variables:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -\beta U \, dA
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from Inlet to Outlet**

Integrating between limits (Area 0 to A, $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -\beta U \int_0^A dA \\
\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) &= -\beta U A \\
\beta U A &= \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Total Heat Transfer**

The total heat transferred equals:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) \\
Q &= \frac{1}{\beta} (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \quad \text{(after algebra)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Eliminate β and Solve for U·A**

Multiplying Step 6 by $\beta U A$ and substituting $\beta U A = \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \frac{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)
\end{aligned}$$

Hmm — let me redo Step 7 cleanly. From Step 5:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\beta} &= \frac{U A}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into the expression for Q:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} \\
  &= (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \cdot \frac{U A}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \\
  &= U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\; Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \;}$$

This completes the derivation of the **LMTD**.

### 3.2 Derivative Worked Example (Model Calculation)

**Given (typical KTU lab values):**

- Hot water (tube side) inlet: $T_{h,in} = 60$ °C; outlet: $T_{h,out} = 50$ °C
- Cold water (shell side) inlet: $T_{c,in} = 30$ °C; outlet: $T_{c,out} = 38$ °C
- Hot water mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_h = 0.05$ kg/s
- Cold water mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_c = 0.08$ kg/s
- $C_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) for both streams
- Tubes: 7 tubes, length $L = 1.0$ m, outer diameter $d_o = 0.0127$ m

**Step 1 — Heat Transfer Rate from Hot Fluid**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) \\
  &= 0.05 \times 4186 \times (60 - 50) \\
  &= 0.05 \times 4186 \times 10 \\
  &= 2093 \; \text{W} \;\; (\approx 2.093 \; \text{kW})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Heat Transfer Rate from Cold Fluid (Energy Balance Check)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) \\
  &= 0.08 \times 4186 \times (38 - 30) \\
  &= 0.08 \times 4186 \times 8 \\
  &= 2679 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

> The two values (2093 W vs. 2679 W) differ by ≈ 22 %. The smaller of the two (2093 W) is conventionally used for U calculation because it is the more conservative estimate. The difference is attributed to **heat loss to the surroundings**.

**Step 3 — LMTD for Counter-Current**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 60 - 38 = 22 \; \text{°C} \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 30 = 20 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lmtd} &= \frac{22 - 20}{\ln(22/20)} \\
               &= \frac{2}{\ln(1.1)} \\
               &= \frac{2}{0.0953} \\
               &= 20.99 \; \text{°C} \;\; (\approx 21 \; \text{K})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= n \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \\
  &= 7 \times \pi \times 0.0127 \times 1.0 \\
  &= 0.2793 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Experimental Overall U**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{exp} &= \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} \\
       &= \frac{2093}{0.2793 \times 20.99} \\
       &= \frac{2093}{5.862} \\
       &= 357 \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Q in W, A in m², ΔT in K (numerically identical to °C for a difference). Resulting units: W/(m²·K). KTU board examiners deduct marks if students write W/m²·°C without checking — although dimensionally valid, it is non-standard.

### 3.3 Dittus-Boelter Inside a Tube (Tube-Side Coefficient)

Given tube inside diameter $d_i = 0.0102$ m, hot water at mean bulk temperature $T_{m,h} = 55$ °C.

**Water properties at 55 °C (from steam tables):**
- $\rho \approx 985.7$ kg/m³
- $\mu \approx 5.05 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- $k \approx 0.649$ W/(m·K)
- $Pr \approx 3.16$

**Velocity inside a single tube:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\dot{m}_h}{n \cdot \rho \cdot (\pi d_i^2/4)} \\
  &= \frac{0.05}{7 \times 985.7 \times (\pi \times 0.0102^2/4)} \\
  &= \frac{0.05}{7 \times 985.7 \times 8.17 \times 10^{-5}} \\
  &= \frac{0.05}{0.5637} \\
  &= 0.0887 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reynolds number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho v d_i}{\mu} \\
   &= \frac{985.7 \times 0.0887 \times 0.0102}{5.05 \times 10^{-4}} \\
   &= \frac{0.892}{5.05 \times 10^{-4}} \\
   &= 1767
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re < 2300$, flow is **laminar** — Dittus-Boelter does NOT apply. Use the Sieder-Tate laminar correlation:

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu &= 1.86 \left( Re \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{d_i}{L} \right)^{1/3} \left( \frac{\mu_b}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming $\mu_b/\mu_w \approx 1.0$ and $d_i/L = 0.0102$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{d_i}{L} &= 1767 \times 3.16 \times 0.0102 \\
                                &= 56.96 \\
(\ldots)^{1/3} &= 3.852 \\
Nu &= 1.86 \times 3.852 = 7.16
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_i &= \frac{Nu \cdot k}{d_i} = \frac{7.16 \times 0.649}{0.0102} = 455.6 \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}
\end{aligned}$$

> This shows the real-world complexity: lab flow rates often produce laminar flow, breaking the textbook assumption of turbulent Dittus-Boelter. KTU viva questions frequently probe this nuance.
