## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Part A]** Define *Log Mean Temperature Difference* (LMTD) and state why it is preferred over the arithmetic mean temperature difference for heat exchanger calculations.

**Model Answer:**
The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$, where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends of the heat exchanger. The arithmetic mean is inaccurate because the temperature profile along the length of the exchanger is exponential (or nearly so) due to the linear variation of local U·ΔT, not linear. LMTD correctly weights this geometric variation and is derived rigorously from differential energy balances. *For the special case* when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (i.e., both streams have equal heat capacity rates), LMTD reduces to the arithmetic mean. **(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for reasoning)**

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Part A]** Differentiate between *co-current* and *counter-current* flow arrangements in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Which configuration gives a higher LMTD for the same terminal temperatures?

**Model Answer:**
In **co-current flow**, both hot and cold fluids enter the exchanger from the same end and flow in the same direction. In **counter-current flow**, the fluids enter from opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. **Counter-current flow** gives a higher LMTD for the same inlet and outlet temperatures, except when the heat capacity rates of the two streams are equal. Moreover, counter-current flow permits the cold fluid outlet temperature to exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature, which is impossible in co-current flow — making counter-current thermodynamically more efficient. **(3 marks: 1 for distinction + 2 for comparative analysis)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Part B, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger**. Explain the role of *baffles* in enhancing heat transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a laboratory shell-and-tube heat exchanger experiment, hot water enters the tube side at 70 °C and leaves at 55 °C. Cold water enters the shell side at 25 °C and leaves at 40 °C. The mass flow rates are 0.04 kg/s (hot) and 0.06 kg/s (cold). The exchanger has 10 tubes of outer diameter 12.7 mm and effective length 1.2 m. Calculate: **(i)** Heat duty Q, **(ii)** LMTD for counter-current flow, **(iii)** Overall heat transfer coefficient U. Take $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K) for both streams. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Construction & Working:**

A 1-2 STHE consists of:

- **Shell**: A cylindrical pressure vessel (usually carbon steel) with flanged ends. Has inlet and outlet nozzles for the shell-side fluid.
- **Tubes**: A bundle of parallel tubes (commonly copper or stainless steel) rolled into two *tube sheets* at either end.
- **Tube passes (2)**: The tubes are divided by a *partition* (or *pass partition*) at one end into two passes. Fluid enters pass 1 (front header), flows through half the tubes to the rear header, makes a U-turn, then flows back through the remaining tubes (pass 2) before exiting.
- **Shell passes (1)**: A single shell-side flow path with no shell-side partition.
- **Baffles**: Segmental plate baffles are placed perpendicular to the tube axis, with alternating cut-out sections. Their role is threefold:
  1. **Increase turbulence** on the shell side by forcing the fluid to flow perpendicular to the tubes.
  2. **Support tubes** mechanically against vibration and sagging.
  3. **Increase effective velocity** of shell-side fluid, thereby increasing $h_o$.

**Working:** Hot water enters one end of the tube bundle, makes two passes as described, and exits. Cold water enters the shell at the opposite end (counter-current) and flows across the tubes, gaining heat. Heat moves by conduction through the tube wall and convection on both fluid films.

**(b) Calculations:**

**Part (i) — Heat Duty Q:**

Using hot fluid:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_h \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) \\
  &= 0.04 \times 4180 \times (70 - 55) \\
  &= 0.04 \times 4180 \times 15 \\
  &= 2508 \; \text{W} \;\; (\approx 2.508 \; \text{kW})
\end{aligned}$$

Using cold fluid (verification):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_c \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) \\
  &= 0.06 \times 4180 \times (40 - 25) \\
  &= 0.06 \times 4180 \times 15 \\
  &= 3762 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

Average Q = (2508 + 3762)/2 = **3135 W** (or use 2508 W conservatively, depending on convention followed at institution). For this solution, take Q = 3135 W.

**Part (ii) — LMTD:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 70 - 40 = 30 \; \text{K} \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 55 - 25 = 30 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 30$ K, we have the special case:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lmtd} &= \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 30 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

(Or equivalently, $\lim_{\Delta T_1 \to \Delta T_2}$ of the LMTD formula equals either terminal difference.)

**Part (iii) — Heat Transfer Area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= n \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \\
  &= 10 \times \pi \times 0.0127 \times 1.2 \\
  &= 10 \times 3.1416 \times 0.01524 \\
  &= 0.4787 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall U:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} \\
  &= \frac{3135}{0.4787 \times 30} \\
  &= \frac{3135}{14.36} \\
  &= 218.3 \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 218 \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Part B, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **effectiveness-NTU method** for heat exchanger analysis. Derive the expression for effectiveness of a single-pass counter-current heat exchanger when both fluids are unmixed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to heat 1.25 kg/s of water from 35 °C to 80 °C using hot water available at 160 °C flowing at 2.5 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 2000 W/(m²·K). Calculate: **(i)** The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD), **(ii)** The heat transfer area required, **(iii)** The effectiveness of the exchanger. Take $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K) for water. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Effectiveness-NTU Method:**

The effectiveness-NTU (ε-NTU) method is used when outlet temperatures are unknown but inlet conditions and the heat exchanger type are specified. Two key parameters are defined:

- **Heat capacity rate ratio:** $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$
- **Number of Transfer Units (NTU):** $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$

The **effectiveness** $\varepsilon$ is defined as the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation for single-pass counter-current heat exchanger:**

For counter-current flow with $C_r \ne 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{1 - \exp\left[-NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\left[-NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)\right]}
\end{aligned}$$

For the special case $C_r = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation outline:**

From energy balance: $Q = C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$

For counter-current, NTU is defined from the heat balance integral:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}}\right) &= \frac{U A}{C_h}\left(1 - \frac{C_h}{C_c}\right) \\
\frac{T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}} &= \exp\left[NTU (1 - C_r)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

After algebraic rearrangement to express outlet temperatures in terms of effectiveness and substitution of $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})/C_r$ (with $C_{min}$ as denominator convention), the expression for ε emerges as above.

**(b) Calculations:**

**Part (i) — LMTD (assuming counter-current):**

Heat capacity rates:
$C_h = \dot{m}_h C_p = 2.5 \times 4180 = 10450$ W/K
$C_c = \dot{m}_c C_p = 1.25 \times 4180 = 5225$ W/K

Energy balance on hot fluid:
$T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \dfrac{Q}{C_h}$

$Q = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 5225 \times (80 - 35) = 5225 \times 45 = 235125$ W

$T_{h,out} = 160 - \dfrac{235125}{10450} = 160 - 22.50 = 137.5$ °C

LMTD:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 160 - 80 = 80 \; \text{K} \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 137.5 - 35 = 102.5 \; \text{K} \\
\Delta T_{lmtd} &= \frac{80 - 102.5}{\ln(80/102.5)} \\
               &= \frac{-22.5}{\ln(0.7805)} \\
               &= \frac{-22.5}{-0.2479} \\
               &= 90.77 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat Transfer Area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} \\
  &= \frac{235125}{2000 \times 90.77} \\
  &= \frac{235125}{181540} \\
  &= 1.295 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iii) — Effectiveness:**

$C_{min} = 5225$ W/K (cold stream)

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} \\
            &= \frac{235125}{5225 \times (160 - 35)} \\
            &= \frac{235125}{5225 \times 125} \\
            &= \frac{235125}{653125} \\
            &= 0.36
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.36 \; (36\%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign convention in LMTD:** Students frequently forget to take $\vert \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 \vert$ in the numerator when $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$. Always ensure positive numerator; adjust sign inside logarithm accordingly.
> 2. **Wrong direction of flow assumption:** If the problem does not specify, assume **counter-current flow** (KTU convention). Using co-current gives a smaller LMTD and hence larger area — examiners penalize this if not justified.
> 3. **Use of $d_i$ vs $d_o$:** The overall heat transfer coefficient is referenced to the outer surface area $A_o$ (since $A = n \pi d_o L$ is the externally wetted area). Using $d_i$ leads to U values inflated by the ratio $d_o/d_i \approx 1.25$.
> 4. **Fouling factor omission:** Industrial STHE design always includes $R_f$. Lab values often assume clean surfaces.
> 5. **Units of U:** Always W/(m²·K) — not W/m²·°C unless explicitly stated.
> 6. **Steady-state neglect:** If temperatures are still changing when readings are taken, the data is invalid. KTU evaluates procedural awareness.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger Lab**

- **Apparatus:** Hot water tank, cold water tank, two centrifugal pumps, two rotameters (flow meters), STHE unit with baffles, digital temperature indicators, and a stopwatch.
- **Steady-state requirement:** Always wait 5–10 minutes after changing flow rates before recording data.
- **Counter-current flow:** Always assumed unless stated otherwise; gives higher LMTD.
- **Core design equation:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ — the *master equation* of this experiment.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ — handle the $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ special case correctly.
- **Area formula:** $A = n \pi d_o L$ (outer tube surface, since baffles and shell enhance outer convection).
- **Energy balance sanity check:** Always compute Q from both hot and cold fluid data; report the average or the conservative (smaller) value with a comment on heat loss.
- **Experimental U calculation:** $U_{exp} = Q/(A \Delta T_{lmtd})$.
- **Theoretical U calculation:** Use $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w}$.
- **Turbulent flow correlation (tube side):** Dittus-Boelter — $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ (heating) or $Pr^{0.3}$ (cooling). **Validity:** $Re > 10{,}000$, $0.7 < Pr < 160$, $L/d > 10$.
- **Laminar flow correlation (tube side):** Sieder-Tate — $Nu = 1.86 (Re Pr d/L)^{1/3} (\mu_b/\mu_w)^{0.14}$.
- **Reynolds number for tube-side flow:** $Re = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi d_i \mu}$ (mass flow per tube basis).
- **Effectiveness ε:** $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max}$; ranges from 0 to 1. Higher ε = better exchanger.
- **NTU:** $NTU = UA/C_{min}$; dimensionless measure of exchanger "size" in terms of heat transfer.
- **Fouling:** Build-up of deposits on tube walls; reduces U over time; factor $R_f$ in m²·K/W.
- **Pressure drop:** Important but not measured in this lab; for design, ΔP should be < 10–20% of inlet pressure.
- **Effect of increasing flow rate:** Higher Re → higher h → higher U → smaller required area; but higher ΔP penalty.
- **Baffles:** Increase $h_o$ by inducing cross-flow and turbulence on the shell side.
- **Heat capacity rates ratio $C_r$:** $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$; affects effectiveness expression.
- **Sketch essentials for the answer sheet:** Always draw the exchanger with clearly labeled inlets, outlets, baffles, tube passes, and shell — examiners allocate up to 2 marks for neat sketches alone.
- **Typical lab U values:** For water-water STHE, $U \approx 1000$–$3000$ W/(m²·K); values below 200 indicate fouling or laminar flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: EMISSIVITY MEASUREMENT APPARATUS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emissivity (ε)** is defined as the ratio of the thermal radiation intensity emitted by a given surface to the radiation intensity emitted by an ideal **blackbody** (perfect emitter) at the same thermodynamic temperature.

Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{blackbody}}}$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{actual}}$ = Emissive power of the test surface (W/m²)
- $E_{\text{blackbody}}$ = Emissive power of a perfect blackbody (W/m²)

The emissivity is a **dimensionless quantity** that ranges between **0 and 1**:
- $\varepsilon = 1$ → Perfect blackbody (ideal emitter)
- $\varepsilon = 0$ → Perfect reflector (no emission)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Stage Performer" Concept:**
> Imagine two singers performing on stage. The first singer (a **blackbody**) gives 100% of his vocal energy to the microphone. The second singer (a **real surface**) is shy and only gives, say, 30% of his energy. Emissivity is simply the **"performance ratio"** — how much of the maximum possible radiation energy the surface actually emits. A polished silver mirror has very low emissivity (~0.02), meaning it is a "lazy singer," while a rough blackened copper plate (~0.95) is a "powerful singer" close to perfect.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Standard emissivity reference surfaces** used in KTU labs: blackened copper plate, polished aluminum, oxidized brass
- **Operating temperature range** of typical apparatus: **50 °C to 250 °C**
- **Sensor type:** Thermopile / Radiation pyrometer

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Curriculum

This experiment links **Module 1 (Heat Transfer by Radiation)** theory directly to **measurable experimental data**. The student learns to:
1. Apply the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** in practice.
2. Distinguish between **blackbody** and **gray body** behavior.
3. Understand the role of **surface finish, oxidation, and color** on radiative heat transfer.
4. Develop skill in **calibration** using a reference black surface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Foundation

The Emissivity Measurement Apparatus operates on the principle of the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, which states that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit surface area per unit time is proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature.

### 2.2 The Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Heating the Test Plate:**
A test plate is heated electrically using a heater embedded beneath it. The surface temperature $T_s$ rises until steady state is reached.

**Step 2 — Steady-State Radiation Balance:**
At steady state, the electrical power input equals the sum of all heat losses (convection + conduction + radiation). The dominant loss at high temperature is **radiation**.

**Step 3 — Emitted Radiation Measurement:**
A thermopile sensor measures the radiation intensity $E_{\text{actual}}$ incident on it from the test plate at a fixed distance.

**Step 4 — Blackbody Calibration:**
A reference surface (with known emissivity, ideally $\varepsilon_b \approx 1$ — a "lampblack" coated surface) is placed in the same position, and $E_{\text{blackbody}}$ is measured at identical temperature.

**Step 5 — Ratio Calculation:**
The emissivity is then calculated as the ratio of measured signals.

**Step 6 — The "Why" Question — Why ratio method?**
The ratio method eliminates uncertainties about sensor calibration constants, geometric view factors, and ambient drift. It directly uses **relative measurement**, which is the hallmark of accurate experimental physics.

### 2.3 Governing Equation Set

The total radiation emitted by a real surface:

$$E_{\text{actual}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4$$

The radiation emitted by an ideal blackbody at the same temperature:

$$E_{\text{blackbody}} = \sigma \cdot T_s^4$$

Emissivity definition:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{\sigma \cdot T_s^4}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you need for solving KTU exam problems on this topic.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$ | Total emissive power of real surface | W/m² |
| $E_b = \sigma \, T^4$ | Total emissive power of blackbody | W/m² |
| $\varepsilon = E / E_b$ | Emissivity ratio definition | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho$ (opaque surface) | Kirchhoff's Law relation | dimensionless |
| $q = h_c (T_s - T_{\infty}) + \varepsilon \sigma T_s^4$ | Total surface heat loss | W/m² |
| $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | W/m²·K⁴ |
| $\text{View factor} \; F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{A_2 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}$ | Small surface-to-large area geometry | dimensionless |
| $Q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Net radiation exchange with surroundings | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, the temperature $T$ MUST be in **Kelvin (K)**. A very common mistake is to plug in Celsius, which leads to wildly wrong numerical answers and a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Furnace & Boiler Design** — Emissivity of flame, refractory bricks, and steel surfaces governs fuel efficiency.
2. **Satellite Thermal Control** — Spacecraft surfaces are coated with materials of controlled emissivity to manage radiative heat loss in vacuum.
3. **Fire Protection Engineering** — Emissivity of PPE clothing determines radiant heat transfer to firefighters.
4. **Solar Energy Engineering** — Selective absorber coatings maximize absorption (high $\alpha$) while minimizing emission (low $\varepsilon$) to retain heat.
5. **Glass & Ceramic Industries** — Quality control uses emissivity to assess firing uniformity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Emissivity from Calibrated Thermopile Signal

Let the thermopile output voltage be proportional to incident radiation intensity:

$$V_{\text{signal}} = K \cdot E_{\text{incident}}$$

Where $K$ is the sensor sensitivity constant (V·m²/W).

**Step A:** Place the test plate at distance $d$ from the sensor. Record voltage $V_t$ and plate temperature $T_s$.

$$V_t = K \cdot \varepsilon_t \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4$$

**Step B:** Replace test plate with the reference black surface ($\varepsilon_b \approx 1$). At the SAME temperature $T_s$, record voltage $V_b$.

$$V_b = K \cdot \varepsilon_b \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4$$

**Step C:** Take the ratio to eliminate $K$, $\sigma$, and $T_s^4$.

$$\frac{V_t}{V_b} = \frac{K \cdot \varepsilon_t \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4}{K \cdot \varepsilon_b \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4}$$

$$\frac{V_t}{V_b} = \frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_b}$$

**Step D:** Since $\varepsilon_b \approx 1$ for a true blackbody reference, the unknown emissivity simplifies.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_t = \frac{V_t}{V_b}}$$

This is the **fundamental working equation** of the apparatus and is the equation examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Balance Under Steady State

Consider the test plate of area $A$ receiving electrical input power $P_{\text{elec}}$ and losing heat by radiation, convection, and conduction through the stem.

$$P_{\text{elec}} = Q_{\text{rad}} + Q_{\text{conv}} + Q_{\text{cond}}$$

Expanding each term:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T_s^4 \;+\; h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_{\infty}) \;+\; \frac{k_s \, A}{L} \, (T_s - T_{\text{base}})$$

**Step E:** At high plate temperatures (above ~150 °C) and a thin well-insulated stem:

$$Q_{\text{cond}} \ll Q_{\text{rad}}$$

So we approximate:

$$P_{\text{elec}} \approx \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T_s^4 + h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_{\infty})$$

**Step F:** Solve for $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{P_{\text{elec}} - h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_{\infty})}{\sigma \, A \, T_s^4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** This equation (Derivation 2) is used when the apparatus is designed with **electrical input measurement** rather than a calibrated thermopile. KTU examiners often give numerical data and ask students to compute $\varepsilon$ using this formula — practice it thoroughly.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Numerical)

**Given Data (from a KTU past problem):**
- Test plate area: $A = 0.02 \; \text{m}^2$
- Plate surface temperature: $T_s = 423 \; \text{K} \; (150\,^\circ\text{C})$
- Ambient temperature: $T_\infty = 303 \; \text{K} \; (30\,^\circ\text{C})$
- Electrical input: $P_{\text{elec}} = 60 \; \text{W}$
- Convective heat transfer coefficient: $h_c = 12 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

**Find:** Emissivity of the test surface.

**Solution — Stepwise:**

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{P_{\text{elec}} - h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)}{\sigma \, A \, T_s^4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{60 - (12)(0.02)(423 - 303)}{(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(0.02)(423)^4}
\end{aligned}

Compute the convection loss:

\begin{aligned}
h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty) &= 12 \times 0.02 \times 120 \\[4pt]
&= 0.24 \times 120 \\[4pt]
&= 28.8 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Net radiative power:

\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{rad}} &= 60 - 28.8 \\[4pt]
&= 31.2 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Compute $T_s^4$:

\begin{aligned}
T_s^4 &= (423)^4 \\[4pt]
&= 423 \times 423 \times 423 \times 423 \\[4pt]
&= 178{,}929 \times 178{,}929 \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.20 \times 10^{10} \; \text{K}^4
\end{aligned}

Compute denominator:

\begin{aligned}
\sigma \, A \, T_s^4 &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.02 \times 3.20 \times 10^{10} \\[4pt]
&= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.40 \times 10^{8} \\[4pt]
&= 5.67 \times 6.40 \\[4pt]
&\approx 36.29 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Final emissivity:

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{31.2}{36.29} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.86
\end{aligned}

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.86}$$

**Interpretation:** This value is consistent with a moderately oxidized or rough metallic surface — physically realistic.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Surface Finish on Emissivity

| **Surface Type** | **Approximate $\varepsilon$** | **Engineering Significance** |
|------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Polished silver | 0.02 | Excellent reflector (low loss) |
| Polished aluminum | 0.05 | Used in insulation foil |
| Polished copper | 0.07 | Common in radiators |
| Rolled sheet steel | 0.65 | Industrial baseline |
| Oxidized copper | 0.78 | Aged equipment surface |
| Lampblack / soot | 0.95 | Reference blackbody paint |
| Black matte paint | 0.97 | Near-ideal emitter |
| Human skin | 0.98 | Medical thermal imaging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Emissivity Measurement Apparatus Architecture

The following diagram maps the functional flow of components inside the standard KTU lab apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[AC Mains Power Supply 230V 50Hz] --> nodeA[Variac Voltage Regulator]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Heater Coil Beneath Test Plate]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Test Specimen Plate Area]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Surface Thermocouple Type K]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Digital Temperature Indicator]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Data Logger / Display Unit]
    nodeG[Cooling Water Inlet] --> nodeH[Sensor Housing Cold Finger]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Thermopile Radiation Sensor]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Radiation Signal Amplifier]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Digital Millivolt Meter]
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeL[Blackbody Reference Plate] -.Calibration Swap.-> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeM[Emissivity Calculation Epsilon equals Vt over Vb]
```

**Component-Level Functional Description:**

1. **AC Mains + Variac** — Provides controllable voltage (0–230 V) to the heater coil, enabling stepwise temperature increase.
2. **Heater Coil** — Nichrome wire wound beneath the test plate, dissipating electrical energy as Joule heat.
3. **Test Specimen Plate** — The actual sample under study (polished metal, oxidized metal, painted surface, etc.).
4. **Surface Thermocouple** — Embedded just beneath the specimen to read true surface temperature without disturbing radiation field.
5. **Thermopile Sensor** — Multi-junction thermal detector that converts incident radiation flux into a measurable voltage (typically microvolts to millivolts).
6. **Cooling Water Jacket** — Maintains thermopile at a stable reference temperature, eliminating drift due to sensor self-heating.
7. **Digital Indicators** — Display both temperature (°C) and radiation voltage (mV) simultaneously.
8. **Blackbody Reference Plate** — Interchangeable with test plate; used during the calibration run to establish the 100% emission baseline.

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Inspect Apparatus and Switch ON] --> nodeB[Step 2: Mount Blackbody Reference Plate]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Set Variac to Low Voltage Start Heating]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Allow Steady State Wait 10 Minutes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Record Reference Voltage Vb and Temperature Ts]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Switch OFF and Cool Down to Ambient]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 7: Replace with Test Specimen Plate]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 8: Repeat Steps 3 to 5 Record Vt and Ts]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 9: Calculate Epsilon equals Vt divided by Vb]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 10: Repeat for Multiple Temperatures Plot Epsilon versus T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Steady-State Matters:** Until the plate reaches thermal equilibrium, transient heat storage in the plate material causes the radiated power to lag the input power. Measuring before steady state gives erroneously LOW emissivity values. Always wait until the temperature reading stabilizes for at least 3 consecutive minutes.

## 4.3 Calibration Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Blackbody Reference] -->|Emits Sigma T4| nodeB[Thermopile Sensor]
    nodeC[Test Specimen] -->|Emits Epsilon Sigma T4| nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Signal Conditioner]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compare Vt over Vb]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Epsilon Output]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define emissivity. What is its range, and what does a value of 1 signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of the radiation emitted by a given surface to the radiation emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. It is given by $\varepsilon = E / E_b$. The value of ε ranges between **0 and 1** (dimensionless). A value of ε = 1 signifies a **perfect blackbody** — a surface that emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at its temperature, absorbing all incident radiation. Examples include a cavity with a small hole or a surface coated with lampblack.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Stefan-Boltzmann Law and write its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. Mathematically: $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant and $T$ is in Kelvin. For a real (gray) surface, the equation modifies to $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of an **Emissivity Measurement Apparatus**. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression used to determine the emissivity of a test specimen from **calibrated thermopile voltages** obtained with a blackbody reference. A test plate at 180 °C produces a thermopile reading of **38 mV**, while the blackbody reference at the same temperature reads **44 mV**. Calculate the emissivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

The apparatus consists of the following components:

1. **Test Plate Holder** — Accommodates interchangeable specimens (test plate and reference plate) at a fixed geometric position relative to the sensor.
2. **Electrical Heater (Nichrome Coil)** — Heats the plate from below via Joule heating; power controlled by a variac.
3. **Surface Thermocouple (Chromel-Alumel, Type K)** — Measures true plate surface temperature.
4. **Thermopile Detector** — Multi-junction sensor with high sensitivity to incident thermal radiation; produces output voltage proportional to incident flux.
5. **Water-Cooled Sensor Shield** — Maintains the thermopile cold-junction temperature constant to eliminate drift.
6. **Digital Temperature Indicator + Millivolt Meter** — Display the surface temperature and sensor output respectively.
7. **Reference Blackbody Plate** — Lampblack-coated plate with $\varepsilon_b \approx 1$ for calibration.

**Working:** Electrical energy heats the plate to a chosen steady temperature. The thermopile measures the radiant flux. By replacing the test plate with a blackbody reference at identical temperature, the ratio of voltages directly yields emissivity.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Derivation:**

\begin{aligned}
V_b &= K \cdot \varepsilon_b \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4 \\[4pt]
V_t &= K \cdot \varepsilon_t \cdot \sigma \cdot T_s^4 \\[4pt]
\frac{V_t}{V_b} &= \frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_b} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_t &= \frac{V_t}{V_b} \cdot \varepsilon_b
\end{aligned}

For an ideal blackbody, $\varepsilon_b = 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_t = \frac{V_t}{V_b}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_t &= \frac{38 \; \text{mV}}{44 \; \text{mV}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.864
\end{aligned}

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_t \approx 0.86}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The test surface behaves like a moderately good emitter, similar to oxidized metal or a rough dark surface.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of an energy balance, derive the expression for emissivity in terms of **electrical input power**, **convective loss**, and **surface temperature**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, a circular test plate of diameter **80 mm** is heated electrically with an input of **75 W**. At steady state, the surface temperature is **200 °C** and ambient is **30 °C**. If $h_c = 15 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, calculate the emissivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

Energy balance on the plate at steady state:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = Q_{\text{rad}} + Q_{\text{conv}} + Q_{\text{cond}}$$

Neglecting conduction (insulated stem):

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T_s^4 + h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Solving for ε:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{P_{\text{elec}} - h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)}{\sigma \, A \, T_s^4}}$$

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

Plate area:

\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}

Temperatures in Kelvin:

\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 200 + 273 = 473 \; \text{K} \\[4pt]
T_\infty &= 30 + 273 = 303 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}

$T_s^4$:

\begin{aligned}
T_s^4 &= (473)^4 \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.006 \times 10^{10} \; \text{K}^4
\end{aligned}

Convection loss:

\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{conv}} &= h_c \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty) \\[4pt]
&= 15 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times (473 - 303) \\[4pt]
&= 0.0754 \times 170 \\[4pt]
&= 12.82 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Net radiation:

\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{rad}} &= 75 - 12.82 = 62.18 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Denominator:

\begin{aligned}
\sigma \, A \, T_s^4 &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.006 \times 10^{10} \\[4pt]
&\approx 14.27 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}

Emissivity:

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{62.18}{14.27} \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.36
\end{aligned}

**Result Analysis:** Since $\varepsilon > 1$ is physically impossible, this indicates either (a) experimental error, (b) neglected conduction losses, or (c) view factor less than unity between plate and environment. A realistic reading must satisfy $\varepsilon \leq 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Error — Kelvin vs Celsius:** Forgetting to convert °C to K is the #1 cause of wrong answers and a guaranteed deduction of **2 marks**.
> 2. **Neglecting Convection:** Many students assume "all electrical power goes to radiation." This is INCORRECT — convection must always be subtracted first.
> 3. **Forgetting the View Factor:** In a real setup, the plate sees only a fraction of its radiation returning from the environment; the formula $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ assumes an ambient blackbody enclosure at $T_\infty$ which is rarely true. Discuss this for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing $\varepsilon_t$ and $\varepsilon_b$:** Always ensure that the numerator is the test surface reading, NOT the blackbody.
> 5. **No Units Justification:** KTU examiners award marks for writing "ε is dimensionless" — never skip this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD QUICK REVISION — MUST MEMORIZE**

- [x] **Emissivity definition:** $\varepsilon = E / E_b$, dimensionless, range **[0, 1]**
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ — memorize the value
- [x] **Core equation:** $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$ — **always use Kelvin**
- [x] **Two methods to find ε:** (i) Thermopile ratio with blackbody reference; (ii) Energy balance using electrical input
- [x] **Thermopile ratio method:** $\varepsilon_t = V_t / V_b$ when $\varepsilon_b \approx 1$
- [x] **Energy balance method:** $\varepsilon = [P - h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)] / [\sigma A T_s^4]$
- [x] **Polished metals** = LOW emissivity (~0.02–0.10)
- [x] **Oxidized / rough surfaces** = HIGH emissivity (~0.70–0.95)
- [x] **Lampblack coating** = ε ≈ 0.95 → used as practical blackbody reference
- [x] **Apparatus key parts:** Variac, Heater, Test plate, Thermocouple, Thermopile, Water cooling, Blackbody reference, Indicators
- [x] **Wait for steady state** before taking readings — minimum 8–10 minutes
- [x] **Kirchhoff's Law** (opaque surfaces): $\varepsilon + \rho = 1$ where $\rho$ is reflectivity
- [x] **Numerical mistakes to avoid:** No K-conversion = -2 marks; No convection subtraction = -2 marks
- [x] **Expected experimental range** of ε for typical lab specimens: **0.05 to 0.95**
- [x] **Units check:** If your final answer for ε is outside [0, 1], recheck your arithmetic
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Furnace design, satellite coatings, solar collectors, fire safety gear, IR thermography

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer in Fins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer in Fins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIN (Extended Surface):** A fin is a passive, solid, extended surface attached to a primary heat-transfer surface (a wall or tube) whose principal function is to increase the rate of heat dissipation to or from the surrounding fluid medium by enlarging the effective convective surface area, without altering the primary surface or fluid conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Heat & Mass Transfer Operations Lab context, fins are studied experimentally to determine:

1. **Fin Effectiveness (ε)** — The ratio of heat transfer with the fin to heat transfer without the fin.
2. **Fin Efficiency (η_fin)** — The ratio of actual heat dissipated by the fin to the heat that *would* be dissipated if the entire fin were at the base (wall) temperature.
3. **Temperature Distribution** — The variation of temperature along the length of the fin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Lab Reference):** All thermal properties are expressed in SI units: thermal conductivity $k$ in $\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$, heat transfer rate $Q$ in Watts (W), convective coefficient $h$ in $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, and length $L$ in metres (m).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hot cup of tea. The small exposed surface of the liquid loses heat slowly. Now imagine dipping a metallic spoon into it — the spoon's handle, sticking out of the cup, is exposed to cooler air along its *entire length*. The hot liquid is now "talking" to the surrounding air not just through the cup's top, but through the *entire metallic handle* as well. **That metallic handle is the "fin."**

> [!TIP]
> Think of a fin as a **"thermal amplifier."** The base carries a fixed temperature, but the fin "spreads" that temperature over a much larger surface, allowing more heat to escape per unit time. The fin is *passive* — it has no moving parts and requires no external energy; it simply conducts heat along its body and convects it away.

## 1.3 Why Fins Are Used (Engineering Motivation)

- The base heat-transfer coefficient $h$ is sometimes too small (e.g., air-cooled engines, transformers, motorcycle cylinder heads, CPU heatsinks).
- Increasing the fluid velocity (to raise $h$) is expensive or impractical.
- Adding a fin of high-thermal-conductivity material (aluminium, copper) is a **cheap, energy-free** way to multiply the surface area.
- Fins are characterized by the dimensionless **Biot number** $\text{Bi} = hL_c/k$ and **Fin Parameter** $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fins

Fins are classified by **geometry** and **fluid-flow direction**:

| Type | Geometry | Typical Use |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Straight / Longitudinal Rectangular Fin** | Constant rectangular cross-section, length along flow | Pipes carrying hot/cool fluids |
| **Pin / Cylindrical / Transverse Fin** | Round cross-section, projecting perpendicular to surface | Engine cylinders, electric motors |
| **Annular Fin** | Circumferential ring on a tube | Tube-type heat exchangers |
| **Spine (Radial) Fin** | Short cylindrical pin on flat surface | Heatsinks for electronics |
| **Straight Triangular Fin** | Tapered profile | Weight-critical aerospace applications |

## 2.2 Fin Equation (1-D Heat Conduction with Convection)

For a fin of constant cross-section, assuming:
- 1-D conduction along fin length
- Constant thermal conductivity $k$
- Constant convective coefficient $h$
- Steady-state, no internal heat generation
- Ambient temperature $T_\infty$ uniform

The governing ODE for temperature excess $\theta = T - T_\infty$ is:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0 \quad\text{where}\quad m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA_c}$$

The general solution is:
$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorise the four boundary-condition cases below. They appear in every KTU numerical.**

| # | Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Fin Parameter | $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Characteristic Length | $L_c = A_c/P$ | m |
| 3 | General Solution | $\theta = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$ | Excess temperature $\theta = T - T_\infty$ |
| 4 | **Case A — Infinitely Long Fin** $(mL \to \infty)$ | $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = e^{-mx}$ | $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b$ |
| 5 | **Case B — Insulated Tip Fin** (adiabatic at $x=L$) | $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}$ | $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh mL$ |
| 6 | **Case C — Fin Losing Heat at Tip** (convection at tip) | Uses corrected length $L_c = L + A_c/P$ | $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh mL_c$ |
| 7 | **Case D — Fixed Tip Temperature** ($T_L = T_\infty$, so $\theta_L = 0$) | $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\sinh m(L-x)}{\sinh mL}$ | $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\coth mL$ |
| 8 | Fin Efficiency | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{\tanh mL}{mL}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < \eta \le 1$ |
| 9 | Fin Effectiveness | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{no\,fin}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b}{hA_c\theta_b} = \sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}}$ | Should exceed 2 for a fin to "pay off" |
| 10 | Temperature at Tip (Insulated) | $T_L = T_\infty + (T_b - T_\infty)/\cosh mL$ | Used in lab to check $T_L$ |
| 11 | Heat Loss Without Fin | $Q_{no\,fin} = h A_c (T_b - T_\infty)$ | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** In the KTU experiment, the most common boundary condition applied is the **Insulated Tip Case (B)** because the lab's pin fin is short and the tip area is small compared to the lateral area. Always use the **corrected length** $L_c = L + (D/4)$ for a cylindrical pin fin in the *exact* tip-convection case.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Cylinder-head fins on air-cooled motorcycles (e.g., Royal Enfield).
- **Electronics:** Aluminium pin-fin heatsinks for CPUs, GPUs, and power transistors.
- **Power Engineering:** Transformer tank radiators with welded plate fins.
- **HVAC:** Convector radiators use long rectangular fins.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade internal cooling passages act as internal fins.
- **Refrigeration:** Condenser and evaporator coils use plate fins to enhance air-side heat transfer.

---

# SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fin Equation (Insulated Tip Case)

### Step 1 — Energy Balance on Element $dx$

Consider a differential element of fin at distance $x$ from the base, of length $dx$, cross-sectional area $A_c$, and perimeter $P$.

Heat in at $x$: $Q_x$ (by conduction)
Heat out at $x+dx$: $Q_{x+dx}$
Heat lost by convection from sides: $dQ_{conv} = hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx$

Steady-state energy balance (no generation):

$$Q_x = Q_{x+dx} + hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

### Step 2 — Taylor Expansion

$$Q_{x+dx} = Q_x + \frac{dQ_x}{dx}dx$$

Substitute:

$$Q_x = Q_x + \frac{dQ_x}{dx}dx + hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

Cancel $Q_x$:

$$0 = \frac{dQ_x}{dx} + hP(T - T_\infty)$$

### Step 3 — Fourier's Law

$$Q_x = -kA_c\frac{dT}{dx}$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{dQ_x}{dx} = -kA_c\frac{d^2T}{dx^2}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Form the ODE

$$-kA_c\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} + hP(T - T_\infty) = 0$$

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{kA_c}\theta = 0$$

Define $m^2 = hP/(kA_c)$:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$$

### Step 5 — General Solution

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Insulated Tip)

**BC 1:** At base $x = 0$, $\theta = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$

$$\theta_b = C_1 + C_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b$$

**BC 2:** At tip $x = L$, no heat loss $\Rightarrow \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = mC_1 e^{mx} - mC_2 e^{-mx}$$

At $x = L$:

$$mC_1 e^{mL} - mC_2 e^{-mL} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL}$$

Therefore:

$$C_1 = C_2 e^{-2mL}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for Constants

$$C_2 e^{-2mL} + C_2 = \theta_b \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_2(e^{-2mL} + 1) = \theta_b$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\theta_b}{1 + e^{-2mL}} = \frac{\theta_b\, e^{mL}}{e^{mL} + e^{-mL}} = \frac{\theta_b\, e^{mL}}{2\cosh mL}$$

$$C_1 = C_2 e^{-2mL} = \frac{\theta_b\, e^{-mL}}{2\cosh mL}$$

### Step 8 — Temperature Distribution

$$\theta(x) = \frac{\theta_b}{2\cosh mL}\left[e^{m(L-x)} + e^{-m(L-x)}\right] = \theta_b\,\frac{\cosh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}\;}$$

### Step 9 — Heat Transfer Rate

Heat is conducted into the fin at the base ($x=0$):

$$Q = -kA_c\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0}$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\theta_b\,\frac{m\sinh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}$$

At $x = 0$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = -\theta_b\,\frac{m\sinh mL}{\cosh mL} = -m\theta_b\tanh mL$$

Therefore:

$$Q = -kA_c(-m\theta_b\tanh mL) = kA_c m\theta_b \tanh mL$$

Substitute $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$:

$$\boxed{\;Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Fin Efficiency

**Maximum possible heat dissipation** occurs if the *entire* fin surface is at the base temperature $T_b$:

$$Q_{max} = hA_s(T_b - T_\infty) = h(PL)\theta_b$$

Fin efficiency:

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL}{hPL\theta_b}$$

Simplify by substituting $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$:

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh mL}{mL}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh mL}{mL}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Fin Effectiveness

Heat transfer *without* the fin (only base area $A_c$ exposed):

$$Q_{no\,fin} = hA_c(T_b - T_\infty) = hA_c\theta_b$$

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{Q_{with\,fin}}{Q_{no\,fin}} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL}{hA_c\theta_b} = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}}\;\tanh mL$$

For an *infinitely long* fin ($\tanh mL \to 1$):

$$\varepsilon_{fin,\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{hL_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** A fin is *worthwhile* if $\varepsilon_{fin} \ge 2$. A "perfect" fin is one where $\varepsilon_{fin} \to \infty$ (high $k$, low $h$, small $L_c$).

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Pin Fin)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 pattern]:** A cylindrical pin fin of diameter $D = 25\,\text{mm}$, length $L = 50\,\text{mm}$, thermal conductivity $k = 200\,\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$ is attached to a base at $T_b = 200^\circ\text{C}$. Ambient air is at $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$ with $h = 50\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Take the tip as insulated. Determine:
(a) Fin efficiency $\eta_{fin}$
(b) Heat dissipation rate $Q$
(c) Tip temperature $T_L$
(d) Fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_{fin}$

### Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.050\,\text{m}$, $k = 200\,\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$
- $T_b = 200^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $h = 50\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- $\theta_b = 200 - 25 = 175\,\text{K}$ (or $^\circ\text{C}$)

**Geometry:**
- $A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi(0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$
- $P = \pi D = \pi(0.025) = 7.854\times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}$

**Fin parameter:**
$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 0.07854}{200 \times 4.909\times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.927}{0.09818}} = \sqrt{40.0} = 6.325\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Compute $mL$:**
$$mL = 6.325 \times 0.050 = 0.3162$$

**(a) Fin Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(0.3162)}{0.3162} = \frac{0.3069}{0.3162} = 0.9707 \approx 97.07\%$$

**(b) Heat Dissipation Rate:**
$$Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL$$
$$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.07854 \times 200 \times 4.909\times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{0.3855} = 0.6209$$
$$Q = 0.6209 \times 175 \times 0.3069 = 33.35\,\text{W}$$

**(c) Tip Temperature:**
$$T_L = T_\infty + \frac{\theta_b}{\cosh mL} = 25 + \frac{175}{\cosh(0.3162)} = 25 + \frac{175}{1.0507} = 25 + 166.55 = 191.55^\circ\text{C}$$

**(d) Fin Effectiveness:**
$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{Q}{hA_c\theta_b} = \frac{33.35}{50 \times 4.909\times 10^{-4} \times 175} = \frac{33.35}{4.295} = 7.76$$

The fin dissipates **7.76 times** more heat than the base would alone — a highly effective design.

    I --> J[Validate mL and Bi assumptions]
```

```

     Ambient Air at T_infinity  --(convection h)--
                  |
                  v
          +-------+--------+
          |                |
   +------+--+ T(x)        |   <-- Pin Fin (cylindrical, dia D)
   |  T_b   |              |

# SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define "Fin Effectiveness" and state the criterion for a fin to be considered worthwhile.*

**Model Answer:**
Fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_{fin}$ is the ratio of heat transfer rate from a surface *with* the fin to the heat transfer rate from the same surface *without* the fin:

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{Q_{with\,fin}}{Q_{without\,fin}} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL}{hA_c\theta_b} = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}}\;\tanh mL$$

**Criterion:** A fin is considered worthwhile (justified) if $\varepsilon_{fin} \ge 2$, meaning the fin must dissipate at least twice the heat that the bare surface would dissipate alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between "Fin Efficiency" and "Fin Effectiveness".*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fin Efficiency ($\eta_{fin}$) | Fin Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_{fin}$) |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of actual heat dissipated to heat dissipated if entire fin were at base temperature | Ratio of heat dissipated with fin to heat dissipated without fin |
| Formula | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh mL}{mL}$ | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}}\,\tanh mL$ |
| Range | $0 < \eta \le 1$ | $\varepsilon \ge 1$ (typically $\ge 2$ for a useful fin) |
| Purpose | Measures how *well the fin is used* internally | Measures how *beneficial* the fin is over no fin |
| Dependency | Function of $mL$ only | Function of fin material, geometry, and $h$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse $A_c$ (cross-sectional area) with $A_s$ (surface area).** $A_c$ is used in the conduction term; $A_s = PL$ appears in $Q_{max}$ and $\eta_{fin}$.
> - **Always include units** in the final numerical answer. KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> - **Check the boundary condition** stated in the problem (insulated tip, fixed temperature, or convection at tip). Using the wrong case costs 3–4 marks.
> - **Do not forget to convert $^\circ\text{C}$ to K** in temperature-difference calculations — but for a *difference*, the numerical value is the same.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 pattern]

A **rectangular longitudinal fin** of length $L = 60\,\text{mm}$, width $w = 50\,\text{mm}$, and thickness $t = 6\,\text{mm}$ is attached to a wall maintained at $T_b = 150^\circ\text{C}$. The fin material has thermal conductivity $k = 150\,\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$. The surrounding air is at $T_\infty = 30^\circ\text{C}$ with a convective heat-transfer coefficient $h = 40\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Assume the fin tip is **insulated**.

**(a)** Derive the temperature distribution $\theta(x)$ for the fin. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the fin efficiency, the heat dissipation rate, and the fin effectiveness. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The general fin ODE with the insulated-tip boundary condition is derived in Section 3.1, steps 1 through 8. The final temperature distribution is:

$$\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh m(L - x)}{\cosh mL}$$

where $\theta = T - T_\infty$, $\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty = 150 - 30 = 120\,\text{K}$, and $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$.

**Computing the geometric parameters:**

- Cross-sectional area: $A_c = w \times t = 0.050 \times 0.006 = 3.0\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$
- Perimeter: $P = 2(w + t) = 2(0.050 + 0.006) = 0.112\,\text{m}$
- Fin parameter:
$$m = \sqrt{\frac{40 \times 0.112}{150 \times 3.0\times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.48}{0.045}} = \sqrt{99.56} = 9.978\,\text{m}^{-1}$$
- $mL = 9.978 \times 0.060 = 0.5987$

**Substituting values, the temperature distribution is:**

$$\frac{T(x) - 30}{120} = \frac{\cosh[9.978(0.060 - x)]}{\cosh(0.5987)} = \frac{\cosh[9.978(0.060 - x)]}{1.1843}$$

$$\boxed{\;T(x) = 30 + 101.32\,\cosh[9.978(0.060 - x)]\;}$$

(For example, at mid-length $x = 0.030\,\text{m}$: $T \approx 30 + 101.32\cosh(0.299) \approx 30 + 105.95 = 135.95^\circ\text{C}$.)

**Part (b) — Efficiency, Heat Rate, Effectiveness:**

**Fin Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{\tanh(0.5987)}{0.5987} = \frac{0.5372}{0.5987} = 0.8974 \approx 89.74\%$$

**Heat Dissipation Rate:**

$$Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh(mL)$$
$$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{40 \times 0.112 \times 150 \times 3.0\times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{0.2016} = 0.4490$$
$$Q = 0.4490 \times 120 \times 0.5372 = 28.94\,\text{W}$$

**Fin Effectiveness:**

$$Q_{no\,fin} = hA_c\theta_b = 40 \times 3.0\times 10^{-4} \times 120 = 1.44\,\text{W}$$
$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{28.94}{1.44} = 20.10$$

The fin dissipates **20.1 times** more heat than the bare base would alone — an excellent design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 pattern]

A **cylindrical pin fin** of diameter $D = 20\,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 80\,\text{mm}$ is fixed to a base whose temperature is $T_b = 250^\circ\text{C}$. The thermal conductivity of the fin material is $k = 180\,\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$, the convective coefficient is $h = 30\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, and the ambient temperature is $T_\infty = 40^\circ\text{C}$. The tip is **insulated**.

**(a)** Calculate the fin parameter $m$ and the temperature at the tip $T_L$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the heat-transfer rate, fin efficiency, and fin effectiveness. Comment on whether the fin is justified. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Fin Parameter and Tip Temperature:**

- $A_c = \pi D^2/4 = \pi(0.020)^2/4 = 3.1416\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$
- $P = \pi D = \pi(0.020) = 0.06283\,\text{m}$
- $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30 \times 0.06283}{180 \times 3.1416\times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.885}{0.05655}} = \sqrt{33.33} = 5.774\,\text{m}^{-1}$
- $mL = 5.774 \times 0.080 = 0.4619$

**Tip Temperature (Insulated-tip case):**

$$T_L = T_\infty + \frac{\theta_b}{\cosh(mL)}$$

$\theta_b = 250 - 40 = 210\,\text{K}$, $\cosh(0.4619) = 1.1087$:

$$T_L = 40 + \frac{210}{1.1087} = 40 + 189.41 = 229.41^\circ\text{C}$$

The tip is **only $\approx 20.6^\circ\text{C}$ cooler** than the base, confirming the assumption of a relatively small temperature drop and validating the use of constant $k$.

**Part (b) — Heat Rate, Efficiency, Effectiveness:**

**Heat-transfer rate:**

$$Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh(mL)$$
$$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{30 \times 0.06283 \times 180 \times 3.1416\times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{1.065} = 1.0321$$
$$\tanh(0.4619) = 0.4315$$
$$Q = 1.0321 \times 210 \times 0.4315 = 93.52\,\text{W}$$

**Fin Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{0.4315}{0.4619} = 0.9342 \approx 93.42\%$$

**Fin Effectiveness:**

$$Q_{no\,fin} = hA_c\theta_b = 30 \times 3.1416\times 10^{-4} \times 210 = 1.979\,\text{W}$$
$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{93.52}{1.979} = 47.26$$

**Comment:** Since $\varepsilon_{fin} = 47.26 \gg 2$, the fin is **highly justified** — it dissipates 47 times more heat than the bare base. The efficiency of 93.42% indicates the fin is well-utilised over its length, with minimal wastage due to tip temperature drop.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Heat Transfer in Fins**

- **Fin definition** = extended surface used to increase convective heat transfer by increasing surface area.
- **Governing ODE** for a constant-cross-section fin: $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$ with $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$.
- **General solution** is always a linear combination of $e^{mx}$ and $e^{-mx}$ (or equivalently, $\cosh$ and $\sinh$).
- **Four boundary-condition cases** to remember:
  1. Infinitely long fin
  2. Insulated tip (most common in KTU labs)
  3. Tip convection (use corrected length $L_c = L + A_c/P$)
  4. Fixed tip temperature $T_L = T_\infty$
- **Insulated tip formulas:**
  - Temperature distribution: $\theta/\theta_b = \cosh m(L-x)/\cosh mL$
  - Heat rate: $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh mL$
  - Efficiency: $\eta_{fin} = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$
- **Effectiveness** is the *only* measure that compares the fin to "no fin at all" — must exceed 2 for the fin to be justified.
- **Biot number** $Bi = hL_c/k$ should be small ($\ll 1$) for the 1-D fin assumption to hold; in KTU lab pin fins, typical $Bi \approx 0.01$ to $0.05$.
- **Units check:** $m$ in $\text{m}^{-1}$, $Q$ in W, $h$ in $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, $k$ in $\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$.
- **Experimentally** in the KTU lab, $Q_{experimental} = V \times I$ (electrical input) and is compared with the theoretical $Q$ derived from the temperature profile.
- **Common mistake:** Using $A_s$ (surface area) instead of $A_c$ (cross-section) in the heat-rate formula. Remember: $A_c$ for conduction, $A_s = PL$ for the efficiency reference.
- **Material selection:** Use high $k$ (aluminium, copper) to maximise both $\eta_{fin}$ and $\varepsilon_{fin}$.
- **Lab validation plot:** Always plot experimental $T(x)$ versus theoretical $\theta(x)/\theta_b = \cosh m(L-x)/\cosh mL$ and comment on deviation (typically due to radiation losses, non-uniform $h$, or tip convection not being negligible).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Unsteady State Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Unsteady State Heat Transfer** (also called **Transient Heat Transfer** or **Lumped Capacitance Heat Transfer**) is the mode of heat transfer in which the temperature at any point within a system varies with **time**. Unlike *steady-state* conditions, where temperatures remain constant, in unsteady-state processes, a system is undergoing a thermal transition from an initial equilibrium state to a new equilibrium state with its surroundings.

In a laboratory context, this involves studying the transient cooling (or heating) of a solid object of known geometry (typically a **sphere**, **cylinder**, or **slab**) initially at a uniform temperature $T_0$, which is suddenly exposed to a fluid medium at a constant bulk temperature $T_\infty$. The temporal variation of the solid's centerline (or surface) temperature is recorded and analyzed to extract key thermal transport properties such as the **convective heat transfer coefficient (h)**, the **thermal diffusivity (α)**, and the **overall time constant (τ)** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab course emphasizes experimental determination of the **convective heat transfer coefficient** during transient cooling, validation of the **lumped system analysis** assumption using the **Biot Number criterion**, and computation of **Fourier Number** as a function of elapsed time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Cup of Tea" Intuition

Imagine you have just poured boiling hot tea into a ceramic cup sitting in a cool room.

- At the very **first instant** ($t = 0$), the entire cup of tea is at a uniform high temperature.
- As time passes, the tea in contact with the cup walls loses heat to the surrounding air, then the bulk tea gradually cools.
- The **temperature of the tea is changing every second** — this is the essence of *unsteady state*.
- Eventually, after a long time, the tea reaches the room temperature and stops changing — at this point, the system becomes *steady-state*.

**Why does this matter in engineering?**
- Designing **batch chemical reactors** that must be heated or cooled within a specific time window.
- Predicting **thermal fatigue** in turbine blades during start-up and shut-down of power plants.
- **Sterilization of food** (pasteurization) and **pharmaceutical processing** where temperature-time history is critical.
- **Quenching** of metals in heat treatment (metallurgy).
- The start-up of **shell-and-tube heat exchangers** in process plants.

In the lab experiment, we mimic this scenario by heating a solid metal ball to a uniform temperature, plunging it into a cold-water bath, and recording the temperature drop as a function of time. The data is then fitted to an exponential decay model derived from energy balance.

## 1.3 Critical Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following bold-faced quantities govern the entire transient heat transfer phenomenon and must be memorized for the KTU lab examination:

- **Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)** — Units: W/(m²·K)
- **Thermal Diffusivity (α)** — Units: m²/s
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** — Units: W/(m·K)
- **Biot Number (Bi)** — Dimensionless number representing the ratio of internal conduction resistance to external convective resistance
- **Fourier Number (Fo)** — Dimensionless time representing the ratio of elapsed time to the characteristic thermal diffusion time
- **Time Constant (τ)** — Units: seconds; represents the time required for the temperature difference to decay to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8 %) of its initial value
- **Characteristic Length (L_c)** — Defined as Volume ÷ Surface Area for non-planar geometries

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Marks are frequently lost in the lab report for not stating units with every numerical answer and not justifying the validity of the lumped capacitance method using the **Biot Number < 0.1** rule.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Setup and Underlying Assumptions

The experiment is built upon a set of well-defined assumptions that allow the partial differential equation of heat conduction to be reduced to a simple **first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE)**.

### 2.1.1 Assumptions of the Lumped Capacitance Method

1. **Spatial Uniformity:** The temperature inside the solid body is spatially uniform at any given instant (i.e., the solid is treated as a "lumped" thermal mass with a single temperature).
2. **Constant Physical Properties:** Thermal conductivity ($k$), density ($\rho$), and specific heat ($C_p$) of the solid remain constant over the temperature range of the experiment.
3. **Constant Fluid Temperature:** The bulk fluid temperature $T_\infty$ is held constant throughout the experiment (large thermal reservoir assumption).
4. **Uniform Convective Coefficient:** The heat transfer coefficient $h$ is constant over the entire surface area $A$ of the solid.
5. **Negligible Radiation:** Heat transfer occurs only by convection; radiative losses are either negligible or are bundled into an effective $h$.
6. **No Internal Heat Generation:** The solid is passive and does not generate heat internally.

### 2.1.2 The Energy Balance Derivation

Consider a solid body of mass $m$, volume $V$, surface area $A$, and specific heat $C_p$, initially at temperature $T_0$ and immersed at $t = 0$ into a fluid of constant temperature $T_\infty$. Applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to the solid:

**Rate of energy storage in solid = Rate of energy entering the solid by convection**

$$ m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T_\infty - T) $$

This is a separable first-order linear ODE. It can be rearranged to:

$$ \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{hA}{mC_p} \, dt $$

Integrating from $T = T_0$ at $t = 0$ to $T = T$ at time $t$:

$$ \ln \left( \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} \right) = -\frac{hA}{mC_p} \, t $$

This is the **master equation of unsteady-state heat transfer** in the lumped regime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Derivation:** The equation tells us that the **dimensionless temperature ratio** $\theta/\theta_0 = (T - T_\infty)/(T_0 - T_\infty)$ decays *exponentially* with time. The constant of proportionality is the inverse of the **time constant** $\tau = mC_p/(hA)$.

## 2.2 Dimensionless Numbers — The Heart of the Analysis

### 2.2.1 Biot Number (Bi)

The **Biot Number** is the *gatekeeper* that determines whether the lumped capacitance method is valid. It is defined as:

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} $$

where the **characteristic length** $L_c$ is defined as:

$$ L_c = \frac{V}{A_s} $$

- For a **sphere** of radius $R$: $L_c = R/3$
- For a long **cylinder** of radius $R$: $L_c = R/2$
- For a **slab** of half-thickness $L$: $L_c = L$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Criterion (MUST MEMORIZE):** Lumped capacitance analysis is valid when **$Bi \leq 0.1$**. For $Bi > 0.1$, temperature gradients inside the solid become significant and the lumped method fails; one must then resort to **Heisler Charts** or **analytical series solutions** involving one-term or two-term approximations. KTU examiners routinely test this criterion.

### 2.2.2 Fourier Number (Fo)

The **Fourier Number** is the dimensionless time:

$$ Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{k \, t}{\rho C_p L_c^2} $$

- $Fo \to 0$ at the start of the process (no time has elapsed).
- $Fo \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$ (steady-state reached).
- $Fo = 1$ corresponds to the *thermal diffusion time*, the natural time scale of the system.

### 2.2.3 Nusselt Number (Nu)

The **Nusselt Number** correlates the convective heat transfer:

$$ Nu = \frac{h L}{k} $$

For a sphere cooling in a fluid, the experimentally determined $Nu$ is compared with established correlations such as:

$$ Nu = 2 + \left( 0.4 \, Re^{1/2} + 0.06 \, Re^{2/3} \right) Pr^{0.4} \left( \frac{\mu_\infty}{\mu_s} \right)^{1/4} $$

(Ranz-Marshall correlation for spheres)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following consolidated table is the **definitive reference** for all calculations in the lab report and for the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Energy Balance (Lumped) | $m C_p \, dT/dt = h A (T_\infty - T)$ | Energy storage = Convective input | W |
| 2 | Integrated Lumped Solution | $\ln[(T - T_\infty)/(T_0 - T_\infty)] = -(hA/mC_p) \, t$ | Linear log plot | dimensionless |
| 3 | Time Constant | $\tau = \rho V C_p / (h A) = m C_p / (h A)$ | Time for 63.2 % decay of $\Delta T$ | s |
| 4 | Dimensionless Temperature | $\theta = (T - T_\infty)/(T_0 - T_\infty)$ | Normalized temperature difference | dimensionless |
| 5 | Biot Number | $Bi = h L_c / k$ | Conduction vs. convection resistance | dimensionless |
| 6 | Characteristic Length | $L_c = V / A_s$ | Sphere: $R/3$, Cylinder: $R/2$ | m |
| 7 | Fourier Number | $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2 = k t / (\rho C_p L_c^2)$ | Dimensionless time | dimensionless |
| 8 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k / (\rho C_p)$ | Material property | m²/s |
| 9 | Nusselt Number | $Nu = h D / k$ | Convection enhancement | dimensionless |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Flow regime indicator | dimensionless |
| 11 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = C_p \mu / k$ | Momentum vs. thermal diffusivity | dimensionless |
| 12 | Slope of Log Plot | $\text{slope} = -h A / (m C_p) = -1/\tau$ | Determined experimentally | s⁻¹ |
| 13 | h from Slope | $h = -\text{slope} \times (m C_p / A)$ | Calculated convective coefficient | W/(m²·K) |
| 14 | Percentage Approach | $\% \text{Approach} = (T - T_\infty)/(T_0 - T_\infty) \times 100$ | Progress of transient process | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-Winning Strategy:** When asked to "comment on the validity of the lumped capacitance method," always (1) calculate $Bi$ using the experimentally determined $h$, (2) state the criterion $Bi \leq 0.1$, and (3) conclude whether the assumption is justified.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The transient heat transfer analysis taught in this lab forms the foundation for:

- **Design of thermal shields** for spacecraft re-entry (transient heating protection).
- **Cooling of nuclear fuel rods** during emergency shutdown (loss-of-coolant accident analysis).
- **Annealing of glass** in the flat-glass industry (controlled cooling to relieve stresses).
- **Curing of concrete** in large civil structures (heat of hydration analysis).
- **Pharmaceutical lyophilization** (freeze-drying), where the transient temperature profile inside vials determines product quality.
- **Solar thermal energy storage**, where a packed-bed of PCM particles is charged and discharged cyclically.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lumped Capacitance Solution

We begin with the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the solid body undergoing transient cooling in a fluid.

**Step 1: State the Energy Conservation Principle**

The rate of decrease of internal energy of the solid equals the rate of heat transfer OUT of the solid by convection.

$$ -\frac{dU}{dt} = q_{\text{conv}} $$

**Step 2: Express Each Term in Terms of Measurable Quantities**

The internal energy change of the solid is $dU = m C_p \, dT$. Newton's Law of Cooling gives the convective heat transfer rate as $q_{\text{conv}} = h A (T - T_\infty)$. Substituting:

$$ m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T_\infty - T) $$

**Step 3: Rearrange into Separable Form**

Divide both sides by $m C_p$ and by $(T - T_\infty)$ to separate variables:

$$ \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{hA}{mC_p} \, dt $$

**Step 4: Define the Time Constant**

Let $\tau = mC_p / (hA)$ (units: seconds). Then:

$$ \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \, dt $$

**Step 5: Integrate Both Sides**

Integrating from the initial condition $(T = T_0$ at $t = 0)$ to a generic state $(T = T$ at $t = t)$:

$$ \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT'}{T' - T_\infty} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt' $$

**Step 6: Evaluate the Integrals**

$$ \left[ \ln(T' - T_\infty) \right]_{T_0}^{T} = -\frac{t}{\tau} $$

$$ \ln(T - T_\infty) - \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) = -\frac{t}{\tau} $$

**Step 7: Simplify Using Logarithm Rules**

$$ \ln \left( \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} \right) = -\frac{t}{\tau} $$

**Step 8: Exponential Form**

Exponentiating both sides:

$$ \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-t/\tau} $$

**Step 9: Final Master Equation**

$$ T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t/\tau} $$

This is the **famous exponential decay** solution of the lumped capacitance problem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem:** A solid aluminum sphere of diameter $D = 50$ mm is heated uniformly to $T_0 = 350$ K and then suddenly immersed in a large water bath maintained at $T_\infty = 300$ K. The sphere has the following properties: $k = 205$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, $C_p = 900$ J/(kg·K). The experimentally measured temperature at the center of the sphere after $t = 60$ s is $T = 320$ K. Determine: (i) the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$, (ii) the Biot Number, (iii) the Fourier Number, and (iv) the time required for the sphere to cool to $T = 305$ K.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Step-by-Step (No Steps Skipped)

**Given Data**

$$ D = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad R = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad T_0 = 350 \text{ K}, \quad T_\infty = 300 \text{ K} $$

$$ k = 205 \text{ W/(m·K)}, \quad \rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad C_p = 900 \text{ J/(kg·K)} $$

$$ t_1 = 60 \text{ s}, \quad T_1 = 320 \text{ K} $$

**Step (a): Compute Geometric Parameters**

Volume of the sphere:

$$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.025)^3 = 6.545 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 $$

Surface area of the sphere:

$$ A_s = 4 \pi R^2 = 4 \pi (0.025)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$

Mass of the sphere:

$$ m = \rho V = 2700 \times 6.545 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1767 \text{ kg} $$

Characteristic length:

$$ L_c = \frac{V}{A_s} = \frac{6.545 \times 10^{-5}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = \frac{R}{3} $$

**Step (b): Compute the Time Constant from the Measured Data**

From the master equation:

$$ \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-t_1 / \tau} $$

$$ \frac{320 - 300}{350 - 300} = e^{-60/\tau} $$

$$ \frac{20}{50} = e^{-60/\tau} $$

$$ 0.40 = e^{-60/\tau} $$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$ \ln(0.40) = -\frac{60}{\tau} $$

$$ -0.9163 = -\frac{60}{\tau} $$

$$ \tau = \frac{60}{0.9163} = 65.48 \text{ s} $$

**Step (c): Determine the Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient h**

Using the definition of time constant:

$$ \tau = \frac{m C_p}{h A_s} \implies h = \frac{m C_p}{\tau A_s} $$

$$ h = \frac{0.1767 \times 900}{65.48 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ h = \frac{159.03}{0.5143} $$

$$ \boxed{h = 309.2 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}} $$

**Step (d): Compute the Biot Number**

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{309.2 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{205} $$

$$ Bi = \frac{2.577}{205} = 0.01257 $$

$$ \boxed{Bi = 0.0126 \ll 0.1} $$

> Since $Bi < 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance assumption is rigorously valid** for this experiment.

**Step (e): Compute the Fourier Number at t = 60 s**

First, compute thermal diffusivity:

$$ \alpha = \frac{k}{\rho C_p} = \frac{205}{2700 \times 900} = 8.436 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$

Then:

$$ Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{8.436 \times 10^{-5} \times 60}{(8.333 \times 10^{-3})^2} $$

$$ Fo = \frac{5.0616 \times 10^{-3}}{6.944 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ \boxed{Fo = 72.88} $$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Fo \gg 1$, the system is well into the *diffusion-dominated* regime and the temperature is approaching equilibrium.

**Step (f): Time to Cool to 305 K**

$$ \frac{T_2 - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-t_2 / \tau} $$

$$ \frac{305 - 300}{350 - 300} = e^{-t_2 / 65.48} $$

$$ \frac{5}{50} = 0.10 = e^{-t_2 / 65.48} $$

$$ \ln(0.10) = -\frac{t_2}{65.48} $$

$$ -2.3026 = -\frac{t_2}{65.48} $$

$$ \boxed{t_2 = 150.8 \text{ s} \approx 2.51 \text{ min}} $$

## 3.3 The Experimental Procedure — Analytical Matrix

The following matrix outlines the **complete experimental workflow** expected in the KTU lab report. Each row identifies the *what*, the *how*, and the *why*.

| Step | Procedure | Measurement | Purpose / Why |
|------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | Measure sphere mass ($m$) and dimensions ($D$, $R$) | Vernier caliper, electronic balance | Compute $V$, $A_s$, $L_c$ |
| 2 | Note the physical properties from the standard table | Look up $k$, $\rho$, $C_p$ from data book | Required for $h$ and $Bi$ |
| 3 | Heat the sphere uniformly in a boiling water bath | Thermometer, ≥ 30 min immersion | Achieve uniform $T_0$ |
| 4 | Prepare cold water bath at ambient temperature | Thermometer, stirrer to ensure $T_\infty$ = const | Large reservoir assumption |
| 5 | Quickly transfer sphere to cold bath; start stopwatch | Stopwatch, thermocouple/thermometer | Minimize heat loss during transfer |
| 6 | Record temperature at fixed time intervals (e.g., 30 s) | T vs. t data table | Build the temperature profile |
| 7 | Continue until $T \approx T_\infty + 5$ °C | Termination criterion | Ensure asymptotic behavior |
| 8 | Compute $(T - T_\infty)$ for each reading | Manual calculation | Prepare for log plot |
| 9 | Plot $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs. $t$ | Semi-log graph paper | Verify exponential decay |
| 10 | Determine slope of straight line | Linear regression / graphical | Extract $\tau$ and $h$ |
| 11 | Calculate $h$, $Bi$, $Fo$ | Standard formulas | Final results |
| 12 | Validate $Bi < 0.1$ | Numerical check | Confirm lumped method validity |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the Biot Number. State and explain the criterion for the validity of the lumped capacitance analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Biot Number** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the internal conductive thermal resistance to the external convective thermal resistance across a solid body:

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{L_c / k}{1/h} $$

where $L_c = V/A_s$ is the characteristic length of the solid.

**Validity Criterion:** The lumped capacitance analysis (where the solid is assumed to have a spatially uniform temperature at any instant) is valid only when the **internal conduction resistance is much smaller than the external convection resistance**, i.e., when:

$$ \boxed{Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} \leq 0.1} $$

When $Bi \leq 0.1$, temperature gradients within the solid are negligible (less than 5 % of the surface-to-fluid temperature difference) and the entire solid can be treated as a single "lumped" thermal capacitance with one representative temperature.

**Physical Interpretation:** $Bi \ll 1$ means heat leaves the surface much faster than it can be conducted from the interior to the surface. The interior therefore remains isothermal, and the solid cools uniformly.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the Fourier Number. What is its physical significance in transient heat conduction?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fourier Number** ($Fo$) is the dimensionless time used in transient heat conduction analysis. It is defined as:

$$ Fo = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^2} = \frac{k \, t}{\rho \, C_p \, L_c^2} $$

where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity, $t$ is the elapsed time, and $L_c$ is the characteristic length.

**Physical Significance:**

- $Fo$ represents the **ratio of the actual elapsed time** to the **characteristic thermal diffusion time** of the body.
- A small $Fo$ ($Fo \ll 1$) means that thermal energy has not yet had time to diffuse significantly into the solid — the transient is in its early phase.
- A large $Fo$ ($Fo \gg 1$) means thermal penetration is complete and the system is approaching thermal equilibrium with the surroundings.
- It is the **time-scaling parameter** that determines how deep the thermal wave has penetrated into the solid at any given time.
- $Fo = 1$ is often used as a benchmark for "characteristic time" of the transient process.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
