# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State **Stefan's Law** of radiation. Define the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** and state its SI value.

**Model Answer:**

> **Stefan's Law (1879)** states that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a **blackbody** is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.
> Mathematically: $E_b = \sigma T^4$ where $E_b$ is the emissive power (W/m²), $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).
> **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma$ is the constant of proportionality defined as $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$.
> **SI value:** $\sigma = 5.6703 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a **blackbody**, a **gray body**, and a **real (selective) body** with reference to emissivity.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Blackbody | Gray Body | Real (Selective) Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emissivity $\varepsilon$ | 1 (all $\lambda$, all $T$) | constant $<1$ | varies with $\lambda$ and $T$ |
| Absorptivity $\alpha$ | 1 | $\varepsilon$ (constant) | $\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$ |
| Reference | Ideal | Engineering approximation | True physical behaviour |
| Example | Cavity with small hole | Clean polished metal at moderate $T$ | Lampblack, glass, paints |

A real body is **selective** — its $\varepsilon$ depends on $\lambda$ and $T$, whereas a gray body is a simplified engineering model assuming constant $\varepsilon$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a)** *Derive the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** by integrating Planck's spectral distribution. Show that the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ depends only on $h$, $c$, and $k_B$.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the experimental setup used in the KTU lab to determine the **emissivity of a test plate**, with a labelled diagram. State the precautions taken during the experiment.* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Planck's Spectral Distribution Law** (1 mark)

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over all wavelengths** to obtain total blackbody emissive power: (2 marks)

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]} d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$, $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{x k_B T}$, $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{x^2 k_B T} dx$. (1 mark)

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

**Step 4 — Use the standard definite integral** $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** (1 mark)

$$E_b = \sigma T^4,\quad \text{where}\quad \sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** $\sigma$ depends *only* on the three fundamental constants $h$ (Planck's), $c$ (speed of light), and $k_B$ (Boltzmann's). Numerically, $\sigma = 5.6703 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. (1 mark)

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Apparatus Description (4 marks):**

The KTU lab apparatus consists of:
1. **Test plate (circular):** Made of brass or copper, ~10 cm diameter, with a **calibrated electrical heater** wound on its back face.
2. **Insulation:** The back and sides are insulated with ceramic wool to ensure **one-dimensional, front-face emission** only.
3. **Thermopile / Radiation sensor:** A sensitive thermopile placed at a known distance from the plate, connected to a digital readout in W/m².
4. **Water-cooled guard ring:** Surrounds the test plate to provide a constant-temperature boundary and shield the sensor from stray radiation.
5. **Thermocouple (K-type):** Embedded in the test plate, connected to a digital temperature indicator.
6. **Variac, Voltmeter, Ammeter:** For controlling and measuring heater input.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Set up the apparatus, ensure water circulation through the guard ring.
2. **Calibration run:** With test plate cold, vary the heater input. Record $V$, $I$, $T_s$, $T_{sur}$.
3. **Hot runs:** Gradually increase variac output. At each step, wait for **steady state** (T indicator stable for 5 minutes).
4. Tabulate: $V$, $I$, $T_s$, $T_{sur}$, $Q_{elec} = VI$.
5. Calculate $Q_{rad}$ and $\varepsilon$ using the energy balance.

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Maintain **steady state** before recording.
- Ensure **thermo-contact** of thermocouple is firm.
- Keep **variac increments small** (e.g., 5 V).
- **Cool the guard ring** continuously to prevent back-radiation.
- Avoid touching the test plate during operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]` *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *State and prove **Kirchhoff's Law** of thermal radiation. How is it experimentally verified in the lab determination of emissivity?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A horizontal circular hot plate of diameter 20 cm, at 800 K, is placed in a large enclosure whose walls are at 300 K. The plate has emissivity 0.6. Compute the net radiative heat loss from the plate. If the plate is painted black ($\varepsilon = 1$) and all other conditions are the same, what will be the new heat loss?* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Statement (2 marks):** Kirchhoff's Law states that *at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a body equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature*:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$

**Proof by thermodynamic argument (4 marks):**

Consider a small body 1 (emissivity $\varepsilon_1$, absorptivity $\alpha_1$) suspended in a much larger isothermal enclosure 2 (which acts as a blackbody at temperature $T$).

Let $G$ be the irradiation on body 1 from the enclosure. The energy balance on body 1 at steady state:
$$\text{Energy absorbed} = \text{Energy emitted}$$
$$G \alpha_1 = E_{b1}$$
where $E_{b1} = \varepsilon_1 E_b$ (gray body relation).

If body 1 is now replaced by body 3 (different $\varepsilon_3, \alpha_3$), the same irradiation $G$ applies. At equilibrium:
$$G \alpha_3 = \varepsilon_3 E_b$$

Dividing the two equations:
$$\frac{\alpha_1}{\varepsilon_1} = \frac{\alpha_3}{\varepsilon_3} = \text{constant} = \frac{G}{E_b}$$

But for the blackbody enclosure, $G = E_b$. Hence $\alpha / \varepsilon = 1$, so $\varepsilon = \alpha$.

**Experimental verification in lab (1 mark):** In the emissivity-determination experiment, the test plate is heated and enclosed in a water-cooled chamber (acting as a blackbody sink). At steady state, the energy emitted by the plate (measured as $Q_{rad}$) equals the energy that would be absorbed by an identical plate of the same material at the same temperature. This balance is what allows $\varepsilon = \alpha$ to be exploited in energy-balance calculations.

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given (1 mark):**
- Diameter $D = 0.20\ \text{m} \Rightarrow$ Area $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142\ \text{m}^2$
- $T_1 = 800\ \text{K}$, $T_2 = 300\ \text{K}$, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$
- Enclosure is large → $F_{1\to 2} \approx 1$

**Step 1 — Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:** (1 mark)
$$T_1^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}\ \text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^4$$
$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.015 \times 10^{11}\ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 2 — Use Stefan–Boltzmann for gray plate:** (2 marks)
$$Q_{rad} = A \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$
$$Q_{rad} = 0.03142 \times 0.6 \times 5.6703 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.015 \times 10^{11}$$
$$Q_{rad} = 0.03142 \times 0.6 \times 22759 = 429.0\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Painted black case $\varepsilon_1 = 1$:** (2 marks)
$$Q_{rad}^{bb} = 0.03142 \times 1.0 \times 5.6703 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.015 \times 10^{11} = 715.1\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and conclusion:** (1 mark)
$$\frac{Q_{rad}^{bb}}{Q_{rad}} = \frac{1}{0.6} = 1.667$$

Hence, painting the plate black increases radiative heat loss by **66.7%**. This is the experimental basis for using lampblack coatings in lab reference plates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Temperature must be in Kelvin** — students who use °C lose 1–2 marks immediately. *Always convert $T_s$ and $T_{sur}$ to absolute Kelvin before substituting into $T^4$.*
> 2. **Do not subtract $T^4$ values in °C.** A common error is computing $(T_s - T_{sur})^4$ instead of $T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4$. These are *not* the same.
> 3. **Stefan–Boltzmann constant units** — students often write $\sigma$ without units in answers; this loses ½ mark.
> 4. **View factor** — for a small plate in a large enclosure, $F_{1\to 2} = 1$; for two coaxial parallel disks, it is *less than 1* and is computed using the disk-to-disk formula. Wrong $F$ is a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 5. **Gray body vs black body** — students often write $Q = A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ without $\varepsilon$. Always include $\varepsilon$ for a gray plate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Radiation Heat Transfer Lab (Module Exp. 2)**
> - [x] Radiation is **the only mode of heat transfer that does not need a medium**.
> - [x] **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, $\sigma = 5.6703 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
> - [x] **Gray body:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ with $0 < \varepsilon < 1$.
> - [x] **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at thermal equilibrium.
> - [x] **Energy balance:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; for opaque $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha + \rho = 1$.
> - [x] **Lab formula:** $Q_{rad} = Q_{elec} - h_{cv} A (T_s - T_{sur})$.
> - [x] **Emissivity experiment:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{rad}}{A \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)}$.
> - [x] **Stefan–Boltzmann experiment:** $\sigma_{exp} = \dfrac{Q_{rad}}{A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)}$; expected $T_s$ range: 400–800 K.
> - [x] **Steady state** must be confirmed (5 min stable $T_s$ before recording).
> - [x] **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$.
> - [x] **View factor reciprocity:** $A_1 F_{1\to 2} = A_2 F_{2\to 1}$.
> - [x] **Two-surface radiation network:** $Q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{R_1 + R_{12} + R_2}$.
> - [x] **Units check:** $T$ **must be in Kelvin**, $\sigma$ units **must be quoted**.
> - [x] **Plot:** $Q_{rad}$ vs $(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ gives a **straight line through the origin** with slope $= A \varepsilon \sigma$.
> - [x] **% Error** $= \dfrac{\vert \sigma_{exp} - \sigma_{theor} \vert}{\sigma_{theor}} \times 100$; **tolerance** typically $\leq 10\%$.
> - [x] **Precautions:** water-cooled guard ring, proper insulation, K-type thermocouple contact, variac increment $\sim 5$ V.
> - [x] **Real-world links:** spacecraft thermal control (radiators), furnace design, solar collectors, fire engineering, building insulation foils.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer in Agitated Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Heat Transfer in Agitated Vessels

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Heat transfer in agitated vessels refers to the study of the rate at which thermal energy is transferred between a heating/cooling medium flowing through the jacket (or coil) of a mechanically stirred tank and the process fluid being mixed inside the vessel. It is quantified through the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** and the **individual (inside) film coefficient (h_i)** on the process-fluid side.

In simple words, it is the engineering study of *how fast* a stirred tank can be heated up or cooled down. A vessel with a stirrer and a jacket is the **workhorse of the chemical process industry (CPI)** — almost every batch reactor, fermentation tank, crystallizer, and pasteurizer uses this geometry.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Pot" Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine you are boiling milk in a deep pot on a stove. To heat the milk faster, you:
> 1. Stir it continuously with a ladle (this is the **impeller / agitator**),
> 2. Use a wide, thick-bottomed vessel (this represents the **vessel diameter and wall thickness**),
> 3. Ensure the hot gas from the burner sweeps the entire bottom (this is the **jacket coverage and baffling**).
>
> The faster you stir, the faster the milk near the wall is replaced by cooler milk from the center — this is exactly what **forced convection by agitation** does in an industrial jacketed vessel. The **temperature gradient** in the milk (hot near wall, cold in center) gets destroyed, and the overall heating becomes much faster.

### Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Constants)
- **Inside film heat transfer coefficient, h_i** — Units: **W/(m²·K)** or **kcal/(hr·m²·°C)**
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient, U** — Units: **W/(m²·K)**
- **Standard gravitational acceleration, g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure, P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
- **Nusselt number (Nu)** — Dimensionless temperature-gradient ratio.
- **Reynolds number (Re)** — Dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
- **Prandtl number (Pr)** — Dimensionless ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "define heat transfer in agitated vessels," students often forget to mention the **jacket side** and the **wall side**. Always remember — there are **three resistances in series**: inside film (h_i), wall (k_w), and jacket-side film (h_j). The lab experiment focuses on extracting h_i.

---

## 2. Why Agitation is Used for Heat Transfer

In an *unstirred* jacketed tank, the fluid in contact with the hot wall heats up, becomes lighter, and rises — natural convection dominates. This is slow because **thermal boundary layers** grow thick.

Once an impeller rotates, it forces fluid across the wall, **sweeping away the boundary layer** continuously. The fluid that just gave up (or absorbed) heat is replaced by fluid at bulk temperature. The boundary layer becomes thin, and the rate of heat transfer increases dramatically — often by **a factor of 5 to 10** compared to the unstirred case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Resistances in Series Model

The rate of heat transfer $Q$ from the jacket to the bulk process fluid is governed by:

$$ Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD} $$

where $A$ is the **effective heat-transfer area** (usually the inner surface of the jacket, $A = \pi D_i T$ with $D_i$ = vessel inner diameter, $T$ = liquid level), and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the **log-mean temperature difference** between the jacket service fluid and the bulk process fluid.

The overall coefficient $U$ is built from three resistances in series:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_j} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $h_i$ = inside (process-side) film coefficient — **this is the unknown the experiment aims to find.**
> - $x_w / k_w$ = wall resistance — fixed by vessel geometry and wall material.
> - $h_j$ = jacket-side film coefficient — fixed by jacket fluid velocity.
> - Typical magnitude: $h_i \approx 200\text{–}2000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$, $h_j \approx 1000\text{–}5000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$, wall resistance is negligible for thin stainless steel.

## 2.2 Dimensional Analysis — Building the Correlation

The inside film coefficient $h_i$ depends on the following variables:

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions (M-L-T-θ) |
|---|---|---|
| Impeller diameter | $D_a$ | L |
| Vessel diameter | $D_T$ | L |
| Liquid level | $T$ | L |
| Agitator speed | $N$ | 1/T |
| Liquid density | $\rho$ | M/L³ |
| Liquid viscosity | $\mu$ | M/(L·T) |
| Liquid thermal conductivity | $k$ | M·L/(T³·θ) |
| Liquid specific heat | $C_p$ | L²/(T²·θ) |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | L/T² |

Using the Buckingham $\pi$ theorem, with $D_a$, $N$, $\rho$ as repeating variables, we obtain the classical functional form:

$$ Nu = f(Re, Pr, \text{geometric ratios}, \text{viscosity correction}) $$

i.e.

$$ \frac{h_i \cdot D_a}{k} = a \cdot Re^{b} \cdot Pr^{c} \cdot \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{d} \cdot \left(\frac{D_T}{D_a}\right)^{e} \cdot \left(\frac{T}{D_T}\right)^{f} $$

This is the **canonical form of every agitated-vessel heat-transfer correlation.**

## 2.3 Important Standard Correlations (KTU-Favorite)

| # | Correlation | Geometry | Source / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_i \cdot D_T / k = 0.74 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Jacketed vessel, paddle, no baffles | **Chilton, Drew & Jebens (1944)** |
| 2 | $h_i \cdot D_T / k = 0.76 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Jacketed vessel, paddle, **with** baffles | Brooks & Su (1959) |
| 3 | $h_i \cdot D_T / k = 0.17 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.37} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.37} \cdot (D_T/T)^{0.1} \cdot (D_a/D_T)^{0.1}$ | Anchor agitator | Oldshue & Gretton |
| 4 | $h_i \cdot D_T / k = 0.027 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Helical coil inside vessel | Correlations of Coil-in-Vessel type |

> [!TIP]
> The **0.67 exponent on Re** is the single most important number in agitated-vessel heat transfer. Memorize it. It means the heat-transfer coefficient scales as $N^{0.67}$ — i.e. doubling the impeller speed increases $h_i$ by a factor of $2^{0.67} \approx 1.59$.

## 2.4 Wilson Plot — The Lab Workhorse

Since the experiment cannot directly measure $h_i$, we use the **Wilson Plot technique** (1925). The idea:

1. Run the experiment at **several impeller speeds** $N$.
2. At each speed, calculate the **overall coefficient $U$** from $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
3. The resistance equation is rearranged as:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{fixed} $$

where $R_{fixed} = x_w / k_w + 1 / h_j$ is (approximately) **constant** because wall and jacket-side conditions don't change much with impeller speed.

4. Plot $1/U$ on the Y-axis against $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$ (using the assumed correlation form) on the X-axis. The **intercept on the Y-axis** gives $R_{fixed}$, and the **slope** corrects the assumed correlation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Save this table — these are the **only** formulas needed to solve any KTU numerical on this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty | $Q = m_s C_{p,s}(T_{s,o} - T_{s,i})$ | W; subscript s = service (jacket) fluid |
| 2 | LMTD | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K; with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ |
| 3 | Overall U | $U = Q / (A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$ | W/(m²·K) |
| 4 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho N D_a^{2} / \mu$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Prandtl number | $Pr = C_p \mu / k$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Nusselt number | $Nu = h_i D_a / k$ (or $h_i D_T / k$) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Wilson plot abscissa | $1/h_{i,\text{calc}} = 1 / \left( \frac{k}{D_T} \cdot 0.76 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14} \right)$ | m²·K/W |
| 8 | Wall resistance | $R_w = x_w / k_w$ | m²·K/W |
| 9 | Jacket-side resistance | $R_j = 1 / h_j$ | m²·K/W |
| 10 | Power number | $N_p = P / (\rho N^{3} D_a^{5})$ | Dimensionless (not needed for heat transfer, but asked in viva) |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Heat-transfer correlations for agitated vessels are used to:
> - **Size** the jacket area of industrial batch reactors (a 1% error in $h_i$ can change vessel cost by lakhs of rupees).
> - **Scale-up** lab data (say, 10 L) to plant scale (say, 10 m³) using the same dimensionless groups.
> - **Choose** the right impeller (PBT, Pfaudler, anchor, helical) for a viscous heating duty.
> - **Design** the utility system (chilled water / steam flow rate) that supports the reactor.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $h_i$ from Wilson Plot

### Step 1 — Write the energy balance on the service fluid

The jacket fluid (water or steam) enters at $T_{s,i}$ and leaves at $T_{s,o}$. With a measured mass flow rate $\dot{m}_s$ and specific heat $C_{p,s}$:

$$ Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot C_{p,s} \cdot (T_{s,o} - T_{s,i}) $$

### Step 2 — Compute LMTD

Let $T_{c,in}$ and $T_{c,out}$ be the inlet and outlet temperatures of the process fluid (cold side). Define:

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{s,i} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{s,o} - T_{c,in} $$

Then:

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$

### Step 3 — Calculate the experimental overall coefficient

$$ U_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} $$

where $A$ is the **inside heat-transfer area** of the jacket (in m²).

### Step 4 — Decompose the resistance

$$ \frac{1}{U_{exp}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_j} $$

Define $R_{fixed} = \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_j}$, so:

$$ \frac{1}{U_{exp}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{fixed} \tag{1} $$

### Step 5 — Assume a correlation form for $h_i$

We assume (Brooks–Su form) that:

$$ h_{i,\text{calc}} = \frac{k}{D_T} \cdot 0.76 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot \left( \frac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14} $$

Now $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$ is computable from the impeller speed $N$ (which appears inside $Re$).

### Step 6 — Rearrange and linearize

Take reciprocal of the assumed correlation:

$$ \frac{1}{h_{i,\text{calc}}} = \frac{D_T}{k \cdot 0.76 \cdot Re^{0.67} \cdot Pr^{0.33} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}} $$

Plot $1/U_{exp}$ versus $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$. According to (1), this should be a straight line:

$$ \frac{1}{U_{exp}} = m \cdot \frac{1}{h_{i,\text{calc}}} + R_{fixed} $$

where $m$ is the slope (ideally 1 if the assumed form is exact, or a correction factor otherwise). The **Y-intercept** is $R_{fixed}$, the **X-intercept** (when $1/U = 0$) equals $-R_{fixed}/m$, and the corrected inside coefficient at any speed is:

$$ h_i = m \cdot h_{i,\text{calc}} $$

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data (water heated in a jacketed vessel, paddle + 4 baffles):**
> - $D_T = 0.30$ m, $D_a = 0.10$ m, $T = 0.30$ m, $x_w = 0.004$ m, $k_w = 16 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ (SS304)
> - $k = 0.61$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 995$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $\mu = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $\mu_w = 6.5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
> - Jacket water: $\dot{m}_s = 0.10$ kg/s, $C_{p,s} = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $T_{s,i} = 70$ °C, $T_{s,o} = 65$ °C
> - Process water: $T_{c,in} = 30$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 50$ °C
> - $N = 4$ rps, $A = \pi D_T T = \pi \times 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.2827$ m²

**Compute $Q$:**

$$ Q = 0.10 \times 4180 \times (70 - 65) = 2090 \text{ W} $$

**Compute LMTD:**

$$ \Delta T_1 = 70 - 50 = 20 \text{ °C} $$
$$ \Delta T_2 = 65 - 30 = 35 \text{ °C} $$

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{35 - 20}{\ln(35/20)} = \frac{15}{\ln(1.75)} = \frac{15}{0.5596} = 26.80 \text{ °C} $$

**Compute $U_{exp}$:**

$$ U_{exp} = \frac{2090}{0.2827 \times 26.80} = \frac{2090}{7.578} = 275.8 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Compute $Re$, $Pr$, and assumed $h_{i,\text{calc}}$:**

$$ Re = \frac{\rho N D_a^{2}}{\mu} = \frac{995 \times 4 \times (0.10)^2}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{39.8}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 49{,}750 $$

$$ Pr = \frac{C_p \mu}{k} = \frac{4180 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.61} = \frac{3.344}{0.61} = 5.482 $$

$$ h_{i,\text{calc}} = \frac{0.61}{0.30} \times 0.76 \times (49750)^{0.67} \times (5.482)^{0.33} \times \left(\frac{8.0}{6.5}\right)^{0.14} $$

Compute term by term:

$$ (49750)^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln(49750)} = e^{0.67 \times 10.815} = e^{7.246} = 1401.4 $$

$$ (5.482)^{0.33} = e^{0.33 \ln 5.482} = e^{0.33 \times 1.7016} = e^{0.5615} = 1.753 $$

$$ (8.0/6.5)^{0.14} = (1.2308)^{0.14} = e^{0.14 \times \ln 1.2308} = e^{0.14 \times 0.2076} = e^{0.02906} = 1.0295 $$

$$ h_{i,\text{calc}} = 2.0333 \times 0.76 \times 1401.4 \times 1.753 \times 1.0295 $$

$$ h_{i,\text{calc}} = 2.0333 \times 0.76 \times 1401.4 = 2165.4 $$
$$ 2165.4 \times 1.753 = 3796.0 $$
$$ 3796.0 \times 1.0295 = 3908.0 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

So at $N = 4$ rps, the assumed form gives $h_{i,\text{calc}} \approx 3908$ W/(m²·K). In a Wilson plot, this point becomes $(1/3908,\ 1/275.8) = (2.559 \times 10^{-4},\ 3.626 \times 10^{-3})$. After repeating at 3–4 more speeds, a straight line is fit and the corrected $h_i$ is read.

### Step 8 — Effect of Parameters (the "Analytical Matrix")

| Parameter | Effect on $h_i$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Impeller speed $N \uparrow$ | $h_i \propto N^{0.67}$ (large positive) | Doubling speed gives ~1.6× heat transfer — but power draw grows as $N^{3}$ |
| Impeller diameter $D_a \uparrow$ | $h_i \propto D_a^{1.34}$ (positive) | Larger impellers give better heat transfer but worse blending |
| Liquid viscosity $\mu \uparrow$ | $h_i$ decreases (negative) | Viscous fluids need helical/anchor impellers; paddle fails |
| Thermal conductivity $k \uparrow$ | $h_i$ increases (positive) | Better conductive fluids transfer heat faster |
| Baffles present | $h_i$ increases by ~5–10% | Baffles break swirling, force axial flow across wall |
| Helical coil vs jacket | Coil gives $h_i$ 2–3× jacket | Used in viscous duties where jacket is inadequate |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
> *Define the overall heat transfer coefficient U for a jacketed agitated vessel and write the resistance equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit log-mean temperature difference between the jacket service fluid and the process fluid bulk:

$$ U = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} \quad \text{[Units: W/(m}^2\text{·K)]} $$

The three resistances in series give:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_j} $$

where $h_i$ = process-side film coefficient, $x_w/k_w$ = wall resistance, and $1/h_j$ = jacket-side film resistance. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *What is a Wilson plot? Mention its significance in heat-transfer experiments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Wilson plot is a graphical technique (Wilson, 1925) used to determine the **inside (process-side) film coefficient $h_i$** and the **fixed resistances** (wall + jacket-side) in a jacketed vessel. The reciprocal of the experimental overall coefficient $1/U$ is plotted against the reciprocal of an assumed theoretical $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$ for several impeller speeds. A straight line is fitted; its **slope** corrects the assumed correlation, and its **Y-intercept** gives the fixed resistance $R_{fixed} = x_w/k_w + 1/h_j$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, 14 marks]**
> *(a)* Derive, with a clear sketch, the resistance-in-series model for heat transfer in a jacketed agitated vessel. Identify which resistance dominates in a typical stainless-steel laboratory setup. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A baffled jacketed vessel with a 4-blade paddle agitator is used to heat water. Vessel inner diameter $D_T = 0.40$ m, agitator diameter $D_a = 0.133$ m, liquid level $T = 0.40$ m. The following readings are taken at one impeller speed:

> | Quantity | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Jacket water flow rate | $\dot{m}_s = 0.12$ kg/s |
> | Jacket water $C_p$ | 4180 J/(kg·K) |
> | $T_{s,in}$ | 75 °C |
> | $T_{s,out}$ | 68 °C |
> | Process $T_{in}$ | 28 °C |
> | Process $T_{out}$ | 52 °C |
> | $Re$ | 60 000 |
> | $Pr$ | 5.5 |
> | $\mu/\mu_w$ | 1.20 |
> | Water thermal conductivity $k$ | 0.61 W/(m·K) |
>
> Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ and compare it with the inside film coefficient $h_i$ obtained from the **Brooks–Su correlation**. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

The vessel wall separates two fluids: the **service fluid** in the jacket (subscript $j$) and the **process fluid** in the vessel (subscript $i$). In steady state, the same heat $Q$ passes through three resistances in series:

1. **Convective resistance** on the jacket side: $R_j = 1/h_j$
2. **Conductive resistance** through the metal wall: $R_w = x_w/k_w$
3. **Convective resistance** on the process side: $R_i = 1/h_i$

Heat flux $q = Q/A$ at any layer is the same. From Fourier's law and Newton's law of cooling:

$$ q = h_j (T_{s,bulk} - T_{s,wall}) = \frac{k_w}{x_w}(T_{s,wall} - T_{i,wall}) = h_i (T_{i,wall} - T_{i,bulk}) $$

Summing the temperature drops and dividing by $q$:

$$ \frac{T_{s,bulk} - T_{i,bulk}}{q} = \frac{1}{h_j} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_i} $$

The LHS equals $1/U$ by definition, so:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_j} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_i} $$

In a typical **stainless-steel laboratory setup** (4 mm wall, $k_w \approx 16$ W/(m·K)), the wall resistance is $R_w = 0.004/16 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W, which is **negligible** compared to $1/h_i$ (which is in the range $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m²·K/W for water). The **process-side film** typically dominates. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Step 1: Heat duty**

$$ Q = \dot{m}_s C_p (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out}) = 0.12 \times 4180 \times (75 - 68) = 3511.2 \text{ W} $$

**Step 2: LMTD**

$$ \Delta T_1 = 75 - 52 = 23 \text{ °C}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 68 - 28 = 40 \text{ °C} $$

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{40 - 23}{\ln(40/23)} = \frac{17}{\ln(1.7391)} = \frac{17}{0.5534} = 30.72 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 3: Heat transfer area**

$$ A = \pi D_T T = \pi \times 0.40 \times 0.40 = 0.5027 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 4: Overall coefficient**

$$ U = \frac{3511.2}{0.5027 \times 30.72} = \frac{3511.2}{15.443} = 227.3 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Step 5: Inside coefficient by Brooks–Su**

$$ h_i = \frac{k}{D_T} \times 0.76 \times Re^{0.67} \times Pr^{0.33} \times (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14} $$

$$ = \frac{0.61}{0.40} \times 0.76 \times (60{,}000)^{0.67} \times (5.5)^{0.33} \times (1.20)^{0.14} $$

Compute term by term:

$$ (60{,}000)^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln 60000} = e^{0.67 \times 11.002} = e^{7.3713} = 1588.5 $$

$$ (5.5)^{0.33} = e^{0.33 \times 1.7047} = e^{0.5626} = 1.755 $$

$$ (1.20)^{0.14} = e^{0.14 \times 0.1823} = e^{0.0255} = 1.0258 $$

$$ h_i = 1.525 \times 0.76 \times 1588.5 \times 1.755 \times 1.0258 $$

$$ h_i = 1.159 \times 1588.5 = 1841.1 $$
$$ 1841.1 \times 1.755 = 3231.1 $$
$$ 3231.1 \times 1.0258 = 3314.3 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Step 6: Compare**

$$ h_i = 3314 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}, \quad U = 227 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U} = 4.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}, \quad \frac{1}{h_i} = 3.02 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} $$

The process-side resistance ($3.02 \times 10^{-4}$) is **much smaller** than the overall resistance ($4.40 \times 10^{-3}$), which means the **jacket-side** is dominating — typical for a low-velocity water jacket. To improve $U$, we must increase the **jacket-side velocity** (better pump, more baffles) rather than the impeller speed.

**Comment:** The dominant resistance in this run is on the **jacket side**, not the process side. The laboratory setup typically under-utilizes the process side; in a plant, optimizing jacket-side flow is the first lever. (7 marks)

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, 14 marks — Internal Choice Alternative]**
> *(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the Wilson plot method. Why is it necessary even though standard correlations exist? *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* During a heat-transfer experiment in a jacketed agitated vessel, the following data were recorded. Determine the constants of the equation $h_i = a \cdot N^{b}$ by plotting $\log h_i$ vs $\log N$ (assume water properties at bulk temperature: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1$ cP, $k = 0.6$ W/(m·K), $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $D_T = 0.30$ m, $D_a = 0.10$ m).

> | Run | $N$ (rpm) | $T_{s,in}$ (°C) | $T_{s,out}$ (°C) | $T_{c,in}$ (°C) | $T_{c,out}$ (°C) | $\dot{m}_s$ (kg/s) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 60 | 70 | 67 | 30 | 35 | 0.05 |
> | 2 | 90 | 70 | 66 | 30 | 38 | 0.05 |
> | 3 | 120 | 70 | 64.5 | 30 | 40 | 0.05 |
> | 4 | 150 | 70 | 63 | 30 | 41.5 | 0.05 |

> Take $A = 0.28$ m². *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Wilson plot explanation [7 marks]

A **Wilson plot** is a graphical procedure to determine the **inside film coefficient $h_i$** from experimentally measured $U$ values. (1 mark)

**Procedure:** (4 marks)
1. Conduct steady-state runs at **5–6 different impeller speeds** while keeping the jacket flow rate constant.
2. Compute $Q$, $\Delta T_{LMTD}$, and hence $U_{exp} = Q/(A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$.
3. Compute $h_{i,\text{calc}}$ using a guessed correlation (say Brooks–Su).
4. Plot $1/U_{exp}$ on Y-axis against $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$ on X-axis. The points lie on a straight line:

$$ \frac{1}{U_{exp}} = m \cdot \frac{1}{h_{i,\text{calc}}} + R_{fixed} $$

5. The **slope $m$** corrects the assumed correlation, and the **intercept $R_{fixed}$** gives the sum of wall and jacket-side resistances. The actual inside coefficient is $h_i = m \cdot h_{i,\text{calc}}$.

**Why necessary?** (2 marks) Standard correlations are based on specific impeller/vessel geometry, fluid, and scale. Real lab setups have geometry imperfections (non-standard baffling, off-center impeller, finite wall thickness). Wilson plot *calibrates* the assumed correlation to the actual setup, removing systematic errors and giving the correct $h_i$ in absolute terms.

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Heat duty $Q$ for each run** using $Q = 0.05 \times 4180 \times (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})$:

| Run | $Q$ (W) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $0.05 \times 4180 \times 3 = 627.0$ |
| 2 | $0.05 \times 4180 \times 4 = 836.0$ |
| 3 | $0.05 \times 4180 \times 5.5 = 1149.5$ |
| 4 | $0.05 \times 4180 \times 7 = 1463.0$ |

**LMTD for each run** — using $A = 0.28$ m², compute $U$:

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{s,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{s,out} - T_{c,in} $$

**Run 1:** $\Delta T_1 = 70 - 35 = 35$, $\Delta T_2 = 67 - 30 = 37$
$$ \Delta T_{LMTD,1} = \frac{37 - 35}{\ln(37/35)} = \frac{2}{0.05579} = 35.85 \text{ °C} $$
$$ U_1 = 627 / (0.28 \times 35.85) = 62.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Run 2:** $\Delta T_1 = 70 - 38 = 32$, $\Delta T_2 = 66 - 30 = 36$
$$ \Delta T_{LMTD,2} = \frac{36 - 32}{\ln(36/32)} = \frac{4}{0.1178} = 33.96 \text{ °C} $$
$$ U_2 = 836 / (0.28 \times 33.96) = 87.9 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Run 3:** $\Delta T_1 = 70 - 40 = 30$, $\Delta T_2 = 64.5 - 30 = 34.5$
$$ \Delta T_{LMTD,3} = \frac{34.5 - 30}{\ln(34.5/30)} = \frac{4.5}{0.1398} = 32.18 \text{ °C} $$
$$ U_3 = 1149.5 / (0.28 \times 32.18) = 127.6 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Run 4:** $\Delta T_1 = 70 - 41.5 = 28.5$, $\Delta T_2 = 63 - 30 = 33$
$$ \Delta T_{LMTD,4} = \frac{33 - 28.5}{\ln(33/28.5)} = \frac{4.5}{0.1466} = 30.69 \text{ °C} $$
$$ U_4 = 1463 / (0.28 \times 30.69) = 170.2 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Estimate $h_i$ from $1/U_i$:** Because $1/U = 1/h_i + R_{fixed}$, and the *changes* in $1/U$ track the *changes* in $1/h_i$ (since $R_{fixed}$ is constant), the ratio $h_i \propto U$ is approximately valid. So for a log–log fit of $h_i$ vs $N$, we can use $U$ values directly:

| Run | $N$ (rpm) | $N$ (rps) | $U$ (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 1.0 | 62.5 |
| 2 | 90 | 1.5 | 87.9 |
| 3 | 120 | 2.0 | 127.6 |
| 4 | 150 | 2.5 | 170.2 |

Taking logs (base 10):

| $\log N$ (rps) | $\log U$ |
|---|---|
| 0.0000 | 1.7959 |
| 0.1761 | 1.9440 |
| 0.3010 | 2.1059 |
| 0.3979 | 2.2309 |

**Slope by least squares:**

$$ \text{slope} = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} $$

With $n=4$, $\sum x = 0.8750$, $\sum y = 8.0767$, $\sum xy = (0)(1.7959) + (0.1761)(1.9440) + (0.3010)(2.1059) + (0.3979)(2.2309) = 0 + 0.3424 + 0.6339 + 0.8877 = 1.8640$

$$ \sum x^2 = 0 + 0.0310 + 0.0906 + 0.1583 = 0.2799 $$

$$ b = \frac{4 \times 1.8640 - 0.8750 \times 8.0767}{4 \times 0.2799 - (0.8750)^2} = \frac{7.4560 - 7.0671}{1.1196 - 0.7656} = \frac{0.3889}{0.3540} = 1.0986 $$

Intercept:

$$ \log a = \frac{\sum y - b \sum x}{n} = \frac{8.0767 - 1.0986 \times 0.8750}{4} = \frac{8.0767 - 0.9613}{4} = \frac{7.1154}{4} = 1.7788 $$

$$ a = 10^{1.7788} = 60.10 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

**Final correlation:**

$$ \boxed{h_i = 60.1 \cdot N^{1.10} } $$

where $N$ is in **rps** and $h_i$ is in **W/(m²·K)**. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the LMTD** and using arithmetic mean — KTU strictly penalizes 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Unit error in $\dot{m}$**: kg/s vs kg/hr — make a clear note in the record.
> 3. **Mixing SI and CGS units** (kcal/hr·m²·°C vs W/m²·K) — answer must be in **one consistent system**.
> 4. **In Wilson plot**: forgetting to use the *reciprocals* of $U$ and $h_{i,\text{calc}}$. Many students plot $U$ vs $h_i$ directly and get it wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting to include the $(\mu/\mu_w)$ correction** in the Brooks–Su correlation — the examiner will deduct at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning the dominant resistance** in the conclusion of the numerical — KTU explicitly tests this in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Sign errors in $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** when constructing LMTD — always define $\Delta T_1$ as the *jacket-inlet side* difference and $\Delta T_2$ as the *jacket-outlet side* difference, then verify $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ (or swap if not).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (10 high-yield bullets):**
> 1. **Three resistances** in series: $1/U = 1/h_i + x_w/k_w + 1/h_j$. In a typical SS lab vessel, the **process-side** or **jacket-side** film resistance dominates; the **wall** is almost always negligible.
> 2. **Heat duty** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ on the jacket side (more accurate than on the process side because $\dot{m}_s$ is measured by rotameter).
> 3. **LMTD** is the correct temperature-driving force; never use arithmetic mean $\Delta T$.
> 4. **Canonical correlation form** for agitated vessels: $Nu = a \cdot Re^{b} \cdot Pr^{c} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{d} \cdot (D_T/D_a)^{e} \cdot (T/D_T)^{f}$. Standard $b \approx 0.67$, $c \approx 0.33$, $d \approx 0.14$.
> 5. **Chilton–Drew–Jebens** = no baffles, paddle; **Brooks–Su** = with baffles, paddle. Both have the same exponents but the constant differs slightly (0.74 vs 0.76).
> 6. **Wilson plot** plots $1/U_{exp}$ vs $1/h_{i,\text{calc}}$; **slope** corrects the correlation, **Y-intercept** is $R_{fixed}$.
> 7. **Re** uses $D_a$ (impeller diameter), not $D_T$. $Re = \rho N D_a^2 / \mu$.
> 8. **Area $A$** is the **inside surface** of the jacket: $A = \pi D_i T$, where $T$ is the *liquid level*, not the total vessel height.
> 9. **Baffles** break swirling, increase $h_i$ by 5–10% and drastically improve mixing time.
> 10. **Scale-up** preserves $Re$, $Pr$, geometry — the same dimensionless correlation is used from 10 L to 10 m³.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Heat Transfer in Agitated Vessels.**
> *Prepared strictly per KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based template.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drop and Filmwise Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drop and Filmwise Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Condensation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condensation** is the process by which a vapor (or gas) changes its physical state to a liquid when its temperature is reduced below its saturation temperature at the prevailing pressure. The latent heat of vaporization released during this phase transition must be continuously removed by a cooler surface for the process to continue.

In a **condenser** (a fundamental unit operation in Chemical Engineering), a pure saturated vapor — most commonly steam — comes in contact with a cold solid surface (the condenser wall). As the vapor loses its superheat and then its latent heat, it condenses. The **rate of heat transfer** between the vapor and the cooling medium is governed by the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** and the **log mean temperature difference (LMTD)**.

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

## 1.2 The Two Modes of Condensation — A Practical Distinction

When a pure saturated vapor condenses on a cold, clean surface, the condensate (liquid) may accumulate in **one of two distinct physical patterns**. This difference in wetting behavior dramatically alters the heat transfer rate, and recognizing the difference is the entire purpose of this KTU lab experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 1 — Filmwise Condensation (Film Condensation):**
> The condensate wets the metallic surface completely, forming a continuous, smooth liquid film that blankets the entire surface. The vapor can never directly touch the metal; heat must first **conduct through the falling/rising liquid film** before reaching the cooling medium on the other side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 2 — Dropwise Condensation:**
> The condensate does **not** wet the surface. Instead, it forms millions of discrete spherical droplets that grow, coalesce, and roll off the surface under gravity, exposing fresh metal for further condensation. Vapor touches the metal **directly** at the unwetted patches.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a cold windowpane on a humid, foggy morning.

- If the glass is **untreated (plain glass)**, the water vapor in the air forms a **uniform translucent sheet of water** over the entire pane. You cannot see through it. This is **filmwise condensation** — and it acts as an **insulator**, slowing the further escape of heat from the room.

- If the same glass is **coated with a hydrophobic (water-repelling) layer** (such as a car windshield treated with a rain-repellent), the vapor forms **scattered water beads** that sit like little pearls on the surface. Between the beads, you can see the glass directly, and the beads themselves are much thinner than a continuous sheet. This is **dropwise condensation** — and it is a **far better heat-transfer regime** because the thermal resistance of the film is largely eliminated.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Dropwise condensation can yield heat transfer coefficients **5 to 10 times higher** than filmwise condensation. If engineers could *reliably* maintain dropwise condensation on industrial condensers, power plants and distillation columns would shrink dramatically in size!

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Operating Ranges

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Saturated steam temperature | **100 °C – 150 °C** (at 1–4.7 bar) |
| Cooling water inlet temperature | **25 °C – 35 °C** |
| Cooling water outlet temperature | **40 °C – 60 °C** |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water (h_fg) | **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C |
| Overall HTC (Filmwise) | **5,000 – 11,000 W/m²·K** |
| Overall HTC (Dropwise) | **40,000 – 100,000 W/m²·K** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Filmwise Condensation — Nusselt's Classical Theory

**Wilhelm Nusselt (1916)** published the first rigorous analytical treatment of laminar film condensation on a vertical plate. The assumptions are:

- The vapor is **pure** and **stationary** (no shear at the liquid-vapor interface).
- The liquid film is **laminar** and **smooth**.
- The liquid properties (ρ_l, μ_l, k_l) are **constant** and evaluated at the **mean film temperature**.
- The heat transfer across the film is by **conduction only** (no convection in the film).
- Gravity is the **sole driving force** for film flow.
- The subcooling of the condensate relative to the saturation temperature is **neglected**.

Under these assumptions, Nusselt derived the famous **average heat transfer coefficient** for a vertical plate of height **L**:

$$h_{L} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg} \cdot k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$$

where:
- ρ_l = density of liquid condensate [kg/m³]
- g = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- h_fg = latent heat of vaporization [J/kg]
- k_l = thermal conductivity of liquid [W/m·K]
- μ_l = dynamic viscosity of liquid [Pa·s or kg/m·s]
- L = height of the vertical surface [m]
- ΔT = (T_sat − T_s) = temperature difference between vapor and wall [K]

For condensation on a **vertical bank of N horizontal tubes** of outer diameter **D**, the Nusselt equation is modified to:

$$h_{N} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg} \cdot k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \cdot N \cdot D \cdot \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the exponent 1/4?** This is the heart of the Nusselt result. As condensation proceeds, the film grows thicker (resistance increases), but the film also flows faster (better mixing). These two effects partially cancel, leaving only a **fourth-root dependence** on the physical parameters. That is why heat transfer coefficient is *not* very sensitive to ΔT — a 16-fold change in ΔT only changes h by a factor of 2.

## 2.2 The Physics of Dropwise Condensation

In dropwise condensation, droplets nucleate at preferred sites (microscopic pits, scratches, or surface imperfections) on the surface. The drop population evolves in three stages:

1. **Nucleation & Growth** — Fresh drops form at active sites; their growth is governed by the **conduction of latent heat through the drop itself**.
2. **Coalescence** — Adjacent drops merge, forming larger drops; the area between them becomes exposed to fresh vapor.
3. **Gravity-Driven Departure** — Once the drop radius exceeds a **critical departure radius** (r_crit), gravity overcomes surface adhesion and the drop rolls/sweeps down, sweeping other drops with it, exposing fresh surface.

Because the vapor can directly contact a large fraction of the metal surface at any instant, the thermal resistance is much lower. The **dropwise heat transfer coefficient** is typically modeled using the **Labuntsov correlation** or the **Tanner correlation**, but for KTU lab purposes, an empirical value is computed directly from experimental data.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Dropwise vs. Filmwise Condensation

| Feature | Filmwise Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
|---|---|---|
| **Wetting behavior** | Complete wetting | Non-wetting |
| **Liquid morphology** | Continuous film | Discrete spherical drops |
| **Typical h (W/m²·K)** | 5,000 – 11,000 | 40,000 – 100,000 |
| **Thermal resistance** | Controlled by falling film | Negligible (vapor touches metal) |
| **Surface condition** | Clean, oxidized, hydrophilic | Hydrophobic coating needed |
| **Promoters** | None (natural for water on clean metal) | Oleic acid, stearic acid, PTFE, gold, Teflon |
| **Stability** | Permanent, reproducible | Unstable, hard to sustain long-term |
| **Surface tension contact angle (θ)** | θ < 30° | θ > 90° |
| **Industrial use** | Universal (default design) | Rare (research & specialized) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m_{c} \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Heat absorbed by cooling water |
| 2 | $Q = m_{s} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heat released by condensing steam |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Overall heat transfer rate |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1} / \Delta T_{2})}$ | LMTD for condenser |
| 5 | $h_{L} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} L \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$ | Nusselt's equation (vertical plate) |
| 6 | $h_{N} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} N D \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$ | Nusselt's equation (N horizontal tubes) |
| 7 | $h_{inside} = \dfrac{Nu_{D} \cdot k_{w}}{D_i}$ | Inside water-side HTC (Sieder–Tate) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_{o}} + \dfrac{x_{w}}{k_{w}} + \dfrac{A_{o}}{A_{i} h_{i}}$ | Overall resistance (series) |
| 9 | $Re_{film} = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_{l}}$ | Reynolds number of condensate film |
| 10 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{m_{c}}{P}$ | Mass flow rate per unit wetted perimeter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Re_critical for film condensation:** The laminar-turbulent transition in the condensate film occurs at **Re_film ≈ 1800**. Below this, Nusselt's equation is valid. Above it, the film becomes wavy/turbulent and the HTC increases faster than predicted.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Condensers in thermal & nuclear power plants operate almost exclusively in **filmwise mode** (titanium tubes, design conservatism).
- **Distillation Column Condensers:** The overhead partial condenser in a petroleum refinery handles multicomponent vapors — filmwise is the safe design assumption.
- **Air Conditioning & Refrigeration:** Modern evaporators use **enhanced surfaces** (micro-finned tubes) to mimic dropwise-style enhancement and boost the inside coefficient.
- **Electronics Cooling:** **Dropwise condensation on micro-structured superhydrophobic surfaces** is a hot research area for chip-level thermal management.
- **Desalination:** Multi-Effect Distillation (MED) and Multi-Stage Flash (MSF) plants benefit from **dropwise promoters** to reduce capital cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Nusselt's Equation for Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

### Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a **vertical flat plate** of height **L** and width **W**, maintained at a uniform temperature **T_s**, exposed to a quiescent saturated vapor at temperature **T_sat**. Condensate drains downward under gravity. Let:
- **x** = vertical distance measured from the top of the plate [m]
- **δ(x)** = local film thickness at position x [m]

### Governing Equations

**1. Mass Balance (Continuity):**
The condensate mass flow rate at any cross-section is determined by the total condensation from 0 to x:

$$\dot{m}(x) = W \int_{0}^{x} \dfrac{k_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{\delta(\eta) \cdot h_{fg}} \, d\eta$$

**2. Momentum Balance (Navier-Stokes simplified for thin film):**
The film is thin and slow. Inertial terms are negligible compared to viscous and gravity terms. For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow, the **momentum equation** in the x-direction reduces to:

$$\mu_{l} \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = -\rho_{l} g$$

where y is measured from the wall (0 ≤ y ≤ δ).

**3. Energy Equation:**
Heat transfer is by conduction through the film. The temperature varies linearly across the film (since velocity profile is parabolic and thermal boundary layer fills the film):

$$T(y) = T_{s} + \dfrac{(T_{sat} - T_{s})}{\delta} \cdot y$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Integrate Momentum Equation:**

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = -\dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}}$$

Integrating once with respect to y:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} y + C_{1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Condition (no-slip at wall, zero shear at free surface):**

At y = 0: u = 0 (no-slip)
At y = δ: ∂u/∂y = 0 (zero shear at liquid-vapor interface)

Applying the second BC:

$$0 = -\dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} \delta + C_{1} \implies C_{1} = \dfrac{\rho_{l} g \delta}{\mu_{l}}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} (\delta - y)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again to get velocity profile:**

$$u(y) = \dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} \left( \delta y - \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} \right) + C_{2}$$

Applying u = 0 at y = 0 gives C_2 = 0.

$$u(y) = \dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} \left( \delta y - \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass flow rate per unit width:**

$$\dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \rho_{l} \int_{0}^{\delta} u(y) \, dy = \rho_{l} \int_{0}^{\delta} \dfrac{\rho_{l} g}{\mu_{l}} \left( \delta y - \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} \right) dy$$

$$\dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g}{\mu_{l}} \left[ \delta \cdot \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{y^{3}}{6} \right]_{0}^{\delta} = \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g}{\mu_{l}} \left( \dfrac{\delta^{3}}{2} - \dfrac{\delta^{3}}{6} \right) = \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3 \mu_{l}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Energy Balance (mass of condensate released = heat conducted through film):**

$$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{\dot{m}}{W}\right) \cdot h_{fg} = \dfrac{k_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{\delta}$$

$$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3 \mu_{l}}\right) = \dfrac{k_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{h_{fg} \cdot \delta}$$

$$\dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g}{3 \mu_{l}} \cdot 3 \delta^{2} \dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = \dfrac{k_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{h_{fg} \cdot \delta}$$

$$\dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g \delta^{2}}{\mu_{l}} \dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = \dfrac{k_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{h_{fg} \cdot \delta}$$

$$\delta^{3} \, d\delta = \dfrac{k_{l} \mu_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{\rho_{l}^{2} g \cdot h_{fg}} \, dx$$

**Step 6 — Integrate with boundary condition δ = 0 at x = 0:**

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} \delta^{3} \, d\delta = \int_{0}^{x} \dfrac{k_{l} \mu_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s})}{\rho_{l}^{2} g \cdot h_{fg}} \, dx$$

$$\dfrac{\delta^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{k_{l} \mu_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s}) \cdot x}{\rho_{l}^{2} g \cdot h_{fg}}$$

$$\delta(x) = \left[ \dfrac{4 k_{l} \mu_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s}) \cdot x}{\rho_{l}^{2} g \cdot h_{fg}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the LOCAL heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_{x} = \dfrac{k_{l}}{\delta(x)} = \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{4 \mu_{l} (T_{sat} - T_{s}) \cdot x} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 8 — Average the local coefficient over the plate height (0 to L):**

$$h_{L} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} h_{x} \, dx = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{4 \mu_{l} \Delta T} \right]^{1/4} x^{-1/4} \, dx$$

$$h_{L} = \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{4 \mu_{l} \Delta T} \right]^{1/4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{4}{3} L^{3/4} \right]$$

$$h_{L} = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{4 \mu_{l} \Delta T} \right]^{1/4} \cdot L^{3/4}$$

$$h_{L} = \dfrac{4}{3 \cdot 4^{1/4}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g h_{fg} k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \Delta T \cdot L} \right]^{1/4}$$

Since $\dfrac{4}{3 \cdot 4^{1/4}} = \dfrac{4}{3 \cdot \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{4}{4.2426} \approx 0.943$, we get the famous result:

$$\boxed{\,h_{L} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg} \cdot k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Lab Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a lab experiment, saturated steam at **100 °C** condenses on a vertical copper plate of height **L = 0.30 m** and width **W = 0.10 m**. The plate surface temperature is **90 °C**. Cooling water at **30 °C** flows on the other side. Compute the theoretical average HTC using Nusselt's equation and the rate of condensate collected per minute.

**Given Data (water at mean film temperature T_f = 95 °C):**
- ρ_l = **961.9 kg/m³**
- μ_l = **2.97 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s**
- k_l = **0.679 W/m·K**
- h_fg = **2257 × 10³ J/kg**
- g = **9.81 m/s²**
- ΔT = 100 − 90 = **10 K**

**Step 1 — Apply Nusselt's equation:**

$$h_{L} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{(961.9)^{2} \times 9.81 \times 2257 \times 10^{3} \times (0.679)^{3}}{2.97 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.30 \times 10} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator computation:**

$(961.9)^{2} = 9.252 \times 10^{5}$

$(0.679)^{3} = 0.3131$

$9.252 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 \times 2257 \times 10^{3} \times 0.3131 = 6.434 \times 10^{12}$

**Step 3 — Denominator computation:**

$2.97 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.30 \times 10 = 8.91 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 4 — Ratio:**

$\dfrac{6.434 \times 10^{12}}{8.91 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.222 \times 10^{15}$

**Step 5 — Fourth root:**

$(7.222 \times 10^{15})^{0.25} = (7.222)^{0.25} \times (10^{15})^{0.25} = 1.640 \times 10^{3.75} = 1.640 \times 5623 = 9222$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 0.943:**

$h_{L} = 0.943 \times 9222 = 8696 \approx 8700 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$

**Step 7 — Total heat transfer and condensate rate:**

Heat transfer area: $A = 0.30 \times 0.10 = 0.03 \, \text{m}^{2}$

$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 8700 \times 0.03 \times 10 = 2610 \, \text{W}$

Condensate rate:

$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{2610}{2257 \times 10^{3}} = 1.156 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/s}$

In grams per minute:

$\dot{m} = 1.156 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 60 = 69.4 \, \text{g/min}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This theoretical value (~8700 W/m²K) is the **theoretical filmwise HTC**. The experimentally observed value in the KTU lab will typically be **between 5000 and 12000 W/m²K** depending on tube cleanliness, steam quality, and non-condensable gases present.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Filmwise vs. Dropwise Condensation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VAPOR["SATURATED VAPOR REGION  T = T_sat"]
        V1["Vapor molecules approach cold surface"]
        V2["Release latent heat  h_fg"]
    end

    subgraph FILM["FILMWISE MODE  Wetting  Theta below 30"]
        F1["Continuous liquid film forms on surface"]
        F2["Film thickness  delta grows with x"]
        F3["Heat conducts through film  only path"]
        F4["HTC  h  5000 to 11000 W per m2K"]
    end

    subgraph DROP["DROPWISE MODE  Non-wetting  Theta above 90"]
        D1["Discrete droplets nucleate at sites"]
        D2["Drops grow and coalesce"]
        D3["Vapor contacts metal DIRECTLY between drops"]
        D4["HTC  h  40000 to 100000 W per m2K"]
    end

    subgraph SINK["COOLING MEDIUM REGION  T = T_cool"]
        S1["Heat absorbed by water"]
        S2["Temperature rises  T_ci to T_co"]
    end

    V1 --> F1
    V1 --> D1
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    F4 --> S1
    D4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2

    style VAPOR fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Experimental Setup Block Diagram (KTU Heat Transfer Lab)

```mermaid

    end

    end

    end
```

## 4.3 Film Profile — Mathematical Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Distinguish between **dropwise** and **filmwise** condensation. Which mode gives a higher heat transfer coefficient and why?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Filmwise condensation:** The condensate forms a continuous liquid film over the entire cold surface because the surface is wetted by the liquid (contact angle θ < 30°). Heat must conduct through this film, which is the dominant thermal resistance.
> - **Dropwise condensation:** The condensate does not wet the surface (contact angle θ > 90° due to hydrophobic coating). Discrete droplets form and roll off, exposing fresh metal to vapor at frequent intervals. Most of the surface is in direct contact with vapor.
> - **Dropwise condensation gives a much higher heat transfer coefficient (5–10× greater)** because the liquid film resistance is largely eliminated — vapor can transfer heat directly to the cold metal at the unwetted regions. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the assumptions made by **Nusselt** in deriving the theoretical equation for laminar film condensation on a vertical plate.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. Pure, quiescent (stationary) saturated vapor with no shear at the liquid-vapor interface.
> 2. Laminar, smooth condensate film with constant properties (ρ_l, μ_l, k_l) evaluated at the mean film temperature.
> 3. The only mechanism of heat transfer across the film is **conduction**; no convection within the film.
> 4. Gravity is the only driving force for film flow; the plate is vertical and isothermal.
> 5. The subcooling of the condensate relative to saturation is neglected; the heat of vaporization is constant. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
> **Q. (a)** Derive the **Nusselt equation** for the average heat transfer coefficient during **laminar film condensation** of a pure saturated vapor on a **vertical flat plate**. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
> **Q. (b)** A vertical condenser surface of height **0.5 m** condenses saturated steam at atmospheric pressure. The surface temperature is **80 °C**. Compute (i) the average heat transfer coefficient and (ii) the condensate mass flow rate per meter width. Properties of water at film temperature T_f = 90 °C: ρ_l = 965.3 kg/m³, μ_l = 3.15 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s, k_l = 0.675 W/m·K, h_fg = 2283 × 10³ J/kg. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

[Student must reproduce the **complete 8-step derivation** from Section 3.1 above, covering:]

1. Assumptions (1 mark)
2. Coordinate system and governing differential equations (1 mark)
3. Integration of momentum equation with BCs to get parabolic velocity profile (2 marks)
4. Energy balance giving film thickness expression (1 mark)
5. Local h_x and integration to get h_L (1 mark)
6. Final boxed Nusselt's equation (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- L = 0.5 m
- T_sat = 100 °C (atmospheric pressure)
- T_s = 80 °C
- ΔT = 20 K
- ρ_l = 965.3 kg/m³, μ_l = 3.15 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s, k_l = 0.675 W/m·K, h_fg = 2283 × 10³ J/kg

**Step 1 — Apply Nusselt's equation:**

$$h_{L} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg} \cdot k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$(965.3)^{2} = 9.318 \times 10^{5}$

$(0.675)^{3} = 0.3075$

$N = 9.318 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 \times 2283 \times 10^{3} \times 0.3075 = 6.42 \times 10^{12}$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$D = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 20 = 3.15 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 4 — Ratio and fourth root:**

$\dfrac{N}{D} = 2.038 \times 10^{15}$

$(2.038 \times 10^{15})^{0.25} = 2.124 \times 10^{3.75} = 2.124 \times 5623 = 11944$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 0.943:**

$$h_{L} = 0.943 \times 11944 = \boxed{11263 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{K} \approx 11.26 \, \text{kW/m}^{2}\text{K}}$$

**Step 6 — Condensate rate per meter width:**

At x = L, the local film thickness:

$$\delta(L) = \left[ \dfrac{4 k_{l} \mu_{l} \Delta T \cdot L}{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg}} \right]^{1/4} = \left[ \dfrac{4 \times 0.675 \times 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 \times 0.5}{(965.3)^{2} \times 9.81 \times 2283 \times 10^{3}} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$\delta(L) = \left[ \dfrac{8.505 \times 10^{-3}}{2.087 \times 10^{13}} \right]^{1/4} = (4.075 \times 10^{-16})^{0.25} = 1.421 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.142 \, \text{mm}$$

Mass flow rate per unit width (from Step 4 of derivation):

$$\dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3 \mu_{l}} = \dfrac{(965.3)^{2} \times 9.81 \times (1.421 \times 10^{-4})^{3}}{3 \times 3.15 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \dfrac{9.318 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 \times 2.870 \times 10^{-12}}{9.45 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{2.622 \times 10^{-5}}{9.45 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.775 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kg/m·s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}/W \approx 27.75 \, \text{g/m·s}}$$

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **mechanism of dropwise condensation**. Discuss the role of **promoters** and state **two practical difficulties** in maintaining dropwise condensation in industrial condensers. **(7 marks)**
> **Q. (b)** In a KTU lab experiment on a horizontal tube condenser of **outer diameter 25 mm** and **length 0.5 m**, saturated steam at **1.2 bar** condenses on the outside while cooling water flows inside. The outside surface temperature is **115 °C**, the condensate flow rate is **3.5 × 10⁻³ kg/s**, and the inlet/outlet water temperatures are **30 °C** and **50 °C** respectively. Calculate (i) the overall heat transfer coefficient **U** and (ii) the average outside HTC using Nusselt's equation for a single horizontal tube, and compare the experimental h_o with the theoretical Nusselt value. Properties: ρ_l = 943 kg/m³, μ_l = 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s, k_l = 0.68 W/m·K, h_fg = 2201 × 10³ J/kg. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Dropwise Condensation (7 marks):**

- **Mechanism (3 marks):** Vapor approaches a cold, hydrophobic surface. Because the surface cannot be wetted (contact angle θ > 90°), the condensate does not spread. Instead, it forms **discrete spherical droplets** at preferred nucleation sites (microscopic surface defects, scratches, cavities). As the droplets grow by condensation, they **coalesce** with neighboring drops. When a drop reaches the **critical departure diameter** (set by gravity vs. surface tension), it rolls/slides down the surface, sweeping other drops with it. Fresh metal is exposed, and the cycle repeats. Most of the metal area is in **direct contact with the vapor**, so thermal resistance is very low.

- **Role of promoters (2 marks):** Promoters are **hydrophobic coatings** such as **oleic acid, stearic acid, Teflon (PTFE), gold, or silver** applied in a thin layer. They raise the contact angle above 90° and **prevent the condensate from spreading** into a film, thereby forcing dropwise behavior. They may also be mixed with steam in trace amounts (e.g., **Promoter 70**).

- **Two practical difficulties (2 marks):**
  1. **Lack of long-term stability:** Hydrophobic coatings degrade, oxidize, erode, or get washed away by high-velocity condensate flow, after which the system reverts to filmwise mode.
  2. **Contamination by non-condensable gases (air, CO₂):** Even trace amounts inhibit the dropwise mechanism and drastically reduce h. Industrial systems cannot easily exclude such gases.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- D = 0.025 m, L = 0.5 m, N = 1 tube
- T_sat = 104.8 °C (at 1.2 bar)
- T_s = 115 °C? (This is inconsistent — assume T_s < T_sat; let T_s = **95 °C** so ΔT = 104.8 − 95 = **9.8 K**)
- m_s = 3.5 × 10⁻³ kg/s
- T_ci = 30 °C, T_co = 50 °C
- m_c assumed from heat balance, properties of water at film temperature.

**Step 1 — Total heat transfer (from steam side):**

$Q = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot h_{fg} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2201 \times 10^{3} = 7703.5 \, \text{W} \approx 7.70 \, \text{kW}$

**Step 2 — Log mean temperature difference (condenser):**

$\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{ci} = 104.8 - 30 = 74.8 \, °C$

$\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{co} = 104.8 - 50 = 54.8 \, °C$

$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{74.8 - 54.8}{\ln(74.8/54.8)} = \dfrac{20.0}{\ln(1.365)} = \dfrac{20.0}{0.3113} = 64.24 \, °C$

**Step 3 — Outside heat transfer area:**

$A_o = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.025 \times 0.5 = 0.03927 \, \text{m}^{2}$

**Step 4 — Overall HTC (U based on outside area):**

$U_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{7703.5}{0.03927 \times 64.24} = \dfrac{7703.5}{2.523} = \boxed{3053 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}$

**Step 5 — Nusselt's theoretical h_o (single horizontal tube, N = 1):**

$$h_{o,th} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{l}^{2} \cdot g \cdot h_{fg} \cdot k_{l}^{3}}{\mu_{l} \cdot N \cdot D \cdot \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator: $(943)^{2} \times 9.81 \times 2201 \times 10^{3} \times (0.68)^{3}$

$= 889,249 \times 9.81 \times 2201 \times 10^{3} \times 0.3144$

$= 6.04 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $2.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 \times 0.025 \times 9.8 = 5.88 \times 10^{-5}$

Ratio: $6.04 \times 10^{12} / 5.88 \times 10^{-5} = 1.027 \times 10^{17}$

Fourth root: $(1.027 \times 10^{17})^{0.25} = 5.66 \times 10^{4}$

$h_{o,th} = 0.725 \times 56,600 = \boxed{41,035 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}$

**Step 6 — Experimental h_o from overall resistance:**

Assuming the wall resistance and inside resistance are small (clean copper tube, water velocity moderate), 1/U_o ≈ 1/h_o (approx). So:

$h_{o,exp} \approx U_o = 3053 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$

**Step 7 — Comparison & Comment:**

Experimental h_o (3053 W/m²K) is **much lower** than Nusselt's theoretical value (41,035 W/m²K). This large discrepancy is expected in KTU lab conditions and is attributed to:
- Presence of **non-condensable gases** (air leaks)
- **Fouling** of the outer surface
- **Vapor velocity** effects ignored by Nusselt
- **Subcooling** of condensate
- **Turbulence / rippling** of actual film (which can either raise or lower h)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in μ_l:** Many students write μ_l in **cP (centipoise)** but plug it into a SI-formula expecting Pa·s. **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s.** A 1000× error silently propagates through the 1/4 power and destroys the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.943 / 0.725 coefficient** in Nusselt's equation: this is a **derivation artifact**, not optional. Dropping it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using steam-side ΔT (T_sat − T_s) inconsistently:** Some students mix LMTD with simple ΔT. KTU strictly wants LMTD for the overall U, and the **local ΔT = T_sat − T_s** for the Nusselt correlation.
> 4. **Confusing inside vs. outside area** in U calculation. Always state "U based on outside area" or "U based on inside area" explicitly.
> 5. **Averaging the wrong temperature** for properties: Nusselt requires **mean film temperature**, T_film = (T_sat + T_s)/2 — NOT the cooling-water temperature.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Drop and Filmwise Condensation**

- ✅ Condensation is a **latent-heat-driven** phase change; the vapor must lose h_fg to a cold surface.
- ✅ **Filmwise mode** is the **default** for pure vapors on clean, wettable metals (steel, copper, brass).
- ✅ **Dropwise mode** requires **hydrophobic surface treatment** (oleic acid, PTFE, gold).
- ✅ **Dropwise h is 5–10× higher** than filmwise h — but it is **hard to maintain** in industry.
- ✅ **Nusselt's equation (vertical plate):** $h_L = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l^{2} g h_{fg} k_l^{3}}{\mu_l L \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$
- ✅ **Nusselt's equation (N horizontal tubes):** $h_N = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l^{2} g h_{fg} k_l^{3}}{\mu_l N D \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$
- ✅ Nusselt's assumptions: pure, quiescent vapor; laminar film; constant properties; conduction-only in film; gravity-driven flow; no subcooling.
- ✅ Properties must be evaluated at the **mean film temperature** T_f = (T_sat + T_s)/2.
- ✅ Critical Reynolds number for film transition: **Re_crit ≈ 1800**; laminar regime valid below this.
- ✅ LMTD (not simple ΔT) is used in the overall heat transfer equation: $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$.
- ✅ **Non-condensable gases** (air, CO₂) are the **enemy of condensation** — they blanket the surface and reduce h by an order of magnitude. Steam supply lines must be properly vented in KTU lab experiments.
- ✅ Dropwise condensation h is **highly empirical** — use the **experimental value** computed from $\dot{m}_{cond} \cdot h_{fg} / (A \cdot \Delta T)$, not a theoretical correlation.
- ✅ In KTU lab viva, expect questions on: contact angle, Reynolds number of the film, why experimental h differs from theoretical, and the role of **promoters** like oleic acid.

---

**END OF NOTES — Module Experiment 2: Drop and Filmwise Condensation**
