<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Modes of Heat Transfer — KTU 2024 Syllabus Framework

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Heat & Mass Transfer Operations Lab, the practical manual dedicates Module Experiment 2 to experiments that map directly onto the three fundamental **modes of heat transfer** governed by the laws of thermodynamics and transport phenomena. Each experiment isolates one (or a combination) of these modes and may either involve **sensible heating (no phase change)** or **latent heat exchange (with phase change)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):**
> *Heat transfer is the thermal energy in transit due to a temperature gradient, occurring by **Conduction** (molecular/atomic energy transfer through a stationary medium), **Convection** (energy transfer by bulk fluid motion coupled with molecular diffusion), and **Radiation** (energy emission as electromagnetic waves from a body at non-zero absolute temperature). Phase change refers to the physical transition of a substance between solid, liquid, and gaseous states, accompanied by the absorption or release of **latent heat**.*

### 1.2 The Three Modes — Quick Cognitive Map

| Mode | Carrier | Driving Potential | Phase Change Capable? |
|------|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Conduction | Phonons / free electrons | Temperature gradient $\nabla T$ | Indirectly (e.g., solidification) |
| Convection | Bulk fluid motion | $\Delta T$ between surface and fluid | Yes (boiling, condensation) |
| Radiation | Electromagnetic waves | $T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann) | No direct phase change |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A First-Year Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hot Tea Mug Story 🍵**
> Imagine holding a ceramic mug of hot tea on a cold morning.
> 1. **Conduction (without phase change):** Heat travels from the hot tea, through the ceramic wall, into your palm. The liquid is *sensible* heating up your hand — no steam, no ice.
> 2. **Convection (without phase change):** The air just above the tea surface rises as it warms, and cooler air sinks to replace it. This is **natural convection** — sensible heat carried by a circulating fluid.
> 3. **Radiation (without phase change):** Even in a vacuum, you can feel the warmth of the sun on your face. No medium, no fluid — pure electromagnetic energy.
> 4. **With Phase Change:** When the tea is boiling ($\approx 100°\text{C}$), liquid water converts to steam. The heat absorbed is **latent heat of vaporization** — temperature stays constant, but energy flows in massively. This is phase-change heat transfer at its classic best.

## 1.4 Mapping Experiments to the KTU Module 2 Syllabus

The KTU 2024 lab syllabus for Module Experiment 2 lists a cluster of canonical experiments, each probing a specific mode. The instructor may assign any of the following (or equivalent) experiments:

**Experiments WITHOUT Phase Change (Sensible Heat Transfer):**
- **A. Composite Wall / Lagged Pipe Apparatus** — Pure conduction, steady-state, 1-D heat flow through series thermal resistances.
- **B. Natural Convection Apparatus** — Free convection from a horizontal/vertical heated surface in air.
- **C. Forced Convection / Heat Exchanger (Single-Pass)** — Energy transfer between two sensible fluids with no phase transition.
- **D. Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation Apparatus** — Verification of $q = \sigma (T_s^4 - T_sur^4)$ using a black-body disc.

**Experiments WITH Phase Change (Latent Heat Transfer):**
- **E. Boiling Heat Transfer / Pool Boiling Apparatus** — Measurement of HTC during nucleate and film boiling.
- **F. Condensation Heat Transfer (Dropwise & Filmwise)** — Nusselt's theory of film condensation on a vertical plate.
- **G. Shell-and-Tube Condenser / Evaporator** — Latent heat exchange with refrigerant or steam.

> [!TIP]
> **For Exam Strategy:** KTU examiners often pick **two questions** — one without phase change (typically composite wall or natural convection) and one with phase change (typically condensation). Prepare both types thoroughly.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Flagged)

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Reference temperature for property data:** typically **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$
- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Latent heat of fusion of ice at 0 °C:** $h_{if} = 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Experiment A — Composite Wall / Lagged Pipe (Conduction, No Phase Change)

### 2.1.1 Theory in Plain Language

A **composite wall** consists of two or more layers of different materials sandwiched together. Heat flows from the high-temperature side to the low-temperature side by **steady-state, one-dimensional conduction**. The thermal resistance concept (analogous to electrical resistance) simplifies the analysis dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Resistance Analogy:** Just as electrical current $I$ flows through resistors in series $R_{elec}$ due to a voltage difference $V$, heat flow rate $Q$ flows through thermal resistances $R_{th}$ in series due to a temperature difference $\Delta T$:
> $$I = \frac{V}{R_{elec}} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Theory

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law of Conduction (1-D steady state):**
For a single homogeneous slab of thickness $L$, area $A$, thermal conductivity $k$:
$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal Resistance of a Single Slab:**
$$R_{th,single} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad [\text{K/W}]$$

**Step 3 — Resistances in Series (composite wall):**
For $n$ layers in series, total resistance is the **sum**:
$$R_{th,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$$

**Step 4 — Overall Heat Transfer Rate:**
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{th,total}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interface Temperatures (Why?):**
To find the temperature at the interface between layer 1 and layer 2:
$$T_{interface} = T_1 - Q \cdot R_{th,1} = T_1 - \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}$$

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

Composite wall analysis underpins the design of **building insulation** (brick + air gap + plaster), **cryogenic storage tanks** (multi-layer vacuum insulation), **furnace walls** (refractory + insulation), and **refrigerator walls**. The same series-resistance principle is the foundation for **heat exchanger design** by the LMTD method.

---

## 2.2 Experiment B — Natural Convection from a Heated Vertical Plate (No Phase Change)

### 2.2.1 Theory in Plain Language

When a vertical plate is heated to a temperature $T_s$ and exposed to quiescent air at $T_\infty$, the air near the plate warms, becomes less dense, and **rises by buoyancy**. This bulk motion transports heat away from the plate — this is **free (natural) convection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Analysis Outcome:** The convective heat transfer coefficient $h_c$ is correlated using two dimensionless groups:
> - **Nusselt number:** $Nu = \frac{h_c L}{k_f}$ (ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance)
> - **Grashof number:** $Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ (ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces)
> - **Prandtl number:** $Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k_f} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$ (fluid thermophysical property)

### 2.2.2 Governing Correlation (Laminar, Vertical Plate, 10⁴ < Gr·Pr < 10⁹)

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.59 \, (Gr_L \cdot Pr)^{1/4}$$

For turbulent regime ($Gr_L \cdot Pr > 10^9$):
$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.10 \, (Gr_L \cdot Pr)^{1/3}$$

From $Nu$, the convective heat transfer coefficient:
$$h_c = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k_f}{L}$$

Heat transfer rate:
$$Q = h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

---

## 2.3 Experiment C — Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation Apparatus (No Phase Change)

### 2.3.1 Theory in Plain Language

A heated **black disc** radiates energy to a cooler surrounding (water-cooled jacket acting as a black-body sink). The **Stefan-Boltzmann law** quantifies this:
$$q = \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

For a **grey body** (emissivity $\varepsilon$):
$$q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

### 2.3.2 Disc Calibration

The test disc has known emissivity. The radiation received by the water-cooled jacket raises the temperature of circulating water at a measured flow rate. The energy balance:
$$Q_{radiation} = m c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Setting $Q_{radiation} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ allows determination of $\varepsilon$ (or verification of $\sigma$).

---

## 2.4 Experiment D — Condensation on a Vertical Plate (WITH Phase Change)

### 2.4.1 Theory in Plain Language

When **saturated vapour** contacts a cold surface, it condenses to liquid, releasing its **latent heat of condensation** $h_{fg}$. If the condensate wets the surface, a **continuous liquid film** forms — this is **film condensation** (Nusselt's classical theory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nusselt's Film Condensation Theory (1916):** Assumes laminar wavy film, constant properties, vapour at rest, negligible inertia in the film.

### 2.4.2 Working Equation (Nusselt)

For a vertical plate of height $L$:
$$\overline{h}_c = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, (T_{sat} - T_s) \, L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Or equivalently in Nusselt form:
$$\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{\overline{h}_c L}{k_l} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, L^3}{\mu_l \, k_l \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

Heat transfer rate:
$$Q = \overline{h}_c A (T_{sat} - T_s)$$

### 2.4.3 Reynolds Number of Condensate Film

A more physically meaningful parameter:
$$Re_{film} = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\mu_l P} = \frac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_l}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the mass flow rate per unit wetted perimeter and $P$ is the perimeter.

> [!TIP]
> **Nusselt's theory predicts the condensate mass flow rate** as:
> $$\dot{m} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, A^2 \, k_l^3 \, \Delta T}{4 \mu_l L}\right]^{1/4}$$
> This is the **fundamental working formula** for the lab's condensation experiment.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside tables — do NOT use `|`.**

| Experiment Type | Working Formula | Variables / Notes |
|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Conduction (single slab) | $Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $k$ in W/mK, $L$ in m, $A$ in m² |
| Thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA}$ | Analogous to $\Omega/V$ in Ohm's law |
| Composite wall (series) | $Q = \frac{\Delta T}{\sum L_i/(k_i A)}$ | Steady-state, 1-D, constant $A$ |
| Interface temperature | $T_{int} = T_1 - Q \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1 A}$ | Use cumulative resistance from hot side |
| Natural convection (laminar) | $h_c = \frac{0.59 \, k_f}{L} (Gr_L Pr)^{1/4}$ | Valid for $10^4 < GrPr < 10^9$ |
| Natural convection (turbulent) | $h_c = \frac{0.10 \, k_f}{L} (Gr_L Pr)^{1/3}$ | Valid for $GrPr > 10^9$ |
| Forced convection (Dittus-Boelter) | $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} Pr^{n}$ | $n = 0.4$ heating, $0.3$ cooling |
| Stefan-Boltzmann | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| Nusselt condensation (vertical plate) | $\bar{h}_c = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l \Delta T L}\right]^{1/4}$ | Laminar film, $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s$ |
| Boiling (Rohsenow correlation) | $q = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\frac{c_{pl} \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n}\right]^3$ | $C_{sf}$ and $n$ depend on surface-fluid pair |
| Latent heat balance | $Q = \dot{m} h_{fg}$ | Mass flow $\dot{m}$ in kg/s |
| Sensible heat balance | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Used in water-cooled radiation jacket |
| Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Heat exchanger design |

---

## 2.6 The 'Why' Behind Each Concept — Engineering Rationale

> [!TIP]
> **Why study conduction experiments?** Because the **insulation thickness** of every reactor, pipe, and storage tank in chemical plants is sized by $Q = kA\Delta T / L$ economics — too thin wastes energy, too thick wastes capital.
>
> **Why study convection correlations?** Because predicting $h_c$ in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger is the difference between a safe design and a runaway reaction.
>
> **Why study condensation?** Because **condensers are the workhorse of distillation columns** — under-design causes column flooding, over-design is a costly mistake.
>
> **Why study radiation?** Because **fired heaters, furnaces, and high-temperature reactors** lose significant energy through radiation — ignoring it leads to under-sized fuel systems.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Composite Wall (Series Thermal Resistance)

### 3.1.1 Setup
A composite wall consists of three layers in series:
- **Layer 1:** Mild steel, $L_1$, $k_1$
- **Layer 2:** Asbestos insulation, $L_2$, $k_2$
- **Layer 3:** Magnesia, $L_3$, $k_3$

Hot face temperature $T_1$ (inner surface) and cold face temperature $T_4$ (outer surface) are measured. The wall area is $A$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation from First Principles

At **steady state**, the heat flow rate $Q$ through each layer must be identical (energy conservation — no accumulation).

**Layer 1 (Fourier's Law):**
$$Q = k_1 A \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L_1}$$

Rearranging:
$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A} = Q R_{1}$$

**Layer 2:**
$$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A} = Q R_{2}$$

**Layer 3:**
$$T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A} = Q R_{3}$$

**Sum the three equations:**
$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) = Q R_{1} + Q R_{2} + Q R_{3}$$

The left side telescopes:
$$T_1 - T_4 = Q (R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{3})$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{3}} = \frac{(T_1 - T_4)}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A}}}$$

### 3.1.3 Verification — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $A = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$ (wall area)
- Layer 1 (M.S.): $L_1 = 0.006 \text{ m}$, $k_1 = 45 \text{ W/mK}$
- Layer 2 (Asbestos): $L_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $k_2 = 0.12 \text{ W/mK}$
- Layer 3 (Magnesia): $L_3 = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $k_3 = 0.07 \text{ W/mK}$
- $T_1 = 150°\text{C} = 423 \text{ K}$, $T_4 = 40°\text{C} = 313 \text{ K}$

**Step 1 — Compute individual resistances:**

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.006}{45 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.006}{11.25} = 5.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.05}{0.12 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.05}{0.03} = 1.6667 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.025}{0.07 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.025}{0.0175} = 1.4286 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.000533 + 1.6667 + 1.4286 = 3.0958 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Overall heat flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{423 - 313}{3.0958} = \frac{110}{3.0958} = 35.53 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Interface temperatures (validity check):**

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q R_1 = 423 - 35.53 \times 0.000533 = 423 - 0.0189 \approx 422.98 \text{ K}$$

$$T_3 = T_4 + Q R_3 = 313 + 35.53 \times 1.4286 = 313 + 50.76 = 363.76 \text{ K}$$

Check: $T_2 - T_3 = 422.98 - 363.76 = 59.22$ K; compare with $QR_2 = 35.53 \times 1.6667 = 59.22$ K ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The mild steel layer has negligible thermal resistance (high $k$). The insulation (asbestos + magnesia) dominates — this is the engineering principle behind why insulation selection matters far more than the structural wall material.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Nusselt's Film Condensation

### 3.2.1 Physical Model
A vertical plate at temperature $T_s$ is exposed to saturated vapour at $T_{sat}$. A liquid film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows downward under gravity. The film is laminar; flow is driven by gravity and retarded by viscosity.

### 3.2.2 Momentum Balance (Navier-Stokes simplification)

For steady, fully-developed laminar film flow in the $x$ direction (downward), the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:
$$\mu_l \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = \rho_l g$$

(The pressure gradient is zero, and the $x$-component of gravity is $g$.)

**First integration** (boundary condition: at $y = \delta$, $du/dy = 0$ — no shear at liquid-vapour interface):
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} (\delta - y)$$

**Second integration** (boundary condition: at $y = 0$, $u = 0$ — no-slip at plate):
$$u(y) = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

### 3.2.3 Volumetric Flow Rate Per Unit Width

$$\dot{V}/w = \int_0^{\delta} u \, dy = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} \int_0^{\delta} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

Evaluating:
$$\dot{V}/w = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} \left[\frac{\delta^3}{2} - \frac{\delta^3}{6}\right] = \frac{\rho_l g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}$$

Therefore, **mass flow per unit width** $\Gamma = \rho_l \dot{V}/w$:
$$\Gamma = \frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}$$

### 3.2.4 Energy Balance — Heat Transfer Through the Film

Heat conducted through the film at position $x$ (per unit width):
$$q_x = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)}$$

Mass flow rate at position $x$ (incremental accumulation of condensate from $0$ to $x$):
$$\Gamma(x) = \int_0^x \frac{q_{x'}}{h_{fg}} \, dx' = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}} \int_0^x \frac{dx'}{\delta(x')}$$

For Nusselt's assumption that the film temperature is **linear** across $\delta$ (i.e., $\delta$ is small enough that $T$ varies linearly with $y$):
$$\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3(x)}{3 \mu_l}$$

Equating both expressions for $\Gamma(x)$:
$$\frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3(x)}{3 \mu_l} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}} \int_0^x \frac{dx'}{\delta(x')}$$

Solving this integral equation gives the film thickness profile:
$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu_l k_l (T_{sat} - T_s) x}{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

### 3.2.5 Local and Average Heat Transfer Coefficients

**Local heat flux at $x$:**
$$q_x = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3 (T_{sat} - T_s)^3}{4 \mu_l x}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Local HTC:**
$$h_x = \frac{q_x}{T_{sat} - T_s} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{4 \mu_l x (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Average HTC over the plate height $L$** (integrating $h_x$ from $0$ to $L$ and dividing by $L$):
$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{4}{3} h_{x=L}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 0.943 constant** comes from $\frac{4}{3} \times 4^{-1/4} = \frac{4}{3} \times 0.7071 = 0.9428$. This is the **Nusselt classical result** that the KTU examiner expects.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Condensation Heat Transfer

**Given:**
- Saturated steam at $T_{sat} = 100°\text{C} = 373 \text{ K}$ condenses on a vertical plate at $T_s = 80°\text{C} = 353 \text{ K}$
- Plate height: $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$, width: $w = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- Water film properties at film temperature $T_f = (373+353)/2 = 363 \text{ K}$:
  - $\rho_l = 967 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - $\mu_l = 3.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·s/m}^2$
  - $k_l = 0.677 \text{ W/mK}$
  - $h_{fg} = 2305 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (steam at 100°C)

**Step 1 — Driving temperature difference:**
$$\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s = 373 - 353 = 20 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the bracketed quantity:**

$$\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l \Delta T \, L} = \frac{(967)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2305 \times 10^3 \times (0.677)^3}{3.13 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 \times 0.5}$$

Numerator:
$$(9.35 \times 10^5) \times 9.81 \times (2.305 \times 10^6) \times (3.10 \times 10^{-1})$$
$$= 9.35 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 2.305 \times 10^6 \times 0.310$$
$$= 9.17 \times 10^6 \times 2.305 \times 10^6 \times 0.310 = 6.55 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:
$$3.13 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 \times 0.5 = 3.13 \times 10^{-3}$$

Ratio:
$$= \frac{6.55 \times 10^{12}}{3.13 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.092 \times 10^{15}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nusselt correlation:**

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times (2.092 \times 10^{15})^{1/4}$$

$$(2.092 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = (2092 \times 10^{12})^{1/4} = (2092)^{1/4} \times 10^3$$

$$(2092)^{1/4} \approx 6.78$$

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times 6.78 \times 10^3 = 6394 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \approx 6394 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer rate:**

Area: $A = L \times w = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2$

$$Q = \bar{h}_L \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 6394 \times 0.15 \times 20 = 19182 \text{ W} \approx 19.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Condensate mass flow rate (sanity check):**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{19182}{2305 \times 10^3} = 8.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 29.95 \text{ kg/h}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** A typical lab condenser produces 20–30 kg/h of condensate per 0.15 m² plate — our value is in the correct order of magnitude. The 0.943 Nusselt constant is dimensionally consistent.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Observation Table (Composite Wall)

A KTU lab record must contain a properly formatted observation table. Here is the **gold-standard template** for a Composite Wall experiment:

| Sl. No. | Heater Voltage $V$ [V] | Heater Current $I$ [A] | Heat input $Q = VI$ [W] | $T_1$ [°C] | $T_2$ [°C] | $T_3$ [°C] | $T_4$ [°C] | $\Delta T_{overall}$ [K] | $R_{total}$ [K/W] | $k_{effective}$ [W/mK] |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|--------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | 40 | 1.2 | 48.0 | 145 | 144 | 60 | 42 | 103 | 2.146 | 0.186 |
| 2 | 50 | 1.4 | 70.0 | 162 | 161 | 65 | 43 | 119 | 1.700 | 0.235 |
| 3 | 60 | 1.6 | 96.0 | 178 | 177 | 70 | 45 | 133 | 1.385 | 0.289 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective thermal conductivity** is calculated from $Q = k_{eff} A \Delta T / L_{total}$. Comparing the calculated $k_{eff}$ with literature values validates the experiment.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Composite Wall Apparatus — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Heater Unit - 0 to 250V Variac] --> node2[Heater Rod inserted in copper block]
    node2 --> node3[Composite Wall Test Section - MS, Asbestos, Magnesia]
    node3 --> node4[Cooling Water Inlet at Cold Face]
    node4 --> node5[Thermocouples T1 T2 T3 T4 embedded at interfaces]
    node5 --> node6[Digital Temperature Indicator - 4 channel]
    node6 --> node7[Data Recording and Resistance Calculation]
    node1 --> node8[Voltmeter and Ammeter for Heat Input Q]
    node8 --> node9[Compare Q_electrical with Q_Fourier]
    node9 --> node10[Plot T vs distance and compute k_effective]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**
- The **heater (Variac + heater rod)** injects a known electrical energy $Q_{elec} = VI$ into the wall.
- **Thermocouples T1–T4** measure interface and surface temperatures.
- The **Fourier conduction equation** is applied across the series resistances to predict the heat flow $Q_{Fourier}$.
- Comparison of $Q_{elec}$ vs $Q_{Fourier}$ validates the experiment (they should agree within ±5%).

## 4.4 Experimental Procedure — Decision Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Experiment] --> check1{Check all Thermocouples calibrated?}
    check1 -- No --> cal[Recalibrate using Ice Bath and Boiling Water]
    cal --> check1

    set --> wait1[Wait for Steady State - 30 to 45 min]
    wait1 --> record[Record V, I, T1, T2, T3, T4, m_dot, T_in, T_out]
    record --> calc[Calculate Q_input, R_th, k_eff, h_c, Nu]
```

## 4.5 Phase Diagram — Mode of Heat Transfer Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Transfer Experiment] --> B{Is there a phase change?}
    B -- No Sensible --> C{Mode of Transfer}
    B -- Yes Latent --> D{Boiling or Condensation}
    C -- Conduction --> E[Composite Wall - Lagged Pipe - Slab]
    C -- Convection --> F{Natural or Forced}
    C -- Radiation --> G[Stefan Boltzmann Disc]
    F -- Natural --> H[Vertical or Horizontal Plate]
    F -- Forced --> I[Heat Exchanger - Pipe Flow]
    D -- Boiling --> J[Pool Boiling - Nucleate and Film]
    D -- Condensation --> K[Film or Dropwise on Plate]
    E --> L[Apply Fourier Law and Series Resistance]
    H --> M[Apply Nu = 0.59 GrPr power 1/4]
    I --> N[Apply Dittus Boelter Nu = 0.023 Re power 0.8 Pr power n]
    G --> O[Apply q = epsilon sigma T_s power 4 minus T_sur power 4]
    J --> P[Apply Rohsenow Correlation]
    K --> Q[Apply Nusselt h_c = 0.943 bracket term power 1/4]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Differentiate between the three modes of heat transfer with one practical example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Mechanism | Practical Example |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Conduction** | Transfer of thermal energy through a medium by molecular vibration and free-electron drift, without bulk motion of the medium. | Heat flowing through the metal wall of a pressure vessel from hot process gas to the outside atmosphere. |
| **Convection** | Combined effect of conduction and bulk fluid motion; energy is carried by the moving fluid. | Cooling of a reactor jacket by recirculating chilled water. |
| **Radiation** | Emission of electromagnetic waves from a body at $T > 0$ K; does not require a medium. | Solar energy reaching the Earth through the vacuum of space. |

> [!NOTE]
> Conduction and convection require a medium; radiation does not. This is the KTU-expected **single-line distinction** worth 1 mark, with examples worth 2 marks.

---

### Q2. Define the term *Nusselt number* and state its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**
The Nusselt number is a dimensionless heat transfer coefficient defined as:
$$Nu = \frac{h L}{k}$$

**Physical Significance:** $Nu$ represents the **ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance** at a fluid-solid interface.
- $Nu = 1$: pure conduction (no convection enhancement).
- $Nu \gg 1$: convection dominates; higher $Nu$ means more efficient convective heat removal.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Without Phase Change

**(a)** A furnace wall is constructed of **0.10 m of fireclay brick** ($k = 1.40 \text{ W/mK}$), followed by **0.08 m of insulating brick** ($k = 0.35 \text{ W/mK}$), and finally **0.05 m of red brick** ($k = 0.70 \text{ W/mK}$). The inner surface temperature is $1200°\text{C}$ and the outer surface temperature is $80°\text{C}$. The wall area is $20 \text{ m}^2$.

Calculate:
1. The total thermal resistance of the wall. **(3 marks)**
2. The rate of heat loss through the wall. **(3 marks)**
3. The temperature at the interface between fireclay and insulating brick. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** A horizontal plate at $90°\text{C}$ is exposed to ambient air at $30°\text{C}$ through natural convection. The plate dimensions are $0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.4 \text{ m}$. Using the appropriate correlation for **natural convection from a horizontal plate (heated, facing upward)**, determine the convective heat transfer coefficient and the heat loss rate. Take air properties at film temperature $T_f = 60°\text{C}$:
- $k_f = 0.028 \text{ W/mK}$, $\nu = 1.89 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $Pr = 0.72$, $\beta = 1/333 \text{ K}^{-1}$.

**Correlation:** $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.54 (Gr_L Pr)^{1/4}$ for $10^4 < GrPr < 10^7$, characteristic length $L = A_s/P$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Composite Wall

**Step 1 — Total Thermal Resistance:**

For a series composite wall with equal area $A = 20 \text{ m}^2$:

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{1.40 \times 20} = \frac{0.10}{28} = 3.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.08}{0.35 \times 20} = \frac{0.08}{7.0} = 11.429 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.05}{0.70 \times 20} = \frac{0.05}{14.0} = 3.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = 3.571 \times 10^{-3} + 11.429 \times 10^{-3} + 3.571 \times 10^{-3} = 18.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Heat Loss Rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{1200 - 80}{18.571 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1120}{0.018571} = 60,310 \text{ W} \approx 60.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Interface Temperature (Fireclay | Insulating):**

$$T_{int} = T_{inner} - Q R_1 = 1200 - 60310 \times 3.571 \times 10^{-3} = 1200 - 215.4 = 984.6°\text{C}$$

(Convert to K if needed: $T_{int} = 1257.6$ K.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verify by checking the total drop:** $T_{int} - T_{outer} = 984.6 - 80 = 904.6$ K; the heat through $R_2 + R_3$ should equal $Q$: $60310 \times (11.429 + 3.571) \times 10^{-3} = 60310 \times 0.015 = 904.6$ K ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Natural Convection from a Horizontal Plate

**Step 1 — Characteristic Length:**

For a horizontal plate, the characteristic length is:
$$L = \frac{A_s}{P} = \frac{L_p \times W_p}{2(L_p + W_p)} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.4}{2(0.4 + 0.4)} = \frac{0.16}{1.6} = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Grashof Number:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ K}$$

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times (1/333) \times 60 \times (0.10)^3}{(1.89 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 1.768 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $(1.89 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.572 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Gr_L = \frac{1.768 \times 10^{-3}}{3.572 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.95 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Check Regime and Compute Nu:**

$$Gr_L \cdot Pr = 4.95 \times 10^6 \times 0.72 = 3.56 \times 10^6 \quad \text{(laminar range, valid)}$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.54 (Gr_L Pr)^{1/4} = 0.54 \times (3.56 \times 10^6)^{1/4}$$

$$(3.56 \times 10^6)^{1/4} = (3.56)^{1/4} \times 10^{1.5} \approx 1.376 \times 31.62 = 43.5$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.54 \times 43.5 = 23.5$$

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$h_c = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k_f}{L} = \frac{23.5 \times 0.028}{0.10} = 6.58 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Loss Rate:**

Area: $A = 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$

$$Q = h_c A \Delta T = 6.58 \times 0.16 \times 60 = 63.2 \text{ W}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — With Phase Change

**(a)** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure condenses on a **vertical plate of height 0.6 m and width 0.3 m**, maintained at $75°\text{C}$. Using **Nusselt's film condensation theory**, calculate:
1. The average heat transfer coefficient. **(3 marks)**
2. The total heat transfer rate. **(2 marks)**
3. The condensate mass flow rate per hour. **(2 marks)**

Given water film properties at $T_f = (100+75)/2 = 87.5°\text{C}$:
- $\rho_l = 968 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $k_l = 0.677 \text{ W/mK}$, $\mu_l = 3.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·s/m}^2$, $h_{fg} = 2297 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**(b)** A radiation heat transfer experiment uses a **black disc of diameter 50 mm** heated to $T_s = 500 \text{ K}$ in a water-cooled enclosure at $T_{sur} = 300 \text{ K}$. Cooling water flows at $0.05 \text{ kg/s}$, entering at $25°\text{C}$ and leaving at $35°\text{C}$. The Stefan-Boltzmann constant is $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. Calculate:
1. The heat absorbed by the cooling water. **(2 marks)**
2. The theoretical radiation heat loss from the disc. **(2 marks)**
3. The **effective emissivity** of the disc. **(3 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Nusselt Film Condensation

**Step 1 — Apply Nusselt's Equation:**

$$\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s = 100 - 75 = 25 \text{ K}$$

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l \Delta T L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Compute the bracketed term:
- $\rho_l^2 = (968)^2 = 9.37 \times 10^5$
- $g h_{fg} = 9.81 \times 2297 \times 10^3 = 2.254 \times 10^7$
- $k_l^3 = (0.677)^3 = 0.3102$
- Numerator: $9.37 \times 10^5 \times 2.254 \times 10^7 \times 0.3102 = 6.55 \times 10^{12}$
- Denominator: $\mu_l \Delta T L = 3.24 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 0.6 = 4.86 \times 10^{-3}$
- Ratio: $6.55 \times 10^{12} / 4.86 \times 10^{-3} = 1.348 \times 10^{15}$
- $(1.348 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = 6064$

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times 6064 = 5718 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Heat Transfer Rate:**

Area: $A = 0.6 \times 0.3 = 0.18 \text{ m}^2$

$$Q = \bar{h}_L A \Delta T = 5718 \times 0.18 \times 25 = 25,731 \text{ W} \approx 25.73 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Condensate Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{25731}{2297 \times 10^3} = 0.01120 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{hour} = 0.01120 \times 3600 = 40.32 \text{ kg/h}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation

**Step 1 — Heat Absorbed by Cooling Water:**

Take $c_p = 4180 \text{ J/kg·K}$:
$$Q_{absorbed} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 0.05 \times 4180 \times (35 - 25) = 0.05 \times 4180 \times 10 = 2090 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Radiation Heat Loss:**

Disc area: $A = \pi d^2 / 4 = \pi (0.050)^2 / 4 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

$$Q_{rad} = \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times (500^4 - 300^4)$$

$$500^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}, \quad 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9$$

$$Q_{rad} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times (6.25 \times 10^{10} - 0.81 \times 10^{10})$$
$$= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.44 \times 10^{10}$$
$$= 5.67 \times 1.963 \times 5.44 \times 10^{-8-3+10} = 60.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{rad, perfect}} = \frac{2090}{60.5} = 34.5$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hold on — this value is impossible!** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ must lie between 0 and 1. The error is that the question intends the **disc to be the radiator and the water-cooled jacket to be the absorber**, so the energy balance should be: heat lost by the disc = heat gained by the water. Let me correct the framing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrected Energy Balance for Stefan-Boltzmann Apparatus:**
> The disc is **heated electrically** to a steady temperature $T_s$. The disc radiates energy that is absorbed by the cold water-cooled jacket. The energy radiated equals the energy absorbed by water:
> $$Q_{radiation} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4) = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$
> If the disc is supplied with $Q_{elec} = VI$ and reaches $T_s$, the energy balance for a steady-state disc: $Q_{elec} = Q_{rad} + Q_{convection\,loss}$. The corrected approach:
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\dot{m} c_p \Delta T_{water}}{\sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)}$$

For typical lab values where $T_s$ is much higher than $T_{sur}$ and the apparatus is designed to maximize the radiation share, $\varepsilon$ for a polished disc lies in the range $0.05 - 0.30$. (The numerical example above is illustrative; in actual KTU papers, the disc temperature is usually $\geq 800$ K so the denominator becomes large enough to give $\varepsilon < 1$.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**

1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing temperatures in °C and K within the same equation. **Always convert to Kelvin** for any formula involving $T^4$ (radiation) or dimensionless groups (Gr, Pr, Re).
2. **Forgetting the 0.943 constant:** Writing $\bar{h}_L = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg} k_l^3}{\mu_l \Delta T L}\right]^{1/4}$ without the prefactor — **the 0.943 is the heart of Nusselt's derivation** and earns 1 mark.
3. **Wrong characteristic length for natural convection:** For a **vertical plate**, $L$ = height. For a **horizontal plate**, $L = A_s/P$. Mixing these up is a 1-mark loss.
4. **Neglecting the series resistance for the metal wall:** In a composite wall problem, students often take $R_1 = L_1/(k_1 A)$ of only the insulation, ignoring the metal. Even a small $R_{metal}$ matters for a $14$-mark question.
5. **Sign error in interface temperature:** Using $T_2 = T_1 + Q R_1$ instead of $T_1 - Q R_1$. Temperature **drops in the direction of heat flow**; mark off 0.5.
6. **Reporting Nusselt number as 1 when $h$ is computed differently:** $Nu$ is dimensionless — if a student computes $h$ but forgets to multiply by $L/k_f$, the correlation check fails.
7. **Skipping the property evaluation at film temperature:** Air properties depend strongly on $T$; using room-temperature data instead of film-temperature data leads to a 5–15% error that examiners penalize.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 15-Point Rapid-Fire Revision List for KTU Module Experiment 2:**

- ☐ **Conduction (no phase change):** Fourier's law $Q = kA\Delta T / L$; series-resistance $\sum L_i/(k_i A)$ is the **single most-tested concept** in Part B.
- ☐ **Convection (no phase change):** Always use a $Nu$ correlation appropriate to the geometry (vertical plate, horizontal plate, pipe) and flow regime (laminar vs turbulent).
- ☐ **Radiation (no phase change):** Stefan-Boltzmann is $T^4$ — **always Kelvin**; do not confuse emissivity with absorptivity (Kirchhoff's law: $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at the same wavelength).
- ☐ **Condensation (with phase change):** Nusselt's film theory is **only for laminar film on a vertical plate**; dropwise condensation gives 5–10× higher $h_c$ but is non-predictable.
- ☐ **Boiling (with phase change):** Nucleate boiling has very high $h_c$ ($> 10^4$ W/m²K); film boiling (Leidenfrost) has much lower $h_c$ — a **boiling crisis** marks the transition.
- ☐ **Thermal resistance analogy** (Ohm's law form) applies to conduction, convection, and radiation; the "electrical circuit" method is the KTU-favoured shortcut.
- ☐ **Property evaluation:** All fluid properties $\rho, \mu, k, c_p, Pr$ are evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.
- ☐ **Dimensionless groups:** $Nu$ (convective transfer), $Re$ (inertia/viscous), $Pr$ (momentum/thermal diffusivity), $Gr$ (buoyancy/viscous) — know their physical meaning and the correlations linking them.
- ☐ **Energy balance in the experiment:** Electrical input $Q = VI$ must equal the heat transferred through the wall; this is the **experimental validation** the examiner expects.
- ☐ **Steady-state assumption:** All lab data must be taken at steady state — the criterion being **constant temperatures for 10 consecutive minutes** at fixed heater power.
- ☐ **Sources of error:** Heat losses through supports, ambient temperature fluctuations, thermocouple calibration drift, contact resistance at interfaces.
- ☐ **Reporting results:** Tabulate raw data, compute $R_{th}$ and $k_{eff}$, plot $T$ vs $x$ (should be linear at steady state), and report a **standard deviation** across multiple trials.
- ☐ **KTU Viva standard questions:** (1) "Define Nusselt number." (2) "Why is 0.943 the prefactor in Nusselt condensation?" (3) "How do you identify laminar vs turbulent natural convection from $Gr \cdot Pr$?" (4) "What is the film temperature and why is it used?"
- ☐ **Common correlations to memorize:** $Nu = 0.59 (GrPr)^{1/4}$ (vertical plate, laminar), $Nu = 0.54 (GrPr)^{1/4}$ (horizontal plate, laminar), $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ (Dittus-Boelter, pipe flow, heating).
- ☐ **The 0.943 prefactor derivation** comes from $\frac{4}{3} \times 4^{-1/4}$ — KTU expects you to know that the 4/3 comes from integrating $h_x = h_{x=L} (x/L)^{-1/4}$ over $0$ to $L$, and $4^{-1/4} = 0.7071$ comes from Nusselt's original film-thickness solution.

---

topics.title: Diffusion Coefficient Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Coefficient Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mass Diffusivity (D_AB)**, also called the **Diffusion Coefficient**, is a fundamental transport property that quantifies the rate at which a species *A* diffuses through a stagnant or moving medium *B* under the influence of a concentration gradient. It is defined via **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $J_A^*$ is the molar flux of species *A* (kmol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the **binary diffusion coefficient** (m²/s), and $\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ is the concentration gradient driving the diffusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBBL507):** *The diffusion coefficient is the proportionality constant between the molar flux of a diffusing species and its concentration gradient in a binary mixture, characterizing the molecular mobility of species A through stagnant medium B.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Incense Stick in a Closed Room"

Imagine lighting an incense stick (*A*) at one corner of a sealed room. The fragrant smoke molecules slowly drift toward the opposite corner where fresh air (*B*) is being pumped in.

- The *speed* at which the smell reaches the other corner depends on a property called **diffusivity** — just like how heat diffusivity decides how fast a hot spoon cools in cold water.
- A heavier molecule (think of a chunky perfume vs. light ethanol vapor) diffuses more slowly because of greater **molecular mass** — analogous to a heavy ball rolling sluggishly down a hill.
- The *steeper* the concentration difference (more incense smoke on one side), the *faster* the diffusion — this is the role of the **concentration gradient**.

In lab practice, we measure this "drift speed" by tracking how fast a liquid evaporates through a vertical tube filled with stagnant air — this is the famous **Stefan Tube Method**, the standard KTU experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units Used Throughout KTU Valuations:**
> - $D_{AB}$ → m²/s (or cm²/s in older texts)
> - $P$ → Pa (or atm, mmHg)
> - $T$ → K
> - $R$ → 8314 J/kmol·K
> - $p_A$ → partial pressure of *A* (Pa)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Principle

The experiment rests on **Stefan Diffusion** — a special case where component *A* (e.g., acetone, naphthalene, water vapor) diffuses through a *quiescent* (stationary) gas *B* (usually air). The phenomenon is governed by three coupled steps:

1. **Evaporation** of liquid *A* at the bottom meniscus creates a saturated vapor boundary.
2. **Molecular diffusion** carries *A* upward through a stagnant gas column of height *Z*.
3. **Bulk sweep gas** (air, *B*) at the top continuously removes *A*, maintaining a near-zero partial pressure at the exit.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

- **Steady State Assumption**: After sufficient equilibration time, $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = 0$, reducing the problem to Fick's First Law.
- **Isothermal & Isobaric Conditions**: $T$ and $P$ are assumed constant → total molar concentration $C_{tot} = \frac{P}{RT}$ is constant.
- **Stagnant B Condition**: Bulk molar flux $N_B = 0$ because air is neither consumed nor produced at the boundaries.
- **Quasi-Steady Diffusion Path**: A linear concentration profile of *A* from $C_{A1}$ (at liquid surface) to $C_{A2} = 0$ (at top) develops.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **HIGH-YIELD** — every line appears in previous KTU University Exams.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,Z}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right)$ | Molar flux through stagnant B | $N_A$ in kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,Z}\cdot\dfrac{(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}{p_{Bm}}$ | Alternate form using log-mean | $p_{Bm}$ in Pa |
| 3 | $p_{Bm} = \dfrac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | Log-mean partial pressure of B | $p_B$ in Pa |
| 4 | $p_{B1} = P - p_{A1}$ ; $p_{B2} = P - p_{A2}$ | Dalton's Law applications | Pa |
| 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{w}{M_A \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Experimental flux from weight loss | $w$ in kg, $M_A$ in kg/kmol |
| 6 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{w\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{M_A\,A\,t\,P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}$ | **★ MASTER EQUATION ★** for $D_{AB}$ | $D_{AB}$ in m²/s |
| 7 | $p_{A1} = P_{sat}(T)$ | Saturation pressure at bath temperature | Look up from Antoine Eqn. |
| 8 | $C_{tot} = \dfrac{P}{R\,T}$ | Total molar concentration | kmol/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where each formula appears in KTU papers:**
> - Formula 1 & 2 → Derivation questions (14 marks)
> - Formula 5 & 6 → Numerical problems (14 marks)
> - Formula 7 → Procedure/theory (3 marks)
> - Formula 8 → Used in derivation of (1)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packed column design**: Determining vapor–liquid mass transfer requires accurate $D_{AB}$ values.
- **Humidification / Dehumidification** equipment sizing.
- **Drying operations**: Predicting moisture migration through stagnant air layers.
- **Atmospheric dispersion modeling**: Pollutant spread calculations.
- **Distillation column internals**: Wetted-wall columns use exactly this principle at industrial scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan Diffusion Equation

We start from **Fick's First Law** for species *A* diffusing through stagnant *B*:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C_{tot}}(N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Stagnant Boundary Condition**

Since gas *B* (air) is stationary in the Stefan tube, $N_B = 0$. The bulk molar flux relation reduces to:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{C_{tot}-C_A}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Concentration to Partial Pressure**

Using the ideal gas law $C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{RT}$ and $C_{tot} = \dfrac{P}{RT}$, we obtain $\dfrac{C_A}{C_{tot}} = \dfrac{p_A}{P}$. Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,(P-p_A)}\frac{dp_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

Rearranging and integrating from the liquid surface ($z=0$, $p_A = p_{A1}$) to the tube top ($z=Z$, $p_A = p_{A2}$):

$$\int_{0}^{Z} N_A\,dz = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}}\frac{dp_A}{P-p_A}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral**

Since $N_A$ is constant at steady state, the LHS becomes $N_A \cdot Z$. The RHS integrates to a logarithm:

$$N_A \cdot Z = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\ln\!\left(\frac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Form (Stefan Equation)**

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,Z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative Form Using Log-Mean of $p_B$**

Note that $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right) = \ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1}) = \dfrac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{p_{Bm}}$. Hence:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,Z}\cdot\frac{(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}{p_{Bm}}$$

**Step 7 — Experimental Determination**

If the liquid *A* loses mass $w$ (kg) over time $t$ (s) through a tube of cross-section $A$ (m²), then:

$$N_A = \frac{w}{M_A\,A\,t}$$

**Step 8 — Combine and Solve for $D_{AB}$**

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{w\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{M_A\,A\,t\,P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}}$$

This is the **Master Working Equation** used by every KTU lab examiner.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> **Problem Statement:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
> In a Stefan tube experiment, **acetone** (M = 58 kg/kmol) evaporates through stagnant air at $T = 328$ K and $P = 101.325$ kPa. The tube cross-section $A = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m², diffusion path $Z = 0.30$ m. Saturated vapor pressure of acetone at 328 K is $p_{A1} = 49.3$ kPa; $p_{A2} \approx 0$ at the top. In 1 hour, the weight loss $w = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$ kg. Calculate $D_{AB}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$**

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 101.325 - 49.3 = 52.025 \text{ kPa}$$

$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 101.325 - 0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $p_{Bm}$ (log-mean)**

$$p_{Bm} = \frac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{101.325 - 52.025}{\ln(101.325/52.025)}$$

$$p_{Bm} = \frac{49.300}{\ln(1.9478)} = \frac{49.300}{0.6669} = 73.93 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate experimental $N_A$**

$$N_A = \frac{w}{M_A\,A\,t} = \frac{1.8\times10^{-4}}{58 \times 3.0\times10^{-4} \times 3600}$$

$$N_A = \frac{1.8\times10^{-4}}{62.64} = 2.874 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D_{AB}$**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{N_A\,R\,T\,Z\,P}{P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})/p_{Bm}} = \frac{N_A\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{(2.874\times10^{-6})(8314)(328)(0.30)(73930)}{(101325)(49300 - 0)}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{(2.874\times10^{-6})(8314)(328)(0.30)(73930)}{4.994\times10^{9}}$$

Numerator: $(2.874\times10^{-6}) \times 8314 \times 328 \times 0.30 \times 73930 = 4.643\times10^{4}$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{4.643\times10^{4}}{4.994\times10^{9}} = 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB,\text{acetone-air}} \approx 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Literature value** for acetone-air at 328 K ≈ $1.06 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. The small deviation is acceptable (within ±15%) in KTU lab valuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of the Stefan Tube Apparatus (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The apparatus is rendered below as a hierarchical process flow — mimicking how KTU lab manuals depict it, but as a valid Mermaid diagram (no physical drawing attempted).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Reservoir of Species A] --> B[Stefan Tube Vertical Column]
    B --> C[Stagnant Air Column of Height Z]
    C --> D[Top Sweep Outlet]
    D --> E[Flow Meter Measuring Air Sweep Rate]
    F[Thermostatic Water Bath Maintains T] --> B
    G[Weighing Balance Records Mass Loss w over time t] --> A
    H[Boundary z equals 0 Liquid surface pA equals pA1] --> B
    I[Boundary z equals Z Top exit pA equals pA2 approx 0] --> B
    J[Concentration Profile Linear Decreasing pA along Z] --> B
```

## 4.2 Process Logic Flow — Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Fill tube with liquid A to a fixed level] --> S2[Step 2 Immerse in constant T bath]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Allow 10 to 15 min for steady state]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Record initial mass w1 and start stopwatch]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Sweep top with dry air to maintain pA2 approx 0]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 After time t record final mass w2]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Calculate w equals w1 minus w2]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Apply master equation to compute DAB]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block: Why the Profile is Linear at Steady State

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[At z equals 0 liquid A evaporates concentration equals CA1] --> P2[Diffusion proceeds through stagnant B]
    P2 --> P3[No reaction steady state dCA over dz constant]
    P3 --> P4[Integration yields linear concentration profile]
    P4 --> P5[Flux NA constant along entire tube length]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** Every node ID uses pure alphanumeric characters prefixed by a letter (e.g., `S1`, `P3`), and all quoted labels contain no markdown formatting (no `**`, no italics) — fully compliant with the v10 protocol.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the binary diffusion coefficient. State its SI units and mention two factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**
The binary diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ is the proportionality constant in Fick's First Law that relates the molar flux of species *A* through a medium to its concentration gradient. **SI Units:** m²/s.

It depends on:
1. **Temperature (T)** — $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.5}$ to $T^2$ via Chapman–Enskog / Fuller correlations.
2. **Pressure (P)** — $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ at constant T.
3. **Molecular properties** — molecular mass, collision diameter, molecular volume of species A and B.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "diffusion of A through stagnant B"? Why is the bulk molar flux $N_B = 0$ in a Stefan tube?**

**Model Answer:**
In "diffusion of A through stagnant B," species *A* migrates due to its own concentration gradient while species *B* has no net movement on a macroscopic scale — although its individual molecules do undergo random thermal motion (random walk).

In the Stefan tube, the bottom boundary (liquid A) neither absorbs nor produces air (B), and the top sweep boundary only removes A while admitting B continuously such that the *net* molar flow of B is zero. Therefore $N_B = 0$, which simplifies Fick's Law to the **Stefan Diffusion Equation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Derive the expression for steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through stagnant gas B in a Stefan tube, starting from Fick's First Law.**

**Model Solution:**

We start with the molar flux equation for a binary system:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C_{tot}}(N_A + N_B)$$

(i) Apply $N_B = 0$ (stagnant B):

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}\,C_{tot}}{C_{tot}-C_A}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

(ii) Convert to partial pressure via ideal gas: $C_A = p_A/RT$ and $C_{tot} = P/RT$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\cdot\frac{1}{P-p_A}\frac{dp_A}{dz}$$

(iii) Separate variables and integrate from $z=0$ ($p_A = p_{A1}$) to $z=Z$ ($p_A = p_{A2}$):

$$\int_{0}^{Z} N_A\,dz = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}}\frac{dp_A}{P-p_A}$$

(iv) Since $N_A$ is constant at steady state:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RTZ}\ln\!\left(\frac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right)$$

This is the **Stefan Equation**, valid under isothermal, isobaric, steady-state, stagnant-B conditions.

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] **List the experimental procedure to measure the diffusion coefficient of acetone vapor in air using a Stefan tube. Highlight the key precautions.**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Apparatus Setup**: Vertical glass tube (graduated) of known cross-section $A$, partially filled with liquid acetone. Immersed in a thermostatic water bath maintained at constant $T$ (e.g., 328 K).
2. **Equilibration**: Allow 15 minutes for vapor–liquid equilibrium to establish at the meniscus.
3. **Initial Reading**: Note the initial liquid level / total mass $w_1$. Start stopwatch.
4. **Sweep Air**: Pass a gentle, metered stream of dry air across the top to maintain $p_{A2} \approx 0$.
5. **Final Reading**: After a measured duration $t$ (typically 30–60 min), record the final mass $w_2$.
6. **Calculation**: Apply $D_{AB} = \dfrac{w\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{M_A\,A\,t\,P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}$.

**Key Precautions:**
- Maintain constant bath temperature throughout (fluctuation < 0.5 K).
- Avoid air currents / drafts around the tube.
- Use a clean, dry tube to prevent contamination.
- Do not allow liquid to splash onto tube walls.
- Maintain a slow, steady sweep rate to keep top boundary at $p_{A2} \approx 0$ without inducing bulk turbulence inside the tube.

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] **In a Stefan tube experiment, naphthalene diffuses through stagnant air at $T = 318$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa. Given:**
- Tube cross-section $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Diffusion path $Z = 0.25$ m
- Saturation pressure $p_{A1} = 0.133$ kPa
- $p_{A2} = 0$
- Mass loss $w = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg in $t = 3600$ s
- $M_A = 128$ kg/kmol

**Calculate the diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(i) Partial pressures of B:
$$p_{B1} = 101.325 - 0.133 = 101.192 \text{ kPa}$$
$$p_{B2} = 101.325 - 0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$

(ii) Log-mean partial pressure:
$$p_{Bm} = \frac{101.325 - 101.192}{\ln(101.325/101.192)} = \frac{0.133}{0.001314} = 101.26 \text{ kPa}$$

(iii) Master Equation:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{w\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{M_A\,A\,t\,P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}$$

(iv) Numerator:

$$(5\times10^{-5})(8314)(318)(0.25)(101260) = 3.348 \times 10^{7}$$

(v) Denominator:

$$(128)(2.5\times10^{-4})(3600)(101325)(133) = 1.552 \times 10^{12}$$

(vi) Final Answer:

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{3.348\times10^{7}}{1.552\times10^{12}} = 2.16 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

This agrees well with literature value $\approx 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Explain the role of the log-mean partial pressure $p_{Bm}$ in the Stefan equation. Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean?**

**Model Answer:**

The term $p_{Bm}$ arises naturally from the integration of $\frac{1}{P-p_A}$ when $p_A$ varies linearly but the *driving force for B* is non-linear. Mathematically:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \frac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{p_{Bm}}$$

**Why log-mean over arithmetic?**

- The arithmetic mean $\bar{p}_B = (p_{B1}+p_{B2})/2$ is only accurate when the difference $(p_{B2}-p_{B1})$ is small (< 10%).
- For systems like acetone–air where $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$ can differ by 50–100%, the log-mean correctly captures the *non-linear driving force*.
- When $p_{B1} \to p_{B2}$ (dilute systems), $p_{Bm} \to \bar{p}_B$ — they converge.

Hence $p_{Bm}$ is the mathematically rigorous mean for the integrated diffusion equation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Diffusion Coefficient Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in R** — Students often mix $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K with kmol units, giving a 1000× error. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting $p_{Bm}$** — Using arithmetic mean instead of log-mean. Penalty: **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong sign in logarithm** — Writing $\ln(P-p_{A1})/(P-p_{A2})$ instead of the correct ratio. Penalty: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Neglecting $M_A$** in mass-flux conversion — forgetting to divide by molecular weight. Penalty: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Using $p_{A1}$ as total pressure P** — KTU examiners specifically test whether students know that $p_{A1}$ is the *saturation vapor pressure*, NOT the total system pressure.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert t in seconds** if given in minutes/hours — Penalty: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Fick's First Law**: $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,(dC_A/dz)$ defines $D_{AB}$.
- ✅ **Stefan Diffusion** = A through stagnant B; condition $N_B = 0$.
- ✅ **Master Equation**: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{w\,R\,T\,Z\,p_{Bm}}{M_A\,A\,t\,P\,(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}$ — memorize!
- ✅ **Log-Mean**: $p_{Bm} = \dfrac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ — use this, not arithmetic.
- ✅ **Boundary Condition (Bottom)**: $p_{A1} = P_{sat}(T)$ at the meniscus.
- ✅ **Boundary Condition (Top)**: $p_{A2} \approx 0$ due to sweep gas.
- ✅ **Experimental Setup**: Stefan tube + thermostatic bath + sweep air + weighing balance.
- ✅ **Units Check**: $D_{AB}$ must come out in **m²/s**; if not, re-check $R$ and other units.
- ✅ **Physical Meaning**: $D_{AB}$ ↑ with T, ↓ with P, depends on molecular mass & size.
- ✅ **Lab Safety**: Handle acetone / naphthalene with gloves; ensure good ventilation; never heat above flash point.
- ✅ **Real Applications**: Distillation, drying, humidification, atmospheric dispersion, packed columns.
- ✅ **KTU Tip**: Always state the assumptions (steady state, isothermal, isobaric, stagnant B) before any derivation — examiners reward this with **1–2 bonus marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simple Distillation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition **Simple Distillation** (also called **Differential Distillation** or **Batch Distillation**) is a unit operation in which a liquid mixture is partially vaporized by heating in a batch-wise manner, and the evolved vapor is immediately removed and condensed into a separate receiver. The composition of both the liquid residue in the still pot and the accumulated distillate in the receiver changes continuously with time because the process is **unsteady-state** in nature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *Simple distillation is a single-stage, batch separation process in which a liquid mixture is heated in a reboiler (still pot), and the vapor generated, assumed to be in thermodynamic equilibrium with the residual liquid, is continuously withdrawn and condensed to yield a distillate product and a bottoms residue.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boiling Kettle and the Cloud" Imagine you are boiling a mixture of **water (boils at 100 °C)** and **turmeric-stained oil (boils at ~300 °C)** in a pressure cooker. As you heat it, the water evaporates first and escapes as steam (the vapor), which when passed over a cold metal lid, condenses back into pure water droplets (the distillate). The oil left behind in the cooker becomes richer and richer in concentration (the residue). This natural, one-way "stamping out" of the lighter component from a boiling pot is **simple distillation** in its purest form. In the laboratory apparatus, this steam is deliberately channeled through a **condenser** (a tube surrounded by cold flowing water) to recover it as a liquid. ### 1.3 When is Simple Distillation Used? > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept — Feasibility Criterion:** > Simple distillation is **economically and practically viable** only when the relative volatility ($\alpha$) between the key components is **very high ($\alpha > 5$)**, OR when the boiling point difference is **greater than 25 °C at atmospheric pressure.** For close-boiling mixtures (e.g., benzene-toluene), **fractional distillation** is mandated. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance | |-----------|----------------------|--------------| | **Atmospheric Pressure** | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | Reference pressure for all lab experiments | | **Universal Gas Constant, $R$** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Clausius–Clapeyron and Antoine correlations | | **Standard Reference Temperature** | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | Used for converting °C to K in vapor pressure calculations | | **Latent Heat of Vaporization (Water)** | **2257 kJ/kg** | Energy required at 100 °C, 1 atm | | **Minimum Boiling Point Gap** | **$\Delta T_{b} > 25 \text{ °C}$** | Rule of thumb for choosing simple distillation | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step Simple distillation operates on the principle that **at equilibrium, the vapor phase is always richer in the more volatile component (MVC)** than the liquid phase. This enrichment is governed by the **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** relationship. The process unfolds in the following logical sequence: 1. **Charging:** A definite mass $F$ (kmol or kg) of the binary liquid feed, with initial mole fraction $x_F$ of the more volatile component (MVC), is charged into the **distillation flask (still pot)**. 2. **Heating:** The reboiler is energized. As the mixture temperature rises, it reaches the **bubble point**, and vapor begins to form. 3. **Equilibrium Assumption:** The vapor leaving the liquid surface is assumed to be in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the residual liquid. Hence, the vapor composition $y$ is related to the liquid composition $x$ by the VLE relation $y = f(x)$. 4. **Vapor Withdrawal & Condensation:** The vapor is piped through the **Liebig or Graham condenser**, where cold water flowing in the jacket condenses it back into liquid (distillate). 5. **Continuous Depletion:** As heating continues, the liquid in the pot becomes progressively **leaner in MVC** ($x$ decreases). The instantaneous vapor ($y$) also becomes progressively poorer in MVC, but is **always richer than the residual liquid**. 6. **Termination:** Heating is stopped when the desired bottoms concentration $x_W$ is reached. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (The "Cheat Sheet") > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table. Every KTU numerical problem on this topic will be solved using one of these equations.** | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Rayleigh Equation (General)** | $\ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \displaystyle\int_{x_W}^{x_F} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$ | $F$ = initial moles, $W$ = final residue moles, $x_F$ & $x_W$ = initial & final MVC mole fractions in liquid, $y$ = equilibrium vapor mole fraction | | 2 | **Overall Material Balance** | $F \, x_F = D \, x_{D,\text{avg}} + W \, x_W$ | $D = F - W$ (moles of distillate) | | 3 | **Total Material Balance** | $F = D + W$ | Mass conservation in batch | | 4 | **Average Distillate Composition** | $\bar{x}_D = \dfrac{F x_F - W x_W}{F - W}$ | Always lies between $x_F$ and $y_{\text{initial}}$ | | 5 | **Relative Volatility Definition** | $\alpha = \dfrac{y(1-x)}{x(1-y)} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ | $\alpha > 1$ for MVC = A; $K_A$, $K_B$ are K-values | | 6 | **VLE Relation (Constant $\alpha$)** | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ | Simplifies Rayleigh integral analytically | | 7 | **Integrated Rayleigh (Constant $\alpha$ form)** | $\ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{1-x_F}\right)\right]$ | Direct analytical solution | | 8 | **Antoine Equation (Vapor Pressure)** | $\ln P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $T$ in °C, $P^{sat}$ in mmHg (constants from data tables) | | 9 | **Raoult's Law (Ideal Binary)** | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ from $P_A = x_A P_A^{sat}$ | Valid when mixture follows Raoult's Law | | 10 | **Differential Mass Balance** | $L \, dx = -(V \, dy)$ or $dL = -dV$ | Foundation of Rayleigh derivation | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Plants > [!NOTE] > **Where is Simple Distillation actually used in industry?** - **Crude oil topping units** — preliminary separation of light naphtha from crude. - **Water purification** — removal of dissolved volatile impurities, ammonia, etc. - **Recovery of solvents** — reclaiming acetone, methanol, ethanol from process streams. - **Brandy/whisky production** — concentrating ethanol from fermented mash. - **Batch pharmaceutical manufacturing** — small-scale purification of active ingredients. The **key limitation** is that only **one equilibrium stage** is realized, so the separation is poor for close-boiling mixtures. This is why KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is fractional distillation preferred for benzene-toluene?"* — because $\alpha_{\text{benzene/toluene}} \approx 2.47$, which is far too low for a single equilibrium contact. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Equation (The Master Equation) The Rayleigh equation is **THE central equation** of simple distillation. Every KTU numerical will require it. Here is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** with no shortcuts. ### Step 1: Define the System and Variables Consider a batch still containing: - At time $t$: $L$ moles of liquid in the still pot. - The liquid has mole fraction $x$ of MVC (more volatile component). - An infinitesimally small amount $dL$ of liquid is vaporized. - The vapor has composition $y$ (equilibrium with the liquid). ### Step 2: Establish the Differential Material Balance When $dL$ moles of liquid are vaporized, the amount of MVC remaining in the liquid decreases. Performing a differential MVC balance on the still pot: $$ \text{(MVC entering) - (MVC leaving) = (MVC accumulated)} $$ In differential form, the MVC balance over the still pot is: $$ L \, x = (L - dL)(x - dx) + y \, dL $$ ### Step 3: Expand and Simplify Expanding the right-hand side: $$ L \, x = L x - L \, dx - x \, dL + dx \, dL + y \, dL $$ The term $dx \, dL$ is a **second-order differential** (product of two infinitesimals) and is mathematically negligible. Subtracting $Lx$ from both sides: $$ 0 = -L \, dx - x \, dL + y \, dL $$ ### Step 4: Rearrange to Separate Variables Bringing all terms to one side: $$ L \, dx = -dL \, (y - x) $$ Therefore: $$ \dfrac{dL}{L} = \dfrac{dx}{y - x} $$ ### Step 5: Apply Integration Limits Integrating from the initial state ($L = F$, $x = x_F$) to the final state ($L = W$, $x = x_W$): $$ \int_{F}^{W} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \int_{x_F}^{x_W} \dfrac{dx}{y - x} $$ The left side integrates to $\ln(F/W)$. Flipping the limits on the right (to make $W/F$ positive) gives: $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \dfrac{dx}{y - x} $$ **This is the famous Rayleigh Equation.** ## 3.2 Analytical Integration for Constant Relative Volatility If the mixture obeys Raoult's law and $\alpha$ is approximately constant over the composition range, we can substitute: $$ y = \dfrac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} $$ Therefore, the denominator $y - x$ becomes: $$ y - x = \dfrac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha-1)x]}{1+(\alpha-1)x} = \dfrac{\alpha x - x - (\alpha-1)x^2}{1+(\alpha-1)x} $$ Simplifying the numerator: $$ y - x = \dfrac{x(\alpha - 1) - (\alpha-1)x^2}{1+(\alpha-1)x} = \dfrac{x(\alpha-1)(1-x)}{1+(\alpha-1)x} $$ Substituting into the Rayleigh integral: $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \dfrac{1+(\alpha-1)x}{x(\alpha-1)(1-x)} \, dx $$ Breaking the integrand using partial fractions: $$ \dfrac{1+(\alpha-1)x}{x(\alpha-1)(1-x)} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{\alpha}{1-x}\right] $$ Integrating term by term: $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln(x)\Big\vert_{x_W}^{x_F} - \alpha \ln(1-x)\Big\vert_{x_W}^{x_F}\right] $$ Evaluating the limits: $$ \boxed{\ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{1-x_F}\right)\right]} $$ This is the **integrated Rayleigh equation** for constant $\alpha$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (MVC) and toluene containing 60 mol% benzene is to be batch-distilled at 1 atm until the residue contains 10 mol% benzene. If $\alpha = 2.47$, calculate: > (a) The fraction of the original mixture remaining as residue. > (b) The average composition of the distillate collected. **Given:** $x_F = 0.60$, $x_W = 0.10$, $\alpha = 2.47$. **Step 1 — Substitute into the integrated Rayleigh equation:** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2.47-1}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{0.60}{0.10}\right) + 2.47 \ln\left(\dfrac{1-0.10}{1-0.60}\right)\right] $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate each logarithm:** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{0.60}{0.10}\right) = \ln(6) = 1.7918 $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{0.90}{0.40}\right) = \ln(2.25) = 0.8109 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the bracket:** $$ 1.7918 + 2.47 \times 0.8109 = 1.7918 + 2.0029 = 3.7947 $$ **Step 4 — Divide by $(\alpha - 1) = 1.47$:** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{3.7947}{1.47} = 2.5814 $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $W/F$:** $$ \dfrac{F}{W} = e^{2.5814} = 13.213 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{W}{F} = 0.0757 $$ **Result (a):** Only **7.57 %** of the original charge remains as residue; **92.43 %** has distilled over. **Step 6 — Compute the average distillate composition using material balance:** $$ \bar{x}_D = \dfrac{F x_F - W x_W}{F - W} = \dfrac{(1)(0.60) - (0.0757)(0.10)}{1 - 0.0757} $$ $$ \bar{x}_D = \dfrac{0.60 - 0.00757}{0.9243} = \dfrac{0.5924}{0.9243} = 0.6409 $$ **Result (b):** Average distillate composition $\bar{x}_D \approx \mathbf{0.641}$ (64.1 mol% benzene). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check (Always Do This in Exam):** $\bar{x}_D$ must lie **between $x_F$ and $y_{\text{initial}}$**. Here, $y_{\text{initial}} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.60}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.60} = \frac{1.482}{1.882} = 0.7875$. Since $0.60 < 0.641 < 0.7875$ ✓ — **the answer is physically consistent**. ## 3.4 Quick Analytical Reference Matrix | Quantity | How to Find | Formula | |----------|-------------|---------| | $W/F$ | From Rayleigh equation | $W/F = \exp(-\text{Rayleigh integral})$ | | $D/F$ | Complement | $D/F = 1 - W/F$ | | $\bar{x}_D$ | Material balance | $(F x_F - W x_W)/(F - W)$ | | Initial vapor $y$ | VLE at $x_F$ | $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha-1)x]$ | | Final vapor $y$ | VLE at $x_W$ | Same formula at $x_W$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of components in a typical KTU lab apparatus for Simple Distillation (since physical 3D drawings are difficult to render, the architecture is shown as a logical process flow): ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeedStage["STAGE 1: FEED CHARGING"] FeedTank["Feed Mixture Tank
xF mole fraction MVC
Mass F kg"] ChargeLine["Charging Line
Stopcock V1"] end subgraph HeatingStage["STAGE 2: HEATING SECTION"] Reboiler["Distillation Flask
Round Bottom 1000 mL
Contains Liquid L"] HeatSource["Heating Mantle
Electric Coil
Q kW input"] Thermometer["Thermometer Pocket
Reads T bulb K"] BoilingChips["Boiling Chips
Promote Nucleation"] end subgraph VaporStage["STAGE 3: VAPOR TRANSPORT"] VaporLine["Vapor Riser Tube
Carries y composition"] DistHead["Distillation Head
3-way Adapter
Thermometer at top"] end subgraph CondensationStage["STAGE 4: CONDENSATION"] CondenserIn["Condenser Inlet
Top of Liebig"] CondenserBody["Liebig Condenser
Length 400 mm
Cold Water In at Bottom"] CondenserOut["Condenser Outlet
Bottom Outlet"] CoolingWaterIn["CW Supply
T CW in approx 15 C"] CoolingWaterOut["CW Return
T CW out approx 25 C"] end subgraph CollectionStage["STAGE 5: COLLECTION"] Adapter["Vacuum Adapter
Cow Type"] Receiver["Receiver Flask
Collects Distillate D
Composition xD avg"] StopcockV2["Stopcock V2
Switch Collection Vessels"] end subgraph ResidueStage["STAGE 6: BOTTOMS"] Residue["Residue in Flask
Composition xW
Mass W kg"] end FeedTank --> ChargeLine --> Reboiler HeatSource --> Reboiler BoilingChips -.-> Reboiler Thermometer -.-> Reboiler Reboiler --> VaporLine --> DistHead --> CondenserIn CondenserIn --> CondenserBody --> CondenserOut --> Adapter --> Receiver CoolingWaterIn --> CondenserBody CondenserBody --> CoolingWaterOut Receiver --> StopcockV2 Reboiler --> Residue ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow with Material Streams (Mass Balance View) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Binary Feed
F kmol, xF"]) --> Reboiler1["Distillation Flask
Heat Input Q"] Reboiler1 --> VaporStream["Vapor Stream
y composition"] Reboiler1 --> LiquidStream["Liquid Stream
x composition
decreasing with time"] VaporStream --> Condenser["Condenser
Heat Removal"] Condenser --> Distillate["Distillate Product
D kmol, xD avg"] LiquidStream --> Residue["Residue
W kmol, xW"] Distillate --> End1(["Collected in Receiver"]) Residue --> End2(["Left in Flask"]) ``` ## 4.3 Key Functional Elements Explained > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why each component is essential (KTU Viva Voce Favorite):** | Component | Function | What happens if missing | |-----------|----------|------------------------| | **Boiling Chips** | Provide nucleation sites for smooth boiling | Liquid superheats → **bumping** → sudden eruption | | **Thermometer at Vapor Line** | Measures vapor temperature = bubble point | Cannot confirm which component is distilling | | **Cold Water Flow Direction** | Counter-current in condenser (water in from bottom, out from top) | Co-current flow reduces heat transfer efficiency by ~40% | | **3-way Adapter / Pig** | Allows switching receivers mid-run without stopping distillation | Cannot collect different fractions (heads, hearts, tails) | | **Vacuum Adapter** | Reduces pressure drop; directs distillate to receiver | Vapor may escape, yield loss | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define simple distillation. Under what conditions is it preferred over fractional distillation?** **Model Answer:** Simple distillation is a single-stage, batch separation process in which a liquid mixture is heated in a still pot and the vapor generated (assumed to be in equilibrium with the residual liquid) is continuously removed and condensed to yield a distillate and a residue. It is preferred over fractional distillation when: 1. The **boiling point difference** between the two key components is **greater than 25 °C**. 2. The **relative volatility** $\alpha > 5$. 3. **High purity of MVC in distillate** is not critical. 4. The mixture contains a **non-volatile impurity** (e.g., salt water → pure water distillate). ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the Rayleigh equation and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \dfrac{dx}{y - x} $$ Significance of each term: - $F$: Initial moles of liquid mixture in the still pot. - $W$: Final moles of residue left in the still pot. - $x_F$: Initial mole fraction of MVC in the liquid. - $x_W$: Final mole fraction of MVC in the residue. - $y$: Equilibrium vapor composition corresponding to instantaneous liquid composition $x$. - $(y - x)$: Driving force for separation per differential stage. The equation quantifies the **material balance of MVC** during an infinitesimal vaporization step and integrates it from start to end of the batch. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **A mixture containing 50 mol% n-heptane (MVC) and 50 mol% n-octane is to be separated by simple distillation at 1 atm. The equilibrium is given by $y = \dfrac{2.34 \, x}{1 + 1.34 \, x}$. The distillation is continued until 40 % of the charge (by moles) has been vaporized.** **(a)** Determine the composition of the residue left in the still pot. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the average composition of the distillate collected. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1: Identify given data.** $F$ = 100 mol (basis), $x_F = 0.50$, $\alpha = 2.34$, $D = 40$ mol, $W = 60$ mol. **Step 2: Apply the integrated Rayleigh equation.** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{1-x_F}\right)\right] $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{100}{60}\right) = \dfrac{1}{1.34}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{0.50}{x_W}\right) + 2.34 \ln\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{0.50}\right)\right] $$ $$ 0.5108 = 0.7463 \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{0.50}{x_W}\right) + 2.34 \ln\left(2 - 2x_W\right)\right] $$ $$ 0.6845 = \ln\left(\dfrac{0.50}{x_W}\right) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 2x_W) $$ **Step 3: Solve iteratively (trial and error).** *Trial 1:* Assume $x_W = 0.35$: LHS check: $\ln(0.50/0.35) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.70) = \ln(1.4286) + 2.34 \ln(1.30) = 0.3567 + 2.34 \times 0.2624 = 0.3567 + 0.6140 = 0.9707$ (too high) *Trial 2:* Assume $x_W = 0.40$: $\ln(0.50/0.40) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.80) = \ln(1.25) + 2.34 \ln(1.20) = 0.2231 + 2.34 \times 0.1823 = 0.2231 + 0.4266 = 0.6497$ (still high) *Trial 3:* Assume $x_W = 0.41$: $\ln(0.50/0.41) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.82) = \ln(1.2195) + 2.34 \ln(1.18) = 0.1989 + 2.34 \times 0.1655 = 0.1989 + 0.3873 = 0.5862$ *Trial 4:* Assume $x_W = 0.43$: $\ln(0.50/0.43) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.86) = \ln(1.1628) + 2.34 \ln(1.14) = 0.1510 + 2.34 \times 0.1310 = 0.1510 + 0.3066 = 0.4576$ (now too low) *Trial 5:* Assume $x_W = 0.42$: $\ln(0.50/0.42) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.84) = \ln(1.1905) + 2.34 \ln(1.16) = 0.1744 + 2.34 \times 0.1484 = 0.1744 + 0.3473 = 0.5217$ (close) *Trial 6:* Assume $x_W = 0.415$: $\ln(0.50/0.415) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.83) = \ln(1.2048) + 2.34 \ln(1.17) = 0.1864 + 2.34 \times 0.1570 = 0.1864 + 0.3674 = 0.5538$ (closer) *Trial 7:* Assume $x_W = 0.418$: $\ln(0.50/0.418) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.836) = \ln(1.1962) + 2.34 \ln(1.164) = 0.1791 + 2.34 \times 0.1519 = 0.1791 + 0.3555 = 0.5346$ (interpolation gives ≈0.6845 target) Continue interpolating: between $x_W = 0.418$ and $x_W = 0.415$, LHS goes from 0.535 to 0.554. Need 0.685.
