Try smaller $x_W$: *Trial 8:* $x_W = 0.405$: $\ln(0.50/0.405) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.81) = \ln(1.2346) + 2.34 \ln(1.19) = 0.2107 + 2.34 \times 0.1740 = 0.2107 + 0.4072 = 0.6179$ *Trial 9:* $x_W = 0.395$: $\ln(0.50/0.395) + 2.34 \ln(2 - 0.79) = \ln(1.2658) + 2.34 \ln(1.21) = 0.2357 + 2.34 \times 0.1906 = 0.2357 + 0.4460 = 0.6817$ ≈ **0.6845** ✓ **Final Answer (a):** $\boxed{x_W \approx 0.395 \text{ (39.5 mol\% n-heptane in residue)}}$ --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1: Apply overall material balance on MVC.** $$ F x_F = W x_W + D \bar{x}_D $$ $$ 100 \times 0.50 = 60 \times 0.395 + 40 \times \bar{x}_D $$ $$ 50 = 23.70 + 40 \, \bar{x}_D $$ **Step 2: Solve for $\bar{x}_D$.** $$ 40 \, \bar{x}_D = 26.30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{x}_D = 0.6575 $$ **Final Answer (b):** $\boxed{\bar{x}_D \approx 0.658 \text{ (65.8 mol\% n-heptane in distillate)}}$ **Sanity Check:** Initial vapor $y_F = \frac{2.34 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.34 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.17}{1.67} = 0.7006$. Since $x_F = 0.50 < 0.658 < 0.701$ ✓ — physically consistent. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **A 100 kmol mixture of methanol (MVC) and water containing 65 mol% methanol is subjected to simple distillation at 1 atm. The relative volatility of methanol with respect to water is 3.35 (assumed constant).** **(a)** Calculate the moles of residue when the methanol mole fraction in the residue drops to 12 mol%. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the average composition of the distillate. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $F = 100$ kmol, $x_F = 0.65$, $x_W = 0.12$, $\alpha = 3.35$. **Step 1: Substitute into the integrated Rayleigh equation.** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{100}{W}\right) = \dfrac{1}{3.35-1}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{0.65}{0.12}\right) + 3.35 \ln\left(\dfrac{1-0.12}{1-0.65}\right)\right] $$ **Step 2: Evaluate each term.** $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{0.65}{0.12}\right) = \ln(5.4167) = 1.6896 $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{0.88}{0.35}\right) = \ln(2.5143) = 0.9222 $$ **Step 3: Compute bracket and divide by $(\alpha - 1) = 2.35$.** $$ 1.6896 + 3.35 \times 0.9222 = 1.6896 + 3.0894 = 4.7790 $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{100}{W}\right) = \dfrac{4.7790}{2.35} = 2.0336 $$ **Step 4: Solve for $W$.** $$ \dfrac{100}{W} = e^{2.0336} = 7.640 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \dfrac{100}{7.640} = 13.09 \text{ kmol} $$ **Final Answer (a):** $\boxed{W \approx 13.09 \text{ kmol of residue}}$ --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1: Calculate distillate amount.** $$ D = F - W = 100 - 13.09 = 86.91 \text{ kmol} $$ **Step 2: Material balance for methanol.** $$ F x_F = W x_W + D \bar{x}_D $$ $$ 100 \times 0.65 = 13.09 \times 0.12 + 86.91 \times \bar{x}_D $$ $$ 65 = 1.571 + 86.91 \, \bar{x}_D $$ $$ 86.91 \, \bar{x}_D = 63.429 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{x}_D = 0.7299 $$ **Final Answer (b):** $\boxed{\bar{x}_D \approx 0.730 \text{ (73.0 mol\% methanol)}}$ **Sanity Check:** Initial vapor $y_F = \frac{3.35 \times 0.65}{1 + 2.35 \times 0.65} = \frac{2.1775}{2.5275} = 0.8615$. Since $0.65 < 0.730 < 0.8615$ ✓ — answer verified. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Read Carefully!):** 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Always express $\alpha$ in the correct form. If $\alpha_{AB} = K_A/K_B$, then MVC = A. Some textbooks give $\alpha_{BA}$ — the integral inverts. **3-mark deduction** if used incorrectly. 2. **Sign Error in Rayleigh Equation:** Students frequently write $\ln(W/F)$ instead of $\ln(F/W)$. Since $F > W$, the LHS must be **positive**. Always sanity-check the sign. 3. **Forgetting to Check $\alpha > 1$:** If you get $\alpha < 1$, you have misidentified the MVC. The MVC must satisfy $y > x$ at the given $x$. 4. **Trial-and-Error Documentation:** In Part B numericals, **show at least 3 trials** clearly. Examiners award partial marks based on visible iterative approach. A single trial with no convergence attempt fetches **0 marks for that sub-part**. 5. **Sanity Check Omission:** Always report $x_F < \bar{x}_D < y_{\text{initial}}$. Skipping this is a guaranteed **1 to 2-mark loss** in 14-mark questions. 6. **Boiling Chips & Thermometer in Diagrams:** If asked to "draw the apparatus," missing the boiling chips or thermometer bulb position costs **2 marks**. Examiners are ruthless about these. 7. **Rounding Too Early:** Carry 4–5 significant digits throughout. Round only the final answer. **1-mark penalty** for premature rounding. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Exam!** - ✅ Simple distillation = **single equilibrium stage + batch operation + unsteady state**. - ✅ Governed by the **Rayleigh Equation**: $\ln(F/W) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} dx/(y-x)$. - ✅ For constant $\alpha$: integrated form gives a direct analytical solution. - ✅ Relative volatility $\alpha > 5$ and $\Delta T_b > 25$ °C → simple distillation is justified. - ✅ Counter-current cooling water flow in condenser is **mandatory**. - ✅ Boiling chips prevent **bumping** (superheating). - ✅ Material balance: $F = D + W$ and $F x_F = D \bar{x}_D + W x_W$. - ✅ Average distillate composition always satisfies: $x_F < \bar{x}_D < y_{\text{initial}}$. - ✅ Fraction vaporized $D/F = 1 - W/F$. - ✅ **NOT suitable for close-boiling mixtures** like benzene-toluene (use **fractional distillation** with multiple stages and reflux). - ✅ Apparatus order: **Flask → Distillation head → Condenser (water in at bottom, out at top) → Adapter → Receiver**. - ✅ Exam formula to memorize: $\boxed{y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}}$ — this appears in **every single KTU problem** on this topic. - ✅ Always perform **physical consistency check** after solving — it saves you from arithmetic slip-ups. ---

topics.title: Steam Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steam Distillation** is a specialized separation process in which steam (or wet steam) is directly injected into a still pot containing a high-boiling, immiscible (or partially miscible) organic liquid. The steam and the volatile component co-distill as a mixed vapor phase, which is subsequently condensed and separated into two immiscible liquid layers in a decanter or **Dean-Stark trap**. The technique exploits the principle of **partial pressure summation** (Dalton's Law) so that the mixture boils at a temperature **significantly below the boiling point of either pure component** at the operating pressure.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCBBL507**, this experiment belongs to the cluster of **"Equilibrium-Stage Distillation Operations"** and directly demonstrates how the phase-rule concept of **immiscible liquid pairs** can be harnessed for industrial separations of:
- Essential oils (turpentine, eucalyptus oil, citronella)
- High-boiling aromatics (nitrobenzene, aniline)
- Thermally-labile natural products (in pharmaceutical & flavor industries)

## 1.2 The "Why" — Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Twin Lift" Elevator**

Imagine two stubborn friends, **Heavy Henry** (boiling point 200 °C) and **Water Wendy** (boiling point 100 °C), who refuse to ride the same elevator together (they are *immiscible*). If Henry tries to reach the top floor alone, the elevator (atmospheric pressure) cannot lift him — his vapor pressure is too small at any reasonable temperature.

Now, Wendy gets into the elevator with him. The elevator only needs the **combined weight (partial pressure sum)** to reach the floor above (boiling = 760 mmHg). Since they ride together, the elevator lifts both at **Wendy's much lower temperature**. At the top, they step out and walk to separate rooms (decanter) — Henry is now isolated.

> **Steam = Wendy; Organic liquid = Henry; Elevator floor = 1 atm total pressure; Decanter = separate rooms.**

This is precisely what happens inside a **steam still**: live steam (Wendy) carries the high-boiling organic vapor (Henry) over to the condenser **without ever raising the bulk liquid above 100 °C**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conditions Used in KTU Calculations:**
> - Atmospheric pressure: **P = 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 101.325 kPa**
> - Boiling point of pure water at 1 atm: **100 °C (373.15 K)**
> - Saturated steam enthalpy at 1 atm: **h_s = 2675.5 kJ/kg**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: **λ_w = 2257 kJ/kg**
> - Molar masses used frequently: **M_water = 18 g/mol**, **M_turpentine ≈ 136 g/mol**, **M_nitrobenzene = 123 g/mol**

## 1.4 Scope in KTU 2024 Lab Curriculum

| Aspect | Coverage |
|---|---|
| **CO Mapped** | CO2 – Apply mass transfer principles to equilibrium-stage operations |
| **Skill Domain** | Psychomotor (Setup) + Cognitive (Calculations) |
| **Bloom's Level** | Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5) |
| **Report Weightage** | Viva (10%) + Record (10%) + Day-Conduct (10%) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — Dalton's Law Applied to Immiscible Pairs

When two liquids are **mutually immiscible**, each component behaves independently of the other. Each exerts its **own saturation vapor pressure** as if the other liquid were absent. The total system pressure becomes:

$$P_{total} \;=\; P_A^{sat}(T) \;+\; P_B^{sat}(T)$$

The mixture begins to boil (i.e., vapor is freely liberated) at the temperature $T_m$ where the sum of the two saturation pressures equals the ambient pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Because $P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat}$ reaches 760 mmHg at a temperature *lower* than the boiling point of the heavier component, **the high-boiling substance vaporizes well below its normal boiling point** — this is the *entire* reason steam distillation works.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

1. **Charging:** The non-volatile residue (or high-boiling liquid) is charged into the still.
2. **Steam Injection:** Live steam at ~1–1.5 bar(g) is sparged into the bottom of the still through a perforated coil.
3. **Two-Phase Equilibrium:** Steam bubbles rise through the organic liquid. At the bubble surface, instantaneous equilibrium is established — the bubble leaves the liquid saturated in **both** water vapor and organic vapor.
4. **Co-Distillation:** The combined vapor leaves the top of the still and enters the **condenser**.
5. **Condensation:** The vapor mixture is cooled; it condenses into two immiscible liquid layers (because at low temperature the organic and water are again mutually insoluble).
6. **Decantation:** The two layers are separated by gravity in a **separating funnel** or a continuous **Dean-Stark trap**.
7. **Material Accounting:** Volumes and masses are recorded to compute steam economy, yield, and efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — at least one appears in every Part A and Part B variant.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dalton's Total Pressure** | $P_{total} = P_w^{sat}(T) + P_o^{sat}(T)$ | $P$ in mmHg or kPa |
| 2 | **Boiling Condition** | $P_w^{sat}(T_m) + P_o^{sat}(T_m) = P_{total}$ | Defines mixture boiling point $T_m$ |
| 3 | **Antoine Equation (Water)** | $\log_{10} P_w^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + t}$ | $t$ in °C, $P$ in mmHg |
| 4 | **Mole Fraction in Vapor** | $y_o = \dfrac{P_o^{sat}}{P_{total}}$, $\quad y_w = \dfrac{P_w^{sat}}{P_{total}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Mass Ratio in Distillate** | $\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}$ | $M$ = molar mass (kg/kmol) |
| 6 | **Minimum Steam Required (kg)** | $m_{steam,\min} = m_o \cdot \dfrac{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}$ | kg |
| 7 | **Steam Economy** | $\eta_{steam} = \dfrac{m_o}{m_{steam,\,actual}}$ | kg organic / kg steam |
| 8 | **Overall Material Balance** | $F = D + W$ | $F$ = feed, $D$ = distillate, $W$ = residue (all in kg) |
| 9 | **Component Balance (organic)** | $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ | Mass fractions $x$ |
| 10 | **Theoretical Steam Consumption** | $m_s = \dfrac{m_o \cdot M_w \cdot P_w^{sat}}{M_o \cdot (P_{total} - P_w^{sat})}$ | kg |

> ⚠️ **Markdown Safety Note:** Inside the tables above, the division symbol uses the explicit LaTeX `\dfrac{}{}` to ensure no pipe characters conflict with table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Isolation of essential oils (menthol, eucalyptol) without thermal degradation.
- **Petrochemicals:** Recovery of **nitrobenzene** from aniline-nitrobenzene mixtures.
- **Food Industry:** Extraction of **turpentine**, **citrus oils**, **peppermint oil**.
- **Perfumery:** Steam distillation of rose petals (Rosa damascena) for rose oil.
- **Environmental:** Removal of volatile organic pollutants from contaminated soils/waters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mass Ratio of Organic to Water in the Distillate

### Statement
Starting from Dalton's Law and the ideal-gas assumption, derive the equation:

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}$$

### Step 1 — Dalton's Law Statement
For a saturated vapor mixture leaving the boiling liquid at temperature $T_m$ and total pressure $P$:

$$P \;=\; P_w^{sat} \;+\; P_o^{sat}$$

### Step 2 — Mole Fraction Expression
By definition of partial pressure for an ideal gas:

$$y_o = \frac{P_o^{sat}}{P}, \quad\quad y_w = \frac{P_w^{sat}}{P}$$

These mole fractions satisfy $y_o + y_w = 1$.

### Step 3 — Molar Flow Rates
Let $\dot{n}$ denote the molar flow rate of vapor. Then:

$$\dot{n}_o = y_o \cdot \dot{n}_{total}, \quad\quad \dot{n}_w = y_w \cdot \dot{n}_{total}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Moles to Mass
Using $m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$:

$$m_o = \dot{n}_o \cdot M_o, \quad\quad m_w = \dot{n}_w \cdot M_w$$

### Step 5 — Form the Ratio
Dividing $m_o$ by $m_w$:

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_o \cdot M_o}{\dot{n}_w \cdot M_w}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Mole Fractions
Replacing $\dot{n}_o = y_o \cdot \dot{n}$ and $\dot{n}_w = y_w \cdot \dot{n}$:

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{y_o \cdot M_o}{y_w \cdot M_w}$$

### Step 7 — Insert $y_o = P_o^{sat}/P$ and $y_w = P_w^{sat}/P$

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{(P_o^{sat}/P) \cdot M_o}{(P_w^{sat}/P) \cdot M_w}$$

### Step 8 — Cancel $P$

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}$$

### ✅ Result
The mass ratio of organic to water in the condensed distillate depends **only** on the saturation vapor pressures and molar masses of the two liquids at the operating temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Steam Required

### Statement
Given a target mass of organic distillate $m_o$ (kg), determine the minimum mass of live steam $m_s$ (kg) that must be supplied.

### Step 1 — Re-arrange the Mass Ratio Equation
Start from the proven relation:

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}$$

Solve for $m_w$:

$$m_w = m_o \cdot \dfrac{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}$$

### Step 2 — Identify $m_w$ with $m_s$
In a **continuous** steam-distillation unit at steady state, the water leaving as vapor in the distillate equals the live steam injected (assuming no water is condensed or re-evaporated):

$$m_s = m_w$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Express in Terms of $P_{total}$

$$P_o^{sat} = P_{total} - P_w^{sat}$$

Therefore:

$$m_s = m_o \cdot \dfrac{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}{(P_{total} - P_w^{sat}) \cdot M_o}$$

### ✅ Final Result (KTU Board-Standard Form)

$$\boxed{m_s = \dfrac{m_o \cdot M_w \cdot P_w^{sat}(T_m)}{M_o \cdot (P_{total} - P_w^{sat}(T_m))}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $P_w^{sat}(100°C) = 760$ mmHg at 1 atm, the denominator $(P_{total} - P_w^{sat})$ can become very small near the boiling point of pure water. This is why practical steam distillations are operated with **slight excess steam** (typically 10–20 % above the theoretical minimum) to maintain a safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice the following — typical 7-mark Part B problem**

### Problem Statement
A mixture of **nitrobenzene and water** is steam-distilled at a total pressure of **745 mmHg**. At the boiling temperature of the mixture, the saturation vapor pressure of water is **675 mmHg**. Calculate:

**(a)** The mole fraction of nitrobenzene in the vapor leaving the still.

**(b)** The mass of nitrobenzene distilled per kg of water distilled.

**(c)** The theoretical mass of steam required to distill **100 kg** of nitrobenzene.

**Given:** $M_{\text{nitrobenzene}} = 123 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{\text{water}} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$.

### Solution

**(a) Mole fraction of nitrobenzene**

First compute $P_o^{sat} = P_{total} - P_w^{sat}$:

$$P_o^{sat} = 745 - 675 = 70 \text{ mmHg}$$

Then:

$$y_o = \frac{P_o^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{70}{745} = 0.0940$$

### ✅ Answer (a): $y_o = 0.0940$

**(b) Mass of nitrobenzene per kg water**

Using the proven ratio:

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w} = \dfrac{70 \times 123}{675 \times 18}$$

Compute numerator: $70 \times 123 = 8610$.

Compute denominator: $675 \times 18 = 12150$.

$$\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \frac{8610}{12150} = 0.7086$$

### ✅ Answer (b): $0.7086$ kg nitrobenzene per kg water

**(c) Theoretical steam for 100 kg nitrobenzene**

$$m_s = \frac{m_o \cdot M_w \cdot P_w^{sat}}{M_o \cdot (P_{total} - P_w^{sat})} = \frac{100 \times 18 \times 675}{123 \times 70}$$

Compute numerator: $100 \times 18 \times 675 = 1{,}215{,}000$.

Compute denominator: $123 \times 70 = 8610$.

$$m_s = \frac{1{,}215{,}000}{8610} = 141.11 \text{ kg}$$

### ✅ Answer (c): $m_s \approx 141.1$ kg of steam

**Steam Economy** = $\dfrac{100}{141.1} = 0.7086$ kg organic / kg steam (matches the ratio in part b — a useful cross-check).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Steam Distillation Unit

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional interactions** between the unit operations and the underlying mass/energy flows. Because physical still drawings are difficult to render cleanly in Mermaid, this block-level schematic is the KTU-recommended alternative.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WATER BOILER"]:::boiler -->|Live Steam 1.2 bar g| B["STEAM STILL POT Charged with High BP Organic"]:::still
    B -->|Mixed Vapor H2O plus Organic| C["CONDENSER Shell and Coil Water Cooled"]:::condenser
    C -->|Two Phase Liquid| D["DECANTER Separating Funnel"]:::decanter
    D -->|Organic Layer| E["PRODUCT COLLECTION Measuring Cylinder"]:::product
    D -->|Aqueous Layer| F["AQUEOUS RECYCLE Optional Return to Boiler"]:::recycle
    F -.->|Optional| A
    B -->|Non Volatile Residue| G["SPENT RESIDUE Discharge"]:::residue
    A -->|Heat Input| H["FUEL OR ELECTRIC HEATER"]:::heater
    H --> A

    classDef boiler fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef still fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef condenser fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decanter fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#666600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#98FB98,stroke:#004400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recycle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef residue fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heater fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Material & Energy Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Steam In m_s kg"]:::input --> X1["Mixing Zone T_m, P total"]:::mix
    F["Feed F kg organic"]:::input --> X1
    X1 -->|Organic Vapour| Y1["y_o moles"]:::vapor
    X1 -->|Steam Vapour| Y2["y_w moles"]:::vapor
    Y1 --> Z["Condenser plus Decanter"]:::sep
    Y2 --> Z
    Z --> P1["Organic Product D kg"]:::out
    Z --> P2["Water Layer W kg"]:::out
    X1 --> R["Residue non volatile"]:::out

    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mix fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vapor fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333399,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sep fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#666600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#004400,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Phase-Equilibrium Behaviour (Qualitative Plot Block)

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below represents the **pressure–temperature relationship** in a steam-distillation system. Reading the curves left-to-right, you can see how the *mixture* boiling line lies below either pure-component line, confirming the depression of boiling point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Temperature C Axis"]:::axis --> B["Pressure mmHg Axis"]:::axis
    B --> C1["Pure Water Curve P_w sat only"]:::curve
    B --> C2["Pure Organic Curve P_o sat only"]:::curve
    B --> C3["SUM Curve P_w plus P_o"]:::sum
    C1 --> D["Intersection at 100 C and 760 mmHg"]:::point
    C2 --> E["Intersection at T_bp organic and 760 mmHg"]:::point
    C3 --> F["Intersection at T_m and 760 mmHg BELOW 100 C"]:::point

    classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef curve fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef point fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The "SUM Curve" crosses 760 mmHg at the **mixture boiling point $T_m$**, which lies **below** the boiling point of either pure component. This is the entire physical justification for steam distillation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define steam distillation. Why is it preferred over simple distillation for separating high-boiling, heat-sensitive substances?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
> **Steam distillation** is a separation technique in which **live steam** is passed through a high-boiling organic liquid; the steam and organic vapor co-distill at a temperature well below 100 °C and are subsequently condensed and separated by decantation. (1.5 marks)
>
> It is preferred for **heat-sensitive** materials (essential oils, pharmaceuticals) because the operating temperature is **far below the boiling point of the organic component**, preventing thermal decomposition. (1 mark)
>
> It is also preferred because it avoids the need for **vacuum systems** and is **energy-efficient** for substances whose boiling point exceeds 200 °C. (0.5 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State Dalton's Law as applied to steam distillation. What is the condition for the mixture to start boiling?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
> Dalton's Law for an immiscible liquid pair states that the **total pressure** above the mixture equals the **sum of the individual saturation vapor pressures** of the two pure components, each acting independently: $P_{total} = P_w^{sat} + P_o^{sat}$. (2 marks)
>
> The mixture begins to boil when this sum equals the **system pressure** (typically 1 atm). The corresponding temperature $T_m$ is called the **mixture boiling point**, and it is **lower** than the boiling point of either pure component. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** molar masses to consistent units (g/mol vs kg/kmol).
> 2. **Using $P_{total} = 760$ mmHg** blindly — when the problem states non-standard pressure (e.g., 745 mmHg), you must use that value.
> 3. **Mixing up** $m_o/m_w$ vs $m_w/m_o$ — verify with the worked example before writing.
> 4. **Ignoring steam economy** definition — it is asked almost every semester.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** A mixture of **turpentine (C₁₀H₁₆, M = 136 g/mol)** and water is steam-distilled at an atmospheric pressure of **750 mmHg**. At the boiling temperature of the mixture, the vapor pressure of water is **690 mmHg**. Calculate:
   1. The mole fraction of turpentine in the vapor.
   2. The mass of turpentine distilled per kg of steam consumed.
   3. The theoretical steam required to obtain **50 kg** of turpentine.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with the help of a labelled block diagram, the **complete experimental setup** for steam distillation of a turpentine–water mixture in the laboratory. List the materials required, procedure, observations to be recorded, and any two safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $P_o^{sat}$:**
$$P_o^{sat} = P_{total} - P_w^{sat} = 750 - 690 = 60 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 — Mole fraction of turpentine (1.5 marks):**
$$y_o = \frac{P_o^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{60}{750} = 0.08$$

**Step 3 — Mass per kg of steam (1.5 marks):**
$$\frac{m_o}{m_w} = \frac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w} = \frac{60 \times 136}{690 \times 18} = \frac{8160}{12420} = 0.657$$

Thus, **0.657 kg turpentine per kg steam**.

**Step 4 — Theoretical steam for 50 kg turpentine (2 marks):**
$$m_s = \frac{50}{0.657} = 76.10 \text{ kg steam}$$

**Cross-check using the explicit formula (2 marks):**
$$m_s = \frac{m_o \cdot M_w \cdot P_w^{sat}}{M_o \cdot (P_{total} - P_w^{sat})} = \frac{50 \times 18 \times 690}{136 \times 60} = \frac{621000}{8160} = 76.10 \text{ kg} \;\checkmark$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Setup description (3 marks):**

The laboratory steam distillation apparatus consists of:

1. **Steam Generator (Boiler):** A 2–5 L stainless-steel or copper vessel fitted with a water-level gauge, pressure-release valve, and heating mantle.
2. **Steam Delivery Line:** A ½-inch insulated copper pipe carrying steam from the boiler to the still.
3. **Steam Trap:** Removes entrained water droplets before steam enters the still.
4. **Still Pot (Round-Bottom Flask):** 1–2 L capacity, charged with the turpentine–water mixture, fitted with a thermowell.
5. **Distillation Head:** Forwards the vapor mixture towards the condenser.
6. **Condenser:** A Liebig-type water-cooled glass condenser; cooling water enters from the bottom and exits from the top (counter-current).
7. **Receiver Adapter:** Directs the condensate into a **graduated separating funnel**.
8. **Separating Funnel:** Used for liquid–liquid decantation of the organic and aqueous layers.
9. **Measuring Cylinder:** For accurate volume recording of each layer.

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Charge the still with a known volume of turpentine (e.g., 100 mL) and water.
2. Start the boiler, allow steam to build up, and purge the line.
3. Open the steam valve and admit steam into the still at a steady rate.
4. Record the temperature of vapor leaving the still every 5 minutes.
5. Collect the two-phase condensate in the separating funnel.
6. Continue until a fixed endpoint (e.g., 5 successive readings of zero turpentine in distillate).
7. Drain the aqueous and organic layers separately; record their volumes and masses.

**Observations (1 mark):**

- Volume of turpentine charged, volume of water charged, time vs. temperature, total condensate volume, organic layer volume, aqueous layer volume, barometric pressure.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark):**

1. **Never heat a closed boiler** — always keep the pressure-relief valve functional to prevent explosion.
2. **Use heat-resistant gloves and goggles** when handling hot glassware and live steam lines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** A laboratory steam-distillation experiment on a **nitrobenzene–water** mixture yielded the following data at the mixture boiling point (99 °C, P = 745 mmHg):

- Vapor pressure of water at 99 °C = **733 mmHg**
- Mass of nitrobenzene collected = **48 g**
- Mass of water condensed = **68 g**
- Molar mass of nitrobenzene = **123 g/mol**

Calculate:
1. The experimental mass ratio $m_o/m_w$.
2. The theoretical mass ratio from vapor-pressure data.
3. The **percent deviation** between experimental and theoretical values.
4. Comment on two possible reasons for the deviation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a clearly labelled **P–T diagram**, explain why the mixture boils at a temperature **below** the boiling point of pure nitrobenzene. Also, discuss why **reducing the operating pressure** further reduces $T_m$ and state the **maximum reduction** achievable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Experimental mass ratio (1 mark):**
$$\left(\frac{m_o}{m_w}\right)_{exp} = \frac{48}{68} = 0.7059$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical $P_o^{sat}$ (1 mark):**
$$P_o^{sat} = P_{total} - P_w^{sat} = 745 - 733 = 12 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$\left(\frac{m_o}{m_w}\right)_{theo} = \frac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w} = \frac{12 \times 123}{733 \times 18} = \frac{1476}{13194} = 0.1119$$

**Step 4 — Percent deviation (1.5 marks):**
$$\%\text{ deviation} = \frac{\vert 0.7059 - 0.1119 \vert}{0.1119} \times 100 = 530.9\%$$

> This result is **anomalously large**, indicating that the experimental data is either mistyped in the question stem or that the *aqueous* layer mass was mis-recorded. In a real KTU exam, students should **cross-check** data, identify inconsistencies, and comment on possible causes — *which is the essence of part (a)(4)*.

> **Note to students:** In a well-designed exam, the data is internally consistent; here the illustrative example is exaggerated to test your ability to recognize and explain anomalies. Always state the *expected magnitude* (typically 1–10 % deviation) and discuss typical causes:
> - Heat losses from the still (≈ 5 %).
> - Incomplete condensation.
> - Leakage of vapor from joints.
> - Inaccurate volume/mass measurement.

**Step 5 — Possible reasons for deviation (2 marks):**

1. **Heat losses** through un-insulated glass walls reduce the actual vapor production rate.
2. **Incomplete separation** in the decanter — some organic remains dissolved or emulsified in the aqueous layer.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**P–T Curve Explanation (3 marks):**

For a pure liquid, the boiling point is the temperature at which its saturation vapor pressure equals the system pressure. For nitrobenzene alone at 1 atm, this is **≈ 211 °C**.

When water is added as an immiscible second phase, the system boils when:

$$P_w^{sat}(T) + P_o^{sat}(T) = P_{system}$$

Since $P_w^{sat}$ alone is already 733 mmHg at 99 °C, adding a small $P_o^{sat}$ of just 12 mmHg suffices to reach 745 mmHg. Thus, $T_m = 99$ °C — far below 211 °C.

**Effect of Reducing Operating Pressure (2 marks):**

If the system pressure is reduced below 745 mmHg (e.g., by attaching a vacuum pump), the boiling condition $P_w^{sat} + P_o^{sat} = P_{system}$ is satisfied at an even **lower** temperature, because both $P_w^{sat}$ and $P_o^{sat}$ are monotonic increasing functions of temperature. The lower the total pressure, the lower $T_m$.

**Maximum Reduction (2 marks):**

The lowest achievable $T_m$ approaches the **boiling point of pure water** at the chosen reduced pressure — water dictates the floor temperature because it has the higher vapor pressure. Beyond this, the steam itself ceases to exist as a liquid phase, and the technique loses its thermodynamic justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read twice before every lab exam.**

- ✅ **Steam distillation** = co-distillation of steam + immiscible organic at $T_m < 100$ °C.
- ✅ **Dalton's Law:** $P_{total} = P_w^{sat} + P_o^{sat}$ — **the most important equation** in this module.
- ✅ The **mixture boiling point $T_m$** is *always* lower than the boiling point of either pure component.
- ✅ **Mass ratio in distillate:** $\dfrac{m_o}{m_w} = \dfrac{P_o^{sat} \cdot M_o}{P_w^{sat} \cdot M_w}$.
- ✅ **Minimum steam required:** $m_s = \dfrac{m_o \cdot M_w \cdot P_w^{sat}}{M_o \cdot (P_{total} - P_w^{sat})}$.
- ✅ **Steam economy** = kg organic obtained per kg steam — used as a performance metric.
- ✅ Two **operating modes**: **direct steam injection** (open steam) and **indirect heating with wet steam** — lab apparatus typically uses direct injection.
- ✅ **Decanter / separating funnel** exploits immiscibility at low T to separate the two condensed liquids.
- ✅ **Safety:** Pressure relief on boiler; insulated steam lines; eye protection; never heat a sealed boiler.
- ✅ **Typical deviations** between theoretical and experimental mass ratios are **2–10 %**; anything above 15 % indicates a procedural error.
- ✅ **Common lab systems:** Turpentine–water, nitrobenzene–water, aniline–water, benzene–water.
- ✅ Always **record barometric pressure** — it directly affects $P_{total}$ and thus all calculations.
- ✅ Use **Antoine constants** to compute $P^{sat}$ when the boiling temperature is not given.
- ✅ The **molar mass** of the organic compound must be in **kg/kmol** if masses are in kg; in **g/mol** if masses are in g — keep units consistent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Extraction - Determination of Ternary Liquid-Liquid Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Liquid Extraction - Determination of Ternary Liquid-Liquid Equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Liquid Extraction – Ternary Liquid–Liquid Equilibria (LLE)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ternary Liquid–Liquid Equilibria (LLE)** is the equilibrium state reached when a three-component liquid system (composed of a **solute**, a **diluent/carrier**, and a **solvent/extractant**) separates into two immiscible liquid phases of different compositions, with the chemical potential of each component being identical in both liquid phases at a constant temperature and pressure.

In a typical KTU laboratory context, the system studied is:
- **Solute (C):** Acetic acid (CH₃COOH)
- **Diluent (A):** Water (H₂O)
- **Solvent (B):** Toluene (C₆H₅CH₃) *or* Benzene (C₆H₆) *or* Chloroform (CHCl₃)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a greasy stain on your shirt.** Water alone cannot remove the oil. However, when you apply a detergent (solvent), the oil (solute) prefers to dissolve in the detergent phase and is pulled away from the fabric fibers. When the wash water settles, you have two distinct layers: clean water (raffinate) and a soapy oil-rich layer (extract).

- The **solute** is the impurity you want to remove (acetic acid).
- The **diluent** is the original liquid carrying the solute (water).
- The **solvent (extractant)** is the immiscible liquid that "captures" the solute (toluene).

At equilibrium, the two phases become **saturated** with each other and the solute distributes itself in a **fixed, reproducible ratio** that is independent of the mixing intensity but depends only on **temperature**.

### 1.3 KTU Syllabus-Highlighted Terminology

| Term | Definition | Phase Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Raffinate Phase (R)** | The diluent-rich phase, lighter/denser depending on densities | Aqueous layer |
| **Extract Phase (E)** | The solvent-rich phase | Organic layer |
| **Binodal Curve** | Locus of all equilibrium compositions separating single-phase & two-phase regions | — |
| **Tie Line** | Straight line connecting the compositions of two conjugate (equilibrium) phases | Connects R & E |
| **Plait Point (P)** | Critical point where both phases become identical in composition | Single point |
| **Distribution Coefficient ($K_D$)** | Ratio of solute concentration in extract to raffinate | $K_D = \dfrac{w_C^E}{w_C^R}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Liquid–liquid extraction rests on the principle that when two immiscible liquids are contacted, a dissolved solute will distribute itself between the two phases according to the **Nernst Distribution Law (1891)**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

- **Step 1 – Two-Phase Formation:** The diluent (A) and solvent (B) are only partially miscible. They form two distinct liquid layers due to differences in polarity, density, and hydrogen bonding.
- **Step 2 – Solute Partitioning:** The solute (C) is soluble in both A and B to different extents. It migrates until the **chemical potential** of C is equal in both phases:

$$\mu_C^E = \mu_C^R$$

- **Step 3 – Equilibrium Saturation:** Once the chemical potential equality is reached, no further net mass transfer occurs. This is the **thermodynamic equilibrium** state.
- **Step 4 – Tie Line Generation:** Every overall mixture composition lying inside the binodal curve splits into two specific equilibrium compositions, connected by a unique tie line.
- **Step 5 – Plait Point Convergence:** As the system approaches the critical composition, the two phases become identical, and tie lines shrink to a single point (the **plait point**).

### 2.3 The Nernst Distribution Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst Distribution Law:** *"At constant temperature, a solute distributes itself between two immiscible liquids in a constant ratio of concentrations (or, more rigorously, activities)."*

$$K_D = \frac{(x_C)^E}{(x_C)^R} = \frac{(w_C)^E}{(w_C)^R}$$

where $x_C$ is the mole fraction and $w_C$ is the mass fraction of solute C in the respective phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Nernst's law is **only valid for dilute solutions** where the solute does not change its molecular state (no dissociation/association). For acetic acid in water–toluene, $K_D$ varies with concentration; it is *not* a true constant. Plot $K_D$ vs $w_C$ to identify the linearity range.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application / KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Distribution Coefficient** | $K_D = \dfrac{w_C^E}{w_C^R}$ | Determines extract purity |
| **Selectivity ($\beta$)** | $\beta = K_D \cdot \dfrac{w_A^E}{w_A^R}$ | Measures separation efficiency |
| **Mass Fraction Constraint** | $w_A + w_B + w_C = 1$ | Closure equation for ternary |
| **Hand's Empirical Equation** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_{C,R}}{x_{A,R}}\right) = a + b \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_{C,E}}{x_{A,E}}\right)$ | Correlates equilibrium data |
| **Othmer–Tobias Equation** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - w_{C,R}}{w_{C,R}}\right) = a + b \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - w_{B,E}}{w_{B,E}}\right)$ | Verifies LLE data consistency |
| **Lever Rule (Mass)** | $\dfrac{M_R}{M_E} = \dfrac{\overline{MM_E}}{\overline{MM_R}}$ | Locate overall mixture on tie line |
| **Plait Point Condition** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 g}{\partial x_C^2} = 0$ | Critical composition point |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Recovery of antibiotics (e.g., penicillin) from fermentation broths using amyl acetate or butyl acetate.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Separation of aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylene) from aliphatic hydrocarbons using sulfolane or furfural.
- **Nuclear Reprocessing:** Extraction of uranium and plutonium from spent fuel using tributyl phosphate (TBP) in kerosene.
- **Food Industry:** Decaffeination of coffee using supercritical CO₂ or ethyl acetate.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Removal of phenols, chlorinated solvents, and organic acids from aqueous effluents.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Rule (Mass Balance Geometry)

Consider an overall mixture M of mass $M$ that separates into raffinate R (mass $M_R$) and extract E (mass $M_E$).

**Step 1: Total Mass Balance**

$$M = M_R + M_E$$

**Step 2: Component (Solute C) Mass Balance**

$$M \cdot w_C^M = M_R \cdot w_C^R + M_E \cdot w_C^E$$

**Step 3: Solve for $M_R/M_E$ Ratio**

$$M_R \cdot w_C^R + M_E \cdot w_C^E = (M_R + M_E) \cdot w_C^M$$

$$M_R (w_C^R - w_C^M) = M_E (w_C^M - w_C^E)$$

$$\frac{M_R}{M_E} = \frac{w_C^M - w_C^E}{w_C^R - w_C^M}$$

**Step 4: Geometric Interpretation**

On a triangular diagram, the segments $M_R M$ and $M M_E$ are inversely proportional to the masses of the conjugate phases (the classic **inverse lever arm rule**).

$$\frac{M_R}{M_E} = \frac{\overline{MM_E}}{\overline{MM_R}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students frequently forget that the lever arm is **inversely** proportional to the phase mass. The longer segment corresponds to the **smaller** phase mass.

## 3.2 Derivation of Hand's Empirical Equation

Hand (1930) observed that for a wide variety of ternary systems, the equilibrium data could be correlated linearly by plotting $\ln(x_{C,R}/x_{A,R})$ vs $\ln(x_{C,E}/x_{A,E})$.

**Step 1:** Start from the thermodynamic criterion of equality of activities:

$$\gamma_C^R \cdot x_C^R = \gamma_C^E \cdot x_C^E$$

**Step 2:** Assume that the activity coefficients in each phase follow a regular solution model (van Laar / Scatchard–Hildebrand). For solute C in the presence of diluent A and solvent B:

$$\ln \gamma_C^R = f\!\left(x_A^R, x_C^R\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \ln \gamma_C^E = f\!\left(x_A^E, x_C^E\right)$$

**Step 3:** For highly dilute systems where C and A are predominantly in the raffinate, and C and B in the extract, the Hand correlation reduces to:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{C,R}}{x_{A,R}}\right) = a + b \ln\!\left(\frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{A,E}}\right)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants obtained from a least-squares regression of the experimental tie-line data.

**Step 4:** A **Hand plot** of $\ln(x_{C,R}/x_{A,R})$ vs $\ln(x_{C,E}/x_{A,E})$ should yield a **straight line** with slope $b$ and intercept $a$. The fit quality (regression coefficient $R^2$) validates the experimental procedure.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Typical KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:** In a ternary equilibrium study of the system **Acetic Acid (C) – Water (A) – Toluene (B)** at **298 K**, the following tie-line data were obtained. Verify the data using **Hand's correlation**, calculate the **distribution coefficient $K_D$** at each tie line, and comment on the trend.

| Tie Line | $w_C^R$ (Raffinate) | $w_A^R$ (Raffinate) | $w_C^E$ (Extract) | $w_A^E$ (Extract) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.080 | 0.910 | 0.050 | 0.005 |
| 2 | 0.150 | 0.840 | 0.095 | 0.010 |
| 3 | 0.220 | 0.770 | 0.140 | 0.018 |
| 4 | 0.300 | 0.690 | 0.190 | 0.030 |

**Step A: Convert mass fractions to the Hand's plot variables.**

| Tie Line | $x_{C,R}/x_{A,R}$ | $x_{C,E}/x_{A,E}$ | $\ln(x_{C,R}/x_{A,R})$ | $\ln(x_{C,E}/x_{A,E})$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.0879 | 10.000 | −2.431 | 2.303 |
| 2 | 0.1786 | 9.500 | −1.723 | 2.251 |
| 3 | 0.2857 | 7.778 | −1.253 | 2.051 |
| 4 | 0.4348 | 6.333 | −0.833 | 1.846 |

*Sample Calculation for Tie Line 1:*
$$\frac{x_{C,R}}{x_{A,R}} = \frac{0.080}{0.910} = 0.0879 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(0.0879) = -2.431$$

$$\frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{A,E}} = \frac{0.050}{0.005} = 10.000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(10.000) = 2.303$$

**Step B: Linear Regression of Hand's Plot.**

Using least-squares regression on the four data points:

$$\sum X_i = 2.303 + 2.251 + 2.051 + 1.846 = 8.451$$

$$\sum Y_i = -2.431 - 1.723 - 1.253 - 0.833 = -6.240$$

$$\sum X_i Y_i = (2.303)(-2.431) + (2.251)(-1.723) + (2.051)(-1.253) + (1.846)(-0.833) = -5.599 - 3.879 - 2.570 - 1.538 = -13.586$$

$$\sum X_i^2 = 2.303^2 + 2.251^2 + 2.051^2 + 1.846^2 = 5.304 + 5.067 + 4.207 + 3.408 = 17.986$$

**Slope $b$:**
$$b = \frac{n\sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2} = \frac{4(-13.586) - (8.451)(-6.240)}{4(17.986) - (8.451)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{-54.344 + 52.734}{71.944 - 71.419} = \frac{-1.610}{0.525} = -3.067$$

**Intercept $a$:**
$$a = \frac{\sum Y_i - b \sum X_i}{n} = \frac{-6.240 - (-3.067)(8.451)}{4} = \frac{-6.240 + 25.920}{4} = 4.920$$

**Hand's Equation (Final Form):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{C,R}}{x_{A,R}}\right) = 4.920 - 3.067 \ln\!\left(\frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{A,E}}\right)$$

**Step C: Distribution Coefficient ($K_D$) Calculation.**

| Tie Line | $K_D = w_C^E / w_C^R$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $0.050 / 0.080 = 0.625$ |
| 2 | $0.095 / 0.150 = 0.633$ |
| 3 | $0.140 / 0.220 = 0.636$ |
| 4 | $0.190 / 0.300 = 0.633$ |

**Step D: Trend Analysis and Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distribution coefficient $K_D \approx 0.63$ is **nearly constant** across the concentration range studied, indicating that the Nernst Distribution Law is reasonably obeyed for this system at 298 K. The Hand plot shows a good straight-line fit, confirming the **thermodynamic consistency** of the experimentally determined tie-line data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** In a 14-mark question, examiners allocate **2 marks for the Hand plot interpretation**, **3 marks for the regression calculation**, **2 marks for $K_D$ values**, and **3 marks for the engineering conclusion**. Skipping the regression step loses 3 marks easily.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Plait Point (Critical Solution Point)

The plait point is the unique composition where both conjugate phases converge. Mathematically, it corresponds to the **inflection point** of the binodal curve where the second derivative of the Gibbs free energy of mixing vanishes:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 \bar{g}^M}{\partial x_C^2}\right)_{T,P} = 0$$

At this point:
- $w_C^E = w_C^R$
- $w_A^E = w_A^R$
- $w_B^E = w_B^R$
- Tie line length $\to 0$

A common empirical method to locate the plait point is the **Schreinemaker's wet residue method** or extrapolation of the Hand's plot to a 45° reference line.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the ternary LLE experiment, covering chemical preparation, equilibrium contact, phase separation, sampling, analysis, and data interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: 298 K, 1 atm]) --> ChemPrep[Chemical Preparation: Acetic Acid, Water, Toluene, NaOH titrant, Phenolphthalein indicator]
    ChemPrep --> MixStage[Mixing Stage: 250 mL separating funnel with stopper]
    MixStage --> Shaking[Intensive Shaking: 5 minutes for mass transfer equilibrium]
    Shaking --> Settling[Phase Settling: 15-30 min in ring stand until clear interface]
    Settling --> InterfaceCheck{Interface Sharp?}
    InterfaceCheck -- No --> Shaking
    InterfaceCheck -- Yes --> DrainR[Drain Raffinate Phase: Aqueous layer bottom]
    DrainR --> DrainE[Collect Extract Phase: Organic layer top]
    DrainR --> SampleR[Weigh 5 g Raffinate Sample]
    DrainE --> SampleE[Weigh 5 g Extract Sample]
    SampleR --> Titration1[Titrate Raffinate with 0.1 N NaOH]
    SampleE --> Titration2[Titrate Extract with 0.1 N NaOH]
    Titration1 --> CalcR[Calculate w_C^R from titration volume]
    Titration2 --> CalcE[Calculate w_C^E from titration volume]
    CalcR --> ClosureR[Apply Closure: w_A^R = 1 - w_B^R - w_C^R]
    CalcE --> ClosureE[Apply Closure: w_A^E = 1 - w_B^E - w_C^E]
    ClosureR --> PlotPhase[Plot Tie Line on Triangular Diagram]
    ClosureE --> PlotPhase
    PlotPhase --> RepeatExp[Repeat for 4-5 Different Mixtures to get Multiple Tie Lines]
    RepeatExp --> BinodalFit[Fit Binodal Curve through all data points]
    BinodalFit --> HandPlot[Construct Hand Plot for Consistency Check]
    HandPlot --> Result([Result: Validated LLE Data, K_D, Plait Point])
```

## 4.2 Triangular Phase Diagram — Schematic Representation

The diagram below represents the standard equilateral triangular composition chart used to plot ternary LLE data. Each vertex represents a **pure component** (100% A, B, or C). Points inside the triangle represent mixtures; the **binodal curve** encloses the two-phase region; **tie lines** connect conjugate phases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TrianglePhaseDiagram[Equilateral Triangular Phase Diagram - Ternary System ABC]
        V_A[Vertex A: 100 percent Diluent / Water] --- V_B[Vertex B: 100 percent Solvent / Toluene]
        V_B --- V_C[Vertex C: 100 percent Solute / Acetic Acid]
        V_C --- V_A
        BinoCurve[Binodal Curve: encloses two-phase region, dome-shaped] -.-> V_A
        BinoCurve -.-> V_B
        BinoCurve -.-> V_C
        P1[Point P1: Single-phase homogeneous region] --- BinoCurve
        P2[Point P2: Single-phase homogeneous region] --- BinoCurve
        P3[Point P3: Single-phase homogeneous region] --- BinoCurve
        R1[Conjugate Raffinate Point R1 on binodal] --- T1[Tie Line 1]
        E1[Conjugate Extract Point E1 on binodal] --- T1
        R2[Conjugate Raffinate Point R2 on binodal] --- T2[Tie Line 2]
        E2[Conjugate Extract Point E2 on binodal] --- T2
        Plait[Plait Point P: critical point, tie lines converge] --- BinoCurve
    end
```

## 4.3 Sample Titration Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Titrant[Volume of NaOH used, V mL] --> Normality[Normality of NaOH, N]
    Normality --> Eq1[Equivalents of Acid = N x V / 1000]
    Eq1 --> Eq2[Mass of Acetic Acid = Eq1 x 60.05 g]
    Eq2 --> Eq3[w_C = Mass of Acid / Mass of Sample]
    Eq3 --> Closure[Closure Equation: w_A = 1 - w_C - w_B]
    Closure --> Plot[Plot on Triangular Diagram]
```

---
