## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Stage Cross-Current Material Balance

**Setup:** A feed containing $L_0$ kg of liquid (with $x_0$ mass fraction of solute) is contacted in **one stage** with $V$ kg of fresh solvent (containing $y_{in} = 0$ mass fraction of solute). After equilibrium, the overflow has composition $y_1$ and the underflow has composition $x_1$, with constant $L$.

**Step 1 — Draw the control volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In:} \quad & L_0 \, (\text{liquid}, x_0) + V \, (\text{solvent}, y_{\text{in}} = 0) \\
\text{Out:} \quad & V \, (\text{overflow}, y_1) + L_1 \, (\text{underflow}, x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass balance on liquid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 + V &= V + L_1 \\
\Rightarrow L_1 &= L_0 \quad \text{(if solvent is fully recovered in overflow)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For constant-underflow assumption, $L_1 = L_0 = L$.

**Step 3 — Solute (A) mass balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Solute In} &= \text{Solute Out} \\
L \cdot x_0 + V \cdot 0 &= V \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_1 \\
L \cdot x_0 &= V \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply ideal equilibrium $y_1 = x_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cdot x_0 &= V \cdot x_1 + L \cdot x_1 \\
L \cdot x_0 &= x_1 \cdot (L + V) \\
x_1 &= \dfrac{L \cdot x_0}{L + V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Fractional solute remaining:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{A_1}{A_0} = \dfrac{x_1}{x_0} = \dfrac{L}{L + V}\;}
$$

This is the **per-stage extraction factor** $\beta = \dfrac{L}{L+V}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Extension to N Stages (Geometric Decay)

**Setup:** Stage 1 produces underflow of composition $x_1$, which becomes the feed to Stage 2 (with $x_2$ produced), and so on. Each stage receives fresh solvent $V$.

**Step 1 — Apply the single-stage result recursively:**

$$
x_1 = x_0 \cdot \dfrac{L}{L+V}, \quad x_2 = x_1 \cdot \dfrac{L}{L+V}, \quad \dots, \quad x_n = x_{n-1} \cdot \dfrac{L}{L+V}
$$

**Step 2 — Telescope the recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^1 \\
x_2 &= x_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right) = x_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
x_N &= x_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\;x_N = x_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^{N} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{A_N}{A_0} = \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^{N}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for N (when target $x_N$ or $A_N$ is given):**

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_N}{A_0}\right) &= N \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right) \\
\Rightarrow N &= \dfrac{\ln(A_N/A_0)}{\ln[L/(L+V)]} = \dfrac{\ln(1 - \eta_{\text{ext}})}{\ln[L/(L+V)]}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**

> *A granular solid containing **25 % by mass of oil** and **75 % inerts** is to be leached in a **3-stage cross-current** system. The underflow has a **constant liquid-to-solid ratio of 2 kg liquid per kg solid**. The mass of fresh solvent used in each stage is **equal to the mass of solute-free solid**. Assuming ideal equilibrium ($y = x$), calculate:*
> *(i) The fraction of oil remaining unextracted.*
> *(ii) The fraction of oil extracted overall.*
> *(iii) The composition of the final underflow.*

**Step 1 — Choose a basis.**

Since $B$ (inerts) is invariant, take **basis = 1 kg of inerts (B)**.

**Step 2 — Determine feed composition.**

Feed is 25 % oil and 75 % inerts by mass. Per 1 kg inerts, feed mass = $1/0.75 = 1.333$ kg, of which oil = $0.25 \times 1.333 = 0.3333$ kg.

Solute in feed: $A_0 = 0.3333$ kg per kg inerts.

**Step 3 — Determine L and V.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= N_s \cdot B = 2 \cdot 1 = 2 \;\text{kg (per kg inerts)} \\
V &= 1 \cdot B = 1 \;\text{kg (per kg inerts, per stage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute extraction factor per stage.**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{L}{L + V} = \dfrac{2}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 5 — Apply N-stage formula for N = 3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{A_N}{A_0} &= \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^{N} = \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^3 = \dfrac{8}{27} = 0.2963
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the requested quantities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Fraction unextracted:} \quad & \dfrac{A_N}{A_0} = 0.2963 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 29.63\% \\
\text{(ii) Fraction extracted:} \quad & \eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - 0.2963 = 0.7037 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 70.37\% \\
\text{(iii) Final underflow composition:} \quad & x_N = x_0 \cdot (2/3)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $x_0$:

$$
x_0 = \dfrac{A_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{0.3333}{2} = 0.1667
$$

Then:

$$
x_N = 0.1667 \cdot 0.2963 = 0.0494
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity check via stage-by-stage iteration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0.1667 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.1111 \\
x_2 &= 0.1111 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.0741 \\
x_3 &= 0.0741 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.0494 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Final results table.**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $x_0$ (initial underflow composition) | **0.1667** |
| $x_3$ (final underflow composition) | **0.0494** |
| Mass solute unextracted per kg inerts | $L \cdot x_3 = 2 \cdot 0.0494 = 0.0988$ kg |
| Mass solute initially per kg inerts | $0.3333$ kg |
| % Unextracted | **29.63 %** |
| % Extracted | **70.37 %** |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single Stage vs Cross-Current

| Feature | Single Stage | Cross-Current (N stages) | Counter-Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solvent usage | Low | High (V per stage) | Moderate |
| Extract concentration | High (rich) | Dilute (combined extracts) | Moderate–High |
| Number of stages needed | Large (impractical) | Fewer than single-stage per extraction | **Minimum** stages |
| Equipment complexity | Trivial | Moderate | Tall column / train |
| Driving force (per stage) | Decays within stage | **Maximized** in every stage | Decays along column |
| KTU exam weightage | High | **Very High** (this topic) | High (next module) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define leaching. With a neat sketch, differentiate between single-stage and cross-current multi-stage leaching operations.*

**Model Answer:**
Leaching is a solid-liquid extraction process in which a soluble component (solute) is selectively dissolved from an insoluble solid matrix (inerts) using a suitable solvent. In **single-stage leaching**, the solid feed and the entire solvent charge are contacted once in a single vessel; the overflow extract and the underflow residue are separated at equilibrium. In **cross-current multi-stage leaching**, the solid is passed sequentially through N stages, while **each stage independently receives a fresh portion of solvent**. This maximizes the concentration driving force in every stage but produces a combined extract that is more dilute than in a single-stage operation. *(3 marks — definition 1 mark, sketch 1 mark, differentiation 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*What is meant by "underflow" and "overflow" in a leaching operation? Why is the mass of insoluble solid (inerts) considered constant across all stages?*

**Model Answer:**
The **underflow** is the stream leaving the bottom of a leaching stage, consisting of the insoluble solid plus the liquid retained within the solid pores (solvent + dissolved solute). The **overflow** is the clarified liquid leaving the top, consisting of solvent plus the extracted solute. The mass of insoluble solid (inerts, denoted $B$) remains constant because the solvent does not dissolve the inert matrix — only the solute is transferred from the solid to the liquid phase. Therefore, $B$ acts as a convenient basis for all material balances across all stages. *(3 marks — definitions 2 marks, reasoning 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Two alternatives Q(A) and Q(B) — answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B). Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Material Balance Derivation + Numerical**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Derive the material balance equation for a single-stage cross-current leaching operation starting from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. Constant underflow liquid mass $L$ (independent of $x$).
3. Ideal equilibrium: overflow composition = underflow composition, $y_1 = x_1$.
4. Fresh solvent contains zero solute, $y_{\text{in}} = 0$.
5. Constant temperature; no solvent loss.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a single stage with feed liquid $L$ (composition $x_0$) and fresh solvent $V$ (composition $0$). Underflow exits at $L, x_1$; overflow exits at $V, y_1$.

Total liquid balance:

$$
L + V = L + V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{(automatically satisfied)}
$$

Solute (A) balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cdot x_0 + V \cdot 0 &= L \cdot x_1 + V \cdot y_1 \\
L \cdot x_0 &= L \cdot x_1 + V \cdot y_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply $y_1 = x_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cdot x_0 &= L \cdot x_1 + V \cdot x_1 \\
L \cdot x_0 &= (L + V) \cdot x_1 \\
x_1 &= \dfrac{L \cdot x_0}{L + V} \\
\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_1}{A_0} &= \dfrac{L}{L + V} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** A solid containing **30 % by mass of soluble oil** and **70 % inerts** is to be leached in a **4-stage cross-current** system. The underflow has a **constant liquid-to-solid ratio of 1.5 kg liquid per kg solid**. The mass of fresh solvent used in each stage is **1.0 kg per kg inerts**. Assuming ideal equilibrium, calculate:
   (i) The mass fraction of oil in the final underflow.
   (ii) The percentage of oil extracted overall.
   (iii) The number of additional stages required to reach **90 % extraction**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Basis:** 1 kg of inerts $B = 1$ kg.

**Step 2 — Initial conditions (1 mark):**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{30}{70} = 0.4286 \;\text{kg oil per kg inerts}
$$

$$
L = 1.5 \cdot B = 1.5 \;\text{kg}, \quad V = 1.0 \cdot B = 1.0 \;\text{kg per stage}
$$

$$
x_0 = \dfrac{A_0}{L} = \dfrac{0.4286}{1.5} = 0.2857
$$

**Step 3 — Extraction factor (1 mark):**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{L}{L + V} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.5 + 1.0} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6
$$

**Step 4 — After 4 stages (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_4 &= x_0 \cdot \beta^4 = 0.2857 \cdot (0.6)^4 = 0.2857 \cdot 0.1296 \\
x_4 &= 0.0370
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Final underflow composition = 0.0370 (mass fraction of oil in retained liquid).**

**(ii) Percentage extracted (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704 = 87.04\%
$$

**(iii) Number of stages for 90 % extraction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \beta^N &= 0.90 \\
\beta^N &= 0.10 \\
(0.6)^N &= 0.10 \\
N \cdot \ln(0.6) &= \ln(0.10) \\
N &= \dfrac{\ln(0.10)}{\ln(0.6)} = \dfrac{-2.3026}{-0.5108} = 4.51
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer (whole stage), round up: **$N = 5$ stages**.

**Additional stages required = $5 - 4 = 1$ additional stage.**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Final $x_4$ | **0.0370** |
| Extraction in 4 stages | **87.04 %** |
| Stages for 90 % extraction | **5 stages** |
| Additional stages needed | **1 stage** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Equipment + Alternative Numerical**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the working principle of a **multi-stage cross-current leaching apparatus** used in chemical engineering laboratories. Clearly describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes — draw a labelled sketch on the answer sheet showing Solvent Tank → Stage 1 (with stirrer + heating jacket) → Stage 2 → Stage 3 → Extract Collection + Final Residue Tray.)*

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A measured mass of solid feed (containing solute + inerts) is charged into Stage 1. A measured volume of fresh solvent is added, and the mixture is agitated for a fixed contact time. Agitation ensures intimate contact between solvent and solid, enhancing mass transfer. After equilibrium, the mixture is allowed to settle; the clear overflow (extract) is decanted into a collection beaker. The wet underflow is manually transferred to Stage 2, where it is again contacted with a fresh portion of solvent. This sequence repeats for all $N$ stages. Each stage operates at the maximum possible concentration driving force because fresh solvent is used, ensuring high per-stage efficiency.

**Component Roles (2 marks):**
- **Solvent Storage Tank:** holds bulk solvent; provides controlled dosing via valve.
- **Leaching Vessel (Stage 1, 2, ..., N):** baffled tank with paddle stirrer for solid-liquid contact; heating jacket for temperature control.
- **Sampling Port:** allows withdrawal of overflow and underflow for analysis.
- **Extract Collection Beaker:** gathers all overflow streams for combined volume/assay.
- **Residue Collection Tray:** receives the final underflow for weighing and solute assay.
- **Stopwatch/Timer:** standardizes contact time across stages for reproducible results.

---

**(b)** In a cross-current leaching experiment, **1000 kg of oil-bearing solid containing 28 % oil and 72 % inerts** is treated with **800 kg of solvent per stage** in a **4-stage operation**. The underflow has a constant ratio of **1.6 kg liquid per kg solid**. Assuming ideal equilibrium ($y = x$), determine:
   (i) The mass of oil extracted in each stage.
   (ii) The total mass of oil extracted in all 4 stages.
   (iii) The % oil remaining unextracted. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Basis (0.5 mark):** Per kg inerts (B = 1 kg). Then scale to 1000 kg feed at the end.

Actually, let me work directly: Total inerts $B = 0.72 \times 1000 = 720$ kg.

**Step 2 — Per kg inerts (1 mark):**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{0.28}{0.72} = 0.3889 \;\text{kg oil per kg inerts}
$$

$$
L = 1.6 \cdot B = 1.6 \;\text{kg per kg inerts}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{800}{720} = 1.111 \;\text{kg per kg inerts, per stage}
$$

**Step 3 — Extraction factor (1 mark):**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{L}{L + V} = \dfrac{1.6}{1.6 + 1.111} = \dfrac{1.6}{2.711} = 0.5902
$$

**Step 4 — Stage-by-stage oil remaining (per kg inerts) (2 marks):**

Using $A_n = A_0 \cdot \beta^n$:

| Stage $n$ | $A_n = A_0 \cdot \beta^n$ (kg/kg inert) | Oil extracted in this stage |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (feed) | 0.3889 | — |
| 1 | $0.3889 \times 0.5902 = 0.2295$ | $0.3889 - 0.2295 = 0.1594$ |
| 2 | $0.2295 \times 0.5902 = 0.1354$ | $0.2295 - 0.1354 = 0.0941$ |
| 3 | $0.1354 \times 0.5902 = 0.0799$ | $0.1354 - 0.0799 = 0.0555$ |
| 4 | $0.0799 \times 0.5902 = 0.0472$ | $0.0799 - 0.0472 = 0.0327$ |

**Step 5 — Scale to 720 kg inerts (1 mark):**

| Stage | Oil extracted (kg) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $0.1594 \times 720 = 114.77$ |
| 2 | $0.0941 \times 720 = 67.75$ |
| 3 | $0.0555 \times 720 = 39.96$ |
| 4 | $0.0327 \times 720 = 23.54$ |

**(i) Oil extracted per stage: 114.77 kg, 67.75 kg, 39.96 kg, 23.54 kg.**

**(ii) Total oil extracted (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Total} = 114.77 + 67.75 + 39.96 + 23.54 = 246.02 \;\text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Percentage oil unextracted (0.5 mark):**

$$
\% \text{ unextracted} = \beta^4 \times 100\% = (0.5902)^4 \times 100\% = 12.13\%
$$

**Verification:** Initial oil = $0.28 \times 1000 = 280$ kg. Extracted = 246.02 kg. Remaining = $280 - 246.02 = 33.98$ kg. $\% = 33.98/280 = 12.14\%$ ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — top 5 mistakes:**
> 1. **Basis confusion (most common):** Students take basis as 100 kg of *feed* instead of 1 kg of *inerts*. This forces them to redo all calculations when $V$ or $L$ is given per kg inerts. **Always choose basis = 1 kg of inerts (B) when $L/B$ or $V/B$ ratios are given.**
> 2. **Forgetting $y_{in} = 0$:** The fresh solvent has zero solute. Including a non-zero $y_{in}$ breaks the simple formula.
> 3. **Mixing up underflow/overflow composition symbols:** $x$ is always underflow, $y$ is always overflow. Reversing them in the material balance leads to wrong signs.
> 4. **Rounding N incorrectly:** $N$ must be an integer. If calculated $N = 4.51$, write "$N = 5$ (rounded up, since partial stages are not possible in cross-current discrete operation)."
> 5. **Unit consistency:** $L$ and $V$ must be in the **same units** (both kg or both per kg inerts) before plugging into the formula $L/(L+V)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Leaching** = selective dissolution of a soluble constituent from a solid using a solvent.
- **Inerts (B)** are constant across all stages — use as the basis of calculation.
- **Cross-current** = each stage gets fresh solvent; solid moves in one direction.
- **Counter-current** (next module) = solvent enters one end, solid enters the other; more solvent-efficient.
- **Key assumption:** Ideal equilibrium → $y_n = x_n$.
- **Master formula:** $\dfrac{A_N}{A_0} = \left(\dfrac{L}{L+V}\right)^N$.
- **Per-stage extraction factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{L}{L+V}$ (always $\leq 1$).
- **% Extraction:** $\eta = 1 - \beta^N$.
- **Number of stages:** $N = \dfrac{\ln(A_N/A_0)}{\ln \beta} = \dfrac{\ln(1-\eta)}{\ln \beta}$ (round up to next integer).
- **Combined extract concentration:** low (dilute), because each stage's extract is mixed.
- **Constant-underflow assumption** lets $L$ be treated as a fixed number throughout all stages.
- **Lab procedure (PCBBL507):** Weigh feed → load Stage 1 → add measured solvent → stir → settle → decant extract → transfer solid to Stage 2 → repeat → weigh & assay final residue → calculate % extraction.
- **Common units:** kg, kg/kg, °C, minutes (contact time), rpm (stirrer speed).
- **Always report:** feed composition, $L/B$ ratio, $V/B$ ratio, number of stages, % extraction, final $x_N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter Current Leaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Counter Current Leaching ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Leaching** is a solid–liquid extraction operation in which a soluble constituent (the **solute**) is selectively dissolved and removed from a solid matrix (the **inert/insoluble solid**) by means of a liquid **solvent**. When the solvent and the solid are made to flow in **opposite directions** through a series of stages, the operation is termed **Counter-Current Leaching** (or Counter-Current Solid–Liquid Extraction). Mathematically, the operation seeks to **maximize the concentration difference (Δy or Δx) — the driving force for mass transfer — at every stage**, by ensuring that the most concentrated solid meets the freshest (leanest) solvent and the most depleted solvent meets the freshest solid feed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Counter-current leaching is a continuous, multistage solid–liquid extraction process in which the solid feed and the solvent travel in opposite directions through a cascade of ideal stages, with the objective of transferring a maximum amount of solute into the solvent (overflow) while producing a residual solid (underflow) of minimum solute content.* ### 1.2 The Three Component System Every leaching system is described by **three component flows**: | Component | Symbol | Role | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Solute (A) | $A$ | The material to be extracted (e.g., oil, sugar, caffeine) | | Inert Solid (B) | $B$ | The insoluble carrier (e.g., crushed seeds, bagasse) | | Solvent (C) | $C$ | The extraction liquid (e.g., hexane, hot water) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Used Tea Bag" vs. "Industrial Continuous Extractor" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (Imagine this):** > Picture a long queue of tea bags being pulled through a long tank of hot water, but in the *opposite* direction to the water flow. The bag that has been in the water the longest (and therefore contains the least tea) meets the *fresh* water entering the tank. The *fresh* tea bag entering the tank meets the water that has already absorbed tea from the previous bags — i.e., the *richest* water. This is **counter-current leaching**. Because concentration gradients are maintained at every point, this arrangement extracts *far more* tea per litre of water than simply dunking a single bag in a cup (a **single-stage** operation). ### 1.4 Industrial Examples - **Oilseeds** (soybean, groundnut) extracted with **hexane**. - **Sugar** extracted from **sugar beet cossettes** with hot water (**Betté / DDS diffuser**). - **Caffeine** extracted from **coffee beans** with supercritical CO₂. - **Tannin / dyes** leached from **bark** or **wood chips**. ### 1.5 Why Counter-Current and Not Co-Current? In **co-current** flow, the rich solid and rich solvent exit the same end; the final mass-transfer driving force is poor, and stage efficiency is wasted. In **counter-current** flow, **the terminal streams (N-th stage solid and 1st stage solvent) carry the minimum residual solute concentration**, leading to a near-uniformly high driving force across all stages. > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway for lab:** In Experiment 2, you will operate a small multi-stage counter-current leaching rig, take overflow and underflow samples at each stage, plot the operating line, and verify the number of ideal stages required for a specified terminal composition. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Exit Streams — Overflow & Underflow At every stage two streams leave: 1. **Overflow (V)** — clear solution phase, contains only **solute (A)** and **solvent (C)**. *No inert solid.* 2. **Underflow (L)** — slurry phase, contains all the **inert solid (B)** plus some retained **solution (A + C)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition: Solution retained by the solid (underflow).** > The solid particles leaving any stage carry a certain amount of solution with them. If $N$ kg solution is held per kg of inert solid $B$, then for every kg of $B$ there is $N$ kg of solution of composition $y$ (solute/solvent ratio in the solution). ### 2.2 The Three Standard Bases Used in Leaching Calculations Because leaching involves a solid that is *never* completely drained, calculations are performed on three equivalent bases: | Basis | Symbol | Definition | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Mass ratio in **overflow** | $y$ | kg solute $A$ / kg solvent $C$ in clear solution | | Mass ratio in **underflow** solution | $x$ | kg solute $A$ / kg solvent $C$ in the *retained* solution (≡ composition of retained solution) | | Solution-to-solid ratio | $N$ | kg solution retained / kg inert solid $B$ | ### 2.3 Material Balance Across One Ideal Stage (Single-Stage Leaching) For a single equilibrium stage with feed $F$ (composition $x_F$ on solvent-free retained solution basis) and fresh solvent $S$ (composition $y_S$): $$ V \, y_V + L \, x_L = F \, x_F + S \, y_S $$ Subject to the **equilibrium tie** that the exiting underflow solution has the *same composition* as the exiting overflow (an **ideal stage** assumption): $$ x_L = y_V $$ Substituting and solving for the **mass of solvent $S$ required per kg of feed** gives the working single-stage equation: $$ S = \dfrac{F\,(x_F - y_V)}{y_V - y_S} $$ ### 2.4 The N + 1 Streams Rule A counter-current cascade of $N$ ideal stages has exactly **$N + 1$ streams** crossing the boundary of the cascade envelope. The two terminal streams are the **rich solvent leaving stage 1** ($V_1$, with composition $y_1$) and the **spent solid leaving stage N** ($L_N$, with composition $x_N$). > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiners love this rule.** It is the foundation of writing the operating line for any counter-current mass-transfer cascade. ### 2.5 Operating Line for Counter-Current Leaching Applying an **overall solute balance from stage 1 through stage $n$** (with $n < N$): $$ L_0 \, x_0 - V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = L_n \, x_n - V_1 \, y_1 $$ If the underflow retains a *constant* mass of solution per kg inert solid (i.e., $L_n \propto$ amount of $B$, which is constant throughout the cascade), the **operating line** becomes a **straight line** on $x$–$y$ coordinates: $$ y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L_n}{V_{n+1}}\,x_n \;+\; \dfrac{V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0}{V_{n+1}} $$ In the simplified form (constant underflow $L$, constant overflow $V$): $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n \;+\; \left(y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N\right)\,} $$ This straight line, plotted against the **equilibrium curve $y^* = f(x)$** in the leaching domain, allows the **graphical (McCabe–Thiele) determination of stages**. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > Memorise this table verbatim — at least 60 % of the 14-mark numericals in Heat & Mass Transfer Operations Lab ask for these relations. | # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | Units (SI) | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Mass ratio in solution (overflow) | $y = \dfrac{\text{kg } A}{\text{kg } C}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Mass ratio in underflow solution | $x = \dfrac{\text{kg } A}{\text{kg } C}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Solution retained ratio | $N = \dfrac{\text{kg solution}}{\text{kg inert } B}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Single-stage solvent requirement | $S = \dfrac{F(x_F - y_V)}{y_V - y_S}$ | kg solvent | | 5 | Number of streams in cascade | $= N + 1$ | — | | 6 | Counter-current operating line | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + \left(y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N\right)$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Operating-line slope | $\dfrac{L}{V}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Operating-line intercept | $y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Slope of equilibrium line (linear) | $y^* = m\,x$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Kremser / stage-efficiency equation | $\dfrac{x_N - y_{S}/m}{x_F - y_S/m} = \left(\dfrac{L}{mV}\right)^{N+1}$ | dimensionless | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of beet-diffusers** in sugar industry — determines diffuser length and rotation speed. - **Sizing of Soxhlet extractors** in pharmaceutical and natural-product labs. - **Scale-up of oilseed extraction plants** — solvent-to-meal ratio governs capital cost. - **Environmental remediation** — washing of contaminated soils in counter-current columns. - **Battery-material recycling** — leaching of Li/Co from spent cathodes. > [!NOTE] > In **KTU lab report viva**, you may be asked: *"Why is counter-current preferred over co-current in a commercial extractor?"* The crisp one-line answer: *Because it preserves a uniform concentration gradient (driving force) across all stages, yielding the maximum extraction with the minimum solvent.* ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating Line of a Counter-Current Leaching Cascade **Step 1 — Define the cascade envelope.** Consider $N$ ideal stages numbered 1 (the *solvent* entry end) to $N$ (the *solid* entry end). Streams entering the envelope from outside: fresh solvent $S$ with composition $y_S$ (at stage 1) and fresh solid feed $F$ with composition $x_F$ (at stage $N$). Streams leaving the envelope: overflow $V_1$ (composition $y_1$) at stage 1 and underflow $L_N$ (composition $x_N$) at stage $N$. **Step 2 — Overall solute balance on the entire cascade.** $$ F \, x_F + S \, y_S = V_1 \, y_1 + L_N \, x_N $$ **Step 3 — Partial balance from stage 1 to stage $n$.** Streams *entering* this sub-envelope: solvent $S$ and the portion of the feed solid that has not yet reached stage $n$. Streams *leaving* the sub-envelope: $V_1$ and the underflow $L_n$ going to stage $n+1$. $$ L_0 \, x_0 + V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} \;(\text{or equivalent}) \quad \Rightarrow $$ A more elegant derivation uses the constant underflow assumption ($L_n = L$ for all $n$). Writing the solute balance for the section between the right-hand boundary and stage $n$: $$ L_N \, x_N + S \, y_S = L_n \, x_n + V_1 \, y_1 \quad \text{(rearranging)} $$ Wait — the cleaner route is to use the **stage-to-stage balance**. For stage $n$ alone: $$ L_{n+1} \, x_{n+1} + V_{n-1} \, y_{n-1} = L_n \, x_n + V_n \, y_n $$ Since an ideal stage gives $x_n = y_n$ (underflow solution leaves in equilibrium with the overflow): $$ L_{n+1} \, x_{n+1} + V_{n-1} \, y_{n-1} = L_n \, x_n + V_n \, y_n $$ **Step 4 — Eliminate the unknowns using constant $L$ and $V$.** With $L_{n+1}=L_n=L$ and $V_{n-1}=V_n=V$: $$ L \, x_{n+1} + V \, y_{n-1} = L \, x_n + V \, y_n $$ Rearrange: $$ V \, (y_n - y_{n-1}) = L \, (x_{n+1} - x_n) $$ Divide by $V$: $$ y_n - y_{n-1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_{n+1} - x_n) $$ **Step 5 — Sum (telescope) from $k=1$ to $k=n$.** $$ \sum_{k=1}^{n} (y_k - y_{k-1}) = \dfrac{L}{V}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{k+1} - x_k) $$ The left side telescopes to $y_n - y_0$ and the right side to $\frac{L}{V}(x_{n+1} - x_1)$. Note that $y_0 \equiv y_S$ (solvent entering) and $x_1 \equiv x_N$ (wait — we must be careful with the indexing convention; using the *standard* KTU convention where stage 1 is the solvent-entry end): $$ y_{n+1} - y_S = \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_{n+1} - x_n) $$ Re-indexing by substituting $m = n+1$: $$ y_m - y_S = \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_m - x_{m-1}) $$ After rearrangement (and noting $y_m = y_{n+1}$ enters stage $n$ from above, $x_m$ leaves stage $n$ as underflow, $x_{m-1}=x_n$ enters stage $n$ from the right), the **final operating-line form** is: $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + y_S - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_S\,} $$ where $y_S$ is the solvent composition (typically $=0$ for fresh solvent) and $x_S$ is the composition of the *retained* solution in the fresh solid feed (often $\equiv x_F$ for the special case of dry solid). For the conventional $y$–$x$ plot with **fresh solvent** ($y_S = 0$): $$ \boxed{\,y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N\,} $$ **Step 6 — Physical interpretation.** The operating line is **straight**, has **slope $L/V < 1$** (because $L$, the underflow carrying retained solution, is typically larger than $V$, the overflow), and connects the point $(x_N,\, y_1)$ to $(x_{F},\, y_S)$ on the $x$–$y$ diagram. The **number of ideal stages** is obtained by stepping off triangles between the operating line and the equilibrium curve, exactly as in McCabe–Thiele distillation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Leaching When the equilibrium relation is **linear**, $y^* = m\,x$, an algebraic (non-graphical) expression for $x_N$ (the *spent solid* composition) can be derived. This is the famous **Kremser equation**. **Step 1 — Operating line for linear equilibrium.** Substitute $y = m\,x$ into the operating line: $$ m \, x_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N $$ **Step 2 — Define the absorption factor.** Let $A = L / (mV)$ (the *absorption* factor, analogous to the Kremser $A$ in gas absorption). Then: $$ x_{n+1} = A\,x_n + \dfrac{y_1 - (L/V)\,x_N}{m} $$ **Step 3 — Iterate from $x_1$ (which equals $x_N$, the terminal underflow) up to $x_{N+1}$ (which equals $x_F$, the feed composition).** The closed-form geometric sum yields: $$ \boxed{\,\dfrac{x_N - y_S/m}{x_F - y_S/m} \;=\; A^{\,N+1} \,=\, \left(\dfrac{L}{mV}\right)^{N+1}\,} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $N$.** $$ \boxed{\,N + 1 \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_N - y_S/m}{x_F - y_S/m}\right)}{\ln(L/(mV))}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This equation is *the* KTU-board favourite for 14-mark numericals. Master the logarithm manipulation. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical (Model KTU 14-Mark Question) **Problem.** A counter-current leaching cascade is to treat $1000 \text{ kg/h}$ of oilseed containing $0.30$ kg oil per kg of inert meal using fresh hexane ($y_S = 0$). The retained solution per kg of inert solid is $N = 0.5$ kg solution/kg inert. The overflow rate is $V = 800 \text{ kg/h}$ of clear solution. The equilibrium is linear: $y^* = 0.95\,x$. The desired residual oil concentration in the spent solid is $x_N = 0.020$ kg oil/kg hexane. Calculate **(a)** the number of ideal stages, and **(b)** the kg of oil extracted per hour. **Solution — Step 1: Compute inert solid flow.** $$ B = \text{inert meal} = 1000\,(1 - 0.30) = 700 \text{ kg/h} $$ Wait — interpret correctly. If the feed contains **0.30 kg oil per kg of inert meal**, then per kg of feed the oil is $0.30/1.30 = 0.2308$ kg and inert is $0.7692$ kg. For $1000 \text{ kg/h}$ feed: $$ \text{Oil in feed} = 1000 \times \dfrac{0.30}{1.30} = 230.77 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ \text{Inert meal } B = 1000 - 230.77 = 769.23 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2: Compute $L$ (underflow rate).** $$ L = B \times (1 + N) = 769.23 \times (1 + 0.5) = 1153.85 \text{ kg/h} $$ So the **retained solvent** $= 769.23 \times 0.5 = 384.62 \text{ kg/h}$. **Step 3: Compute $L/(mV)$.** $$ A = \dfrac{L}{m\,V} = \dfrac{1153.85}{0.95 \times 800} = \dfrac{1153.85}{760} = 1.518 $$ **Step 4: Compute $x_F$.** The oil in the retained solution of the fresh feed = oil in feed / solvent in feed. For a *dry* meal entering, $x_F \to \infty$; we therefore must work with a finite feed. Assume the fresh solid is moist-free (typical industrial assumption: fresh meal is dry, so $x_F$ is taken as the *composition of the retained solution in the feed*). For a *dry* feed, $x_F$ is undefined, so we instead use the alternative Kremser form in terms of $y$: $$ y_{N+1} = y_1 \cdot A^{N+1} \quad \text{(when } y_S = 0 \text{)} $$ **Step 4 (corrected): Use the underflow form.** The Kremser equation in underflow composition for $y_S = 0$: $$ \dfrac{x_N}{x_F - x_N} = A^{N+1} \quad \text{(since } y_S = 0 \text{ and } y_S/m = 0 \text{)} $$ We need $x_F$. For the oil-rich feed (oil = solute, hexane = solvent) and the *retained* solution in the feed meal. The feed meal contains 230.77 kg oil and (assume for simplicity) some hexane — but a *dry* feed has zero hexane. **Standard KTU practice:** in such cases, take the *equilibrium* of the feed slurry: $x_F$ is the $x$ corresponding to the feed oil-to-solvent ratio in the *retained solution*, which equals $y_F^*$ on the equilibrium line. Here, $y_F = 0.30$ kg oil/kg solvent (interpreting feed as the oil "concentration" relative to retained solvent in the underflow). Actually, for this problem we use the simpler absolute basis: Take the feed underflow composition as the oil-to-inert ratio divided by retained solvent ratio: $$ x_F = \dfrac{0.30}{N} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.5} = 0.60 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane (in retained solution)} $$ **Step 5: Apply the Kremser equation.** $$ \dfrac{x_N}{x_F - x_N} = A^{N+1} \;\Rightarrow\; A^{N+1} = \dfrac{0.020}{0.60 - 0.020} = \dfrac{0.020}{0.580} = 0.03448 $$ $$ N+1 = \dfrac{\ln(0.03448)}{\ln(1.518)} = \dfrac{-3.367}{0.4175} = 8.066 $$ $$ \boxed{\,N = 7.07 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{round up to } N = 8 \text{ ideal stages.}\,} $$ **Step 6: Oil extracted per hour.** From the overall balance: $$ \text{Oil extracted} = \text{Oil in feed} - \text{Oil in spent solid} $$ Oil in spent solid = $B \times \dfrac{x_N}{1/N + x_N}$ — actually, using the **mass ratio of oil-to-inert** in the underflow: The underflow from stage $N$ contains $B$ kg of inert and $B \times N$ kg of solution (composition $x_N$ kg oil/kg hexane). So: $$ \text{Oil in spent solid} = \dfrac{B \times N \times x_N}{1} = 769.23 \times 0.5 \times 0.020 = 7.69 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\text{Oil extracted} = 230.77 - 7.69 = 223.08 \text{ kg/h.}\,} $$ **Recovery** = $223.08 / 230.77 \times 100 = 96.7\%$. > [!TIP] > In your lab report, present the Kremser-equation result alongside the graphical McCabe–Thiele result. They should match within ½ stage. If they don't, the discrepancy is usually due to the **linear-equilibrium assumption** versus the true curved isotherm. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Counter-Current Leaching Cascade The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **process flow** of an $N$-stage counter-current leaching rig (the typical setup in the KTU PCBBL507 lab), highlighting the **stage interactions, terminal streams, and sample points**. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph feedBank["FRESH FEED BANK"] feedSilo["FEED SILO
Crushed oilseed
or solid matrix"] end subgraph solidLoop["SOLID LOOP Direction A to B"] conv1["Conveyor / Screw 1"] stage1["STAGE 1
Solvent entry end
Mixer-Settler"] stage2["STAGE 2
Counter-current stage"] stage3["STAGE 3
Counter-current stage"] stageN["STAGE N
Solid entry end
Mixer-Settler"] conv2["Conveyor / Screw 2"] end subgraph solvLoop["SOLVENT LOOP Direction B to A"] solvTank["FRESH SOLVENT TANK
Hexane or water yS approx 0"] pump1["Pump P1"] stageN2["STAGE N solvent inlet"] stage32["STAGE 3 solvent inlet"] stage22["STAGE 2 solvent inlet"] stage12["STAGE 1 overflow outlet"] pump2["Pump P2"] evap["EVAPORATOR
Recovers solvent"] end subgraph lab["LAB ANALYTICAL SECTION"] samp1["Sample point S1 overflow y1"] samp2["Sample point SN underflow xN"] refract["Refractometer / GC"] end feedSilo --> conv1 conv1 --> stageN stageN --> stage3 stage3 --> stage2 stage2 --> stage1 stage1 --> conv2 conv2 --> waste["SPENT MEAL OUT"] solvTank --> pump1 pump1 --> stageN2 stageN2 --> stage32 stage32 --> stage22 stage22 --> stage12 stage12 --> pump2 pump2 --> evap evap --> solvTank stage12 -.-> samp1 stageN -.-> samp2 samp1 --> refract samp2 --> refract ``` ### 4.2 Stage-by-Stage McCabe-Thiele Construction (Block Schematic) The following diagram illustrates how the **operating line** and **equilibrium curve** interact graphically to determine the number of ideal stages. ```mermaid flowchart TB axis["x y DIAGRAM
x = kg solute per kg solvent in underflow solution
y = kg solute per kg solvent in overflow"] pt1["Point P1 at xF yS
Feed solid end"] pt2["Point P2 at xN y1
Solvent end"] opLine["OPERATING LINE
y n plus 1 equals L over V times x n plus y1 minus L over V times xN"] eqCurve["EQUILIBRIUM CURVE
y star equals m x for linear case"] pt1 --> opLine pt2 --> opLine opLine --> step["STAGE STEPPING
Triangles between op line and eq curve"] eqCurve --> step step --> nstag["Total ideal stages equals N"] ``` ### 4.3 Operating-Line Geometry — Reading the Slope ```mermaid flowchart LR origin["ORIGIN 0,0"] xn["Point at xN y1
LOWER RIGHT corner"] xf["Point at xF yS
UPPER LEFT corner"] slope["Slope of line equals L over V
always less than 1 in leaching
Intercept equals y1 minus L over V times xN"] origin --> xn origin --> xf xn --> slope xf --> slope ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read the diagram for your lab record:** > 1. Mark the **terminal compositions** $(x_F,\, y_S)$ and $(x_N,\, y_1)$. > 2. Draw the **operating line** between them with slope $L/V$. > 3. Draw the **equilibrium curve** $y^* = m x$ (or the experimentally measured $y^*$ vs $x$). > 4. **Step off** triangles starting from $(x_N,\, y_1)$. The number of triangles on the operating line = the number of ideal stages $N$. ### 4.4 Real Apparatus Layout (Lab Rig) ```mermaid flowchart TB hopper["HOPPER
Solid feed"] screw1["SCREW FEEDER 1"] mix1["MIXER 1 with impeller"] set1["SETTLER 1"] mix2["MIXER 2 with impeller"] set2["SETTLER 2"] mix3["MIXER 3 with impeller"] set3["SETTLER 3"] out1["OVERFLOW COLLECTION y1 y2 y3"] out2["UNDERFLOW DISCHARGE xN"] solvIn["FRESH SOLVENT INLET yS approx 0"] hopper --> screw1 screw1 --> mix1 mix1 --> set1 set1 --> mix2 mix2 --> set2 set2 --> mix3 mix3 --> set3 set3 --> out2 solvIn --> mix3 set3 --> mix2 set2 --> mix1 set1 --> out1 ``` > [!TIP] > The actual KTU lab rig (Büchi-type or similar) typically has **3–5 mixer-settler stages in series**. Verify the number on your particular rig and adjust the operating-line plot accordingly. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define counter-current leaching. Why is it preferred over single-stage leaching in industry?** **Model Answer:** Counter-current leaching is a multistage solid–liquid extraction process in which the solid feed and the solvent flow in *opposite directions* through a cascade of ideal stages, so that the most-depleted solid meets the freshest solvent and the freshest solid meets the most-enriched solvent. It is preferred over single-stage leaching because: - It maintains a **high and nearly uniform concentration driving force (Δy or Δx)** across all stages. - It achieves a **higher recovery** of solute (often > 95 %) using **less solvent** than a single stage would require. - It produces a **more concentrated overflow (rich solvent)**, reducing downstream evaporation cost. *(3 marks: definition 1, advantage 1, advantage 1)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between overflow and underflow in a leaching stage.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Overflow | Underflow | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Phase | Clear solution | Slurry | | Composition | Solute (A) + Solvent (C) | Solute (A) + Solvent (C) + Inert solid (B) | | Origin | Decanted from settler | Settled slurry discharged | | Composition symbol | $y$ | $x$ (in retained solution) | | Carrier of solute to next stage | Yes (enters previous stage) | Yes (enters next stage) | *(3 marks: 3 valid distinctions)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > In leaching numericals, students almost always **forget to subtract the retained solvent** when computing the oil in the spent solid, OR they **confuse $x$ (kg solute/kg solvent) with the kg oil per kg of inert meal**. Both errors lead to a 4–5 mark deduction. Always write a *basis statement* first. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]` **(a)** Derive the **operating-line equation** for a counter-current multistage leaching cascade under the assumption of *constant underflow* and *constant overflow*. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solid containing $0.40$ kg oil/kg dry meal is leached counter-currently with pure hexane in a 4-stage cascade. The underflow carries $0.6$ kg solution per kg of inert solid, the overflow rate is $V = 600 \text{ kg/h}$, the feed is $F = 1000 \text{ kg/h}$, and the equilibrium is linear with $y^* = 0.9\,x$. Determine the **residual oil concentration in the spent solid ($x_N$)** using the Kremser equation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a) — Operating Line Derivation:** *Assumptions:* 1. Steady state, isothermal operation. 2. Constant mass of solution retained per kg of inert solid ($N$ = constant) → $L$ = constant. 3. Constant overflow rate $V$. 4. Equilibrium stages (underflow solution in equilibrium with overflow). 5. No chemical reaction; solvent is non-volatile and inert to the solid. *Derivation:* (See Section 3.1 above for the full algebraic step-by-step.) **Result:** $$ y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N $$ *Physical interpretation:* The operating line is straight with slope $L/V$ and intercept $y_1 - (L/V)x_N$. Plotted against the equilibrium curve on an $x$–$y$ diagram, it enables the McCabe–Thiele stepping for ideal-stage determination. *(7 marks: assumptions 2, derivation 4, interpretation 1)* **Model Solution to (b) — Kremser Calculation:** *Step 1 — Compute inert solid flow.* Oil in feed $= 1000 \times 0.40 = 400 \text{ kg/h}$ (taking 0.40 kg oil/kg *dry meal*, the feed per kg of *dry meal* basis is $0.40 / (1 - 0.40) \cdot 0.40$... let us instead use **mass per kg of feed**). For simplicity, take the feed as $1000 \text{ kg/h}$ of total feed in which oil is $400 \text{ kg/h}$ and inert meal is $B = 600 \text{ kg/h}$. *Step 2 — Underflow $L$.* $$ L = B\,(1 + N) = 600\,(1 + 0.6) = 960 \text{ kg/h} $$ *Step 3 — Absorption factor $A$.* $$ A = \dfrac{L}{mV} = \dfrac{960}{0.9 \times 600} = \dfrac{960}{540} = 1.778 $$ *Step 4 — Kremser equation for $x_F$ (feed underflow composition).* Feed meal is *dry* (no hexane in fresh solid) so the retained solution in feed has $x_F \to \infty$ — but practically we define $x_F$ as the oil-to-solvent ratio of the retained solution of the *first* underflow sample (i.e., the oil-rich underflow leaving the solid-entry stage). For dry feed, the equivalent form in terms of *oil-in-solvent* in the entering solvent is: $$ x_F = \dfrac{\text{oil in feed}}{\text{solvent in feed underflow}} = \dfrac{400}{B \times N} = \dfrac{400}{600 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{400}{360} = 1.111 $$ *Step 5 — Apply the Kremser equation for fresh solvent ($y_S = 0$):* $$ \dfrac{x_N - 0}{x_F - 0} = A^{-(N+1)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_N = x_F \cdot A^{-(N+1)} $$ Wait — the form with the denominator (spent solid leaves lower) is: $$ \dfrac{x_F - x_N}{x_F} = 1 - A^{-(N+1)} $$ Using the standard Kremser for leaching with fresh solvent: $$ x_N = x_F \cdot \left(\dfrac{V\,m}{L}\right)^{N+1} = x_F \cdot A^{-(N+1)} $$ For $N = 4$: $$ A^{-(N+1)} = (1.778)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.778^5} = \dfrac{1}{17.78} = 0.0562 $$ $$ x_N = 1.111 \times 0.0562 = 0.0624 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane} $$ *Step 6 — Convert to kg oil in spent solid per hour.* Retained solvent in spent solid $= B \times N = 600 \times 0.6 = 360 \text{ kg/h}$. $$ \text{Oil in spent solid} = 360 \times 0.0624 = 22.46 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ \text{Oil extracted} = 400 - 22.46 = 377.5 \text{ kg/h} \quad (\text{recovery} = 94.4\%) $$ **Result:** $x_N = 0.0624$ kg oil/kg hexane; **recovery = 94.4 %**. *(7 marks: 1 mark basis, 1 mark $A$, 1 mark $x_F$, 2 marks Kremser substitution, 1 mark $x_N$, 1 mark recovery)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]` **(a)** With the help of a **clearly labelled $x$–$y$ diagram**, explain the **McCabe–Thiele graphical method** of determining the number of ideal stages in a counter-current leaching cascade. Mention the significance of the operating line and the equilibrium curve. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A counter-current leaching cascade with 3 ideal stages treats a feed of $500 \text{ kg/h}$ of oilseed containing $0.25$ kg oil per kg inert meal. The retained solution is $N = 0.4$ kg solution/kg inert, the solvent is pure hexane, the overflow is $V = 400 \text{ kg/h}$, and the equilibrium is $y^* = x$ (i.e., $m = 1$). Calculate the **mass of oil extracted per hour**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a) — McCabe–Thiele Method:** 1. **Plot axes**: $x$ (mass ratio in underflow solution) horizontal, $y$ (mass ratio in overflow) vertical. 2. **Draw the equilibrium curve** $y^*$ vs $x$ from experimental data or the linear relation $y^* = m x$. 3. **Mark the two terminal points**: $P_1 = (x_F,\, y_S)$ at the *solid-feed* end and $P_2 = (x_N,\, y_1)$ at the *solvent* end. 4. **Draw the operating line** joining $P_1$ and $P_2$. The slope is $L/V$ and the intercept on the $y$-axis is $y_1 - (L/V)x_N$. 5. **Step off triangles**: starting at $P_2 = (x_N,\, y_1)$, draw a **horizontal** line to the equilibrium curve, then a **vertical** line back to the operating line. Each such triangle represents one ideal stage. 6. **Count the steps** until the $x$-coordinate equals or exceeds $x_F$. The number of triangles is $N$. **Significance:** - The **operating line** is dictated solely by *material balances* (no thermodynamics). - The **equilibrium curve** is dictated solely by *thermodynamics* (no material balance). - The *intersection* of stepping gives the actual number of stages; the closer the two lines, the more stages are needed. *(7 marks: axes 1, eq curve 1, terminal pts 1, op line 1, stepping 2, significance 1)* **Model Solution to (b) — 3-Stage Counter-Current Calculation:** *Step 1 — Inert solid and underflow rate.* $$ B = 500\,(1 - 0.25) = 375 \text{ kg/h of inert meal (interpreted as 0.25 kg oil/kg *inert*)} $$ Oil in feed $= 500 \times 0.25 = 125 \text{ kg/h}$ — but this is on a *per-kg-inert* basis. So total oil $= 375 \times 0.25 = 93.75 \text{ kg/h}$ and total feed $= 375 + 93.75 = 468.75 \text{ kg/h}$. We will use the latter basis. *Step 2 — Underflow rate.* $$ L = B\,(1 + N) = 375\,(1 + 0.4) = 525 \text{ kg/h} $$ Retained solvent in underflow $= 375 \times 0.4 = 150 \text{ kg/h}$. *Step 3 — Kremser factor.* $$ A = \dfrac{L}{mV} = \dfrac{525}{1 \times 400} = 1.3125 $$ *Step 4 — Feed underflow composition $x_F$.* $$ x_F = \dfrac{\text{oil in feed}}{\text{retained solvent in feed}} = \dfrac{93.75}{150} = 0.625 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane} $$ *Step 5 — Kremser with $N = 3$ stages, $y_S = 0$:* $$ x_N = x_F \cdot A^{-(N+1)} = 0.625 \times (1.3125)^{-4} $$ $$ (1.3125)^4 = 1.3125^2 \times 1.3125^2 = 1.7227 \times 1.7227 = 2.968 $$ $$ x_N = 0.625 / 2.968 = 0.2106 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane} $$ *Step 6 — Oil in spent solid.* Retained solvent in spent solid $= 150 \text{ kg/h}$. $$ \text{Oil in spent solid} = 150 \times 0.2106 = 31.59 \text{ kg/h} $$ *Step 7 — Oil extracted.* $$ \boxed{\,\text{Oil extracted} = 93.75 - 31.59 = 62.16 \text{ kg/h.}\,} $$ **Recovery** = $62.16 / 93.75 = 66.3\%$. *(Note: only 3 stages is insufficient to achieve >90% recovery — student should comment on this in the viva.)* *(7 marks: 1 basis, 1 $L$, 1 $A$, 1 $x_F$, 2 Kremser, 1 final answer)* --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must be at your fingertips before entering the lab exam hall):** - **Leaching = solid–liquid extraction**; always **3 components** — solute $A$, inert $B$, solvent $C$. - **Counter-current** = solid and solvent move in *opposite* directions; yields maximum driving force. - **Overflow** $V$ = clear solution; **Underflow** $L$ = slurry with inert $B$ + retained solution. - **Solution retained** $N$ = kg solution / kg inert $B$ — assumed *constant* in standard derivations. - **Mass ratios**: $y$ = kg $A$ / kg $C$ in overflow; $x$ = kg $A$ / kg $C$ in underflow solution. - **Operating line** (counter-current, constant $L$ & $V$): - $\;y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_n + y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V}\,x_N$ - **Slope = $L/V < 1$** typically; **intercept = $y_1 - (L/V)x_N$**. - **Equilibrium line**: $y^* = m\,x$ for linear case; stepped off in McCabe–Thiele method. - **Kremser equation** (for linear equilibrium, $y_S = 0$): - $\;x_N = x_F \cdot \left(\dfrac{V\,m}{L}\right)^{N+1}$ - $\;N+1 = \dfrac{\ln(x_N/x_F)}{\ln(Vm/L)}$ - **Number of streams in cascade** = $N + 1$. - **Industrial examples**: oilseed–hexane (Soxhlet), beet–hot water (Betté diffuser), coffee bean–supercritical CO₂. - **Lab safety**: hexane is flammable; operate under fume hood; check all gaskets and pump seals before starting. - **Common viva questions**: 1. *Why counter-current and not co-current?* → uniform Δx, higher recovery, less solvent. 2. *What is the slope of the operating line?* → $L/V$. 3. *If $L/V$ increases, what happens to the number of stages?* → increases (operating line becomes steeper, more steps needed). 4. *Why is $y_S = 0$ in fresh-solvent leaching?* → pure solvent has no solute. 5. *Difference between leaching and washing?* → washing uses already-saturated solvent to remove *traces* of solute; leaching uses *fresh* solvent to remove the *bulk* of solute.

topics.title: Adsorption Isotherm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Adsorption Isotherm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a fluid (gas or liquid) accumulate on the surface of a solid substrate, forming a thin film of the adsorbate on the adsorbent. The solid that provides the surface is called the **adsorbent**, while the substance that gets adsorbed is called the **adsorbate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adsorption Isotherm (KTU Definition):** An *adsorption isotherm* is the equilibrium relationship, at a **constant temperature**, between the amount of solute adsorbed per unit mass of the solid adsorbent ($q_e$, in mg/g) and the equilibrium concentration of the solute remaining in the bulk solution ($C_e$, in mg/L).

In Module Experiment 2, the standard KTU 2024 lab setup uses **acetic acid (CH₃COOH) as the adsorbate** and **activated charcoal (carbon)** as the adsorbent. The isotherm is constructed by varying the initial concentration of acetic acid and determining how much acid is taken up by a fixed mass of carbon at equilibrium.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Think of a crowded bus on a wayanad (Kerala) route. The seats are limited (the surface of the activated carbon), and passengers (acetic acid molecules) are trying to find a place to "stick" on those seats. As more and more passengers board (higher concentration), the seats start filling up. Eventually, every seat is occupied and no more passengers can sit, no matter how crowded the bus becomes. The relationship between *"how many passengers got a seat"* and *"how many were still standing"* is exactly your **adsorption isotherm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaways for B.Tech Viva:**
> 1. Adsorption is a **surface** phenomenon, **not** bulk absorption (no penetration into the interior of the solid).
> 2. It is **exothermic** in nature; heat is released when molecules attach to the surface.
> 3. It is **reversible** (desorption) and reaches **dynamic equilibrium** when the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption.
> 4. The **standard metric** is the **distribution coefficient $K_d$**, expressed in L/g, representing the partitioning of solute between solid and liquid phases.

## 1.3 Standard Isotherm Models (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight)

KTU expects students to be familiar with the following two classical isotherms:

| Isotherm Model | Assumes | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | Monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with finite identical sites | Chemisorption, surface saturation data |
| **Freundlich Isotherm** | Multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface | Physical adsorption over wide concentration range |

The standard operational temperature in the KTU lab experiment is the **ambient lab temperature** (typically **30 °C** or **303.15 K**), which is held constant by allowing the system to equilibrate in a **thermostatic water bath** or simply at room temperature with periodic shaking.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Process of Adsorption

When a solid adsorbent is brought into contact with a bulk liquid containing dissolved solute, the following sequence of mass-transfer events occurs:

1. **Bulk Transport:** The solute molecules move from the bulk of the solution toward the hydrodynamic boundary layer surrounding the adsorbent particle (driven by concentration gradient).
2. **External Film Diffusion:** The solute molecules diffuse through the stagnant liquid film around the solid particle.
3. **Intraparticle Diffusion:** The solute penetrates into the pores of the adsorbent (relevant for porous activated carbon).
4. **Surface Reaction / Adsorption:** The solute molecules attach to the active sites on the internal pore surface via physical (van der Waals) or chemical (covalent/ionic) forces.
5. **Equilibrium:** After a sufficient contact time, the system reaches a **steady state** where the rate of adsorption onto the surface equals the rate of desorption from the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> The **contact time** in the KTU experiment is typically **30 minutes to 1 hour** with continuous shaking on an orbital shaker at a fixed RPM (around 120–150 rpm). This ensures equilibrium is reached before titration.

## 2.2 Mass Balance for the Batch Adsorber

Consider a beaker containing:
- Volume of solution = $V$ (in L)
- Mass of adsorbent = $W$ (in g)
- Initial concentration of solute = $C_0$ (in mg/L)
- Equilibrium concentration = $C_e$ (in mg/L)

The **amount of solute adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium** ($q_e$) is determined by performing a mass balance on the solute:

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{W}
$$

Where:
- $q_e$ = mass of solute adsorbed per gram of adsorbent (mg/g)
- $C_0$ = initial solute concentration (mg/L)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium solute concentration (mg/L)
- $V$ = volume of the solution (L)
- $W$ = mass of the dry adsorbent (g)

The **percentage removal** of solute is:

$$
\% \text{ Removal} = \frac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Linearized Form (for plotting) | Y-axis | X-axis | Constants Obtained |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m \, K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ | $C_e / q_e$ | $C_e$ | Slope = $1/q_m$; Intercept = $1/(q_m K_L)$ |
| 2 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$ | $\log q_e$ | $\log C_e$ | Slope = $1/n$; Intercept = $\log K_F$ |
| 3 | **Mass Balance** | $q_e = (C_0 - C_e) V / W$ | — | — | $q_e$ directly |
| 4 | **% Removal** | $\%R = 100 \cdot (C_0 - C_e)/C_0$ | — | — | — |
| 5 | **Separation Factor $R_L$** | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$ | — | — | Favourable: $0 < R_L < 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant meanings:**
> - $q_m$ = **maximum monolayer capacity** (mg/g) — theoretical saturation value.
> - $K_L$ = **Langmuir constant** (L/mg) — relates to enthalpy of adsorption.
> - $K_F$ = **Freundlich constant** (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$ — adsorption capacity indicator.
> - $1/n$ = **heterogeneity factor**; $0 < 1/n < 1$ indicates favourable adsorption.
> - **Standard Temperature** in lab: **303 K (30 °C)**, **1 atm**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems

Adsorption isotherms are **not just lab exercises** — they are the design foundation of:
- **Water treatment plants** in Kerala municipalities (removal of dyes, phenols, heavy metals from industrial effluent).
- **Decolourization units** in sugar refineries.
- **Pharmaceutical purification** columns.
- **Gas masks and air purifiers** (activated carbon filters).
- **Ammonia synthesis** gas cleaning in fertilizer plants.

The constants $q_m$ and $K_L$ obtained from a Langmuir plot allow an engineer to **scale up** a batch lab result to a packed-bed industrial adsorber using the **Thomas model** or **BDST (Bed Depth Service Time)** model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm Equation

**Starting Postulates (Irving Langmuir, 1918):**
1. The surface of the adsorbent has a **fixed number of identical active sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer).
3. The heat of adsorption is **constant and independent** of surface coverage.
4. There is **no interaction** between adsorbed molecules on adjacent sites.

**Step 1 — Define Surface Coverage:**

Let $\theta$ be the fraction of the surface covered by the adsorbate. Then $(1 - \theta)$ is the fraction of the surface that is bare.

$$
\theta = \frac{q_e}{q_m}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Kinetic Rate Equations:**

At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption.

Rate of adsorption = $k_a \, C_e \, (1 - \theta)$  
Rate of desorption = $k_d \, \theta$

Setting them equal:

$$
k_a \, C_e \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$
k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta
$$

$$
k_a C_e = \theta \, (k_a C_e + k_d)
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{k_a C_e}{k_a C_e + k_d}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Langmuir Constant $K_L$:**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_d$:

$$
\theta = \frac{(k_a/k_d) \, C_e}{(k_a/k_d) \, C_e + 1} = \frac{K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back $\theta = q_e / q_m$:**

$$
\frac{q_e}{q_m} = \frac{K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into the linear form for plotting:**

Cross-multiplying:

$$
q_e \, (1 + K_L C_e) = q_m \, K_L \, C_e
$$

$$
q_e + q_e K_L C_e = q_m K_L C_e
$$

Dividing both sides by $q_e \, K_L \, C_e$:

$$
\frac{1}{K_L C_e} + 1 = \frac{q_m}{q_e}
$$

A more standard rearrangement:

$$
\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}
$$

This is a **straight line** of the form $y = mx + c$ where:
- $y = C_e / q_e$
- $x = C_e$
- Slope $m = 1 / q_m$ → gives $q_m$
- Intercept $c = 1 / (q_m K_L)$ → gives $K_L$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Freundlich Isotherm Equation

**Empirical starting relation (Herbert Freundlich, 1906):**

$$
q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}
$$

**Step 1 — Take logarithms of both sides:**

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

This is also a **straight line** of the form $y = mx + c$ where:
- $y = \log q_e$
- $x = \log C_e$
- Slope $m = 1/n$ → gives $n$ (intensity parameter)
- Intercept $c = \log K_F$ → gives $K_F$ (capacity parameter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two isotherms?** Langmuir is best at low-to-moderate concentrations and describes saturation. Freundlich is purely empirical, fits data well over a wide concentration range but **does not predict a finite saturation capacity** (as $C_e \to \infty$, $q_e \to \infty$). KTU examiners often ask: *"Which isotherm is more physically meaningful?"* — Answer: **Langmuir**, because it is rooted in mechanistic assumptions.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Calculation)

**Given Data** (typical KTU lab values for acetic acid on activated carbon):

| Flask | $C_0$ (mg/L) | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $V$ (mL) | $W$ (g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 18.2 | 100 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 200 | 49.5 | 100 | 2.0 |
| 3 | 300 | 92.7 | 100 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 400 | 154.3 | 100 | 2.0 |
| 5 | 500 | 226.8 | 100 | 2.0 |

**Step A — Compute $q_e$ for Flask 1:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(100 - 18.2) \times 0.100 \text{ L}}{2.0 \text{ g}} = \frac{8.18}{2.0} = 4.09 \text{ mg/g}
$$

**Step B — Compute $C_e / q_e$ for Langmuir plot:**

$$
\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{18.2}{4.09} = 4.45 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step C — Compile all rows:**

| Flask | $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ | $\log C_e$ | $\log q_e$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18.2 | 4.09 | 4.45 | 1.260 | 0.612 |
| 2 | 49.5 | 7.53 | 6.58 | 1.694 | 0.877 |
| 3 | 92.7 | 10.37 | 8.94 | 1.967 | 1.016 |
| 4 | 154.3 | 12.29 | 12.56 | 2.188 | 1.089 |
| 5 | 226.8 | 13.66 | 16.60 | 2.356 | 1.135 |

**Step D — Linear regression on Langmuir data:**

Slope $\approx 0.0545$ → $q_m = 1/0.0545 = 18.35$ mg/g  
Intercept $\approx 3.32$ → $K_L = 1/(q_m \cdot \text{intercept}) = 1/(18.35 \times 3.32) = 0.0164$ L/mg

**Step E — Verify fitness ($R^2$):**

A good KTU lab report should report $R^2 > 0.95$ for a satisfactory fit. If $R^2 < 0.9$ for both, the data is likely compromised by **incomplete equilibration**, **inaccurate titration**, or **insufficient carbon dosage**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Separation Factor $R_L$

The dimensionless separation factor (also called the equilibrium parameter) indicates whether an isotherm is favourable:

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L \, C_0}
$$

| $R_L$ Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavourable adsorption |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear adsorption |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | **Favourable adsorption** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible adsorption |

For our example, taking $C_0 = 100$ mg/L and $K_L = 0.0164$ L/mg:

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0164 \times 100} = \frac{1}{2.64} = 0.379
$$

Since $0 < 0.379 < 1$, the adsorption is **favourable**. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Batch Adsorption Experimental Setup (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stock Acetic Acid Solution C0] --> B[Prepare 5 Standard Solutions of varying C0]
    B --> C[Add 2g Activated Carbon to each flask]
    C --> D[Place on Orbital Shaker 120 rpm, 30 min]
    D --> E[Filter using Whatman No. 1 Filter Paper]
    E --> F[Titrate filtrate with Standard 0.1 N NaOH using Phenolphthalein]
    F --> G[Determine Equilibrium Concentration Ce]
    G --> H[Calculate qe using mass balance]
    H --> I[Plot Ce vs qe for Langmuir Isotherm]
    H --> J[Plot log Ce vs log qe for Freundlich Isotherm]
    I --> K[Determine qm and KL from slope and intercept]
    J --> L[Determine KF and n from slope and intercept]
    K --> M[Compute RL and conclude favourability]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mass Transfer Pathway (Mechanistic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Liquid Phase] -->|Bulk Diffusion| B[Liquid Film Boundary Layer]
    B -->|External Film Diffusion| C[Adsorbent Particle Surface]
    C -->|Intraparticle Diffusion| D[Internal Pore Network]
    D -->|Surface Reaction| E[Active Site Adsorption]
    E -->|Desorption Reverse Step| D
    D -->|Reverse Diffusion| C
    C -->|Reverse Diffusion| B
    B -->|Reverse Diffusion| A
```

## 4.3 Data Reduction Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Titration Volumes Va, Vb] --> R2[Calculate Ce from Va-Vb]
    R3[Known C0, V, W] --> R4[Compute qe = C0-Ce x V / W]
    R2 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Compute Ce/qe and log Ce, log qe]
    R5 --> S1[Langmuir Plot: Ce vs Ce/qe]
    R5 --> S2[Freundlich Plot: log Ce vs log qe]
    S1 --> T1[Slope = 1/qm, Intercept = 1/qmKL]
    S2 --> T2[Slope = 1/n, Intercept = log KF]
    T1 --> U1[qm in mg/g, KL in L/mg]
    T2 --> U2[n dimensionless, KF in mg/g]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define the term "adsorption isotherm". Mention any two isotherm models used to describe equilibrium adsorption data.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An adsorption isotherm is the equilibrium relationship, at a constant temperature, between the amount of solute adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent ($q_e$) and the equilibrium concentration of the solute ($C_e$) in the bulk solution. **(1 mark)**
- **Langmuir Isotherm** — assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with finite identical sites. The linear form is $\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Freundlich Isotherm** — empirical model assuming multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface. The linear form is $\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e$. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Distinguish between physisorption and chemisorption. Which type does the adsorption of acetic acid on activated carbon represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Physisorption** involves weak van der Waals forces, is reversible, has low enthalpy (20–40 kJ/mol), and is non-specific. **(1 mark)**
- **Chemisorption** involves strong chemical (covalent/ionic) bonds, is often irreversible, has high enthalpy (40–400 kJ/mol), and is site-specific (monolayer). **(1 mark)**
- The adsorption of acetic acid on activated carbon is primarily **physisorption** because the process is reversible, occurs readily at room temperature, and the heat evolved is small. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **OPTION A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Langmuir isotherm is derived from the following postulates (assumptions) — **(1 mark each for any four):**
1. The adsorbent surface has a **finite number of identical active sites**.
2. Each site can adsorb **only one molecule**, leading to **monolayer coverage**.
3. The **heat of adsorption is constant** and independent of the fraction of surface covered.
4. There is **no lateral interaction** between molecules adsorbed on neighbouring sites.
5. The adsorption is **localized** — molecules do not migrate on the surface.

**Derivation (remaining 3 marks):**

Let $\theta$ be the fraction of surface covered. At equilibrium:

$$
k_a \, C_e \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta
$$

Define $K_L = k_a / k_d$:

$$
\theta = \frac{K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}
$$

Substituting $\theta = q_e / q_m$ and rearranging:

$$
\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}
$$

This linear form is used to determine $q_m$ (from slope) and $K_L$ (from intercept) by plotting $C_e / q_e$ vs $C_e$.

---

**(b)** In a batch adsorption experiment, 2 g of activated carbon was added to 100 mL of acetic acid solution of initial concentration 250 mg/L. After equilibrium, the concentration dropped to 75 mg/L. Calculate:  
(i) The amount adsorbed per gram ($q_e$).  
(ii) The percentage removal.  
(iii) The value of the dimensionless separation factor $R_L$ if $K_L = 0.025$ L/mg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Amount adsorbed per gram:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{W} = \frac{(250 - 75) \times 0.100}{2.0} = \frac{17.5}{2.0} = 8.75 \text{ mg/g}
$$

**Full marks: 3**

**(ii) Percentage removal:**

$$
\% R = \frac{(250 - 75)}{250} \times 100 = \frac{175}{250} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**Full marks: 2**

**(iii) Separation factor $R_L$:**

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L \cdot C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.025 \times 250} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.25} = \frac{1}{7.25} = 0.138
$$

Since $0 < 0.138 < 1$, the adsorption is **favourable**. **(2 marks)**

---

### **OPTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the Freundlich adsorption isotherm. How are the constants $K_F$ and $n$ determined experimentally? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Freundlich isotherm** is an **empirical relationship** given by:

$$
q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}
$$

It assumes:
- **Multilayer adsorption** occurs on a **heterogeneous** surface.
- The heat of adsorption varies exponentially with surface coverage.
- It is valid **only for low-to-intermediate** concentrations and does not predict a finite saturation value.

**Experimental determination of constants:** **(5 marks)**

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

A plot of $\log q_e$ (y-axis) versus $\log C_e$ (x-axis) yields a straight line.
- **Slope** = $1/n$ → hence $n$ is the **heterogeneity / intensity parameter**.
- **Intercept** = $\log K_F$ → hence $K_F$ is the **adsorption capacity** parameter.
- If $0 < 1/n < 1$, adsorption is **favourable**.
- The coefficient of determination $R^2$ from linear regression indicates the **goodness of fit**.

---

**(b)** The following equilibrium data were obtained for adsorption of a dye on activated carbon at 30 °C. Determine the Langmuir constants $q_m$ and $K_L$ using a linear plot. **(7 marks)**

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 5 | 12 | 22 | 40 | 65 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 4.2 | 8.9 | 14.6 | 21.1 | 26.7 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_e / q_e$ for each row:**

| $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 4.2 | 1.190 |
| 12 | 8.9 | 1.348 |
| 22 | 14.6 | 1.507 |
| 40 | 21.1 | 1.896 |
| 65 | 26.7 | 2.434 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of $C_e / q_e$ vs $C_e$:**

Applying the least-squares method (or graphical method):

- Slope $m = 0.0205$  
- Intercept $c = 1.043$  
- $R^2 = 0.992$ (excellent fit)

**Step 3 — Compute $q_m$:**

$$
q_m = \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{0.0205} = 48.78 \text{ mg/g}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_L$:**

$$
K_L = \frac{1}{q_m \cdot c} = \frac{1}{48.78 \times 1.043} = 0.0197 \text{ L/mg}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum monolayer adsorption capacity is **48.78 mg/g** and the Langmuir constant is **0.0197 L/mg**, indicating favourable adsorption. The high $R^2$ value confirms excellent agreement with the Langmuir model. **(2 marks for final interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Failing to convert $V$ from mL to L when calculating $q_e$ is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in this experiment. Always remember: $q_e$ requires $V$ in **litres**.
> 2. **Wrong linear form:** Plotting $q_e$ vs $C_e$ (instead of $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$) and trying to fit a curve. KTU strictly expects the **linearised** form.
> 3. **Forgetting $R^2$:** The examiner will deduct marks if you don't report the **regression coefficient** and the **slope/intercept interpretation**.
> 4. **Sign convention in $R_L$:** If you forget that favourable adsorption means $0 < R_L < 1$, you will lose the final 1–2 marks in Part B sub-question (b).
> 5. **No report of $n$ value interpretation:** A common Freundlich-plot mistake is to compute $n$ but not comment on whether it lies in the favourable range ($1 < n < 10$).
> 6. **Endpoint of titration:** Over-titrating past the faint pink endpoint is a **practical lab error** that costs full practical marks. Always titrate slowly near the endpoint.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adsorption** = surface accumulation; **Absorption** = bulk penetration. **Do not confuse the two.**
- **Isotherm** = constant-temperature equilibrium relationship between $q_e$ and $C_e$.
- **Langmuir** = monolayer, finite sites, gives $q_m$ (mg/g) and $K_L$ (L/mg). Plot: $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$.
- **Freundlich** = empirical, multilayer, gives $K_F$ and $n$. Plot: $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$.
- **Mass balance equation:** $q_e = (C_0 - C_e) V / W$ — always convert **V to litres**.
- **Favourability test:** $0 < R_L < 1$ for Langmuir; $1 < n < 10$ for Freundlich.
- **Standard lab conditions:** $T = 30$ °C, 2 g activated carbon, 100 mL solution, 30-min shake, titration with **0.1 N NaOH** using **phenolphthalein** indicator.
- **Indicator:** Acetic acid + NaOH → CH₃COONa + H₂O; endpoint = faint pink (phenolphthalein in basic range, pH 8.2–10).
- **$R^2$ value** must be reported; a value above 0.95 indicates a good isotherm fit.
- **Industrial relevance:** This experiment scales to **water treatment plants, dye-removal columns, and pharmaceutical purification units**.
- **Common error trap:** Plotting $q_e$ vs $C_e$ directly and drawing a smooth curve is **NOT** the KTU-accepted method — always use the **linearised** forms.
- **KTU viva favourite:** *"Why is the Langmuir isotherm considered more physically meaningful than Freundlich?"* — Answer: Because it has a **mechanistic basis** (assumptions listed) and predicts a **finite saturation capacity** $q_m$, whereas Freundlich is purely empirical and predicts infinite uptake as $C_e \to \infty$.
- **Expected lab report sections:** Aim, Theory, Apparatus, Chemicals, Procedure, Observations Table, Calculations, Graphs (Langmuir + Freundlich), Results (with $q_m$, $K_L$, $K_F$, $n$, $R^2$), Conclusion, and Precautions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric Batch Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL507 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric Batch Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Batch Drying** is defined as the unit operation in which a fixed quantity (batch) of wet solid material is dried by passing heated air over/through it at **atmospheric pressure (≈ 101.325 kPa)**, until the moisture content of the solid reaches a pre-determined safe-storage level. The process is **transient (unsteady-state)** in nature and is most commonly carried out in a **Tray Dryer (Shelf Dryer)** in the KTU undergraduate laboratory.

The driving force for drying is the **vapor pressure gradient** between the moisture at the surface of the solid and the moisture in the bulk air stream. The simultaneous transfer of **heat (from hot air to wet solid)** and **mass (water vapor from solid to air)** makes this a coupled heat-and-mass transfer operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wet Laundry on a Clothesline:**
> Imagine hanging a wet shirt on a clothesline on a hot summer afternoon. The sun heats the fabric, water from the surface evaporates, and the shirt progressively becomes drier. You cannot add more wet clothes while the first one is drying — this is a **batch** operation. The drying happens in open atmosphere (≈ 1 atm) — hence **atmospheric**. The rate of drying is high initially (lots of water on the surface) and then drops as the fabric becomes nearly dry. The same physics governs the tray dryer in the KTU lab.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Atmospheric Pressure (P):** **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg**
- **Standard Drying Air Temperature Range:** **333 K to 363 K (60 °C to 90 °C)** in lab-scale tray dryers
- **Sample Material (typical):** **Wet sand, soaked silica gel, or soaked granular solid** of known dry mass
- **Tray Surface Area (A):** typically **0.09 m² to 0.25 m²**
- **Weighing Interval (Δt):** **2 to 5 minutes** during constant rate period
- **Total Drying Time:** ranges from **45 min to 3 hours** depending on material

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to plot the **drying rate curve (N vs X)**, identify the **constant rate period** and the **falling rate period**, and determine the **critical moisture content (Xc)** and **equilibrium moisture content (X\*)** experimentally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Moisture Content Definitions (Wet basis vs Dry basis)**
- Moisture can be expressed on a *wet basis* (mass of water per unit **total mass**) or a *dry basis* (mass of water per unit **bone-dry solid mass*).
- Engineering drying calculations **always use dry-basis moisture (X)** because the bone-dry mass remains invariant during drying.

**Step 2 — Drying Stages**
- **Stage A: Initial Adjustment Period** — brief unsteady warming of the solid to wet-bulb temperature.
- **Stage B: Constant Rate Period (CRP)** — surface is fully saturated with free water. Evaporation rate is governed entirely by **external mass/heat transfer** between air and solid surface. Drying rate is **constant** and equals the evaporation rate from a free water surface.
- **Stage C: First Falling Rate Period (FFRP)** — surface starts drying out; wet patches shrink. Rate of drying **decreases linearly** with X.
- **Stage D: Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP)** — internal moisture movement (by diffusion/capillary action) controls the rate. Drying rate falls rapidly toward zero.

**Step 3 — Why the Rate Falls**
- In CRP, water reaches the surface as fast as it evaporates → **surface remains wet**.
- In FFRP/SFRP, water cannot reach the surface fast enough → **dry patches form**, and the wet area shrinks.
- The boundary between CRP and FFRP is the **critical moisture content (Xc)**.
- As t → ∞, X → X\* (equilibrium moisture content) → drying stops.

**Step 4 — Heat & Mass Balance Logic**
- Latent heat supplied by hot air = heat used to vaporize water at the wet-bulb temperature.
- Mass of water evaporated = mass loss of the wet solid.
- This gives the direct experimental link: **drying rate N = rate of mass loss per unit area**.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

Atmospheric batch drying is used in **pharmaceuticals (API drying), food processing (spices, herbs, grains), ceramics (greenware before firing), dye intermediates, pigments, polymer pellets, and detergent manufacture**. Lab-scale tray dryers are used to obtain **pilot-plant design data** (scale-up) for industrial batch dryers and continuous dryers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Wet-basis moisture, $w$ | $w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | % w.b. |
| 2 | Dry-basis moisture, $X$ | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 3 | Conversion $w \to X$ | $X = \dfrac{w}{1 - w}$ | $w$ as fraction |
| 4 | Conversion $X \to w$ | $w = \dfrac{X}{1 + X}$ | $X$ dimensionless |
| 5 | Free moisture content, $X_f$ | $X_f = X - X^{*}$ | kg/kg dry solid |
| 6 | Drying rate, $N$ | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg / (m² · h) |
| 7 | Constant Rate, $N_c$ | $N_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A}\,\dfrac{(X_1 - X_c)}{t_1}$ | kg / (m² · h) |
